Back to hub

havenbrook

The east side of Havenbrook has its own rules.

01: the beginning of something

The east side of Havenbrook had a smell.

Not garbage — people said that, people who'd never lived near it. It smelled like takeout grease and chain-link and something underneath both of those things, something mineral, something that lived in the pavement. Solar had grown up three blocks from the line DREAM drew, which meant she'd grown up knowing that smell the way you know the sound of your own building's elevator: specific, unremarkable, proof of exactly where you were.

She was fifteen the first time she understood what it meant to be noticed.

Not looked at. Not pointed at in the hallway after the incident had made her name a punchline. That kind of attention had been following her for a year by then, and she'd developed a very practiced way of letting it pass over her — eyes forward, chin level, the careful geography of someone who has learned to move through a room without occupying any of it.

Being noticed was different. Being noticed meant someone had made a decision.

She found out later that Cheng was the one who told him about her. That girl from DMHS, the one everyone said started all that stuff. She found out later that Haechan's first response was to say she didn't start anything, which she would turn over in her mind many times in the years that followed, wondering how a boy who'd never spoken to her had somehow already reached the correct conclusion. She never found a satisfying answer. She eventually stopped looking for one.

What she knew at fifteen was only this: there was a red-haired boy who leaned against the brick wall outside the school's east entrance like he had made a personal arrangement with the architecture, like the building was holding him up because it had agreed to. He was there most mornings. He was there most afternoons. Every girl Solar knew had opinions about him. She had spent significant effort not forming one.

She'd been good at it, too, right up until Tuesday of the third week of October, when she came out of the east entrance at 3:47 p.m. and found a girl named Bora and two of Bora's friends positioned directly between Solar and the sidewalk.

Solar stopped. Did the math.

There was a wall behind her. There was Bora in front of her. There was the very specific smile Bora was wearing, which Solar had learned to categorize in the same way she'd learned to categorize weather — not as a moral judgment, just a forecast of what the next ten minutes were going to cost her.

"Heard you were in Soojin's boyfriend's messages again," Bora said.

"I wasn't," Solar said.

"That's not what she said."

"I know."

The two flanking girls moved closer. One of them pulled out a phone. Solar clocked it the way she clocked everything: quickly, quietly, already calculating what it would be used for and when.

"You've got, like, zero self-respect," Bora said. "You know that? After everything last year. You just — keep going. Like it doesn't stick." She tilted her head. "Doesn't it stick?"

Solar kept her face still. It was the only currency she had left.

She had learned, in the fourteen months since the incident, that the goal was not to be safe. The goal was to appear unaffected, because unaffected was the one thing they could never fully confirm they'd ruined. The moment her eyes went wet or her voice went thin, she became the kind of evidence they were looking for. She had not given them evidence in fourteen months.

She did not give it to them now.

What happened instead was this: a voice from the wall said, "Bora."

Just that. One word. Not loud.

Bora turned. Her two friends turned. Solar, who did not want to turn — who understood, on an animal level, that turning was the beginning of a different kind of problem — turned anyway, because the voice had done something she hadn't expected. It hadn't been aggressive. It had been flat. It had been the tone of someone addressing a thing they found tedious.

The red-haired boy was still leaning against the wall. He hadn't moved. He had a cigarette in his hand, unlit, which he was turning between two fingers like a coin trick that had gone slack with familiarity. His eyes were on Bora with the particular quality of someone who is looking at you while thinking about something else entirely.

"Don't you have somewhere to be," he said. It was not a question.

Bora's smile went complicated. "Haechan —"

"I'm asking," he said, "because I can't think of a reason you'd still be standing there."

The silence that followed had a shape to it. Solar felt it press against her sternum. She watched Bora look at the two flanking girls, and then look back at Haechan, and then perform the fast, private mathematics of someone deciding whether the cost was worth it. It took about four seconds.

It wasn't.

They left.

Solar stood where she was. The wind came down the sidewalk and moved a piece of trash against the base of the wall. Haechan looked back at whatever he'd been thinking about before any of this happened.

She should have walked away. She would have told anyone who asked that she was going to walk away, that the smart thing and the safe thing and the only rational thing was to walk away, because she knew what DREAM was and she knew what that name meant and she had enough problems without adding to them deliberately.

"You didn't have to do that," she said.

He glanced over. Not surprised, exactly. Something more like patient.

"I know," he said.

"I had it handled."

"I know that too."

She waited for something else — a follow-up, a price, the part where it turned out this had been an opening rather than a kindness. She had enough experience by now to know that most things people did for you eventually had a facing, and the facing was what mattered.

He put the unlit cigarette behind his ear and pushed off the wall. "Cheng says you're good at Overwatch," he said.

The pivot was so strange she almost laughed. "What?"

"Tracer, he said." He looked at her the way someone looks at a fact they've been wanting to verify. "Is he right?"

She had no idea what to do with this. "Yes."

"Hm." He nodded, like she'd confirmed something useful. "Good to know."

Then he walked away. Hands in his pockets, unhurried, into the east side like it was just a neighborhood and he was just a boy going home.

Solar stood on the sidewalk for a long moment after he'd gone.

She had been good at not forming opinions. She had been careful about it, diligent, with the particular thoroughness of someone who knew the cost of carelessness.

She told herself she still hadn't formed one.

She was fifteen. She didn't know yet that this was what the beginning of something felt like — not electricity, not a sudden knowing, but a small persistent wrongness in the shape of an ordinary afternoon, a door that had been opened in a wall she hadn't known was there.

She didn't know yet that she would still be thinking about this sidewalk when she was twenty-two and famous and twelve thousand kilometers away.

She picked up her bag and walked home through the smell of the east side and didn't let herself look back.

She found out his name that night.

Not Haechan — she'd known that. Everyone knew that. Haechan was the name the school whispered, the name that appeared in certain conversations in a certain careful tone, the name attached to a reputation that Solar had spent a year carefully deciding was not her problem.

Donghyuck was different.

Donghyuck came in a text, ten-forty-three p.m., from a number she didn't recognize:

this is cheng's friend

She stared at her phone.

Then:

he said you play tracer

for the record i don't think that was cheng's place to share

but i'm curious so

She typed: who is this.

lee donghyuck

you probably know me as something else

She sat with that for a moment. She was lying in bed with her laptop open on an Overwatch forum, the screen the only light in the room. The house was quiet in the particular way of houses where no one was entirely at peace in the silence. She listened to it for a second — the hum of the refrigerator, her mother's TV through the wall, the specific emptiness of a place that was never quite warm.

She typed: haechan.

yeah

but you don't have to call me that

She read it twice. Then she typed: why not.

because everyone calls me that

and i didn't ask everyone for their number

She put the phone down on her chest and looked at the ceiling. She had a list of reasons this was a bad idea. She had developed the list rapidly, in the fourteen months since the incident, as a kind of ongoing project. The list was thorough. It accounted for a number of things.

It did not account for this.

She picked the phone back up.

okay, she typed. donghyuck.

The response came fast — faster than she expected:

yeah

there you go

She fell asleep with the phone in her hand. She would have denied it if pressed. She was fifteen and had learned to be careful with evidence.

But she dreamed of nothing in particular, which was not the same as dreaming of nothing, and when she woke up there was a message waiting from a number she'd saved, without deciding to, as Haechannie 🐭:

good morning

how do you play tracer without getting one-shot by every pharah

She smiled before she could stop herself. It was the worst kind — the one that arrived before her defenses did, that she couldn't revise or retract, that said something she hadn't given permission to be said.

She typed: you just don't die.

helpful

incredibly helpful

thanks for that

She pressed her face into the pillow and made no sound and felt, for the first time in fourteen months, like something was funny.

She would not call it the beginning.

She would call it a Tuesday. She would call it a coincidence of timing and geography. She would call it a lot of things, in the years that followed, that did not quite stick — that kept sliding off the surface of what it actually was.

What it actually was:

She was fifteen and surviving and her name was a joke in every hallway she walked through and then there was a boy who already knew the punchline and had decided, without asking her, that it wasn't one.

She knew, in the careful architecture of herself, that she should not let this mean anything.

She knew, with the certainty of someone who had been a fool before and survived it, that the most dangerous thing in her world right now was not the girls on the sidewalk or the whispers in the hallway.

It was the possibility that someone was exactly what they seemed.

Three blocks from the east side. The refrigerator humming. Her mother's TV through the wall.

Haechannie 🐭 in her phone.

She did not let it mean anything.

She was very, very careful about it.

The east side of Havenbrook had a smell.

She would spend the rest of her life knowing it.

That was the year she stopped looking away.


02: for the first time in forever

The thing about Jiwoo Han was that she had never once, in three years of friendship, waited to be invited into a conversation she felt was relevant to her.

She dropped into the seat next to Solar at lunch on a Thursday in November, the same week Solar had been wearing the same grey hoodie every day and eating the same corner-of-the-cafeteria lunch alone, and she said: "You can eat here if you want but you have to tell me one true thing."

Solar looked at her. "What?"

"One true thing." Jiwoo set her tray down. "About anything. I don't care what. I just hate how everyone in this school lies with their whole body and I want to eat lunch next to someone who doesn't."

Solar considered this for a moment. Then she said: "I'm better at Overwatch than anyone in this school and it's not close."

Jiwoo stared at her. Then she laughed — not a polite laugh, a real one, sudden and loud enough that two tables turned. "That's the most unhinged one-true-thing I've ever heard in my life," she said. "I love it. We're friends."

"You can't just decide that."

"I literally just did. What's your rank."

And that was that.

Three years later, Solar still wasn't entirely sure what to do with the fact that Jiwoo had walked into her life the same way she did everything else: loudly, without asking, and with an accuracy that was almost offensive. She had not required an explanation about the incident. She had not performed the careful negotiation of someone deciding whether Solar was worth the social cost. She had simply appeared — large, certain, completely uninterested in the city's assessment of Solar Lee — and stayed.

This was either the best thing that had ever happened to Solar or the most terrifying.

Most days it was both.

She didn't tell Jiwoo about Donghyuck for eleven days.

Not because she was hiding anything — she had nothing to hide, she reminded herself frequently, sometimes while rereading their entire conversation thread — but because saying it out loud would give it a shape she wasn't ready for it to have.

What it was, objectively, was: a boy she barely knew had texted her every day for eleven days, mostly about Overwatch, occasionally about other things, always as himself. He had opinions about Ana players that Solar found objectively correct. He had asked her once, at eleven-fifteen p.m., whether she thought the person you were at thirteen was still in you somewhere, and she had not been able to sleep for an hour afterward trying to figure out the right answer. He had never once mentioned anything about DREAM, or the jacket, or the reputation, or the particular gravitational field he occupied at DMHS that made everyone around him adjust without acknowledging they were doing it.

He just — talked to her. Like she was a person he was interested in. Like the rest of it didn't follow her everywhere she went.

She was not making anything of this. She was being very careful.

On the eleventh day, Jiwoo noticed anyway.

"You have a face," Jiwoo said. They were in Jiwoo's bedroom, Solar cross-legged on the floor with her laptop, Jiwoo on the bed eating crackers with the dedicated focus of someone who had already used up all her patience on actual work.

"I have a face. Yes. Everyone does."

"A new face. A face I haven't seen before." Jiwoo tilted her head. "Who is he."

Solar looked up from her screen. "That's a significant inferential leap."

"Is it wrong."

Solar looked back at her screen.

"Solar Lee."

"It's nothing."

"The face doesn't agree."

Solar closed her laptop. She did it slowly, the way you make a decision before you've finished making it — the action preceding the thought. She looked at Jiwoo's expression, which was not performing concern or performing interest, which was just — open. The same way it always was. Not waiting for anything in particular.

"He texted me," Solar said. "Out of nowhere. Like eleven days ago. And we've just — been talking."

"Who?"

Solar paused for exactly one beat. "Haechan."

The silence lasted about two seconds.

"LEE HAECHAN from DMHS Haechan," Jiwoo said, with the enunciation of someone filing this information in a very specific cabinet. "Red hair Haechan. DREAM Haechan."

"He told me to call him Donghyuck."

Jiwoo stared at her. Then she picked up another cracker. "Okay," she said, very slowly. "Walk me through the conversation."

"I'm not walking you through eleven days of texts."

"Give me the highlight reel then. What do you talk about?"

"Overwatch, mostly."

"Mostly?"

Solar looked at the window. Outside, the November dark had settled early the way it always did in Havenbrook — not dramatically, just done, like the sky had decided and moved on. "He asked me once what I thought about whether who you were at thirteen was still in you somewhere."

Jiwoo put down the cracker.

"I know," Solar said.

"Solar."

"I know."

"That is not Overwatch."

"I am aware."

Jiwoo was quiet for a moment with the particular quality of someone organizing a response rather than formulating it — like the words already existed, she just needed to decide which ones to give first. "Do you like him," she said finally.

"I don't know him."

"That wasn't the question."

Solar looked back at the window. "I don't know," she said. Which was more honest than she'd intended to be. Which was, she realized, the specific thing that happened around Jiwoo — things came out truer than she'd planned because Jiwoo was not interested in the edited version and some part of Solar had apparently understood this from the first lunch.

"Okay," Jiwoo said again, in a different register. Gentler. "So what's the problem?"

"You know what the problem is."

"He's DREAM."

"He's DREAM."

Jiwoo considered this with the seriousness it deserved, which Solar appreciated. She didn't dismiss it, didn't perform bravado about how it didn't matter, didn't tell Solar she was being dramatic. The east side was real. DREAM was real. The particular arithmetic of what it meant to be close to someone in that world was something both of them had enough proximity to understand without it being explained.

"Is he," Jiwoo said carefully, "being weird about it."

"No."

"Does he know you know?"

"Yes. I told him I'd figured it out. He just said yeah, and kept talking."

"Like it wasn't a thing."

"Like it was my information to do what I wanted with."

Jiwoo looked at her for a long moment. Outside, a car moved down the street and the headlights swept briefly across the ceiling. "That's interesting," Jiwoo said.

"I'm not making it into something."

"I didn't say you were."

"I'm just talking to someone. People talk."

"Solar." Jiwoo's voice was even. "You have not voluntarily told me a single personal thing in three years without me extracting it like a tooth. You just walked in here and told me about this unprompted within thirty seconds of me mentioning your face."

Solar opened her mouth. Closed it.

"That's interesting," Jiwoo said again. Not unkind. Just accurate.

Solar picked up her laptop and opened it back up. "I hate you," she said, without heat.

"No you don't," Jiwoo said, and ate another cracker.

She had not intended to figure it out at school.

That had been the plan — to keep these two things separate, the way she kept most things separate, in the organized compartmentalization that had become the main architecture of her life since fifteen. Donghyuck in her phone. Haechan on the sidewalk. The two names lived in different places and she had been very deliberate about not introducing them.

She was not the one who broke this arrangement. The arrangement shifted on its own, the way these things tended to.

It was a Wednesday, a week after the conversation with Jiwoo. Solar was walking to second period when she passed a group of junior girls in the main hallway, and one of them was showing the others something on her phone, and the phone said leehyuck across the notification banner.

She already knew the handle. She had known it for days — leehyuck sitting in a group chat she'd glimpsed on Cheng's phone once, and then turning up in her own notifications when Donghyuck switched to a different account to send her a clip. What she had not known, until this hallway, on this Wednesday, was that the girls were talking about him in the present tense — did you see what he posted this morning, he goes here — which confirmed the thing she had suspected and not yet verified: that Donghyuck and the east-side sidewalk and DMHS all occupied the same physical space.

She did not stop walking.

She also did not stop thinking about it.

By lunch she had Cheng's number pulled up on her screen, cursor blinking in the message field. Cheng was not, technically, DREAM. He was adjacent to DREAM in the way that a wall is adjacent to the rooms on either side of it — he had a function that both parties understood and neither fully claimed. He had been Solar's friend since eighth grade, which predated everything, and he was one of the only people in Havenbrook whose loyalty to Solar she had never had cause to question.

He was also the reason Donghyuck had her number in the first place, which she had not examined as carefully as she probably should have.

She typed: how long have you known haechan.

Cheng's response came fast: long time, why

who gave him my number

A longer pause this time.

why do you ask

cheng.

ok so. don't be mad

Solar stared at the screen.

you were having a bad few weeks and he asked about you and i may have mentioned some things and then he asked if you'd be weirded out if he texted and i said probably but you could handle it

and then i gave him your number

solar

solar please respond

ok i'm getting the silent treatment

for the record i thought it would help and also i think he likes you and you could use someone who

solar

She put the phone face-down on the table. She looked at the cafeteria ceiling — white tile, fluorescent light, the particular institutional blankness of a place that was not designed for anything to matter inside it.

he asked about you.

He had been watching out for her for two weeks before she knew his name, Cheng had said once, in passing, in a way that Solar had filed away and not retrieved. Because Cheng's friends are DREAM's concern by default, she'd assumed. Routine. The way the east side worked — you knew the names on your territory, you knew who was whose.

Not this. Not this specific, not this deliberate.

there was something about this particular girl, Cheng had said. he just kept checking.

She picked the phone back up.

She did not text Cheng back. She opened a different thread.

did cheng tell you to text me or did you ask him for my number

The response took four minutes. She knew this because she counted.

i asked

why

She typed: just wanted to know if this was a cheng idea or a you idea.

why does it matter

She thought about that. Outside the cafeteria windows, the sky was the particular flat grey of Havenbrook in November, the kind that looked the same at nine a.m. as it did at three p.m. She'd been looking at that sky for years. She had a very detailed internal map of every variation it produced.

She typed: it matters to me which one it is.

The longest pause yet. Long enough that she looked up from her phone, ate a few bites of her lunch, looked back down.

it was a me idea

cheng just told me you were going through something

i don't know, i wanted to see if you were okay

is that weird

Solar read it twice. Something in her chest performed an action she did not give it permission to perform.

She typed: you could have just said that at the beginning.

i didn't know how to say it at the beginning

i'm better in text

you already know that

yeah, she typed. i do.

She sat with that for a moment — the small particular warmth of it, the way it landed differently than it should have, the way she was doing the thing she told herself she wasn't doing. She was cataloguing him. She had been cataloguing him for eleven days and would probably continue.

She typed: your handle on everything is leehyuck.

yeah

why

some junior was showing it on her phone in the hallway today. that's how i figured out you go here.

Another pause.

wait you didn't know i went to dmhs

you never said.

*sol. He'd used it without thinking, the short version, the one that was a different kind of familiar than she'd expected. Then: *i go to dmhs. i'm in the senior hall. i'm literally outside your school every day.

i know where you are every day. i didn't know where you went.

that's kind of funny

is it.

in a terrible way yes

She closed the thread and looked out the window at the flat grey sky. The lunch bell would ring in four minutes. She had the second half of a Wednesday left to get through, and then the walk home, and then the evening with the game and the refrigerator hum and her mother's television through the wall.

And in her phone: a boy who had asked for her number specifically, who had wanted to know if she was okay, who was better in text, who had been a name before he was a face and a face before he was a voice and was only now, slowly, becoming a person.

She was being very careful about what she made of this.

She was, she admitted to herself, possibly not careful enough.

It was the following week — the second Tuesday of December, the temperature having made its final commitment to the cold — when Cheng found her after school and said, casually, in the way Cheng said most things: "Hyuck's going to the Cha's place tonight. If you wanted to come."

Solar looked at him.

"It's just a few people," Cheng said. "Mark, couple of others. Nothing serious."

"Cheng."

"I'm just saying."

"You're just saying."

He looked at her with the expression he used when he was declining to argue. "You've been talking to him for three weeks and you've only seen him from a distance. Figured you might want to —"

"I'm fine seeing him from a distance."

"Solar."

"Cheng."

He was quiet for a moment. Then: "He's better in person than from a distance. That's all I'll say."

She knew, standing there on the cold sidewalk with her bag over her shoulder and the east side two blocks east, that she should say no. She had a good list of reasons. The reasons were valid. She had been maintaining a careful and considered approach to the situation and continuing to maintain it was the sensible choice.

She said: "How long."

Cheng's mouth moved in something that was not quite a smile. "Couple hours."

"I'm not staying late."

"No one's asking you to."

She readjusted her bag. Looked at the flat grey sky one more time, as though it might offer something new. It didn't. "Fine," she said.

Cheng nodded. He was very good at not reacting visibly when he got what he wanted. It was one of his more annoying qualities.

They walked east.

The Cha's place was a third-floor apartment above a dry cleaner on Maren Street, halfway into the east side, with a stairwell that smelled like old wood and industrial solvent and the very specific smell of a building that had been holding its breath for a long time. Solar walked up the stairs behind Cheng and told herself, with some firmness, that the feeling in her chest was not anticipation. It was the stairs. She was not in great cardiovascular shape. That was a separate issue.

She heard the voices before she reached the landing.

Not aggressive — not the sound she associated, in the abstract, with rooms like this. Just people. The low back-and-forth of a few boys in a space that was comfortable with itself. Someone said something Solar didn't catch and someone else laughed, and then Cheng pushed the door open and said, "I brought Solar," with the tone of someone announcing a weather change.

She stepped in.

The room was smaller than she expected. Living room, the furniture pushed back or moved aside entirely, a TV running a game on mute in the corner. Mark Lee on the couch, who she knew from school — quiet in a way that had always seemed purposeful, like someone who had decided economy was the right approach and stuck to it. A boy she didn't recognize by the window. Jeno, who she knew by reputation, sitting backwards on a chair.

Donghyuck — Haechan — was leaning against the far wall with his arms crossed.

He was wearing a dark jacket over a grey t-shirt. His hair was the same red she'd been cataloguing from a distance for weeks, not aggressively dyed, just present, the particular shade of someone who hadn't bothered to maintain it. He had his phone in his hand and he wasn't looking at her yet, which meant he was either not aware she'd arrived or was deciding how to handle the fact that she had.

Then he looked up.

And this was the thing about the Donghyuck/Haechan problem, the specific problem Solar had been carefully not having for three weeks: she had built a very complete picture of him from text. The version in her phone was direct and occasionally funny and startlingly earnest in ways that arrived without warning and then were never referenced again, like he'd handed her something and then looked away while she decided what to do with it. She had felt, over three weeks, that she knew a person.

She had not been prepared for the person to look at her like that.

Not like anything dramatic. Not a performance. He just looked at her the way he texted — like he had already done the math on whatever he was feeling and arrived somewhere steady before she walked in.

Like she was not a surprise.

Like she was, in fact, the most expected thing in the room.

hi, he said. Not out loud. Just with the slight tilt of his chin — the same nod she'd seen him give teachers and students and occasionally doors, as if checking in with the world was a habit he'd picked up young.

She kept her face still with the practice of someone who had been doing it for two years.

"Hey," she said. Out loud, at normal volume, like a person with a working voice.

Cheng made himself immediately useful by appearing in the kitchen, which she recognized as him giving her the room even though there was no room to give in a literal sense. Mark said something quiet to Jeno. The boy by the window did not look up from his phone.

Donghyuck pushed off the wall. He moved the way he always did outside — unhurried, no wasted motion, the specific ease of someone who had never needed to perform comfort because they were actually comfortable. He stopped a few feet from her, the appropriate distance, hands going into his pockets.

"You came," he said.

"Apparently."

"Was it Cheng's idea or yours."

She looked at him. "You know why I asked you that."

The corner of his mouth moved. Not a full smile — something quieter than that, like the idea of one. "Yeah," he said. "I know why you asked."

"So I'm asking you the same thing."

He considered this with what she was beginning to recognize as his actual thinking face — not performed deliberation, just a brief stillness before he answered, the pause of someone who did not say things he didn't mean. "Cheng told me he was going to ask you," he said. "I didn't tell him not to."

"That's not the same as it being your idea."

"No," he agreed. "It's not."

She waited.

"I wanted you to come," he said. Simple. Without dressing it up. "That's the true version."

The room was not silent — the TV murmured, Mark and Jeno were in a quiet conversation that Solar was no longer tracking, somewhere in the kitchen Cheng was doing something that sounded like looking for a glass. The world was continuing to operate normally around them.

Solar looked at him for a moment. At the red hair and the dark jacket and the expression that asked for nothing back.

"Okay," she said.

He nodded once, like she'd confirmed something. Like okay was sufficient. Like he wasn't going to push past it.

He didn't say: so does that mean something? He didn't say: I knew you'd come. He didn't make it into a thing to be leveraged.

He just said: "Cheng told me you had a bad run at school today. Before you got here. Was it Bora again?"

And that was the second thing she had not been prepared for — not the looking-at-her, but the asking. The specific, accurate, already-been-paying-attention asking. The fact that there was a version of him in her phone who knew her days in the kind of granular detail that came from reading every message carefully, and the version in front of her had all the same information.

They were the same person. She knew they were the same person. She had known for three weeks.

It was somehow different, standing in the same room with both of them at once.

"It wasn't Bora," she said. "It was one of Bora's friends."

He made a sound that was not quite a word — some low register acknowledgment, the kind that meant I heard you and I'm not going to make it bigger than you made it unless you want me to.

"You going to do the thing again," she said. "With the sidewalk."

"Was the sidewalk not useful."

"I didn't say that."

"Then I might do the thing again," he said. "If it comes up."

She looked at him for one more second. Something in the room's temperature had shifted — not dramatically, not in a way anyone else would have noticed. Just by a degree. Just enough to be registered.

"Okay," she said, for the second time.

He nodded again. Then he said: "Do you want something to drink. There's probably nothing good here."

"There's never anything good at Cheng's."

"This isn't Cheng's."

"There's never anything good anywhere Cheng is."

He laughed — actual this time, not quiet, and she felt it move through the room like something given back that she hadn't known she'd lent out. Jeno looked over. Mark looked over. Even the boy by the window glanced up briefly.

She kept her face still for half a second longer than necessary. Then she stopped keeping it still.

"Fine," she said. "Show me what there isn't."

He turned toward the kitchen and she followed, and the room did not make anything of it, which she understood was because this was how things worked in a space like this — things were allowed to be what they were without being announced. Cheng was in the kitchen holding a glass and wearing an expression of supreme innocence that meant he had heard every word.

"We don't have anything good," Donghyuck told him.

"We have water," Cheng said. "We have a mysterious orange drink."

"What kind of orange drink," Solar said.

"The mysterious kind," Cheng said. "I wouldn't."

"Water," Solar decided.

Donghyuck got her a glass of water. He did not make a production of it. He filled the glass and handed it to her and then leaned against the counter with his own drink, and the kitchen was small enough that the three of them were closer than any other room would have allowed, and none of them mentioned this.

"How's the rank," Donghyuck said.

"Masters one," Solar said.

"Still on Tracer."

"Always on Tracer."

"What's the winrate."

"Sixty-one percent this season."

He looked at Cheng. "I told you."

"You told me what," Solar said.

"He said you'd either be Diamond or somewhere crazy above it," Cheng said, betraying him without hesitation. "I said he didn't know."

"I knew," Donghyuck said, without heat, without pride, just stating a thing. "You play like you refuse to lose."

Solar looked at the water glass in her hand. Something in the sentence had lodged somewhere it was going to take a while to locate. Like you refuse to lose. Not like you're talented, not like you're good — like it's a decision you've made and are continuing to make.

She didn't know what to do with how well that was seen.

"Diamond two," she said again. For no reason.

"Diamond two," he agreed. Like it was a toast.

Outside, the Havenbrook sky had completed its commitment to the dark. Somewhere in the apartment, the TV murmured over muted footage of a game none of them were watching. The refrigerator ran its familiar hum. In the living room, Mark said something that made Jeno laugh.

Solar stood in the kitchen of a stranger's apartment on the edge of the east side and held a glass of water and told herself, with less conviction than she had managed three weeks ago, that she was not making anything of this.

Cheng looked between the two of them and said nothing at all.

He was, among his many qualities, very good at that.


03: a seed planted

She was three blocks from home when her phone buzzed.

The night was the kind of cold that didn't announce itself — not wind, not rain, just a quiet dropping of the temperature that made the air feel thinner. The streetlights on the border of the east side were spaced farther apart than they were in other parts of Havenbrook, which produced long stretches of half-dark between pools of amber light. Solar walked through them without breaking stride, hood up, hands deep in her jacket pockets, the particular gait of someone who had learned to move through this neighborhood like she belonged to it without ever quite claiming it.

The buzz was a text. She knew whose before she looked.

Haechannie🐭: you good

She kept walking. Typed with one thumb.

yeah

Haechannie🐭: cheng said you left fast

i said i wasn't staying late

Haechannie🐭: you did say that

A pause. Then:

Haechannie🐭: next time stay for the mysterious orange drink

cheng said not to

Haechannie🐭: cheng's a coward

She pressed her mouth flat against something that was trying to be a smile. Ahead of her, the next streetlight made a pool of amber on the wet pavement — it had rained earlier, the kind of thin grey rain Havenbrook produced in November without drama, and the sidewalk still held it in shallow mirrors. She stepped around one.

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone in her pocket and walked the last two blocks with the specific quality of someone trying to think about nothing in particular.

She did not succeed.

Thursday at DMHS had a texture she had catalogued over three years of surviving it.

The school smelled, in November, like industrial floor cleaner and other people's lunches and the particular staleness of a building that had been breathing the same air for decades. The radiators in the main hallway knocked and hissed when the heat came on, which it did inconsistently, which meant the temperature swung between too warm and too cold in the same fifty-foot stretch. Someone had taped a paper turkey to the water fountain near the sophomore lockers. Someone else had drawn something on it that the administration had partially covered with a Post-it note, which had the effect of drawing more attention to it than if they'd left it alone.

Solar had her hood up before she reached her locker. Not because of the cold. The hood was a small geography she could control in a day that did not offer much.

She was rounding the corner into the senior hallway when she saw him.

Jihoon was leaning against the bank of lockers at the far end — the effortless lean of someone who understood that posture was information, that the way you occupied a space told everyone in it exactly how much of it you considered yours. He was wearing the grey jacket she recognized from two years of knowing exactly what grey jacket meant about what kind of day it was going to be. His phone was in his hand, screen dark, which meant he wasn't looking at it, which meant he was looking at something else.

Solar turned before he could clock her. She redirected toward the science wing without breaking stride — three extra minutes, the longer route, arriving at her locker from the opposite direction. She had done this before. She knew the exact cost in time and the exact benefit in geometry. Her body had learned the map of avoidance before her brain had fully endorsed it, and she had spent fourteen months now moving through DMHS in the choreography of someone who understood which spaces he occupied and planned accordingly.

It was not dramatic. It was just how it worked.

Down the science hallway, a group of sophomore girls were clustered outside the biology room, and as Solar passed she caught the words have you seen and the one with the red hair in the same breath, followed by the specific register of laughter that meant someone was performing for someone else. She did not slow down. She was used to the particular gravitational field that Haechan's reputation produced from a distance — the way it preceded him through conversations he wasn't part of, the way girls who had never spoken to him had very detailed opinions about him. She had been one of those girls, briefly, before she had been something she didn't have a clean word for yet.

Jiwoo was at Solar's locker when she arrived, granola bar in hand, wearing the expression of someone who had already arrived at several conclusions and was waiting with moderate patience for confirmation.

"Science wing," Jiwoo said. It was not a question.

"I needed the exercise."

Jiwoo took a bite of the granola bar. Chewed. Outside the narrow window at the end of the hall, the flat November sky sat like a lid on the day, the kind of grey that made everything look like it had been copied from another grey thing. A teacher pushed a cart past them, wheels squeaking against the tile, and two boys from the junior class turned sideways to let it pass without stopping their conversation, which was apparently about something that required a lot of hand gestures.

"He's down by the senior lockers," Solar said, because Jiwoo would establish it herself within thirty seconds anyway.

"Is Cheng here today." Jiwoo said it in the register she used for logistics — not alarmed, just gathering information.

"I don't know."

"I'll check." Jiwoo produced her phone from her hoodie pocket without looking away from Solar. There was something Jiwoo did in moments like this that Solar had never been able to fully name — a kind of orientation, the way a compass needle moved, settling in the direction of Solar's problem without requiring it to be explained. She texted with one thumb and put the phone away. "He's here. Period two."

Solar opened her locker. The inside of it was the usual archive of a person trying to keep their life organized in a twelve-by-sixteen-inch space — textbooks, a spare hoodie she hadn't needed in two weeks, the small mirror she'd taped inside the door at fifteen for reasons she no longer entirely remembered. The face that looked back at her from the mirror was composed. Level. The face she'd been developing since the incident, the one that gave nothing away.

Her phone buzzed.

She looked down. The thread was at the top, the way it always was now.

Haechannie🐭: hey

Haechannie🐭: how's thursday

She stared at that for a second. It was eight fifty-six in the morning. She had just come from the science wing. Jihoon was a hundred feet away. How's thursday was possibly the most ordinary text she had ever received and it landed with the specific weight of something that was pretending to be ordinary and wasn't.

fine, she typed. the radiators are loud today

Haechannie🐭: they're always loud

Haechannie🐭: the one outside the east stairwell sounds like it's dying

you're in the building right now?

Haechannie🐭: unfortunately

Haechannie🐭: mark made us come in before first period

Haechannie🐭: we have a thing

She had not thought, until this moment, about the texture of his mornings — that DREAM had logistics that operated before the school day started, that there were things that required all of them in a room before eight a.m., that the version of him she had in her phone coexisted with a version that had operational obligations she only understood in the abstract. It arrived like a small recalibration. Not alarming. Just — real.

what kind of thing, she typed, and then immediately: never mind, you don't have to say

Haechannie🐭: nothing interesting

Haechannie🐭: you want interesting, come find the dying radiator

i'll pass

Haechannie🐭: probably smart

She put the phone away. Jiwoo was watching her with the expression she wore when she was being deliberately generous with her silence. The first bell rang somewhere above them, a long metallic complaint that echoed off the tile, and the hallway picked up its pace — the shuffle of backpacks, the squeak of sneakers, two boys from the wrestling team moving with the particular certainty of people who had never had to calculate their route through a hallway.

"You're doing the thing," Jiwoo said.

Solar looked at her.

"The corner of your mouth." Jiwoo gestured vaguely at her own face. "Since you got on your phone."

Solar pressed her mouth flat. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"I'm sure," Jiwoo said, and finished her granola bar.

Cheng found her at lunch.

The cafeteria was running at its usual Thursday volume — the clatter of trays, the specific acoustics of a room full of people who were all performing some version of themselves for whoever was watching. Solar had her corner, the one with two walls and a clear sightline to the main doors, which she had claimed in freshman year and held through social attrition. Jiwoo usually sat here too, but Jiwoo had chorus today, which left Solar with her food and the middle distance.

Cheng sat down across from her without announcement, which was how Cheng did most things. He was wearing his green jacket, the one he'd had since sophomore year, and he had a coffee that didn't come from the cafeteria, which meant he'd been somewhere this morning before school. He set the tray down and looked at her with the particular quality he used when he had something to say and was deciding the order to say it in.

"Jihoon's been asking around," he said.

Solar set her fork down carefully. "Asking what."

"About last night." He wrapped both hands around his coffee. "Someone saw you walking east after six. Said something to the wrong person."

"How much does he know."

"Direction. Timeframe." Cheng's voice had the specific flatness he used when he was managing the dose. "Not whose place. Not who was there."

"That's enough," Solar said.

"Yeah." He didn't elaborate. They both understood Jihoon's particular methodology — he didn't need certainty to act, he only needed a thread. He would follow it with the patience of someone who had nowhere better to be, which was the most dangerous thing about him. He had no code that required him to be direct. He would just accumulate.

Solar looked at the table. Around them the cafeteria continued its Thursday performance. Two tables over, a girl was showing her phone to three other girls, all of them making the same compressed expression that meant either something funny or something cruel — from this distance it was impossible to distinguish. A teacher moved through the center of the room, hands clasped behind their back, doing the slow circuit that was about presence rather than enforcement.

"Does he know about Donghyuck," she said.

Cheng was quiet for a moment. "He knows I run with DREAM. He's always known that. He doesn't know there's anything specific." He looked at her directly. "Sol."

She looked up.

"Last night was the first time you've been anywhere east since — " he stopped. "It was notable. You understand that."

"I understand that."

"I'm not saying don't go." He turned his coffee cup a quarter turn. "I'm saying be careful about how you go. And how often."

Solar absorbed this. She had grown up on the border of the east side, which meant she had grown up understanding the specific information economy of Havenbrook — how things moved, through whom, at what speed. Jihoon had always understood it better than she did, which had been one of his primary advantages for the better part of a year and a half. She had spent fourteen months since trying to learn the same map he used, with limited success.

"He asked one of the girls in second period if you'd been seeing someone," Cheng said. He said it to the coffee cup, which she recognized as him giving her somewhere to look that wasn't at him.

She was quiet.

"She didn't know," he said. "Which is true."

"Which is also the kind of answer that tells him there might be something," Solar said.

"Yeah." Cheng finally looked up. "Look. Hyuck knows. I told him this morning."

The name dropped into the conversation with a different weight than she expected. She had known, on some level, that Cheng would have told him — they existed in the same information network, Cheng was the node that connected her to that network, this was the shape of how things worked. Knowing it and having it stated were slightly different experiences.

"What did he say," she said.

Cheng's expression did the thing that was not quite a smile. "He said okay."

"That's all."

"That's all." He picked up his fork. "Which, from Hyuck, is not nothing."

Solar looked at the window. The flat grey sky was doing its flat grey thing. She thought about the text this morning — how's thursday — and understood now that it had not been as casual as it appeared. He had already known by then. He'd been asking anyway. He'd wanted to know how she was moving through the day with the information already circulating, without telling her he knew it was circulating, giving her the space to be fine rather than making her perform being fine at him.

That was — a very specific thing to do.

She picked her fork back up. "Tell me if it escalates."

"I always do," Cheng said.

They ate in silence for a few minutes — the comfortable kind, the kind that had developed over three years of corner tables and not requiring anything from each other's quiet. A group of boys settled at the table nearest them, loud and immediate, one of them dropping a backpack that hit the floor with a sound like a minor disaster. At the far end of the cafeteria, Solar caught a flash of red hair in the crowd near the main entrance — Mark, she thought, and then beside him Donghyuck, and then a girl stepping into his path with the particular body language of someone who had rehearsed this approach.

She looked away before she could watch what happened next.

"He's popular," she said, to no one in particular.

"Very," Cheng said, with the flatness of someone describing weather.

After school, the east entrance was loud.

The end of the day at DMHS produced a specific kind of release — the doors opening and three hundred people flowing out at once, the noise level climbing and then gradually dispersing as people spread across the parking lot and the sidewalk and the stretch of grass by the chain-link fence. Someone was playing music from a phone speaker. A group of girls were taking photos in the low afternoon light. Two boys from the football team were arguing about something with the cheerful intensity of people who expected to agree by the end of it.

Solar came through the side door, the one by the gym, which emptied onto the narrower path along the building's east wall. It was quieter here — not empty, but not the main current. She had a particular route home that used this path for the first half-block before joining the sidewalk proper, and she had used it since freshman year.

She almost missed him.

Donghyuck was leaning against the building's east wall about twenty feet from the side door, one shoulder to the brick, looking at his phone. He was alone, which was unusual enough that she registered it before she registered him — Haechan was rarely alone in visible spaces, the social physics of his reputation made that nearly impossible, and yet here was a gap in the current, a small eddy in the school-day flood where he simply existed without anyone orbiting him.

He looked up when she came through the door.

She stopped. "I didn't know you came this way."

"I don't usually," he said.

She looked at him. He looked back. He had a cigarette behind his ear — unlit, the way it sometimes was — and the dark jacket, and the particular expression she was starting to recognize as his waiting face, the one that meant he had something to say and was letting her set the terms for when.

"Cheng told you this morning," she said.

"Yeah."

"You texted me anyway. The normal stuff."

"It was a normal morning." He straightened from the wall. "You were dealing with it. Didn't need me making it louder."

Solar absorbed that. Down the path, two sophomore boys cut through from the parking lot, one of them saying something that made the other one shove him, both of them laughing. The afternoon light had gone to that flat particular grey that preceded the dark, the sky making its low commitment over the rooftops of Havenbrook.

"He's going to follow the thread," she said. "That's how he works. He doesn't need to know anything specific, he just needs a direction."

"I know how he works," Donghyuck said. Not sharp. Just factual.

She looked at him. "Do you."

"I know the type." He glanced down the path, then back. "He doesn't get to know about me. About any of this." He gestured between them briefly, an economical motion, not dramatic. "We're on the same page about that."

"I wasn't sure if we were."

"We are." He said it the way he said most things — without needing her to confirm it, without requiring a response. Just putting it on the table.

She pulled her hood tighter. The cold had arrived in the particular way it did at the end of a Havenbrook school day, finding the gaps between collar and scarf, reminding you it had been there all along. Down the east side, she could already catch the smell of it — takeout grease, chain-link, the mineral thing underneath. Her map. Her whole life's geography.

"You should go," she said. "You're very visible out here."

The corner of his mouth moved. "Is that your way of saying you want me to leave."

"It's my way of saying standing with me in public is information."

He considered that with the brief stillness that was his actual thinking face. Then: "Okay." He pushed off from the wall. "I'll go the other way."

He started past her, toward the far end of the path that ran along the building's back. He had gone about five feet when he stopped and said, without turning around:

"Masters one."

She had been watching him walk away. She recalibrated. "You heard that last night."

"I did." Now he turned, walking backwards a few steps in the way that was either coordination or habit. "I've been thinking about it."

"Why."

"Because I didn't expect it." He said it simply, the way he said things that were true. "I thought you were good. I didn't think you were that."

She looked at him. The flat grey light made the red of his hair darker, and he was doing the backwards-walking thing without looking where he was going, which should have looked careless and instead just looked like someone who trusted the ground behind him.

"How good do you think I'll be," she said. She didn't know entirely why she asked it. It came out as something closer to a real question than she'd intended.

He stopped walking. He looked at her for a moment with the quality she could not entirely name — the one that arrived like he had already done the math and wasn't going to make her watch him do it.

"Better than here," he said. "Way better than here."

She held that for a second. Then she said: "Go."

He went. She listened to his footsteps on the path until the sound folded into the general noise of end-of-day Havenbrook — the music from the parking lot, the football field arguments, the school day loosening its grip — and then she started walking home.

The cold found the gap at her collar again. She didn't fix it.

That night she lay in bed with the thread open, not typing.

The television murmured through the wall. The refrigerator ran its hum. The glow-in-the-dark stars on her ceiling were barely visible — she'd put them up at twelve and never taken them down, which was a decision she had made by not making it, the way most of her decorating decisions worked.

She thought about better than here. She thought about the way he'd said it — not like a compliment designed to make her feel something, but like a fact he had arrived at through reasoning and was simply reporting. She had grown up in a house and a school and a city where most things said to her were designed to produce an effect. She had learned to parse the engineering behind words almost before she learned to receive the words themselves.

She could not find the engineering in what he said.

That was either very good or the thing she should be most careful about.

She typed: why do you text me as yourself

She read it back. Sent it before she could revise it.

The three dots appeared. Stayed for a while. She counted the seconds without meaning to.

Haechannie🐭: because everyone else gets haechan

Haechannie🐭: i don't really know how to explain it better than that

She read that twice.

everyone else gets haechan, she typed back. what does that mean

Haechannie🐭: it means

Haechannie🐭: okay so

Haechannie🐭: you know how you play Kiriko

Haechannie🐭: and no one has ever made you switch

She sat up slightly in bed.

yes

Haechannie🐭: it's kind of like that

Haechannie🐭: some things you don't perform

Haechannie🐭: you just are them

She read that three times. Then she put the phone down on her chest and looked at the ceiling and breathed, once, carefully.

She picked it back up.

that's the most you've ever said at once, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: don't make it weird

Haechannie🐭: i'm going to sleep

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She smiled at the ceiling. She pressed her mouth flat. The smile came back.

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

She locked her phone and lay in the dark and looked at the barely-visible stars and did not examine, too carefully, the specific warmth of being someone who gets the version no one else gets.

She would examine it later.

For now she just lay in the Havenbrook dark and let it be what it was.


04: rain brings the truth

The rain started on a Friday.

Not dramatically — not the kind of rain that announced itself with thunder and committed to a premise. Just the Havenbrook kind, which arrived without warning and settled in for the long term, a low grey ceiling pulling down over the city like someone closing a lid. By the time school let out, the gutters were running and the parking lot had become a thing people moved through quickly with their hoods up and their eyes down.

Solar had an umbrella. She had forgotten it in her locker.

She stood under the narrow overhang of the school's side exit and looked at the rain with the expression she used for problems she had caused herself, which was different from the expression she used for problems other people had caused her. Both were composed. The distinction was in the eyes.

She had her hood, which would be sufficient. It was seven blocks. She had walked seven blocks in worse.

She was about to step off the curb when she heard behind her: "Your locker's in the east wing."

She turned. Donghyuck was leaning against the wall of the overhang, a few feet down, in the dark jacket with his hands in his pockets. He had his hood up too, which meant he'd been out here long enough to put it up, which meant he'd been here before she arrived. She was not sure what to do with that information so she set it aside temporarily.

"How do you know where my locker is," she said.

"East wing, third floor, near the stairwell." He said it without particular emphasis. "Cheng pointed it out once."

She looked at him. "When."

"August." He looked at the rain. "First week of school."

August was before she knew his name. It was before she knew Donghyuck and Haechan were the same person. It was before any of this. She ran the arithmetic on that quickly, arrived somewhere she didn't immediately know how to categorize, and set that aside temporarily too.

"I have a hood," she said.

"It's seven blocks."

"I know how far it is."

"I know you know." He looked back at her from the rain. "I have a car."

She held his gaze for a moment. The rain came down against the pavement with a steadiness that had committed to the evening, the kind that wasn't going to stop soon. Down the parking lot, two girls were running for a car with a jacket held over their heads, laughing at the uselessness of it. A group of boys from the wrestling team were standing under a different overhang, apparently having a debate about whether the rain counted as an obstacle or a character building opportunity.

"You're going the same direction," she said.

"Close enough," he said.

She considered this. She was very careful, in the way she was careful about most things lately, about which choices she was making and what they constituted. This one was: it was raining, she had forgotten her umbrella, he was offering a car. The fact that it was his car was a detail that she was filing separately from the decision itself, in the organized compartmentalization that had kept her functional for fourteen months.

"Okay," she said.

He nodded once and pushed off the wall.

His car was a dark red, the kind of color that looked black at a distance and revealed itself up close. It was parked at the far end of the lot, which meant they crossed the full length of the parking lot in the rain, hoods up, the water finding the gaps between collar and jaw. He didn't hurry. She matched his pace without deciding to.

The interior smelled like — she registered it in layers without meaning to. Rain, from both of them, their jackets carrying it in. Something underneath that, something that was just the specific smell of someone's car after years of use, not unpleasant. And faintly, underneath that, something that was probably just the cold air they'd brought in with them.

She pulled her hood down. He started the engine.

The heat came on. The windshield wipers started their rhythm against the rain, a steady back-and-forth that filled the silence without requiring anything from it. Through the glass, the parking lot had mostly emptied — a few cars remaining, the wrestling boys still under their overhang making their philosophical points about the rain.

She looked straight ahead.

He pulled out of the lot and onto the main street, both hands on the wheel, driving with the particular ease of someone who had been doing this long enough that it required no performance. The Friday afternoon traffic was slow — the rain had made everyone cautious, headlights reflecting off the wet road in long broken lines.

"You don't have music on," she said, because it was the first neutral thing she could find.

"Do you want music."

"I didn't say that."

"I can put something on."

"I didn't say I wanted that either."

He made a sound that was not quite a laugh. "Then what did you say."

"I said you don't have music on."

"Accurate observation," he said.

The wipers went back and forth. The heater pushed warm air into the car. Outside, Havenbrook moved past the windows in the particular way it did on a rainy Friday — the storefronts with their lights on, the people moving fast under awnings, the convenience store on the corner of Fifth with the broken sign that had been broken since Solar could remember. She had grown up in the radius of these blocks. She had a specific and detailed internal map of every inch of this city and she was currently sitting in the passenger seat of a car driven by someone who apparently had his own map of her, had had it since August, had known where her locker was before she knew his name.

She had set that aside temporarily. She retrieved it.

"Why did Cheng point out my locker," she said.

Donghyuck was quiet for a moment. Not evasive — just the brief stillness that meant he was choosing something. "He wanted me to know where you were."

"Why."

"Because I asked." He stopped at a red light. The rain drummed on the roof of the car with the particular sound of rain on metal, a higher register than rain on pavement, a more immediate thing. "This was before you were Cheng's friend in my phone. You were just — someone Cheng mentioned. Someone who was having a rough go."

She looked at the side of his face. He was watching the light.

"So you looked up my locker," she said.

"I didn't look it up. I asked Cheng to point it out." He glanced at her briefly. "There's a difference."

"Is there."

"I didn't go looking for information," he said. "I just wanted to know where you were in the building. In case."

"In case what."

The light went green. He looked back at the road. "In case it mattered."

She processed that. The car moved through the intersection, the wet road hissing under the tires. She was very aware of the specific dimensions of the car's interior — the proximity of the gear shift, the angle from the passenger seat to the driver's seat, the way the heater was doing its job effectively and the car was now genuinely warm.

"That was August," she said.

"Yeah."

"We didn't talk until October."

"Yeah," he said again.

She sat with that. Two months of knowing where her locker was before they'd spoken. Two months of watching out for her, in the way he apparently watched out for people, which was not loudly and not with announcement and apparently without requiring her to know it was happening.

She should have a response to this. She was working on one.

"That's — " she started.

"Weird," he said. "I know. You don't have to tell me."

"I wasn't going to say weird."

He glanced at her. "What were you going to say."

She looked out the window. They were coming up on the border of the east side — she could feel it before she could see it, the specific quality of the streets changing, the spacing of the lights, the particular architecture of a neighborhood that had been built for function rather than impression. "I don't know yet," she said. "I'm still working it out."

"Take your time," he said.

She almost said something to that. The word that came to mind was typical, in the particular register of something that was infuriating because it was too considered. He was too considered. He made adjustments before she asked for them and let things sit without requiring her to pick them up and it was the most persistently disarming quality she had ever encountered in a person and she had been trying to build immunity to it for approximately six weeks with no measurable success.

She said nothing. He drove.

They were two blocks from her house when she saw Jihoon.

He was on the corner of Maren and Fifth — the corner outside the dry cleaner, the same block as the Cha's apartment, which meant he had been in this neighborhood on a Friday evening and she was in a car going past it. She registered him the way she registered all threats: fast, complete, the whole picture arriving at once. Dark jacket. Hands in pockets. The particular quality of someone who was not waiting for anything specific but was available to wait for something in general.

He hadn't seen her. The car was moving, the windows fogged at the edges from the heat differential, and she was on the passenger side — his side. She stayed very still until the corner was behind them.

Donghyuck's hands had shifted on the wheel. Slightly. She noticed because she was sitting a foot and a half away and she had developed, over fourteen months, a specific sensitivity to the physics of threat in a room.

"You saw him," she said.

"Yeah." His voice was level. Not tight — level, which was different. The specific level of someone who had decided on a register and was holding it.

"He didn't see us."

"Probably not." He turned onto her street. The houses here were close together, small front yards, the particular density of a neighborhood that had been built for people who needed proximity more than space. "Does he know where you live."

The question landed with a specific weight. "Yes," she said.

Donghyuck nodded. He didn't say anything else about it. He pulled up in front of her house — she hadn't given him the address, which meant Cheng, which she was adding to the running list of things Cheng had arranged without announcement — and put the car in park.

The rain was still coming down. Through the windshield, her house was the usual grey-and-white, the porch light on, the specific shape of somewhere she had lived her whole life.

"I'm going to sit here until you're inside," he said.

She looked at him. He was looking at the house.

"You don't have to," she said.

"I know."

She held that for a moment. Then she reached for the door handle. "Donghyuck."

He looked at her. This close, in the warm car, the rain on all the windows creating a specific enclosure — the particular quality of being looked at directly by him was more immediate than it was from across a room or across a parking lot. She understood, in a way that was very clear and very unhelpful, that she had been using distance as a management strategy and the car had removed that option.

"Thank you," she said. "For the ride."

He held her gaze for a second. Then he nodded, the small one, the one she had learned. "Go," he said. Not unkindly.

She went.

She was on the porch, key in the door, when she heard the car still running. She did not look back. She let herself in, shut the door behind her, and stood in the dark hallway of her house with her wet jacket and the specific feeling of someone who has just exited an enclosed space and is still carrying its temperature.

Her mother's television murmured through the wall. The refrigerator hummed.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: inside?

She looked at the screen.

inside, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

Then, after a pause:

Haechannie🐭: he's going to be a problem sol

She read that. The rain was coming down outside, she could hear it on the roof, the specific sound of it hitting the gutter above the front door. The hallway was dark and her jacket was dripping onto the mat.

i know, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: just wanted to say it out loud

She stood there for a moment longer. Then she went to hang up her jacket and thought about the way he'd said go — not like a dismissal, like the thing you say when you want someone to be somewhere safe and can't follow them there. She thought about it and told herself she was not thinking about it and went to make dinner in the kitchen while her mother's television talked to the wall.

The text came at eleven-forty.

She was already in bed, laptop open, in a ranked queue that had gone unexpectedly long — she'd been on a four-game win streak that she was not going to interrupt for sleep, because the streak was the streak and sleep could wait. The queue was in its final stretch, her team up by a significant margin on Ilios, and she was playing the way she played when she was fully in it — not performing, not calculating, just moving.

She caught the buzz on her phone from the corner of her eye.

Haechannie🐭: are you up

She finished the match — her team closed it cleanly, the post-game screen showing her stats in a column that she allowed herself to look at for exactly three seconds — and then picked up the phone.

yes, she typed. were you going to text me regardless of what i said

Haechannie🐭: probably

Haechannie🐭: what are you doing

ranked

Haechannie🐭: how's the streak

She paused. how do you know about the streak

Haechannie🐭: you mentioned you were on one last week

Haechannie🐭: four games right

She had mentioned it, in the way she sometimes mentioned things in their texts that she would not have mentioned to most people — offhand, like it was nothing, because with him it had started to feel like it was allowed to be something without being made into something. The fact that he remembered the specific number was — she set it aside.

five now, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: nice

Haechannie🐭: hey

Haechannie🐭: about tonight

She looked at the screen. The queue had found a new match and she declined it — the streak could wait.

you don't have to say anything, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: i want to though

Haechannie🐭: he's going to keep doing this sol

Haechannie🐭: the corner stuff, the asking around

Haechannie🐭: i've seen this before with people like him and it doesn't get smaller

She read that twice. There was something in it she hadn't expected — not the content, which she knew, which she had been managing for fourteen months, but the specific quality of him naming it directly. Most people in her life worked around Jihoon — Jiwoo with her logistics, Cheng with his information management, her own mother who did not know the full shape of it. Nobody said people like him. Nobody named the category.

i know how he works, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i know you do

Haechannie🐭: i'm not trying to tell you something you don't know

Haechannie🐭: i'm trying to say

A pause. Longer than usual.

Haechannie🐭: i'm trying to say you don't have to manage it alone

Haechannie🐭: that's all

Haechannie🐭: you can tell me when something happens

She held the phone. Outside, the rain was still going. It had been going for hours, the committed kind, the Havenbrook kind that set in and meant it. She could hear it on the roof and on the gutter and against her window, the specific polyphony of rain finding all the surfaces of a house.

She was sixteen years old and an only child and she had been managing things alone for fourteen months and before that, for longer — the incident being the visible part of a pattern that had started earlier, in the specific architecture of being a person who had learned to keep her own counsel because the alternative was more expensive.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay?

okay, she sent again. i'll tell you when something happens

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: thank you

She read that. She had expected something deflective — the don't make it weird, the subject change, the oblique sidestep. Not thank you, unadorned, sitting on the screen like something that had meant to be said and had finally been given the chance.

you're weird, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: extremely

Haechannie🐭: go win your sixth

She set the phone down and opened the queue and accepted the first match that came. The map was Lijiang Tower. Her team was competent. She locked Kiriko without thinking about it — had been locking Kiriko without thinking about it for three years, the choice so automatic it had long since stopped feeling like a choice and started feeling like just the thing she did, the thing that was hers.

She played the way she played on good nights — fast, clean, the version of herself that had existed before the incident and had survived it and was still, somehow, running.

She won the sixth.

She won the seventh.

She fell asleep at one-twelve with her laptop still open, the post-game screen glowing in the dark, the rain still coming down outside, and her phone face-up on the pillow with the thread at the top of it where it always was now.

She did not examine this.

She was asleep before she could.


05: an existence unnamed

The rain cleared by Saturday morning.

It left the city the way it always did — not dramatically, just gone, the sky rinsed to a flat white that held no particular promise. The streets were still wet, the gutters still trickling, the smell of Havenbrook after rain specific and familiar: wet concrete and dead leaves and the mineral thing that lived in the east side's pavement, deeper and older than anything built on top of it.

Donghyuck was on the roof of the Cha's building at seven forty-three a.m.

Not for any operational reason. The roof had a water tower and a low concrete ledge and a view of three blocks in every direction, and he came up here sometimes when he needed to think without anyone asking him what he was thinking about, which was a thing the Cha's apartment did not naturally accommodate. The building's door to the roof didn't lock properly, which was a security concern he had mentioned twice and which Cheng had acknowledged and done nothing about, and which he had therefore stopped mentioning and started using.

He had his jacket on and his hands in his pockets and a cigarette behind his ear that he had put there and not lit, which was a habit he had developed at fifteen and never fully examined. The cold was the kind that came after rain — clean, without the thick grey weight of the rain itself, just the temperature left behind when the weather moved on.

He was thinking about the corner of Maren and Fifth.

More specifically, he was thinking about Jihoon on the corner of Maren and Fifth — the particular quality of his lean, the dark jacket, the hands in pockets, the specific body language of someone who was not waiting for anything and was available to wait for anything. He had seen that posture before in people he knew better than he wanted to, and he had learned through those people what it meant: not aggression, which was easier to manage, but patience. The specific patience of someone who had decided on an outcome and was simply awaiting the conditions.

He had not said everything he wanted to say to Solar last night. He had said what was useful and stopped before he said the rest, which was what he always did, which was a skill he had learned young and practiced consistently.

What he had not said was: he had known who Jihoon was before Friday. Not from Cheng — from the school itself, which was its own information system and which had decided, two years ago, that Solar Lee was a certain kind of girl and had been maintaining that verdict ever since. DREAM paid attention to the school the way it paid attention to everything in its environment: not obsessively, just thoroughly. You knew the names. You knew the stories. You knew which ones were true and which ones were the version the hallways had agreed on.

He had known the hallway version before he knew her name. What Cheng had given him later — reluctantly, in the flat register he used for information that required care — was the part underneath it. That Jihoon was the origin. That it wasn't what it looked like. That Solar had been managing him, specifically and actively, for over a year.

He had reached out to her in October already having decided the school's verdict was wrong. He had not told her this because she had not asked, and because some things were better demonstrated than stated.

He had not said any of this because knowing it and having her know he knew it were two different things. She was careful about what she let people carry. He respected that. He was being patient.

The roof door opened.

He didn't look. He knew the sound of that door and the sound of the only person who came up here besides him.

Mark settled onto the ledge beside him — not close, the way two people who had known each other for years didn't need to be close to be together. He had a coffee in one hand, both hands wrapped around it for the warmth, his breath making small clouds in the cold air. He looked out over the three-block view for a moment without saying anything.

"You've been up here a while," Mark said finally.

"Hour, maybe."

"Mm." Mark took a sip of his coffee. He had the quality, when he wasn't running something, of a person who could be still without requiring anything from the stillness. Donghyuck had known him since they were both in something that turned out to be worse than where they were now, and this quality had been present in him even then — the ability to sit next to something without making it worse. "The guys want to do a run tonight."

"I know. Cheng told me."

"You coming."

It wasn't a question in the way it would have been a question from anyone else. From Mark it was closer to a confirmation — he already had the answer, he was just giving Donghyuck the space to say it out loud.

"Yeah," Donghyuck said. "I'm coming."

Mark nodded. Below them, a car moved slowly down Maren, windows still fogged. A dog somewhere two buildings over was announcing something to the neighborhood with the dedicated energy of an animal who had made barking its purpose. The water tower stood at the far end of the roof and made the low groaning sound it made when the temperature changed, which was a sound Donghyuck had long since stopped hearing unless he listened for it.

"Cheng said you drove her home," Mark said.

Donghyuck looked at the view. "Yeah."

"In the rain."

"It was raining."

Mark held his coffee cup with both hands and looked at the middle distance in the way he looked at things he was choosing not to make more complicated than they were. He had a face that was very good at this — neutral in a way that was kind rather than withholding, the face of someone who had decided a long time ago that you could know something about a person without using it against them.

"She live far?" Mark asked.

"Seven blocks."

"Mm."

Silence. The dog continued its announcement. Somewhere on the street below, someone started a car and let it idle before pulling away.

"She's good," Donghyuck said. He didn't know entirely why he said it. It was not information Mark had asked for. He said it the way you said things that had been sitting in your chest long enough to find their own way out.

"Yeah?" Mark's voice was even.
