"At Overwatch. The game." He looked at the view. "Competitive shooter. You play ranked, there's a ladder — Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond, Masters, Grandmaster, Top 500. Most people who've played for years sit somewhere in the middle. She's Masters one already.

Mark was quiet for a moment, processing the scale of it. "That's — how long does that take, normally."

"Years. And most people never get there." Donghyuck turned the cigarette between his fingers. "She plays Support. Kiriko. Nobody makes her switch."

"Hm." Mark held his coffee cup. "And this matters because."

Donghyuck said nothing.

"Right," Mark said.

Another silence. The particular quality of a silence between two people who had been through enough together that the silences had their own vocabulary. Mark finished his coffee and held the empty cup loosely, not looking at it.

"Hyuck," he said.

Donghyuck waited.

"You know what you're doing?" Mark asked. Not accusatory. Just asking, the way he asked things — directly, without the engineering of someone who wanted a specific answer.

Donghyuck thought about that for a moment. He thought about August, when he'd asked Cheng to point out her locker. He thought about October, when he'd asked for her number and had spent three days figuring out what to say first. He thought about the kitchen at the Cha's place, and the specific way she'd laughed, and the way he'd been very careful not to make anything of it in front of her because she would have clocked it immediately and filed it somewhere he couldn't reach.

"No," he said honestly.

Mark made a sound that was not quite a laugh. "Okay," he said. "At least you know that."

"Does it matter."

"Probably not." Mark stood from the ledge, tucked the empty coffee cup under his arm. He looked at Donghyuck for a moment with the expression that meant he had something more to say and was deciding whether it served anything. "She's got Jihoon on her," he said finally.

"I know."

"And she's not ours to protect."

Donghyuck looked at him.

Mark held the look evenly. "I'm not saying stay away. I'm saying know the difference between watching out for someone and making promises you can't keep."

Donghyuck held that. It was the most Mark had said about it, and it was the right thing, and he knew it was the right thing, which was the specifically irritating quality of Mark's advice — it was almost always correct and almost always inconvenient.

"Yeah," he said. "I know the difference."

Mark nodded. He turned for the roof door, pulling it open, and the building exhaled its stairwell smell into the cold morning air — old wood, detergent, the particular staleness of a building that had been holding its breath for a long time.

"Run's at nine," he said. "Don't be late."

The door closed behind him.

Donghyuck stood on the roof for another few minutes, looking at the three-block view, the wet streets catching the flat white sky. He reached up and took the cigarette from behind his ear. He turned it between his fingers once, twice — the coin-trick habit, going slack. Then he put it back.

He took out his phone.

Haechannie🐭: morning

He sent it before he talked himself out of it, which was the way he operated — decision first, justification never. He pocketed the phone and went downstairs.

The apartment was running at its Saturday morning register by the time he got back down to it.

This meant: Renjun on the couch with his laptop, doing something that required enough concentration to make him frown at it. Chenle cross-legged on the floor eating cereal with the dedicated focus of someone fueling for a day of nothing in particular, occasionally saying things to Renjun that Renjun responded to without looking up. Jisung in the kitchen, which was a thing that always altered the temperature of a room regardless of whether he was doing anything specific — he had the quality of a variable that changed the output of an equation just by being present in it. He was making coffee with the focused efficiency of someone who had made coffee a thousand times and found no joy in it, which was roughly how Jisung approached most domestic tasks.

Donghyuck came through the front door and Chenle looked up. "You were on the roof again."

"Observant."

"Your jacket smells like outside." Chenle went back to his cereal. "Mark came back looking very whatever. What did you talk about."

"The run tonight."

"And."

"And nothing."

Chenle looked up again with the expression he used when he knew there was more and had decided to let it go, which was an expression he used with some frequency and which Donghyuck appreciated more than he said. "Okay," Chenle said.

Renjun did not look up from his laptop. "The girl," he said, to the screen.

The room did the thing it did when Haechan was thinking — a brief, particular quiet, not uncomfortable, just aware. Chenle's spoon moved slower. Jisung, in the kitchen, poured his coffee without comment.

"What about her," Donghyuck said.

"Nothing." Renjun closed his laptop slightly. "Just. She was in the kitchen for twenty minutes and you were in the kitchen for twenty minutes and Cheng looked very pleased with himself." He opened the laptop again. "Observation."

Donghyuck crossed the room and dropped onto the other end of the couch. He picked up the remote control and turned the television on without looking at what was playing. Some morning program, a woman interviewing someone about something in a tone that was relentlessly optimistic.

"She's good at Overwatch," Donghyuck said, to the television.

Chenle stopped moving his spoon entirely.

Renjun looked up from his laptop.

Jisung came out of the kitchen with his coffee, leaned in the doorway, and looked at Donghyuck with the expression he used for things he considered operational problems. Not hostile. Just — evaluating. Jisung was always evaluating.

"Overwatch," Renjun said.

"Masters one," Donghyuck said. "Sixteen."

"Okay," Renjun said slowly. "That's — yes. Okay."

"She's good," Chenle said, with the tone of someone confirming that this was the relevant information.

Jisung drank his coffee. He said nothing. He looked at Donghyuck over the rim of the mug with the calculating quality that was Jisung's default state — not unkind, not anything, just running the numbers.

Donghyuck met his eyes and held them for a moment. Then he looked back at the television. The woman was still interviewing someone. The program had moved on to a segment about how to make something with pumpkin.

"Tonight," Jisung said finally. "Nine."

"Mark told me," Donghyuck said.

Jisung went back into the kitchen. Chenle began moving his spoon again. Renjun opened his laptop fully. The television continued its optimistic morning programming.

In Donghyuck's pocket, his phone buzzed.

He didn't reach for it for thirty seconds. Then he did, because thirty seconds was the amount of time required for it not to look like what it was.

Solar: good morning

He looked at that for a moment. Then he looked at the television, where the pumpkin segment was demonstrating something that required a mixer.

He typed back: how's the streak

The run that night was east side business — nothing dramatic, just the particular logistics of a Saturday, the movement of things and money through the specific geography that DREAM occupied and maintained. Donghyuck had been doing this long enough that it had its own muscle memory, the way anything practiced long enough developed muscle memory. He knew his part. He did it cleanly.

What he always noticed, on nights like this, was the way the city looked from inside it rather than around it. From the school or the apartment or the roof, Havenbrook had a certain quality — the flat sky, the spaced-out lights, the smell of the east side as a descriptor of a place rather than a location. From inside a run, moving through the actual streets, it was different. More specific. The particular face of a neighborhood that had been handed specific options and made the best of them, which was a thing Donghyuck understood about Havenbrook from the inside in a way he could not have articulated to someone who had not grown up in it.

He was thinking about this — about what it meant to know a place from the inside and what it cost — when his phone buzzed.

He was between stops, in the car with Cheng, who was driving with both hands on the wheel and the particular focus he used on run nights, which was a different focus from his usual register. Not tense. Just present.

Donghyuck looked at the screen.

Solar: random question

Solar: do you actually like it here

He stared at that for a second.

like what here, he typed. havenbrook?

Solar: yeah

what made you ask

Solar: i don't know

Solar: i was just thinking about it

Solar: like whether you'd leave if you could

He looked out the window. They were moving through the east side, the streetlights at their wider intervals, the architecture of a neighborhood built for function. He knew every block of this. He knew the corner store that stayed open past midnight and the one that didn't and why. He knew which roads flooded when the rain was heavy and which were safe. He knew it the way you knew your own house in the dark — by feel, without needing to look.

i think about it sometimes, he typed. leaving.

Solar: and?

and i don't know yet

Solar: that's honest

i try to be

Solar: i know you do

He looked at that for a moment. Cheng drove. The east side moved past the windows.

what about you, he typed. you staying?

A pause. Long enough that he had pocketed the phone and was looking out the window when it buzzed again.

Solar: no

Solar: i'm not staying

He read it. He read it again. He thought about better than here, which he had said on Friday and had meant as a fact and which was, he understood more fully now, also a different kind of answer to this question.

yeah, he typed. i know.

Cheng glanced at him from the driver's seat. Said nothing. Looked back at the road.

Donghyuck put his phone away and watched the east side pass and thought about the specific weight of knowing something was going and deciding to pay attention to it anyway, which was its own kind of choice, which was the choice he had apparently been making since August without quite naming it.

They made the last stop. The night went the way it was supposed to go. Nothing dramatic — just the east side on a Saturday, the specific logistics of a life built in the particular geography of Havenbrook.

He got home at eleven-thirty.

His phone had one unread message, sent while he was on the last leg of the run, at eleven-oh-four.

Solar: eight now

He smiled at the screen in the dark of the car before he could stop himself, which was the thing he had been doing for six weeks running and which he had not told anyone about and which was, he understood, the kind of information that would provoke a specific reaction from at least three of the people he lived with.

He typed back: don't stay up too late

Solar: says who

says me

Solar: not a good enough reason

go to sleep sol

Solar: make me

He stared at that. He laughed — short, involuntary, the kind that got out before the door closed on it. In the front seat, Cheng, who was still at the wheel, said nothing and looked extremely neutral.

goodnight, Donghyuck typed.

Solar: goodnight donghyuck

He got out of the car. The Havenbrook night was the particular cold of late November, the kind that had committed, the kind that was telling you winter was not a threat anymore but a fact. The sky above the east side was its usual dark — too much ambient light for stars, not enough to actually illuminate anything.

He stood outside the building for a moment with his hands in his pockets, looking at nothing, the cold finding the gap at his collar.

He went inside.

He did not examine the specific feeling of the last two hours — the run and the texts occupying the same time, the east side business and make me in the same evening, the weight of the life he was inside and the thing that was beginning to exist alongside it. He did not examine it because examining it would require him to name it and naming it would require him to decide something, and he was not ready to decide anything yet.

He just went upstairs, and the building held its breath around him, and the cold stayed outside where he'd left it.


06: library essays

December arrived the way it always did in Havenbrook — not with a particular event, just with a progressive tightening of the cold, the days going short and the dark arriving by four-thirty with the certainty of something that had already decided. The trees in the park two blocks from DMHS had been bare since mid-November but December made them look more intentional about it, the branches stripped down to their actual structure, nothing left to perform.

Solar noticed this on the walk to school Monday morning. She had been noticing things more acutely lately, which she attributed to the season and not to any other variable.

Jiwoo was waiting for her at the corner of Elm and Fifth with two coffees and the expression of someone who had been thinking since approximately six a.m. and was prepared to say all of it.

"Here," Jiwoo said, handing her one of the cups. It was gas station coffee, the kind that was aggressively warm and aggressively mediocre, which was the only kind available at six forty-five on a Monday in Havenbrook. Solar took it and they fell into step together, their breath making small clouds in the cold air.

"Thanks," Solar said.

"You're welcome." Jiwoo held her own cup in both hands. "So."

"It's six forty-five, Jiwoo."

"I know what time it is." Jiwoo looked at the sidewalk ahead of them. "I've been thinking about you and Donghyuck."

Solar drank her coffee. It was exactly as advertised — aggressively warm, aggressively mediocre. The street was quiet at this hour, the particular Monday morning quiet of a city still finding its pace. A few cars, a woman walking a dog that had the aggrieved expression of a dog who had strong opinions about early mornings. The bare trees casting their stripped shadows on the pavement.

"Nothing's happening," Solar said.

"I didn't say something was happening." Jiwoo was careful about it. "I said I've been thinking about it. There's a difference." She took a sip of her coffee. "You've been smiling at your phone. In public. Multiple times."

"People smile at their phones."

"You don't." Jiwoo looked at her sideways. "Not like that."

Solar looked at the sidewalk.

"I know who he is," Jiwoo continued, in the register she used when she was distinguishing between what she knew and what she was asking about. "I'm not asking about that. I'm asking about what's happening between you. Because something is, and I can see it, and you've been very careful not to say it out loud even to me."

Solar was quiet for half a block. The school was visible now at the end of the street, the lights in the building already on, a few early arrivals making their way up the front path. Someone had put a string of lights in one of the front office windows, the white kind, probably not sanctioned.

"I'm not making it into anything," Solar said.

Jiwoo waited.

"I'm just — talking to someone," Solar said. "We text. It's not complicated."

"Okay," Jiwoo said.

"It's not."

"I said okay."

Solar glanced at her. Jiwoo was looking at the school with the expression of someone being very deliberately serene, which was the expression she used when she had already arrived at a conclusion and was giving Solar the space to get there in her own time.

"He's careful," Solar said, before she entirely meant to.

Jiwoo's serene expression did not change. "Careful how."

"Like — " Solar tried to find the right word. "He doesn't push past what I give him. He just — receives it and waits."

"Mm," Jiwoo said, in the register of a person who had several things to say and was editing them in real time.

"What."

"Nothing."

"Jiwoo."

"I'm just noting," Jiwoo said carefully, "that you described the way someone treats you and your first word was careful. Like it's the most notable thing. Like you're surprised by it."

Solar held her coffee cup. The school was closer now. Through the front entrance, she could see the usual Monday morning geometry — students filing through the doors, the particular shuffle of people who had two days of weekend still lodged in their bones.

"I'm not surprised," Solar said.

"You said it like you were surprised."

"I said it because it's accurate."

Jiwoo accepted this with the particular grace of someone who was right and knew it and didn't need to press it further. They reached the front path and slowed automatically, the way they always did at the point where the school's social physics kicked in — who was visible, who was watching, the specific calculations of arrival.

"Is he good to you," Jiwoo said. Quietly. Not like a question, exactly.

Solar looked at her. Jiwoo was looking at the entrance, one last moment before the school day put its noise over everything. Her expression had lost the strategic quality and was just — open. The way she'd looked at Solar on the first lunch, when she'd asked for one true thing and been satisfied with whatever came back.

"Yes," Solar said. And because Jiwoo had earned it: "Yeah. He is."

Jiwoo nodded, once. Then she tipped her coffee cup toward the door. "Okay," she said. "Let's go."

Tuesday she had a free period at two.

The library at DMHS was the kind of room that had been designed for one purpose and had quietly accumulated others — there were study tables in the center, yes, but also two sagging armchairs by the window that had been there since before anyone could remember, and a corner with bean bags that the administration pretended wasn't there because acknowledging it would require having an opinion about it. The librarian, Ms. Park, had the quality of someone who had made peace with her environment and was no longer in conflict with it, which meant the library was, in practice, one of the few places in DMHS where people could exist without being managed.

Solar had a paper to finish. She had come specifically to finish it in the two hours before her mother expected her home.

She was forty-five minutes in, which was enough progress to feel good about and not enough to feel finished, when someone sat down across from her at the study table.

She looked up.

Donghyuck had a textbook open in front of him that she suspected he had not been reading before he sat down. He looked at it with the expression of someone providing evidence for a narrative. Then he looked at her.

"Free period," he said.

"Me too," she said, because it was apparently the day for obvious statements.

He looked at the textbook. She looked at her paper. Ms. Park, at the front desk, was doing something with a cart of returned books, the wheels making a small regular sound against the tile floor. Through the window, the December sky was its flat white, the bare trees in the courtyard completely still in the windless cold.

She wrote a sentence. Read it back. Changed a word.

"What's the paper," he said. Not disruptive — just the low register of someone in a library, which was a register she had not heard him use before and which was slightly different from his regular register in a way she didn't immediately have language for. Quieter. More internal. Like the volume had been turned down on the performance of being wherever he was, and this was what was underneath.

"History," she said. "Reconstruction era."

"Hm." He looked at his textbook. Turned a page without reading it. "You need quiet to write or can you work with talking."

She considered. "Quiet, usually. But it doesn't have to be total."

He nodded. He turned back to the previous page of his textbook, which suggested he had turned the first page without any purpose. Then he stopped pretending to read it and just rested his arm on the table, looking at the middle distance, and was quiet.

She wrote another sentence.

The library had its particular afternoon quality — the small sounds of a room being used for what it was meant for, the cart wheels, the occasional page turn from another student at a far table, Ms. Park reshelfing with the efficiency of someone who had done this so many times the individual books no longer needed to be consciously considered. Through the window, a bird landed on the courtyard fence, sat for a moment, and left.

She wrote another sentence. A paragraph. Her pen moved in the specific groove she got into when things were going right — not fast, just right, the words arriving in the correct order without requiring her to chase them.

At some point she became aware that she had written half a page without once checking whether he was still there.

She looked up.

He was still there. He had his chin resting on his hand, looking at the window, and there was nothing performing about it — no awareness of being watched, no adjustment when she looked. Just a person in an afternoon library being whatever he was when nobody required him to be anything specific.

She looked at him for a moment longer than she meant to.

He didn't look back. He was watching the courtyard, the bare trees, the flat white sky that had been the same for four days running. Something in his expression was — not sad, exactly. Settled. Like a question that had been living in him for a while and had decided it could wait.

She went back to her paper.

She wrote the rest of it in forty minutes, which was faster than she had written anything in two months.

When she finished, she put her pen down and rolled her shoulder, which had been holding the same position long enough to announce itself. Across the table, he had given up on the textbook entirely and was doing something on his phone, the screen tilted slightly away with the automatic privacy of someone who existed in spaces where being seen doing certain things had consequences.

"Done," she said.

He looked up. "Good?"

"Decent."

He looked back at his phone. Then he put it face-down on the table. "Reconstruction era," he said. "What's your argument."

She looked at him. "Did you actually want to know or are you being polite."

"When am I polite," he said.

She almost smiled. She didn't. "That the period's failures weren't failures of intention so much as failures of infrastructure. The political will existed. The systems to protect it didn't."

He thought about that for a moment. She had the specific sensation of being heard — not just waited out, but actually heard — which was the thing that kept happening with him and which she kept failing to build immunity to.

"Infrastructure," he said.

"Yeah."

"That's — " he paused. "That's a good word for it."

"It's the correct word for it."

"Yeah," he said. "It is." He said it the way he said things he agreed with — not readily, but with the quality of someone who had run the thought and arrived at the same place.

They sat for a moment. The library continued its small sounds around them. Ms. Park had finished with the cart and was now at her desk, reading something, her reading glasses making her look slightly more formidable than she probably was.

Solar looked at the window. The courtyard was empty now, the bird long gone, the bare trees doing their December thing — stripped, structural, more honest about what they were than they'd been in October.

She had been in this library a hundred times. She had sat at this table, specifically, more times than she could count. She had done papers and studied for tests and occasionally, in the worst months of last year, just sat here because it was the one room in DMHS that felt like a pressure valve — quiet enough to breathe in, managed enough to feel safe.

This was the first time she had ever sat here and felt the room as warm rather than simply neutral.

"Can I ask you something," she said.

"Yeah."

She looked at the window rather than at him. "Do they ever ask about me. The other guys."

A pause. The brief, specific pause that meant he was choosing something. "Renjun made an observation," he said. "Chenle confirmed it was an observation. Jisung said nothing."

"What kind of observation."

"That I was in the kitchen for twenty minutes at the Cha's place." He said it with the particular evenness of someone recounting something that had happened in a room he had been fine with and was fine with now. "That Cheng looked pleased."

She was quiet for a moment. "What did you say."

"Nothing." He looked at the table. "Chenle confirmed his observation at me and then ate his cereal. Mark already knew. Jisung running the numbers is just — Jisung."

She absorbed this. The hierarchy of DREAM's reaction, in miniature: Renjun the observer, Chenle the confirmer, Mark who was already ahead of it, Jisung who was always calculating. She had been existing in Donghyuck's orbit for six weeks and she had already, without meaning to, developed a small internal map of the people he moved through his life with.

"Does Jisung running the numbers mean something specific," she said.

Donghyuck looked at the window for a moment. "He runs numbers on everything. It doesn't mean he acts on them."

"But he might."

"He might." He said it honestly, which she appreciated. "He's careful about things that touch DREAM's business. You're not touching DREAM's business. So for now, it's just numbers."

She filed that. She filed the for now separately, which was a small and specific thing to notice and she noticed it.

"What about you," she said.

He looked at her then. The afternoon light — flat, December, already thinning — came through the library window at the angle that made the dust in the air visible, small slow particles doing their slow work. He looked at her the way he looked at things he had already processed. Not surprised. Not performing.

"What about me," he said.

"Is it just numbers for you too."

The library was very quiet. Ms. Park turned a page. At the far table, the other student gathered their things and left without looking at either of them. The cart sat by the shelving with one of its wheels still pointed slightly outward from where Ms. Park had left it.

He didn't answer right away. When he did, it was in the register that had become specifically his — the low, direct, I-mean-this register that she had learned to distinguish from his other registers because it arrived without announcement and without a follow-up hedge.

"No," he said.

Just that. One word. Sitting on the table between them like an object placed there deliberately, not requiring anything.

She held it for a moment. She looked at the window. The bare trees. The white sky. December in Havenbrook, stripped down to what it actually was.

She said: "Okay."

Not as a conclusion. Not as a question. Just — okay. The word she used when something landed correctly and she had received it and wasn't yet sure what to do with it but was willing to hold it rather than hand it back.

He looked at the window too.

They sat in the quiet of the library for another twenty minutes. Neither of them spoke. She read back over her paper and made small corrections. He had gone back to his phone, doing whatever he was doing, the screen still slightly angled. Ms. Park moved through her afternoon. The light outside thinned toward four o'clock, the day doing its December thing.

At some point she understood that this was the silence — not the absence of something, but the presence of something that didn't require words to be. She had spent two years in this school managing every room she walked into, calculating the geometry of every space, deciding what she could afford to let be visible. She had been good at it. She had been very good at it.

She could not find, in this particular moment, the thing she was supposed to be managing.

It was either very good or very dangerous and she was no longer entirely sure the distinction was as meaningful as she had thought.

She was at the east entrance at three-fifteen, waiting for the crowd to thin, when she saw Bora.

Not the specific geometry of Bora with intent — just Bora, moving through the parking lot with two of her usual people, in the middle of a conversation that had nothing to do with Solar. Solar noted her the way she noted everything in this school: fast, complete, categorized.

Bora noted her back.

It lasted about three seconds. Long enough for Solar to recognize the specific calculation running behind Bora's eyes — not hostility, exactly, but the particular awareness of someone who had made a project of Solar at some point and still had opinions about the project even in its dormant phase.

Then Bora's gaze moved past Solar. Not at Solar. Past her.

Solar turned slightly.

Donghyuck was coming through the east entrance, two steps behind her, in the dark jacket with his hands in his pockets. He wasn't looking at Bora. He wasn't looking at much of anything specifically — just moving through the end of the school day the way he always moved through things, unhurried, occupying exactly as much space as he chose.

But he was coming through the same door at the same time, and Bora's calculation had updated itself in real time, and Solar watched it happen.

Bora looked at Donghyuck. Then at Solar. Then at the distance between them — which was approximately four feet, which was nothing specific, which was also not nothing.

Something moved across Bora's expression. Not the smile from last October. Something more considered than that. Something that was filing something away.

Donghyuck came up beside Solar on the path. He didn't look at Bora.

"I know," Solar said, before he could say anything.

"I didn't say anything," he said.

"I know," she said again.

They walked out of the school's orbit together, not touching, not talking, and she felt the weight of what had just been witnessed and knew, with the particular clarity of someone who had spent a year learning how information moved in this school, that it was now moving.

The question was only what direction.

That night, at nine-forty, her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: the library thing today

She looked at the screen.

what about it, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i've been thinking about what you said

Haechannie🐭: about infrastructure

at nine forty pm

Haechannie🐭: i think about things

Haechannie🐭: systems that were supposed to protect something

Haechannie🐭: and didn't, because they weren't built right

Haechannie🐭: not because the intention was wrong

She read that. Then she read it again, more carefully, understanding that the conversation had moved sideways — that he was not talking about Reconstruction anymore and they both knew it and he had found the long way around to saying something he was not going to say directly and she was going to have to decide whether to take the long way with him or name what he was actually saying.

She chose the long way.

yeah, she typed. that's the argument.

Haechannie🐭: it's a good argument

Haechannie🐭: i think you're right about it

A pause. Then:

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: bora's going to say something

Haechannie🐭: to someone

Haechannie🐭: probably already has

She had known this since the parking lot. She had been sitting with it all evening, turning it over without finding a face it could resolve into. Yes, she typed. I know.

Haechannie🐭: it'll get to jihoon

probably

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i just wanted to make sure we were looking at the same thing

She considered that phrasing for a long moment. We were looking at the same thing. Not I'll handle it. Not don't worry. Just — confirmation that they were in the same room with the same information, which was, she was beginning to understand, the specific kind of solidarity he offered. Not resolution. Just presence.

we are, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: how's the streak

She smiled at that — at the pivot, at the way he moved from the weight of something to the smaller thing without making the transition feel like a dismissal. Like both things could be true at once. Like she was allowed to have the heavy thing and the streak in the same evening.

eleven, she typed.

Haechannie🐭:

She waited, then: Haechannie🐭: that's insane

Haechannie🐭: go play

you're telling me to go

Haechannie🐭: i'm telling you to go win twelve

She locked her phone and opened her laptop and accepted the next queue. The map was King's Row. She locked Kiriko. She played the way she played on nights when everything else was too much and the game was the one place where all of it resolved into something clean: fast, decisive, carrying her own weight.

She won twelve.

She texted him when she did, at eleven-fifty-two, because she had told herself she wasn't going to and did anyway, which was a pattern she was beginning to recognize.

Haechannie🐭: i knew

you couldn't have known

Haechannie🐭: sol

Haechannie🐭: i knew

She held the phone in the dark. The refrigerator hummed. Her mother's television had gone quiet, which meant it was late enough that she'd fallen asleep in front of it.

She thought about the library. The flat December light. The dust in the air. His answer — no — sitting on the table between them like something placed there deliberately.

She thought about infrastructure. About systems built to protect something. About the difference between intention and the architecture that either held it or didn't.

She put her phone down. She looked at the barely-visible stars on her ceiling.

She was not making anything of this.

She understood, lying in the Havenbrook dark at eleven fifty-two on a Tuesday night with a twelve-game win streak and his voice in her phone, that she had not been not-making-anything-of-this for quite some time.

She was just the last one to say it out loud.


07: the devil talks

The thing about Bora was that she was efficient.

Solar had known this since freshman year, when Bora had taken a piece of information that was only half-true and made it load-bearing within forty-eight hours. She was not malicious in the way that required effort — she was malicious the way water was, which was to say she found the path of least resistance and followed it, and the damage she did was a function of gravity rather than intention. This made her, in Solar's estimation, more dangerous than someone who had to work at it.

By Wednesday, the information from the parking lot had moved.

Solar could feel it before she could see it — the specific quality of a hallway that has adjusted itself around a new piece of information, the way people's eyes moved slightly differently, the recalibration of who was looking at her and from where. She had developed this sensitivity over fourteen months of being the subject of the school's ongoing revision of itself, and it was very accurate. It had not been wrong yet.

Jiwoo confirmed it at lunch.

"Three people asked me about it this morning," she said, without preamble, setting her tray down. "All different people. All asking the same thing in different words."

"What thing," Solar said.

"Whether you and Haechan were—" Jiwoo made a gesture that could have meant several things and meant all of them. "You know."

Solar looked at her food. The cafeteria was running its usual Wednesday volume around them, the noise level calibrated to the midweek low — not the energy of Monday's fresh start, not the anticipation of Friday, just the particular flatness of a day that was exactly what it was. Someone had left a tray of something that smelled strongly of something at the table behind them. A group of juniors were having an argument about something that required one of them to gesture repeatedly at a phone.

"What did you say," Solar said.

"That I didn't know," Jiwoo said. "Which is not technically true but is defensible." She picked up her fork. "Sol."

Solar looked at her.

"He knows," Jiwoo said quietly. "Probably already."

She meant Jihoon. She didn't have to say it. They had spent enough time over the past year navigating the geography of his awareness that he had developed a specific weight in Jiwoo's mouth, a density that didn't require the name attached.

"I know," Solar said.

"Okay." Jiwoo ate a bite of her lunch. She was being very careful — Solar could see her being careful, the specific restraint of someone who had more to say and was measuring it. "Are you scared."

Solar considered being honest and considered not being honest and chose somewhere between the two. "I'm prepared," she said.

Jiwoo held that for a moment. "That's not the same thing."

"No," Solar agreed. "It's not."

They ate in silence for a moment. At the far end of the cafeteria, near the windows, Solar could see the DREAM table — or rather, the table that had become the DREAM table through the particular process by which territory gets established without anyone formally claiming it. Mark was there, and Renjun, and two other boys she recognized but had never learned the names of. Donghyuck was not there, which she noticed the way she noticed his absence from most spaces she was in, which was a thing she was noting and not analyzing.

She looked back at her food.

"The thing about Bora," Jiwoo said, with the tone of someone completing a thought they'd been having, "is that she doesn't know why she does what she does. She just moves information because moving information is what she does. She's not a weapon." She paused. "Jihoon is a weapon."

"I know what Jihoon is," Solar said.

"I know you do." Jiwoo was very even about it. "I just want to make sure you're not treating this like the same kind of problem."

Solar looked at her. Jiwoo was looking at the cafeteria with the expression of someone who cared enough to say the hard thing and had learned that saying it once was sufficient.

"It's not the same kind of problem," Solar said.

"Good," Jiwoo said. And that was that.

He found her Thursday after school.

Not the east entrance this time — the side exit by the gym, the quieter path, the one she used specifically because it reduced the probability of running into anything she didn't want to run into. She was moving fast, head down, her bag over one shoulder and her hands jammed in her jacket pockets against the December cold, when she registered someone falling into step beside her.

She knew before she looked. She knew because of the specific quality of the footsteps — not hurried, not announcing themselves, just — there. Parallel to hers. The particular unhurried ease of someone who was not following so much as arriving at the same place.

She looked.

Jihoon.

He had his hands in his pockets too, which was the thing he always did when he wanted to appear casual — the pockets were a management tool, a way of taking up a certain kind of space without being obvious about the taking. He was a year and a half older than her and three inches taller than her, and he had learned, over the course of the year and a half they had spent in the specific proximity of a relationship she no longer named that way, exactly how to use both of those facts without appearing to use them.

"Sol," he said. Like a greeting. Like there was nothing particular about this.

She kept walking. "Jihoon."

"Haven't talked in a while," he said. He matched her pace without appearing to, which was one of his skills. "Feels like you've been avoiding me."

"I've been busy."

"Yeah." He looked at the path ahead with the expression that wasn't quite a smile — the one that meant he had already arrived at his point and was taking the scenic route to it. "I heard something interesting."

She said nothing. She kept walking. The path curved along the building's east wall and then joined the main sidewalk — another forty feet, and she'd be on the public street, which was not safety but was a different kind of terrain. She counted the distance automatically, the way she had been counting distances in his presence for over a year.

"About you and Lee Haechan," he said.

He said the name the way you said the name of something you'd already categorized. Not alarmed. Not threatened. Just — noting it, the way he noted everything, with the patience of someone who had decided an outcome and was establishing facts around it.

"People talk," she said.

"They do," he agreed. "Usually because something's worth talking about."

She said nothing.

"Haechan," he said again, in the register of someone turning something over. "That's interesting, Sol. That's a specific choice."

"I haven't made any choices."

"No?" He glanced at her sideways. "You're just spending time with DREAM for fun."

"I'm spending time with Cheng," she said. "I've been spending time with Cheng for three years."

"Right." He nodded slowly. "Cheng." He said Cheng's name with the specific quality he used for things he'd already assessed and dismissed as obstacles. "And Haechan just happened to be there."

"Yes."

"And in the library on Tuesday."

She did not let herself react to that. She kept her face level, her pace steady, the particular composure she had been building and maintaining since fifteen working the way it was supposed to. But she registered it — the library, which was inside the school, which meant someone in the library had been worth something to him, which meant his network was more granular than Bora's parking lot observation.

She had known it would be. She had been prepared for it to be. Being prepared did not make it less cold.

"Yes," she said. Her voice came out level.

"Hm." He was quiet for four steps. The path joined the sidewalk and she felt the small geography shift — more open, less corridor, the street with its cars and its movement and its different kind of visibility. "You know what's interesting," he said.

She did not ask what.

"Haechan's DREAM," he said. "And DREAM knows things about you." He said it conversationally, with the warmth of someone making an observation rather than a threat. "Interesting people to get close to. When you're already — " he paused, selecting — "complicated."

She stopped walking.

She turned to look at him. This was a choice — stopping was a choice, turning was a choice, because both gave him information about where her line was. But the alternative was continuing to walk while he said things that required her to respond to them, and the geometry of that was worse.

"I don't know what you think you're building toward," she said. She kept her voice level. "But I'm going to need you to be direct."

Something moved in his expression. Not surprise — Jihoon was rarely surprised, he had too much patience for surprise — but something more like recalibration, the adjustment of someone who had expected the walk to go longer before she stopped it.

"I'm not building toward anything," he said. "I'm just talking."

"You're never just talking."

He looked at her for a moment. The December afternoon was dark already, four-thirty and the light almost gone, the streetlights coming on in their staggered way along the block. His face in the half-dark had the quality she remembered from before she'd understood what she was looking at — handsome in a way that required context to be dangerous, the kind of face that had done a lot of work before she knew enough to read it correctly.

"I'm just saying," he said, more carefully, "that you might want to think about who you're spending time with. What they know. What they might do with it."

She held his gaze. "Is that a warning."

"It's a thought," he said.

"It sounds like a warning."

He smiled then — the real one, the one that meant he had gotten to the place he'd been heading. "You always were good at reading the room," he said. "That's what I liked about you."

She looked at him for one more second. Long enough to make it a choice, not a flinch.

Then she turned and walked away.

She did not hurry. She kept her pace even, her posture straight, the geography of her exit as composed as she could make it. She heard him behind her — not following, just standing there, which was its own kind of message. The sound of someone who was in no hurry because he had all the time he needed.

She turned the corner and walked two blocks before she let herself breathe properly.

She texted Cheng from the corner of Fourth and Maren, hands cold because she'd forgotten her gloves, the screen bright in the early dark.

he talked to me today, she typed.

The response came in under a minute.

Cheng: i heard

Cheng: where are you

walking home

Cheng: alone?

yes

Cheng: okay

Cheng: go straight home sol

Cheng: i mean it

She pocketed her phone and walked. The east side moved around her in the particular way it did at this hour — people heading home from things, the convenience stores lit up, the smell of whatever was being cooked in the apartments above the storefronts finding its way down to street level. A car rolled past with music she didn't recognize coming from the open window despite the cold. Two kids on bikes moved against the foot traffic with the specific disregard of children who had not yet learned to care about being in the way.

She was half a block from home when her phone buzzed again.

Not Cheng.

Haechannie🐭: hey

She looked at the screen. The timing was too close to be coincidence. Cheng had told him — or he had his own network, which was the more accurate answer, which was a thing she kept forgetting to fully account for.

did cheng tell you, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: no

Haechannie🐭: i was there

She stopped on the sidewalk.

where

Haechannie🐭: i saw you turn off the side exit

Haechannie🐭: and i saw him follow

Her hands were cold and the screen was very bright in the dark and she stood on the sidewalk half a block from her house and read that twice.

you were watching, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: not watching

Haechannie🐭: i was on the other side of the building

Haechannie🐭: i saw it happen

Haechannie🐭: sol are you home

She looked up from the screen. The street was the street — her street, the specific architecture of it, the house with the porch light on that was her house. Half a block away.

almost, she typed. thirty seconds.

She walked the thirty seconds. She let herself in. She stood in the hallway the way she had been standing in the hallway lately, in the particular way of someone who needed to confirm the shape of her own life before she could operate inside it again.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: what did he say

She set her bag down and sat on the floor of the hallway with her back against the wall, which was not how she usually used the hallway but was what her legs had decided. The cold from outside was still in her jacket. Through the wall, the television murmured.

he said to think about who i'm spending time with, she typed. what they know. what they might do with it.

A pause. Long enough to mean he was thinking rather than composing.

Haechannie🐭: that's not a threat

Haechannie🐭: that's a setup for a threat

Haechannie🐭: he's telling you he has something

i know, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: does he

She thought about the library. The specific way he'd said on Tuesday — not i heard you were in the library but the day, the precise detail, the kind of specificity that came from someone who had asked a follow-up question.

yes, she typed. i think so.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i need you to tell me something

what

Haechannie🐭: is there anything

Haechannie🐭: anything he could actually use

She held her phone. The hallway was cold because she hadn't taken her jacket off yet. The television talked to the wall. The refrigerator ran its hum.

There was something. She had known there was something since she was fourteen years old and had watched Jihoon take a piece of her life and make it into a weapon, and she had known since then that the footage existed somewhere and would always exist somewhere and was the specific kind of insurance that someone like Jihoon did not give up without reason.

She thought about how to answer. She thought about all the careful management of the last fourteen months, the geography of avoidance, the performance of composure. She thought about you can tell me when something happens, which he had said on a rainy Friday night over text, which she had agreed to and had been slowly actually meaning.

yes, she typed. there is.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: you don't have to tell me what

Haechannie🐭: i just needed to know if it was real

it's real

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i'm not going anywhere

She read that. She read it the way she read things that needed several passes to fully receive — not because it was complicated, but because it was simple in a way that required her to be still to let it in. I'm not going anywhere. Not I'll fix it. Not I'll handle it. Just — presence. The announcement of continued presence as the only thing being offered, which was, she was beginning to understand, the most he ever offered anyone and the most anyone had ever offered her.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: take your jacket off sol

Haechannie🐭: you've been wearing it this whole time

She looked down. She was still wearing her jacket. She had been sitting on the floor of her hallway in her jacket for however many minutes this conversation had been.

She took the jacket off. She held it in her lap.

how did you know, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: because you would've said something warmer by now

She sat with that. The hallway was slightly warmer without the cold jacket. Through the wall, the television said something that required a laugh track. The refrigerator hummed.

She thought about the word warmer and what it meant when used by someone who paid attention to the specific temperature of her responses, who had been paying that kind of attention since August, who was currently sitting somewhere in the east side having apparently tracked her route home close enough to know which exit she'd used and who had followed her.

She thought about all of this and put it in the file where she'd been keeping the other things and understood the file was getting too full to maintain as a temporary measure.

thank you, she typed. for watching.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Then, after a beat:

Haechannie🐭: don't thank me

why not

Haechannie🐭: because it's not something i was going to stop doing regardless

She held the phone. The file was too full. She was going to have to decide what to do with it soon.

Not tonight. Tonight she was sitting on the hallway floor with her jacket in her lap and the particular specific warmth of being someone whose exits were being watched by someone who didn't require her to ask.

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

Haechannie🐭: lock the door

She looked up. The door was locked. She had locked it automatically, by habit, without thinking — the habit of fourteen months of being careful. She almost typed it's locked and then understood he probably knew that too, that the reminder was not really about the door.

She got up off the floor. She hung up her jacket. She went into the kitchen and made something to eat in the murmuring company of her mother's television through the wall, and the refrigerator's hum, and the specific quiet of a December night in Havenbrook, and somewhere in the east side, someone who was not going anywhere.

Friday morning the temperature dropped six degrees.

Solar felt it when she opened the front door — the particular shock of a cold that had committed overnight, the kind that found your face immediately and made your eyes water in the first thirty seconds. She pulled her scarf up and her hood down over it and took stock of the grey sky, which was the color of something that had decided.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: it's cold

yes, she typed. thank you for the update.

Haechannie🐭: i'm just saying

Haechannie🐭: dress accordingly

i'm already outside

Haechannie🐭: sol

i have a scarf

Haechannie🐭:

Haechannie🐭: okay fine

She pressed her mouth flat against something she was no longer trying to call nothing. She walked to school through the December cold, her breath making small regular clouds, the bare trees in the park doing their honest December thing, and she thought about I'm not going anywhere and don't thank me and lock the door, and she thought about the file that was too full, and she thought about the word that was sitting at the center of all of it waiting to be named.

She was not going to name it today.

But she was going to stop pretending she didn't know what it was.

That felt, in the specific careful accounting of Solar Lee, like enough for a Friday.


08: she'll always come back

The week after Jihoon's warning was quiet in the specific way that things were quiet before they weren't.

Solar had learned to read this kind of quiet. It was not the absence of something — it was the presence of something holding its breath, a pressure system moving in from a direction you couldn't see yet, the specific atmospheric weight of a city that was about to do something and hadn't done it yet. She had felt this quality in the weeks before the incident two years ago. She felt it now, in the particular way December settled over Havenbrook — the cold committed, the days so short the dark was present for more hours than the light, the school hallways running at a low tension that had no specific origin.

She went to school. She did her routes. She checked the geometry of every hallway before she entered it.

Nothing happened.

Which was the thing about Jihoon — he was patient in the way that made patience itself a weapon. He did not rush. He had never rushed. He had spent a year and a half teaching her, slowly and completely, that the waiting was the point. The waiting meant he had something and was deciding when to use it, which meant the threat was always present even when the action wasn't, which meant she was always calculating even when there was nothing to calculate.

She was good at it now. That didn't mean it was free.

Saturday she went to the Cha's apartment for the second time.

Not at Cheng's invitation specifically — the invitation had been open since October, a standing arrangement that she had been navigating carefully, and she had decided, sitting in her room on Friday night with the file too full and the week's quiet pressing on her, that careful navigation was not the same as not going. She was allowed to go. She had decided she was going. She went.

Cheng was there, and Mark, and Renjun on the couch with the same laptop position as last time, which made her think he was conducting an ongoing project that the couch was specifically required for. Chenle was not there, which Renjun explained by gesturing vaguely at the window. Jisung was in the kitchen doing something that produced a sound like two objects disagreeing with each other.

Donghyuck was not there when she arrived.

She knew this immediately and made nothing of it and sat on the far end of the couch near the window, close enough to the lamp to read if she wanted to, which she did want to and had brought a book for, which was a choice she had made deliberately and which Cheng had acknowledged with the extremely neutral expression he used when he was being very diplomatic about something.

Mark said hello. Renjun raised two fingers from his laptop. The kitchen continued its disagreement.

She read for forty minutes. The lamp made a warm pool in the corner. Outside the window, the December evening had committed to the dark by four-thirty, the streetlights making their amber contributions to the specific Havenbrook night. The radiator in the corner knocked and hissed with the irregular rhythm she was beginning to find familiar, the sound of an old building maintaining its warmth with effort.

The front door opened.

She kept reading. She was aware, in the peripheral way she was always aware of certain things, of the quality of the room adjusting slightly — not dramatically, just the small shift that happened when a space's center of gravity rearranged itself. Cheng said something low. Mark responded. There was the sound of a jacket being dropped somewhere.

"You came back," Donghyuck said.

She looked up from her book. He was standing in the middle of the room, jacket in hand now, in the dark grey hoodie she had seen before. His hair was slightly damp from the cold air outside, the red of it darker when it was damp. He was looking at her with the expression she had catalogued — the one that meant he was receiving information and sitting with it without requiring a response.

"Apparently," she said.

Something in his expression did the thing that wasn't quite a smile.

"Move," he said to Renjun, who moved six inches without looking up from his laptop, which was apparently sufficient. Donghyuck dropped onto the couch and looked at her book. "What are you reading."

She showed him the cover.

He looked at it. "Any good."

"Getting there," she said.

He nodded. He looked at the window. She looked at her book. The radiator knocked. Cheng and Mark were talking in the kitchen in the low register of people discussing something that had a practical dimension, and Renjun was typing, and the lamp did its warm work in the corner.

She read a page. She read another.

"Jisung's in a mood," Donghyuck said, to the window.

"I heard," she said, to her book.

"Don't go in the kitchen."

"I wasn't planning to."

"Good plan," he said.

She almost smiled. She turned a page.

An hour later she went outside for air.

The December cold hit her the moment she stepped through the building's front door — the full committed cold of a Saturday evening in Havenbrook, the kind that found every gap and held it. She had her jacket and her scarf and she pulled both tighter, stepping to the side of the doorway, leaning against the building's brick front. The street was mostly quiet — the particular Saturday evening lull between the afternoon's activity and the night's, the city in the middle of deciding what to do with itself.

She stood there for a few minutes, watching the street, letting the cold do the thing that cold did — clarifying, stripping back the particular fog that accumulated when she spent too long inside rooms with too much happening in them. The lamp in the window above her cast a small warm square of light on the pavement. A car moved past. Someone's dog was conducting its nightly survey of the block.

The door opened beside her.

Donghyuck stepped out, his jacket back on, his hands going into his pockets. He had a cigarette behind his ear — the usual unlit one. He stood beside her against the brick, looking at the same section of street, and didn't say anything.

They stood in the cold for a moment.

Then she registered it — the specific thing she had been bracing for without knowing she was bracing for it. He was going to light the cigarette. They were outside, it was cold, he had it behind his ear, this was the natural sequence. She was standing approximately two feet from where it would be lit. She had not yet said anything. She was not going to say anything because it was his cigarette and his choices and she had no standing to say anything about it.

She was calculating the wind direction before she'd consciously acknowledged what she was doing.

He reached up. He took the cigarette from behind his ear.

She watched him look at it. Turn it between his fingers the way he did — the coin-trick habit, going slack. He looked at it for a moment that lasted exactly as long as a decision being made.

Then he put it in his jacket pocket.

Not behind his ear. His pocket. Done.

He looked at the street.

She looked at the street.

She had not said anything. She had not made a sound or a movement or given any particular indication. He had not looked at her before he put it away. He had simply — known. The way he knew things, the way he had always apparently known things about her before she knew he was paying attention. The way he'd known her locker was in the east wing in August. The way he'd known she was still wearing her jacket on her hallway floor.

She felt the weight of this sit in her chest in the specific way of something that couldn't be filed anymore because the file was already too full and this was the thing that finally tipped it.

"You know," she said, carefully, "I was going to tell you it was fine."

"I know," he said.

"It would have been."

"Maybe," he said. He looked at the street. "But you wouldn't have come back out here after."

She held that. The cold moved around them, the particular quality of December in Havenbrook, the stripped bare trees visible at the end of the block where the park started. The streetlight two doors down buzzed faintly with the effort of the cold.

"How do you do that," she said.

"What."

"Know things." She looked at him. "You don't ask. You just — know."

He looked at her then. Not sidelong — directly, the way he occasionally looked at her when he had decided something deserved a direct look. In the amber light from the streetlamp, the red of his hair had gone dark again, and he had the expression she had started thinking of as his honest face — not the ease he wore for rooms, not the carefully calculated non-performance of Haechan, just something underneath both of those.

"I pay attention," he said. "That's all it is."

"To me."

"Yes." He said it without hesitation and without qualifying it, the way he said things that were true and had decided to be true without requiring discussion.

She held his gaze for a moment. The street was quiet around them, the dog a block away wrapping up its survey, a light going on in the apartment across the street. Somewhere in the building behind them, the radiator knocked.

"That's—" she started.

"A lot," he said. "I know."

"I wasn't going to say that."

"What were you going to say."

She looked at the street. Her breath made a small cloud in the cold. She thought about all the things she had been not-saying for eight weeks, the accumulation of them, the specific weight of a file that was too full. She thought about infrastructure — the word she'd used in the library, the one he'd turned over at nine-forty on a Tuesday and found a different meaning in. Systems built to protect something. The architecture that either held it or didn't.

"I was going to say," she said, "that it's the kind of thing that would be very easy to—" she stopped. "To let mean something."

He was quiet for a moment.

"And?" he said.

"And I've been trying not to," she said. "Let things mean things. For a while now."

"I know," he said.

"You know."

"Yeah." He looked at the street. "I know why, too."

She looked at him.

He was still looking at the street, the particular settled quality of someone who is saying something they've been holding for a while and is being careful about the speed of it. "You don't have to say it," he said. "I'm not asking you to say it. I just—" He stopped. Started again. "I'm not him, Sol."

The sentence landed in the cold between them.

She did not react visibly. She kept her face level, her posture even, all the practiced mechanics of composure doing their work. But something shifted underneath all of it — some load-bearing piece of the architecture she'd been maintaining, the one that kept certain things from reaching certain places, moving just slightly, just enough to be felt.

"I know," she said. Which was the truest thing she had said out loud in a very long time.

He looked at her then, and she looked at him, and the specific quality of that — two people standing in December cold outside a building on Maren Street in Havenbrook, having just said the two sentences that contained the most relevant information of the last two years — lasted approximately four seconds before he looked away.

"Cold," he said.

"Very," she said.

"We should go back in."

"Probably."

Neither of them moved for another thirty seconds.

Then he pushed off the brick and she pushed off the brick and they went back inside, into the lamp-warm apartment with its knocking radiator and Renjun's laptop and Cheng's diplomatic neutrality, and neither of them said anything about what had happened on the street, because some things didn't require being said about.

Later, after Cheng walked her home — Donghyuck had not offered and she had noticed that he hadn't offered, which she understood was a choice, the same way putting the cigarette in his pocket was a choice, giving her the distance she might need — she stood in her hallway and took off her jacket and stood still for a moment.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: home?

home, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i meant what i said

She held the phone. The refrigerator hummed. Her mother's television had gone quiet — late enough for sleep. The house had the specific quality it had at this hour, all its ordinary sounds subsided, nothing left but the essential ones.

I know, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: that's all i wanted to say

She stood in the quiet of the hallway for a long time after that. She thought about the cigarette in the pocket. She thought about I'm not him. She thought about the specific architecture of a person who paid attention and asked nothing back for it, who had been doing this since August, who had just said the most important thing that had been left unsaid and had said it simply and then looked away.

She thought about the file. She thought about the thing she'd been refusing to name.

She went to her room. She lay in the dark. The barely-visible stars were on the ceiling the way they always were.

She thought: I know what this is.

She thought: I have known for a while.

She thought: okay.

And for the first time in a very long time, the word didn't feel like a concession.

It felt like a door she was choosing to walk through.

Three days later Cheng called her at eight p.m.

She was in the middle of a queue — sitting at her desk with her headset on, the matchmaking screen cycling through its search — and she almost let it go to voicemail, which she had done before with Cheng and which Cheng had never once been upset about.

She looked at the screen. The call was running. Cheng called at eight p.m. on a Tuesday when he had something to say that was better said by phone than text.

She cancelled the queue. She picked up.

"Hey," she said.

"Hey." Cheng's voice had the register. The flat one. The one that meant he was managing the dose. "You okay?"

"I was in a queue," she said. "What happened."

A pause. "Jihoon's throwing a party Friday."

She was quiet.

"The kind," Cheng said. And didn't finish the sentence, because he didn't need to. The kind was sufficient. The kind with the specific function, the kind that served Jihoon's particular purpose, the kind she knew from the inside in the way that things you've survived stay inside you.

"Okay," she said.

"He's been talking about it," Cheng said carefully. "To certain people. The way he talks about things when he wants them to get back to someone."

She understood. Jihoon's parties were his domain — the one place where the rules were his and the information flowed in the direction he chose. He talked about this party to specific people, knowing those people talked to other people, knowing it would reach her. The party was the message before the party was anything else.

"Is he expecting me to come," she said.

Cheng was quiet for a moment. "I think he's expecting you to be afraid of it."

She held that. The bedroom was quiet around her, the screen of her laptop still showing the match results she'd abandoned, the lamp making its usual warm light. She thought about the week of quiet. She thought about the waiting is the point. She thought about two years of managing Jihoon's patience with her own, and what it had cost, and whether she was willing to keep paying it.

"Is Donghyuck going," she said.

A longer pause. "I haven't told him yet."

"But DREAM will be there."

"DREAM goes to his parties sometimes," Cheng said. "They don't like him. They're useful to each other." He paused. "It's separate from you, Sol. You know that."

"I know," she said.

She sat with the phone for a moment, the weight of Friday settling into the week like something that had already happened and was waiting for her to catch up to it.

"Cheng," she said.

"Yeah."

"Tell Donghyuck."

A brief silence. "You sure."

"Yes," she said. "Tell him."

Cheng was quiet for a moment longer — not hesitation, just the particular quality he had when he was absorbing something and deciding it was right. "Okay," he said.

"Okay," she said.

She hung up. She looked at the match results on her laptop screen, the stats from the game she'd abandoned. She thought about Friday. She thought about what it meant to stop treating Jihoon's patience with her own.

She thought about the file. About okay as a door.

She put her headset back on. She queued for ranked. The matchmaking screen cycled through its search — thirty seconds, forty, the familiar wait — and when the match found her she was already settled, already in it, the champion select screen opening and Kiriko appearing under her cursor without hesitation.

She locked in.

She played.


09: not ready

Wednesday and Thursday passed the way the days before something passed — with the particular quality of time that knew it was running toward a fixed point and was filling itself accordingly. Solar went to school. She did her routes. She played ranked in the evenings and climbed and did not think about Friday and thought about Friday constantly.

On Thursday afternoon Donghyuck texted her.

Haechannie🐭: cheng told me

She had been sitting at her desk doing homework, the lamp on, the December dark already full outside the window, the radiator in her room doing its halting best against the cold. She picked up the phone and looked at the text and put it down and picked it up again.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: you don't have to go

i know

Haechannie🐭: sol

i know i don't have to

A pause. Long enough to mean he was thinking.

Haechannie🐭: why are you going

She held the phone. She thought about the answer, which she had been thinking about for two days and which kept resolving into the same thing no matter how she approached it. She typed:

because he's been waiting for me to be afraid of it for two years

and i'm done

She read it back. Sent it.

The response took a moment.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: we'll be there

She read we'll and understood it and did not ask him to explain it and did not tell him not to come.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: don't go alone

cheng's taking me

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: the decision is yours

Haechannie🐭: whatever it is

Haechannie🐭: i'm just going to be there

She held that for a long time.

i know, she typed, and meant it the way she had been learning to mean things with him — completely, without the usual architectural reservation, through the door she had decided to walk through.

Jihoon's parties happened in a rented space three blocks into the east side — a converted basement below a building that had been other things before it was this, a place that existed in the specific grey area of Havenbrook's geography that nobody claimed officially and everybody used. The kind of space that had no lighting decisions, just the lights that were there, which were low and amber and contributed to the general atmosphere of a place that had decided not to be answerable to anything.

It was already running when they arrived — Cheng and Solar, at nine-thirty, which was late enough to not be the first ones there and early enough that the night had not yet reached whatever it was going to reach. The cold outside hit the warmth of the space the moment they opened the door, two different climates briefly arguing.

Solar had worn her dark jacket, her hood down because she was not going to walk in with her hood up. She had decided this on the walk over. There were things she could control and this was one of them.

The music was the kind that ran at a consistent level, not quiet enough to talk over easily, not loud enough to lose yourself in — a specific calibration, she had always thought, that kept people slightly off-balance without explaining why. The room was full enough. She recognized some faces, some from school, some from the specific social world that surrounded Jihoon's parties like a weather system. She kept her face level and her movements deliberate.

Cheng stayed close. Not hovering — just present, which was the version of presence he had always offered, the reliable kind.

She got a drink she didn't plan to touch and held it as a prop and looked at the room.

Jihoon was at the far end, in a group that contained several people she recognized and several she didn't. He was doing the thing he did at his own parties — the host performance, the easy authority of someone on his own ground, the casual management of a room that understood itself to belong to him. He had not looked at her yet.

She understood, standing there with her untouched drink and Cheng at her shoulder, that this was deliberate. He knew she was here. He had seen her come in. He was letting her look at him looking away, which was its own kind of message.

She gave it thirty seconds. Then she looked elsewhere.

DREAM was there — she found them without searching, which was a function of knowing where to look. Mark near the back wall with the particular posture of someone conducting an assessment. Renjun with a drink, talking to someone Solar didn't know. Chenle not immediately visible.

Donghyuck was by the far door, the one that led to the corridor. His jacket on, his hands in his pockets. He was not looking at her. He was looking at the middle of the room with the expression of someone who had been watching something for a while and had already done the calculations.

She looked at him for exactly one second. He did not look back. She looked away.

It was forty-five minutes before Jihoon made his approach.

She had been expecting it — she had been monitoring the room's geometry all evening, the way she always monitored rooms, and she had tracked him moving toward her over the course of ten minutes, the approach patient and unhurried, the approach of someone who had all night and knew it. She was standing near the wall, Cheng having stepped away briefly to deal with something she hadn't asked about, and she did not move when she heard him arrive beside her.

"You came," he said.

"I did," she said.

He looked at the room with the expression he used at parties — the proprietary ease of a person in their own territory. "Didn't think you would."

"I know," she said. "That's why I did."

Something moved in his expression. He looked at her then — the full version, the assessment he usually kept managed and partial. She held it. She had her drink in her hand and her face level and her shoulders exactly where she wanted them.

"You're different," he said.

"Yes," she said.

"Donghyuck," he said. Not as an accusation — as a fact being placed on a table.

"Partially," she said.

He looked at the room again. "You know what I have," he said, with the conversational lightness of someone discussing weather.

"Yes," she said.

"And you came anyway."

"Yes."

He was quiet for a moment. She could feel the calculation running — she had spent two years learning to read the specific quality of his silence, and this one was different from the Thursday sidewalk silence, less patient, more active. He was updating.

"What do you want," she said, because she had decided on the walk over that if she got the chance she was going to be the one to ask it directly.

He looked at her. "What do I want."

"You've been building toward something for two months," she said. "The sidewalk. The party. All of it." She kept her voice level. "What do you actually want."

He studied her for a moment. "I want," he said carefully, "to understand what's changed."

"I have," she said. "That's what's changed."

"Because of him."

"Because of me," she said. "He's incidental."

The distinction was not entirely true and they both knew it and she said it anyway because it was the most important version of it — that whatever had changed in her had changed in her, was hers, was not contingent on anyone else's continued presence. She had built it herself. She was going to keep it.

Something in his face shifted. She recognized it with the specific clarity of someone who had spent a year and a half learning to read a person's unguarded moments — the thing underneath the patience, the thing that was not authority but needed to feel like authority. The thing she had been managed by for so long that she had stopped being able to see it.

"You're going to leave," he said. It was not a question. "You're going to Seoul or wherever it is you've decided. You're going to be something. And you think that means I don't—"

"It doesn't mean anything about you," she said. "That's the point." She looked at him. "You don't get to be the reason. Not for any of it. Not anymore."

He was very still.

She had been afraid of this moment for two years. She had mapped its geometry in her head, had calculated its cost, had decided it was too expensive and then decided the alternative was more expensive and then arrived here anyway, standing in Jihoon's party with an untouched drink and her voice level and everything she was made of pointed in the same direction.

He was very still.

Then he said, quietly, with the particular coldness of someone who has had something taken from them that they considered theirs: "I still have the footage."

"I know," she said.

"I can use it."

"I know you can." She held his gaze. "I've known that for two years. It hasn't changed what I'm telling you."

The silence between them had a different quality now — not the pregnant kind, not the weapon kind. Something more like the silence after a wall has been removed, when both people in a room suddenly have to account for the different dimensions.

"Sol." He said her name the way he had always said it, with the specific weight of someone who had decided a long time ago that her name was something he could use.

"Don't," she said.

He looked at her for one more moment. She did not look away. She had not looked away once during this entire conversation and she was not going to start now.

Then he moved. Not toward her — past her. The crowd absorbed him with the ease of a room that had been built around his movements, and he was gone.

She stood where she was. She took one breath, and then another. Her drink was still in her hand, still untouched. The room moved around her and the music ran its calibrated level and the lights were what they were.

She turned.

Donghyuck was standing ten feet away. He had moved from the far door at some point — she had not tracked when — and was standing with his hands in his pockets, not close enough to have been part of it but close enough to have seen all of it.

He was looking at her.

Not differently. Not with assessment or calculation or the particular expression of someone deciding what something meant. Just — the way he always looked at her. Receiving. Present. The same way he had looked at her across the apartment that first night in October, like she was the most expected thing in the room.

Not different.

Not less.

The same.

She held that for a moment. The room continued to do what rooms did. The music ran. Somewhere behind her, the party went on.

She walked toward him.

She stopped when she was close enough to talk without raising her voice over the music — the distance that was not quite the distance they usually maintained, slightly closer than that, the gap between the easy version and the honest version.

"You heard that," she said.

"Some of it," he said. "Enough."
