She looked at him. "Say something."

He looked at her with the honest face — the one underneath the performance, the one she had been cataloguing since October. "You didn't need me," he said.

"No," she agreed.

"You knew that already," he said. "Before tonight."

"I thought I did," she said. "It's different to know it in a room."

He was quiet for a moment. Around them, the party did its indifferent thing. "Are you okay," he said.

She ran the actual inventory. The drink still in her hand. The room. The cold that would be waiting when she went back outside. The footage that still existed somewhere and always would and which she had just decided was not going to keep costing her what it had been costing her.

"Yes," she said. Which was true in the specific way that true things were true when they came out of a hard thing rather than before it.

He nodded once.

"I want to go," she said.

"Okay," he said.

"Will you — " she stopped. Started again. "Come outside with me."

It was not nothing to ask. She knew it was not nothing and he knew it was not nothing and she asked it anyway, which was the version of herself she was working on — the one that asked for things when she needed them rather than the version that managed alone because it was safer.

"Yeah," he said. "Come on."

The cold outside was absolute after the warmth of the basement — the full December kind, the committed kind, the kind that had decided weeks ago and was not reconsidering. The street was quieter than the party had been. The amber streetlights did their work on the wet pavement, which had gotten wet again while they were inside from a thin rain nobody had announced.

She stood on the sidewalk and looked at the street and breathed. The air was cold enough to feel clean, the specific quality of cold air after a room full of other things, and she breathed it in deliberately and let the room leave her.

He stood beside her. Not talking. Not filling the space with anything. Just — there. The way he was always there.

She became aware, standing in the cold after the longest conversation she had ever had with Jihoon and the first one she had won, that she was shaking slightly. Not visibly. Just the deep interior kind, the kind that lived in your chest rather than your hands, the aftershock of something that had required everything you had.

She felt him shift beside her. Not moving toward her — just a slight adjustment of his weight, the way someone shifted when they became aware of something happening next to them. He looked at her sideways.

"You're cold," he said.

"I'm fine," she said.

"Sol."

She looked at him. He had his hands out of his pockets now, which was the first time she had ever seen him voluntarily remove his hands from his pockets in the cold without being about to do something with them.

He held one out.

Not reaching. Just — available. The specific offering of something that was not demanded and would not be mentioned again if she didn't take it.

She looked at his hand for a moment. She thought about the file. About the door. About I'm not him and I pay attention and the decision is yours, whatever it is.

She put her hand in his.

His fingers closed around hers, unhurried, with the particular care of someone who understood the weight of what they were holding. His hand was warmer than she expected, which was a thing she had not been thinking about and noted anyway.

They stood on the sidewalk outside Jihoon's party in the December cold and she was still shaking slightly and his hand was warm and the street was wet and quiet and neither of them said anything.

She had spent two years managing. Two years of routes and composure and the specific architecture of someone who had learned that the cost of letting things mean something was too high. She had built it carefully and maintained it well and it had kept her functional and it had also kept everything else out including this, whatever this was, the specific quality of being known by someone who had never once asked for it back.

She was done maintaining it at the cost of this.

She was not ready to say that out loud yet. But she was done maintaining it.

They stood there until the shaking stopped. Then she squeezed his hand once, briefly, and let go, and they stood in the cold for another moment as two separate people who had just briefly been something else.

"Cheng's going to want to know you're okay," Donghyuck said.

"I'll text him."

"Yeah."

She pulled out her phone with her free hand. I'm outside, she texted Cheng. I'm fine. Come find me when you're done.

She put her phone away. The cold moved around them.

"Thank you," she said. "For being there."

"I didn't do anything," he said.

"I know," she said. "That's why."

He looked at the street. She looked at the street. Something in the air between them had the quality of a room whose furniture had been rearranged — the same objects, the same space, everything in a slightly different configuration that would take time to map.

She heard the door behind them open, the party's noise briefly filling the street. Cheng came through it, did a fast inventory of both of them with the efficiency of someone used to conducting fast inventories, and arrived at a conclusion.

"Okay?" he said to Solar.

"Yes," she said.

He looked at her for one more second. Then he looked at Donghyuck, and Donghyuck looked back at him, and something passed between them that was the kind of communication that didn't require Solar to be excluded from it, just that it was theirs.

"Let's go," Cheng said.

They walked home through the December cold, the three of them, the wet streets catching the amber of the streetlights. Donghyuck walked on Solar's left side, Cheng on her right, and nobody said much, because it was late and cold and the night had been what it had been and some things were better carried quietly through the dark toward home.

At her door, Cheng hugged her briefly — the kind of hug that contained more than it expressed — and went on.

Donghyuck stopped at the bottom of her porch steps.

She was already at the door, key in hand. She turned.

He was looking at her. The porch light was on, the amber cast of it on the steps, his breath making small regular clouds in the cold. He had his hands back in his pockets and his face had the honest quality, the one she had learned to recognize, the one that was just him without anything managing it.

"Go inside," he said. "It's cold."

"I know," she said.

She looked at him for one more moment — this specific person, in this specific light, at the bottom of her specific porch steps, who had stood beside her all night and not done anything except be exactly where she needed him to be.

"Goodnight," she said.

"Goodnight, Sol," he said.

She let herself in. She locked the door. She stood in the hallway in the dark for a long moment.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: inside?

inside, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: for what it's worth

Haechannie🐭: i've never seen anyone do what you did tonight

She read that. She read it again.

it didn't feel like much, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: that's the thing about you

Haechannie🐭: you just do what has to be done

Haechannie🐭: like it's obvious

Haechannie🐭: it's not obvious sol

Haechannie🐭: it's not obvious at all

She held the phone in the dark hallway of her house. The refrigerator hummed. The television was off — late enough that her mother had gone to bed. The house was the shape of itself, ordinary and specific, every sound she had grown up with doing its work in the quiet.

She thought about what he'd said. She thought about I've never seen anyone do what you did tonight. She thought about the hand on the sidewalk, the warmth of it, the way he'd held hers like it was something that required care.

She thought about all of it and did not file any of it.

She just held it.

thank you, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight

She went to her room. She lay in the dark. The barely-visible stars were on the ceiling the way they always were.

She lay in the dark and did not try to manage it.

Donghyuck at the bottom of the porch steps, breath making small clouds, hands back in his pockets, looking at her with the honest face. The warmth of his hand around hers on the cold sidewalk — the specific warmth of it, more than she'd expected, the way his fingers had closed carefully, like the thing he was holding required care. The shaking in her chest that had eventually stopped not because she'd controlled it but because he'd been there long enough that it ran out on its own.

I am not ready for this to be over, she thought, and the thought did not arrive with the usual flinch, the usual move to revise it into something safer. It just arrived, and settled, and stayed.

She had spent two years deciding what she couldn't afford to feel. She had been very thorough about it. Very good at it.

Tonight she let herself be bad at it.

The barely-visible stars on her ceiling held their light in the dark above her. She'd put them up at twelve and never taken them down, and they had watched her through everything — the incident, the fourteen months of survival years, the eight weeks of not making anything of it. They had seen her build every wall she'd ever built in this room and they had seen her lie here tonight unable to locate any of them.

She fell asleep like that, unguarded, which was something she had not done in a very long time, and the Havenbrook dark held her without requiring anything back.


10: in the air

She woke up at seven-twelve on a Saturday morning in December feeling like herself in a way she had not felt like herself in a long time.

Not fixed. Not resolved. Just — present, in the specific way of someone who has set something down that they have been carrying for too long and can feel the difference in their hands. The room was the room. The barely-visible stars on the ceiling. The particular grey-white light of a Havenbrook winter morning coming through the curtains, flat and honest, making no promises about the day.

She lay there for a few minutes without reaching for her phone, which was unusual. She lay there and looked at the ceiling and felt the shape of the previous night's residue — the confrontation, the cold sidewalk, the warmth of his hand — and did not move it anywhere or file it. Just let it be in the room with her, which was apparently a thing she was learning to do.

Then she reached for her phone.

No messages from Donghyuck, which she had not expected and which she was fine with and which she noted anyway. One from Jiwoo, sent at eleven forty-seven the previous night:

Jiwoo: cheng told me something happened at the party

Jiwoo: you don't have to say anything

Jiwoo: just let me know you're okay when you wake up

Solar looked at that for a moment. She typed: I'm okay. Better than.

She put the phone down. She went to make breakfast, which she rarely did on weekends because the mornings were usually something to get through rather than inhabit, but today the kitchen looked like itself in the flat winter light and she stood in it and made eggs and toast and ate them at the table and looked out the window at the grey Havenbrook sky.

Her mother came through in her robe at seven-forty, visibly surprised to find Solar at the table with a finished plate. "You're up early."

"Couldn't sleep late," Solar said, which was technically true.

Her mother poured coffee and sat across from her and they occupied the kitchen in the comfortable parallel quiet of two people who had learned to be in the same room without requiring anything from each other. It had always been this way between them — not distant, not cold, just the particular shape of a household that had organized itself around Solar's independence so thoroughly that neither of them knew how to undo it. Her mother loved her in the practical register: the coffee made before Solar woke up when her mother left early, the lights left on when Solar came home late, the specific absence of questions that was its own kind of courtesy.

"You seem different," her mother said, after a while.

Solar looked at her. "Different how."

Her mother considered. "Lighter," she said, and went back to her coffee.

Solar looked at her plate. She thought about lighter and what it meant to have someone notice the weight before the weight was gone.

"Mom," she said.

Her mother looked up.

"I've been thinking about Seoul," Solar said. "For a while now." She said it the way you said things you had been carrying alone long enough that saying them out loud felt like putting something on a table rather than building something from scratch. "About trying. For real."

Her mother held her coffee cup. Her expression was careful in the way of someone who had been expecting something and was deciding how to receive it now that it had arrived. "Trying," she said.

"The idol industry," Solar said. "The labels. There are global auditions. You submit a video, and if they like it —"

"I know what it is," her mother said.

Solar looked at her.

Her mother looked at the window. The grey sky. The bare tree in the front yard that had been there since Solar was small, that she had climbed once at seven and fallen out of and not climbed again. "I've known," her mother said, "since you were about twelve. Maybe earlier. You have a face when you're watching certain things." She paused. "I was waiting for you to say it."

Solar absorbed this. The specific quality of being known by a parent — not the version you perform for them, but the actual version — was something she rarely encountered and which always arrived with a small shock, the recalibration of realizing the people who had watched you grow up had been watching more carefully than you thought.

"I haven't submitted anything yet," Solar said. "I wanted to tell you first."

Her mother looked at her. Something in her expression moved — not sentiment exactly, more like the relief of a conversation finally happening. "Okay," she said.

"Okay?"

"Solar." Her mother set the coffee cup down. "You have been the most self-sufficient person in this house since you were eight years old. I have not always known what to do with that." She looked at her hands. "But I have always known you were going somewhere I couldn't follow. That's not a sad thing. That's just what you are."

Solar looked at the table.

"So okay," her mother said. "Tell me how it works."

She texted Cheng at noon.

can you come over

He arrived at one, with the efficiency of someone who had been expecting this call, which was either perceptive or the result of knowing her for three years, which were probably the same thing.

She told him about the audition — the research she'd been doing quietly since September, the specific label she'd been looking at, the submission requirements she had read so many times she could recite them. She told him about the conversation with her mother. She told him all of it in the order it had happened, which was something she rarely did — usually she gave people the edited version, the already-processed version, the one that didn't require them to watch her figure it out.

Cheng listened with the quality he had when information was confirming something rather than surprising him.

"I know," he said, when she finished.

She looked at him.

"About the label," he said. "About what you've been looking at." He had his hands wrapped around the mug she'd made him, looking at the kitchen table with the expression of someone who had been sitting on something. "I looked into it too. A few months ago."

"Cheng."

"I wasn't going to say anything until you did," he said. "It's your thing. I didn't want to make it into something it wasn't yet."

She looked at him for a moment. This was Cheng — the specific form his loyalty took, careful and unannounced, the same way he'd pointed her locker out to Donghyuck and told her about Jihoon and walked her home from the party without making any of it a production. He did things without requiring her to thank him for them, which was the quality she had valued in him since eighth grade and which she understood now, with more precision than she had before, was the thing she had been looking for in everyone since.

"I need someone to film me," she said.

"I know," he said.

"The submission is a video. Singing, dancing, both ideally." She looked at the table. "I've been practicing. I just haven't — I need to actually do it."

Cheng nodded. "When."

"Soon," she said. "Before I find a reason not to."

He nodded again. "Okay," he said, and she understood from the specific quality of his okay that it was already handled, that he was already in it, that this was what Cheng did — not fanfare, just presence.

She looked at the table. She thought about her mother this morning: you are going somewhere I can't follow. She thought about Donghyuck at the bottom of the porch steps. She thought about all of it together, the simultaneous weight of it — the going and the staying and the thing between those two states that had no clean name.

"Does Donghyuck know," she said. Not accusatorially. Just asking.

Cheng was quiet for a moment. "I haven't told him anything you haven't told me."

She held that. Which meant Donghyuck didn't know about the audition. He knew she was leaving — she had told him as much, in the specific way she said things to him, better than here and I'm not staying — but the specifics, the when and the how and the label, those were still hers.

"I'm going to tell him," she said.

Cheng looked at her with the expression he used when he was being very careful about not reacting.

"I know," he said.

"Not today," she said. "But soon."

He nodded. He drank his coffee. Outside, the December sky had made a small decision toward grey-blue, the specific color it went in late afternoon in Havenbrook when the light came through the clouds at an angle, which was one of the few things she considered beautiful about the city she was going to leave.

"Cheng," she said.

"Yeah."

"I'm scared," she said. Which was maybe the most honest thing she had said to him in three years and which she said with the specific ease of someone who had just spent the previous night learning that honesty did not always require courage. Sometimes it just required being tired enough of the alternative.

Cheng looked at her with the expression she knew best on him — not the logistics face, not the information-management face. The one underneath those. "Yeah," he said. "I know."

"Is that bad."

"No," he said. "It means it's real."

The text came at nine-seventeen that evening.

She was in her room, at her desk, her laptop open to the audition submission page which she had been looking at for forty minutes without doing anything to it. The form, which she had filled out weeks ago, sat in the draft state it had been in since October. The cursor in the submission field blinked with the patience of something that was prepared to wait as long as it took.

Haechannie🐭: hey

She picked up the phone.

hey, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: you okay today

yes, she typed. Then, because she had decided something: better than yesterday actually

Haechannie🐭: yeah?

yeah

A pause. The cursor on her laptop blinked.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: last night

Haechannie🐭: i just want to make sure

Haechannie🐭: that we're —

He stopped. The three dots appeared and disappeared twice. She watched them come and go with the specific attention of someone who understood that whatever he was trying to say was something he was choosing carefully.

Haechannie🐭: i don't want things to be weird

Haechannie🐭: between us

Haechannie🐭: the hand thing

She read that. She read the hand thing and felt the specific warmth of it — the fact that he had named it as a thing, the fact that he had been thinking about it too, the fact that he was asking about it in the careful register of someone who was more concerned about her comfort than his own.

it's not weird, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: good

donghyuck

Haechannie🐭: yeah

i'm glad it happened, she typed. And sent it before she revised it. Because she had decided to stop revising.

The three dots appeared. Stayed for a long time.

Haechannie🐭: me too

Then, after a beat:

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: how's the streak

She laughed — actually laughed, short and involuntary, into the quiet of her room — at the pivot, the immediate return to the thing that was theirs before any of this and would be theirs after, the way he could hold something and then set it down without making the setting-down feel like dismissal.

haven't played today, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: sol

i know

Haechannie🐭: go play

in a minute, she typed. She looked at the laptop screen. The audition form. The cursor blinking.

She put the phone down on her desk. She looked at the form for a long moment — the fields she had filled out, the video files she had attached months ago in a surge of four-a.m. clarity that she had been second-guessing ever since, the submission button she had been looking at for the better part of an hour.

She thought about her mother this morning. You are going somewhere I can't follow.

She thought about Cheng: it means it's real.

She thought about better than here — the way he'd said it, not as a compliment but as a fact he had run through his reasoning and arrived at with confidence.

She moved the cursor to the submission button.

She held it there for a long moment. The laptop hummed its quiet hum. Outside the window, the Havenbrook night was the Havenbrook night — the streetlights, the cold, the specific amber of a city that didn't know yet that it was going to lose her.

She clicked.

The confirmation screen came up immediately: Thank you for your submission. You will be contacted within four to six weeks regarding next steps.

She looked at it.

Four to six weeks.

She looked at it for another moment. Then she minimized the tab, put her headset on, opened the matchmaking screen, and queued for ranked. The familiar interface. The search cycling through. Thirty seconds, forty, the wait.

The match found her.

She locked Kiriko.

She played.


11: two mugs

January in Havenbrook was a different kind of cold than December.

December cold was ambient — it surrounded you, found the gaps, held the city in the particular suspended quality of a season making its presence known. January cold was personal. It came through walls. It sat in your bones at two in the afternoon and hadn't warmed by seven. It made the school hallways smell like wet coats and the particular steam of too many people in an enclosed space, and it made the east side look stripped in a way December hadn't quite managed, the streets more honest about what they were without the holiday lights that had briefly suggested something warmer.

Solar had always liked January. She had never been able to fully explain why, given that it was by most measures the worst month. But there was something about it — the absence of pretense, the city having dropped any performance of being festive and simply being what it was — that she found clarifying. January told the truth. She could work with that.

Three weeks had passed since the party.

Three weeks since the confrontation with Jihoon, who had been notably absent from her immediate orbit since — present in the school, but maintaining a distance that she understood as a choice rather than a defeat. He had not approached her again. He had not circulated anything. The footage sat wherever it sat, waiting, which was the thing it had always done, and she had decided to stop letting it be the loudest thing in her life.

Three weeks since the hand on the sidewalk.

Three weeks since the submission, sitting in an inbox somewhere with her singing and her dancing and three years of wanting attached to it, waiting for four to six weeks to become something or nothing.

She had not told Donghyuck about the submission yet. She was going to. She knew she was going to. She kept arriving at the edge of telling him and then the conversation would go somewhere else or she would decide it was not the right moment or she would simply — not be ready, which was the honest version, which was the version she was trying to stay honest about.

The configuration between them had shifted.

Not dramatically — nothing between them had ever been dramatic, which was part of what made it what it was. But the I'm glad it happened had changed something in the room the same way moving furniture changed a room without replacing it. The same objects. The same space. Everything in a slightly different configuration that you were still mapping.

He texted her in the morning now, sometimes. Not every morning — he was not the kind of person who required a daily structure from the people in his life, and she was not the kind of person who would have wanted one — but occasionally, a thing that had started without announcement. A text about nothing: the radiator, the cold, a song he'd heard somewhere and couldn't place, a question about whether she'd seen some specific piece of news he'd found interesting. The specific small dailiness of someone existing in proximity to you.

She texted back. She had stopped making herself wait the particular number of minutes that had previously felt like a reasonable defense.

At the Cha's apartment on Saturdays, the couch had become theirs in the way that spaces became theirs — not claimed, not discussed, just settled into through repetition until not sitting there would have been a statement. Renjun had stopped moving six inches when Donghyuck said move. He now moved twelve, without being asked, and then returned to his laptop with the expression of someone who found the whole situation deeply uninteresting, which was either true or a very good performance.

Cheng observed all of this with the diplomatic neutrality of someone who had arranged it and was prepared to deny any involvement.

Mark said nothing about any of it, which was the most eloquent thing he could have done.

Jisung ran numbers, which Solar had now accepted as simply a thing Jisung did, like weather.

On the third Saturday of January, it snowed.

Not heavily — Havenbrook rarely got heavy snow, the geography of it wrong for accumulation, but enough to coat the sidewalks and the cars and the bare branches of the park trees, the specific hush of a city that has been briefly made to stop by something it didn't plan for. Solar walked to the Cha's building through it, her boots making prints in the thin white layer, her breath making clouds that dissipated faster in the cold than they had in December.

Inside, Chenle was standing at the window looking at the street below with the expression of someone encountering weather for the first time.

"It's snowing," he announced, to the room.

"We can see that," Renjun replied, without looking up from his laptop.

"It's snowing here," Chenle said, with the specific emphasis of someone for whom this remained genuinely surprising. "Like, on the ground."

"It's January," Donghyuck called from the kitchen, where he was doing something with the toaster that required more attention than the toaster probably warranted. "It snows in January."

"It doesn't snow like this at home," Chenle protested.

"You're not at home," Donghyuck returned.

"I'm aware," Chenle said, but not unhappily. He pressed his nose briefly to the glass in the manner of someone who had decided to fully commit to the experience, then turned back to the room. He noticed Solar, who had come in without announcing herself and was unwinding her scarf by the door. "Oh. Hi."

"Hi," Solar said.

Chenle glanced between her and the kitchen with the expression he used when he was tracking something he found interesting. "Hyuck," he called. "Sol's here."

"I heard the door," Donghyuck said, from the kitchen.

"Right," Chenle said, clearly delighted by everything.

Solar settled onto the couch in the established configuration. Renjun lifted two fingers without looking up. She had brought a book, which she opened, and which she read for about four minutes before she gave up and looked at the window instead, the snow coming down in the particular way it did in Havenbrook — thin, unhurried, not committed to anything in particular but present.

Donghyuck came out of the kitchen with two mugs. He set one on the cushion beside her without asking whether she wanted it. She looked at it — steam rising, the pale gold of something with honey in it.

"You made tea," she said.

"The toaster was occupied," he said, which was not an explanation, and dropped onto the couch beside her.

She picked up the mug. Warm in her hands, made with the cold in mind. She held it and looked at the snow and said nothing, and he looked at the snow too, and the apartment held its particular Saturday warmth around them — Renjun's laptop, Chenle's ongoing relationship with the window, the radiator doing its irregular best.

After a while Chenle turned from the glass with the air of someone making a formal declaration. "Renjun thinks you two are dating."

The room went the specific quiet it went when Haechan was thinking, except this time Donghyuck wasn't thinking so much as very carefully not reacting, which Solar could now identify at a granular level.

"I don't think that," Renjun said, still addressing his laptop. "I made an observation."

"What observation," Donghyuck said, in an entirely level voice.

"That you made her tea," Renjun returned. "Specifically. Without asking."

"I made tea," Donghyuck said. "There were two mugs."

"There were four mugs in the cabinet," Renjun noted. "You took two."

Donghyuck looked at Renjun. Renjun looked at his laptop.

Solar was looking at her mug. She pressed her mouth flat against something and the something was winning. She took a sip — honey, exactly the right temperature — which was probably not a coincidence.

"Chenle," Donghyuck said.

"I'm just repeating what Renjun said," Chenle returned, from the window.

"Renjun made an observation," Donghyuck said. "You escalated it."

"I clarified it," Chenle corrected, with dignity.

"Sol," Donghyuck said.

She looked at him. He had the expression of someone deciding whether to land on exasperated or amused and hadn't committed yet. The light from the snow-grey window did the thing winter light did with the red of his hair, which she had long since stopped pretending she didn't notice.

"I'm staying out of it," she said.

"You're literally the subject of it," he pointed out.

"Which is exactly why I'm staying out of it," she said.

He looked at her for one more second. Then he looked back at the window. The not-quite smile arrived at the corner of his mouth before he could manage it.

Renjun closed his laptop with a soft click. "For the record," he stated, in the tone of someone filing a document, "I stand by the observation."

"Noted," Donghyuck said flatly.

"Just wanted that on record," Renjun said, and opened his laptop again.

Chenle pressed his nose back to the glass, apparently satisfied with how things had resolved.

Solar drank her tea. Donghyuck watched the snow. The radiator knocked. Outside, Havenbrook accumulated its thin white layer and the afternoon went its grey-white way, unhurried, present.

"I submitted something," she said. They were walking, their breath making small clouds, the street doing its January thing around them. "An audition."

He glanced at her. Not surprised — the way he was often not quite surprised, processing things at a pace slightly ahead of the conversation. "For Seoul," he said.

"For a label," she said. "Video submission. They review it and if they want to take it further — there are more rounds. A global audition. Then Seoul, if you make it that far."

He turned that over quietly for a moment. She could feel him doing it.

"When," he said.

"December," she said. "After the party."

He nodded. She watched him absorb the sequence of it — the party, the submission, the order of things. You were always going to leave. He had known. He had said it himself, months ago on the east side sidewalk. What he was working through now was not the fact of it but the shape, the when and the how becoming concrete.

"Did you tell Cheng," he asked.

"After I submitted," she said. "He already knew I was looking into it."

He nodded again. They walked for half a block without speaking. The street narrowed toward the park, the bare trees on either side holding their thin snow, the patches of white on the grass that hadn't melted since Saturday.

"Are you scared," he asked.

She thought about Cheng: it means it's real.

"Yes," she said.

"Good," he said.

She looked at him.

"Not good that you're scared," he clarified, glancing at her. "Good that you're doing it anyway."

They had reached the corner where their routes diverged, the end of the walking-together section by default. They stopped there, facing slightly different directions, the way they always stopped here.

"Donghyuck," she said.

"Yeah."

"If I get a callback — " she stopped. Found the right order. "I don't know what the timeline looks like yet. The global auditions are in different cities. New York, maybe. It wouldn't be soon." She looked at the street. "But I wanted you to know. Before it happens."

He gazed at the corner, the specific point where the east side's geography shifted into a different block. "I know," he said.

"You knew I was going to submit."

"I knew you were going to leave eventually," he said. "That's different from knowing the shape of it."

She looked at him. Dark jacket, hands in pockets, the red of his hair in the flat winter light. Exactly himself. The thing she had been noting since October and had long since stopped trying to file anywhere.

"Does it change anything," she said. "Between — " she gestured at the air between them, which was the most directly she had ever acknowledged that there was something between them to gesture at.

He held her gaze. The honest face, the one underneath everything else. "No," he said. And then, more carefully: "But Sol."

She waited.

"I need you to tell me things," he said. "When they happen. I don't want to find out after."

She understood what he meant. Not the audition specifically — the pattern. The managing alone. The carrying things until they resolved. The version of herself she was actively trying to change, which did not change overnight.

"I know," she said.

"I'm not going anywhere," he said. "But you have to let me be here."

She held that. The cold moved between them. A car passed on the cross street. The park trees held their winter stillness.

"Okay," she said. And meant it through the door.

The corner of his mouth moved. "Okay," he said back. Receiving it.

She started to turn. Then stopped.

"The tea," she said.

He looked at her.

"You took two mugs," she said.

The not-quite smile arrived fully then — the one she had catalogued enough times to recognize but which still arrived with the particular small shock of something that happened before his management caught it. He looked away at the street. He was working very hard at serenity. He was not entirely succeeding.

"The toaster was occupied," he said.

"Donghyuck."

"Go home, Sol," he said. "It's cold."

She went. She was three blocks away before she let herself smile properly — a thing she was learning, that some things were allowed to show on her face when nobody was watching, that composure was not required in an empty January street.

The waiting continued. Four to six weeks. The cursor in the inbox, blinking its patient blink.

She walked home through the Havenbrook cold and let it.

12: the shape of it

February at DMHS had its own social physics.

It was the month that followed the month that followed the holidays, which meant whatever had accumulated over January — the new configurations, the updated assessments, the specific shifts in who was looking at whom and from where — had had enough time to settle into fact. Havenbrook's school year ran on a particular current of information that moved in cycles, and February was when the cycle completed itself: what had been rumor in November was established record by February, and the established record was the thing you operated inside whether you wanted to or not.

Solar had known this since freshman year. She had been navigating established records for two years.

What was new in February was that the established record had updated.

She noticed it in small increments the way she noticed most things — the specific quality of a hallway adjusting itself around a new piece of information, the recalibration of eyes. She had become, over the last four months, someone who appeared at the Cha's building on Maren Street on Saturday afternoons. She had been seen walking part of the route home from DMHS in adjacent company. She had been in the library. She had been in the parking lot after the party in December, walking out with Cheng and Donghyuck, which three people Solar could name had seen.

Havenbrook's social infrastructure had processed all of this and arrived at a conclusion she had been expecting since November.

The conclusion arrived formally on a Wednesday morning, in the form of a girl named Seo Yuna.

Seo Yuna was a junior. She had been at DMHS since freshman year, had a large social footprint built primarily on knowing things and deploying them strategically, and had a specific relationship with Jihoon's orbit that Solar had mapped early and maintained careful awareness of. Not his — not that kind of relationship — but proximate. The kind of person who moved in the adjacent territory, who attended the parties and absorbed the information and occasionally surfaced it when it served something.

She found Solar at her locker before first period.

"Hey," she said, in the register of someone who had decided to be casual about something that was not casual.

Solar looked at her. "Hey."

Yuna leaned against the adjacent locker with the practiced ease of someone who had done this before in other configurations. She had her phone in her hand, screen down, which Solar noted. "I just wanted to ask," she said, "because I think you should know — people are talking."

"People always talk," Solar said. She turned back to her locker.

"About you and Haechan," Yuna said.

Solar did not react visibly. She took her textbook out. "Okay," she said.

"I mean — " Yuna paused. The pause had the quality of someone performing hesitation they had pre-planned. "It's just that people remember things. From before. And they're saying — " another pause, this one shorter — "that it's interesting. Who you're spending time with. Given."

Solar closed her locker. She turned and looked at Yuna with the level expression she had been developing since fifteen and which had reached, by now, a quality of completeness she found occasionally useful.

"Given what," she said.

Yuna held her gaze for a moment. Then the phone in her hand — the one she had been keeping face-down since she arrived — tipped slightly, just enough. Not showing Solar anything specific. Just indicating it was there.

Solar understood. The footage. Not a threat from Yuna — Yuna was not Jihoon, Yuna did not operate that way — but a message that had been sent through Yuna, who was a convenient and deniable channel. People remember. It's interesting. Given.

The phone.

"Thank you for letting me know," Solar said, and walked to class.

She texted Cheng from the bathroom between first and second period.

yuna talked to me this morning. she had her phone.

The response came in four minutes.

Cheng: i heard something was moving

Cheng: are you okay

yes. it wasn't a confrontation. it was a message.

Cheng: from him

through her. same thing.

Cheng: sol

Cheng: he's recalibrating

Cheng: after december he went quiet but this is him finding a new angle

I know, she typed. What angle is it.

A pause. Cheng's pauses were always calibrated — she had learned to read the length of them. This one was the length of someone deciding how much to give.

Cheng: the angle is haechan

Cheng: specifically

Cheng: that being connected to haechan makes the footage more valuable not less

She held the phone. She understood it immediately — the specific logic of it, which was exactly Jihoon's kind of logic. The footage had always been about Solar's reputation. But Solar's reputation had been destroyed two years ago, which meant the footage was already spent at the school level, which meant its primary value going forward was at a different scale. If Solar became visible — if Seoul happened, if any of it happened — the footage became a weapon pointed at something much larger than DMHS.

Donghyuck being DREAM made the footage more interesting, not less. Two stories for the price of one. The kind of thing that traveled.

he's positioning it, she typed.

Cheng: yes

Cheng: not using it yet

Cheng: just making sure you know he has it

I've always known he has it

Cheng: now he wants you to know he knows how to use it better than before

She put the phone in her pocket and went to second period and sat through fifty minutes of American literature with the specific focus of someone who was managing something in the background of everything else.

She told Donghyuck on Saturday.

Not immediately when she arrived — she sat on the couch and read and let the apartment be the apartment for an hour first, the radiator and Chenle and Renjun's laptop and the particular quality of a space she had been coming to for four months and which had stopped feeling like someone else's a long time ago. She let it settle around her and then, when the room had reached its Saturday equilibrium and Donghyuck was beside her looking at something on his phone, she said:

"Jihoon is using you."

He looked up. Not startled — alert, the specific sharpness of someone who had been half-expecting something.

"As leverage," she said. "The footage from before. He's recalibrating how to use it. The angle is that being connected to you makes it more valuable." She said it evenly, the way she said things she had already processed. "I wanted you to know."

He held her gaze for a moment. Across the room Renjun's typing had slowed, which was the closest thing to an acknowledgment she would get from Renjun. The room had its particular quality — the way it went when Donghyuck was thinking, the specific awareness.

"Since when," he said.

"Wednesday," she said. "A girl named Yuna."

He nodded, slowly, the gesture of someone absorbing a fact and placing it in the correct location. "He sent her," he said. Not a question.

"She's adjacent to him," Solar said. "He doesn't send people. He positions them."

The corner of his jaw shifted. Not anger exactly — something more like recognition. "You've been studying him," he said.

"For two years," she said. "You learn the method."

He looked at her for a moment with the honest face. "You should have told me sooner," he said. "About the footage. That it still existed. That it was—"

"I know," she said. "I'm telling you now."

He held that. She watched him decide something — the brief internal process of someone who had something to say and was choosing whether it served anything.

"Sol," he said.

"Yeah."

"He's going to keep doing this," he said. "He's not going to stop because of December. That was a setback, not an ending."

"I know," she said.

"I want to be clear about something," he said. He said it in the specific register she had learned to pay attention to — the one that meant what followed was not performance. "Whatever he does with that footage. Whatever he does with my name next to yours. It doesn't change anything. On my side." He paused. "I need you to know that."

She held his gaze. The apartment moved around them. Chenle, who had been very quiet for the last ten minutes, shifted in the armchair with the deliberateness of someone pretending to be absorbed in their phone.

"I know," she said. And then, because she was in February and four months into this and had stopped revising: "Thank you for saying it out loud."

He looked at her. The not-quite-smile arrived and then was managed and the managing was visible, which she had come to find was its own kind of honesty.

"Go back to your book," he said.

She went back to her book.

The week of Valentine's Day, Jiwoo cornered her at the corner of Elm and Fifth with two gas station coffees and an expression that meant she had been thinking since before school.

"I need to say something," Jiwoo opened with, handing over the cup.

"It's seven-forty," Solar said.

"I know what time it is," Jiwoo said. They fell into step. The February morning was the specific grey that had been grey for weeks, the sky fully committed to the monochrome. Their breath made small clouds. The bare trees were still bare. "I've been watching you since October," Jiwoo said, "and I need to say it."

"Say what," Solar said, though she had a reasonable idea.

"That you're in love with him," Jiwoo said. Simply. Without the usual strategic layering. Just that.

Solar looked at the sidewalk ahead of them.

"You don't have to confirm it," Jiwoo said. "I'm not asking you to confirm it. I'm saying I can see it and I wanted to name it so that you know I know and you don't have to manage it around me." She paused. "That's all."

Solar held her coffee. The grey morning moved around them. A car passed. The convenience store on Fifth had its lights on, the fluorescent kind that looked the same at any hour.

"It's not — " Solar started.

"Sol," Jiwoo said.

Solar stopped.

"I know it's complicated," Jiwoo said. "I know he's DREAM. I know about Jihoon and the footage and the whole shape of it. I know you're maybe leaving eventually. I know all of that." She looked at Solar sideways. "None of that changes what I just said."

Solar looked at the sidewalk. She thought about October — the texts about Kiriko, the thirteen-year-old question, the eight texts in the rain. She thought about the library. The hand on the sidewalk. The mug with honey in it. The I know why / you already know. She thought about all of it arranged in a line and understood that Jiwoo was right, which she had known for a while, which she had been carrying in the file that was too full to close.

"I know," she said. Quietly. The most honest version of it. "I know."

Jiwoo nodded once, satisfied, and drank her coffee.

"Okay," she said. "That's all I wanted to say."

They walked the rest of the way to school through the grey February morning and neither of them said anything else about it, because Jiwoo understood that some things only needed to be said once and Solar had received it and that was enough.

The submission window closed in the third week of February.

Solar calculated it on a Thursday morning at her desk before school, the grey light, the phone in her hand. Six weeks from December. Six weeks was the top of the window. She opened the email app. She looked at the inbox.

Nothing.

She held the phone for a moment. She closed the email app. She put the phone in her bag.

She went to school.

She did not open the email app at lunch. She did not open it after school. She opened it once before bed, out of habit, and looked at the inbox with the specific quality of someone confirming something they had already decided was likely, and closed it again.

She lay in the dark. The barely-visible stars. The refrigerator hum. The ordinary house.

Six weeks, she thought. Six weeks and nothing.

She did not text Donghyuck about it. She had told him about the submission. He knew the window. He had said I don't think it's a no in his particular register of someone who had decided a thing and was not moving off it. She did not want to tell him the window had closed because she did not want to have the conversation where he argued and she agreed and still felt what she felt, which was the small specific quiet of a door that had not quite opened.

She lay in the dark and held it and eventually the holding became sleep, which was what the body did when it ran out of ways to hold something.

Outside, February did its grey committed thing.

The email was silent.

She was still here.


13: the walls have teeth

Yuna's message had moved the way information moved in Havenbrook — not loudly, not in a straight line, but thoroughly. By the following Monday, Solar could feel it in the hallways the way she felt pressure changes: something atmospheric, preceding weather.

The specific weather arrived Thursday fourth period, in the form of two girls she didn't know well — sophomores, Bora-adjacent in the sense that they occupied the same social territory without being the same person — who were talking in the corridor outside the chemistry lab when Solar passed. They didn't stop talking when she passed. That was the tell. People who had something to say about you and wanted you to know it didn't lower their voices when you walked by.

"— just think it's interesting, like, given what she did—"

"—I know, and now she's suddenly with Haechan—"

"—doesn't it make you wonder what she's actually—"

She kept walking. Face level. Pace even. The practiced geography of someone who had learned to let things pass over her rather than through her.

She made it to the bathroom at the end of the corridor, which was the one on the third floor that nobody used before lunch because of the way the heating vents made it smell. She stood at the sink and ran cold water and looked at herself in the mirror for approximately fifteen seconds — the green eyes, the stillness that read as confidence from a distance, the face she had been building since fifteen.

She was fine. She had been fine through worse than two sophomores in a hallway.

She dried her hands. She went to class.

What she had not accounted for was that Donghyuck would hear it before she told him.

She had been going to tell him — she had made the calculation on the walk to class, the specific arithmetic of information management, and had decided she would say something at the apartment on Saturday because it was Thursday and there was no reason to make it immediate. It was two sophomores. It was not a crisis. It could wait until Saturday.

It did not wait until Saturday.

He texted her at seven-twelve Thursday evening.

Haechannie🐭: i heard something today

She was at her desk, laptop open, not doing homework with the focused attention of someone who was pretending to do homework. She looked at the text.

what did you hear, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: that some girls were talking about you in the third floor corridor

Haechannie🐭: fourth period

She held the phone. Of course. He had people who heard things the way DREAM had people who heard things, the east side's information network running through the school the same way it ran through the neighborhood.

it was nothing, she typed. two sophomores.

Haechannie🐭: what did they say

the usual. given what she did. now she's with haechan. you know.

A pause. She could feel him sitting with it.

Haechannie🐭: sol

i'm fine, she typed. i've been fine through worse. you know that.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: that's not what i was going to say

what were you going to say

Another pause, longer this time.

Haechannie🐭: i was going to say i'm sorry

Haechannie🐭: that being connected to me is giving them something to use

She held the phone. The bedroom was quiet around her, the lamp making its warm circle on the desk, the barely-visible stars on the ceiling doing their barely-visible thing. She thought about the Yuna conversation — Cheng saying being connected to haechan makes the footage more valuable not less. She thought about the Saturday conversation, Donghyuck saying it doesn't change anything on my side. She thought about all of it together, the specific accumulation of someone who had been watching and thinking and had arrived at a position he was now trying to hand her, which was responsibility for something that was not his fault.

stop, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: sol—

no, she typed. listen to me. you did not give them anything to use. the footage exists because of Jihoon. the rumor exists because of Jihoon. you are not the reason any of this is happening and I need you to not say that again.

She sent it before she second-guessed the directness. She had been working on that — the second-guessing. The not-revising.

The three dots appeared. Stayed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: okay, i hear you

Haechannie🐭: but sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i still hate that it happened

i know, she typed. me too. but i walked to class and i was fine. that's the whole story.

Haechannie🐭: is it

She looked at the screen. She thought about the fifteen seconds in the bathroom, the cold water, the mirror.

yes, she typed. Which was true. The part about being fine was entirely true. The part about the whole story was — close enough.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: thank you for telling me

you already knew

Haechannie🐭: you still told me

She held that. He was right — she could have deflected, could have said you already knew so there was nothing to tell. She had chosen to confirm it instead, which was its own kind of telling.

yeah, she typed. I did.

Friday Cheng found her at lunch.

He sat down with the specific economy of someone who had information that required delivery in person. She looked at him and understood from the quality of his face — not alarmed, not flat, something in the register between the two — that this was not a crisis but was not nothing.

"There's a thing tonight," he said. He was looking at the table. "DREAM thing. Operational."

"Okay," she said.

"Hyuck's going to be unavailable this weekend," Cheng said. "Saturday and possibly Sunday."

She held that. "Is he okay."

"Yes," Cheng said. "It's business. It's just — the timing is what it is." He looked up at her. "I wanted you to know before you showed up Saturday."

She held the information. She thought about the April scene she had not yet had, which was still ahead of her — the room compressed, the papers Mark wasn't reading, the thing Donghyuck had carried in the set of his shoulders. She thought about the Cha's apartment and the warmth of it and the radiator and Renjun's laptop and how she had learned the inside of this world by degrees over five months.

She thought about the fact that DREAM thing, operational was a sentence that had real content she was not being given, which was both true and fine, which was the specific arrangement she had been inside since October.

"Thank you for telling me," she said.

Cheng nodded. He ate his lunch. She ate hers.

"Is it dangerous," she said, after a while.

Cheng looked at her. He held the question for a moment — the particular quality of him deciding not how to answer but how much to answer.

"It's what they do," he said finally. Which was not no.

She looked at her food. She thought about all the times she had sat on that couch and heard the door and known it was him coming back from something she didn't know the shape of. She thought about the run in Chapter Five — which she knew only in the abstract, the way she knew most of DREAM's operational life, through inference and the specific quality of what was not said. She thought about what it meant to choose a person whose life contained things that came with that answer: it's what they do.

She had been choosing it, she understood, since October. The understanding was not new. It was just louder today, with Cheng sitting across from her saying possibly Sunday in the flat voice.

"Okay," she said.

Cheng looked at her with the expression he wore when he thought she was handling something well and did not want to say so because saying so would make it a thing.

"He'll text you," Cheng said.

"I know," she said.

The text came at eleven-forty Friday night.

She had been in the ranked queue — two games in, the streak at seventeen, the specific focus of someone who was playing with intent — when the notification came through. She finished the game, which she won, and then picked up her phone.

Haechannie🐭: hey

Haechannie🐭: cheng told you

yeah, she typed. he told me this afternoon.

Haechannie🐭: i'm sorry about saturday

don't apologize for your life, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: sol

i mean it. don't.

A pause. She could feel him deciding something.

Haechannie🐭: it's not nothing

Haechannie🐭: what we do

Haechannie🐭: i know you know that

Haechannie🐭: but i want to make sure you know that i know that you know

She read that twice. It was possibly the most convoluted sentence he had ever sent her, which meant he had been thinking about it carefully and had arrived at that and sent it anyway.

that's a lot of knowing, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: i'm trying to say

Haechannie🐭: that i don't want you to be okay with things because you've decided to be okay with them

Haechannie🐭: i want you to actually be okay with them

She held the phone. The queue had cycled back. She declined it. She sat in her room in the February dark and thought about what he was actually saying, which was: you can leave. if this is too much. I'm giving you the opening.

donghyuck, she typed.

yeah

Haechannie🐭: yeah

I'm not okay with it because I decided to be, she typed. I'm okay with it because it's you. That's different.

The three dots appeared. Stayed for a very long time.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭:

Another long pause. Longer than the first.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i'll be back sunday

okay, she typed. be safe.

Haechannie🐭: always

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

goodnight donghyuck

She put the phone down. The barely-visible stars. The quiet house. The February dark outside the window.

I'm not okay with it because I decided to be. I'm okay with it because it's you.

She had sent it before she'd second-guessed it. She was getting better at that.

She reopened the queue. The matchmaking screen cycled. Thirty seconds, forty. The match found her.

She locked Kiriko.

She played.

Sunday evening Donghyuck texted at eight-fifty-two.

Haechannie🐭: back

She had been reading — the same book from the apartment, the one she'd made slow progress through over five months of Saturday afternoons. She put it face-down on her bed.

okay, she typed. how are you.

Haechannie🐭: fine

Haechannie🐭: tired

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: what you said friday

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i've been thinking about it

She waited.

Haechannie🐭: i'm okay with it too

Haechannie🐭: being here

Haechannie🐭: because it's you

Haechannie🐭: that's all i wanted to say

She held the phone. The room was quiet. The barely-visible stars. The refrigerator hum. The specific weight of two sentences from a person who chose them carefully and meant every word of them.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: go to sleep sol

you first

Haechannie🐭: i'm already in bed

liar

Haechannie🐭: i'm on the couch

Haechannie🐭: close enough

She smiled. The involuntary kind, the door-before kind, the one she had stopped managing in empty rooms.

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone down on her chest. Outside, February continued its February. Inside, the house held its ordinary sounds — refrigerator, the distant television, the specific quiet of a Sunday evening winding down.

She lay in the dark and did not examine the specific warmth of because it's you sitting in her chest, which was the thing they had both just said in their own words, which was the closest they had come to naming it, which was enough for a Sunday in February.

She was going to say it eventually.

Not tonight.

But soon.


14: the world you don't know

She saw it on a Wednesday.

Not the first time she had seen it — she had been seeing the shape of it since October, peripherally, the way you saw something in your side vision and chose not to turn your head. But Wednesday was the first time she saw it directly, with enough proximity to understand what she had been choosing not to look at.

She was cutting through the main hall after second period, taking the longer route because Jihoon had been near the south stairwell that morning and the longer route was the calculation she still made on certain days without deciding to. The main hall was busy — the specific mid-morning traffic of a school day finding its rhythm, students moving between classes, a teacher pushing the squeaking cart.

She turned the corner near the senior lockers and saw Haechan.

He was leaning against the wall — the specific lean she had known since the first day of October, the one that made the architecture look like it had agreed to hold him — and he was not alone. Three girls she recognized from the junior class were in his orbit, the particular configuration of proximity that meant they had arranged themselves there rather than arrived by accident. One of them was talking, gesturing with her phone, laughing at something. Another had her hand on the wall near his shoulder, not quite touching him, in the specific geography of someone who wanted to be touching him. The third was watching him with the expression Solar had seen on girls' faces when they talked about him in hallways she was passing through.

He had his hands in his pockets. He was listening to the one talking with the easy attention of someone who had been in this configuration so many times it no longer required anything from him. He smiled at something she said — not the corner-of-the-mouth version Solar had been cataloguing since October, but a different one, wider, the public version, the one that was very good at being warm without being specific.

He was performing ease.

She had known this was the shape of him in rooms she wasn't in. She had known it theoretically, in the way you knew things about people that you had assembled from inference and other people's observations rather than direct experience. She had never watched it happen.

She watched it happen.

She kept walking. She did not change her pace. She was six feet past him when she heard, from behind her:

"Sol."

She stopped. Turned.

He had straightened from the wall. The three girls had registered the interruption and were doing the specific recalibration of people who had been in a configuration that had just changed. He was looking at Solar with the expression she knew — the actual one, the receiving one, the one that had nothing performing about it. Not the public smile. This.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

One of the girls — the one with the hand near his shoulder — was looking between them with the specific quality of someone conducting an assessment and not liking the results. Solar registered it without letting it show.

"You good?" he said.

"Fine," she said. "You?"

"Yeah," he said.

The exchange took approximately seven seconds. It contained nothing specific — no information, no gesture, no public declaration of anything. But the seven seconds had a quality the three girls could feel and were processing, Solar could see them processing it, the specific recalibration of people updating a picture they had thought was already complete.

"Later," he said.

"Later," she said.

She turned and walked to class.

She thought about it during third period, which was the period she had the most difficulty paying attention in on account of it being chemistry and her having a specific adversarial relationship with the subject. She thought about the public smile versus the other one. She thought about the seven seconds and what they had contained without containing anything.

She thought about the fact that he had stopped the conversation with three girls who were clearly happy to have his attention to say one word to her. Not a long word. Just her name. Just — flagging her. Making her visible in a room where she had been choosing to be invisible.

She did not know entirely what to do with that.

Haechannie🐭: you were about to walk past

She looked at the screen.

you stopped me, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: i wasn't going to just let you walk by

you were in the middle of something

Haechannie🐭: i'm never in the middle of something that matters more than that

She held the phone. Something in her chest did the thing that had become familiar.

the girls didn't look happy, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: they'll survive

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: you know the difference right

She looked at that. She thought about the public smile versus the real one. The lean versus the straightening. The performing ease versus the actual ease he had only ever had around her and, she understood now having watched both, nowhere else.

yeah, she typed. i know the difference.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: just wanted to make sure

She put the phone away. Across the cafeteria, at the table that had become DREAM's table through territorial gravity, she could see Mark eating with the deliberate economy of someone refueling rather than enjoying a meal. Jeno was there too — she had seen Jeno enough times now to recognize the specific quality of his presence, which was warm in a different way than Donghyuck's warmth, more immediate, less considered. He was saying something to Mark that required a hand gesture and was apparently very funny to Jeno if not entirely to Mark.

Donghyuck was not at the table. Which meant he was somewhere in the building she was not currently looking at, probably in a configuration she now had more precise mental imagery of than she'd had this morning.

She ate her lunch. She thought about I know the difference and what it meant to know it from the inside rather than the outside, which was a thing she had just acquired and which she was going to need some time to fully process.

Saturday arrived with the quality it had been having since January — the specific warmth of a fixed point in the week, something she moved toward rather than simply through.

She got to the Cha's building at half past one. The door to the apartment was unlocked, the usual state on Saturday afternoons. She came up the stairs — the second step creaking the way it always did — and pushed the door open.

The apartment was running differently than usual.

She registered it in the first three seconds: the absence of Renjun's laptop sound, the specific compressed quality of the air she had felt once before, the quality she had learned to read as something happened. Mark was at the table with Jeno beside him, both looking at something on Mark's phone with the focused economy of people reviewing rather than discussing. Chenle was in the corner armchair, present but quieter than his usual register, his phone face-up in his lap in a way that meant he was waiting for something on it. Renjun was standing at the kitchen doorway with a coffee, leaning against the frame, watching the room with the particular quality of someone keeping inventory.

Jisung was not visible.

The door to the back room — the one that was always closed, the one Solar had never been in, the one whose existence she had been aware of since October in the specific way you were aware of things you had decided not to ask about — was closed now with a quality that was different from its usual closed. A light under the door. Low voices she couldn't distinguish.

She set her bag down. She sat on the couch. The back room's voices continued at their indistinct register.

Mark looked up from his phone. "Hey, Solar."

"Hey," she said.

Jeno glanced over with the easy warmth he had even in compressed rooms. "Coffee?"

"I'm fine," she said. "Thanks."

She looked at her hands. She looked at the back room door. She looked at the window, where Donghyuck was standing with his back to the room, looking at the street below with the quality of someone who had been looking at it for a while.

She had been in this room enough times to know its registers. She knew the difference between a Saturday where the week had been ordinary and one where it hadn't. But the back room voices were new — or rather, the awareness of them was new, their content becoming something she could almost catch at the edges if she listened. She didn't listen. She had learned, through five months of Saturdays, the specific discipline of not reaching for information she hadn't been given.

She sat on the couch and let the room be what it was.

After a few minutes the back room door opened.

Two men she had never seen before came out. Not boys — men, older than anyone in DREAM by several years, with the specific quality of people who moved through spaces with full awareness of what the spaces contained and what they were worth. One of them was carrying something in his jacket — she registered it the way she registered threats, fast and complete, the shape of it under the fabric unmistakable once you knew what you were looking at. Neither of them looked at her. They moved through the apartment with the efficiency of people completing a transaction they had completed before, in other rooms, with other people, and went out the front door without a word to anyone.

The door closed.

The apartment exhaled.

Mark put his phone down. Jeno leaned back in his chair. Renjun came off the kitchen doorframe. Chenle's eyes went to his phone screen and then back up.

Solar did not move. She looked at the front door for a moment after it closed. Then she looked at her hands in her lap. Then she looked at the window, where Donghyuck had turned from the street and was looking at her with the expression she had come to know — the honest one, the one that asked something without asking it.

She held his gaze. She kept her face level. She breathed.

This is what they do, she thought. Cheng's words, from the lunch table. It's what they do.

She had understood it then as a concept. She understood it now as a room she was sitting in.

Donghyuck came away from the window. He dropped onto the couch beside her — close, the shoulder-adjacent position — and looked at the middle distance with the economy of movement she recognized as him having already done a lot of moving.

"Rough week," she said.

"Yeah," he said.

"You don't have to say anything else," she said.

He looked at her sideways. The honest face, worn at the edges. "I know," he said. "That's why I like sitting here."

She held that. The room moved around them — the low sounds of people resuming their ordinary registers, Renjun's laptop finally opening, Chenle's thumb moving on his phone. The radiator knocked. Outside, the February street was the February street.

She thought: I know what this is now. Not theoretically. Actually.

She thought: I'm still here.

She looked at the window.

He looked at the window.

The room held them both.

It was almost five when Jisung came out.

He looked the way Jisung looked when he had been working — not tired, Jisung didn't do tired in any visible register, but focused in a way that had narrowed his usual expression to its most essential form. He looked at the room with the inventory quality. His eyes moved to Solar and stayed there for approximately two seconds.

She held his gaze.

He looked at Donghyuck. Something passed between them — wordless, the specific shorthand of people who had been in enough rooms together to have a language for things that couldn't be said in front of certain people.

Donghyuck said, without inflection: "She stays."

The sentence sat in the room.

It was not a long sentence. It was not a dramatic one. But it had the quality of something that had been decided rather than suggested, and Jisung's expression ran its calculation on it and arrived somewhere, and he said nothing. He went to the kitchen. The sound of the kettle.

Solar looked at Donghyuck.

He was looking at the window again. His jaw had the particular set of someone who had just done something small that had cost something.

She said nothing. She understood what she stays meant in this room, with these people, in the specific hierarchy of DREAM. It meant she was not a certain person anymore. It meant he had placed her on a different side of a line, in front of Jisung, without asking her if she wanted to be there.

She should probably have something to say about that.

She found, sitting with it for a moment in the compressed warmth of the apartment, that what she had was not an objection.

"Donghyuck," she said, quietly.

"Yeah," he said.

"Thank you," she said.

He looked at her. The honest face, worn at the edges, with the specific quality of someone who had made a decision and was waiting to find out if it was the right one.

"Don't thank me yet," he said.

She held his gaze for a moment. Through the kitchen doorway, the kettle was doing its thing. Mark and Jeno had gone quiet. Renjun was looking at his phone with enormous concentration.

"Okay," she said.

He nodded once and looked back at the window.

Outside, February moved through its final days. The city held its breath the way it held its breath before things changed. The room held its particular warmth — the warmth of a place that had become real to her over five months of Saturdays, the warmth of people she now knew better than she had ever expected to, the warmth of sitting beside someone who had just said she stays with the quiet certainty of a person who had decided something and was not moving off it.

She stayed.

Cheng walked her home at seven.

The February evening was the kind of cold that had softened slightly from its January commitment — not warm, not even close, but the specific cold of a month approaching its end, the hard edge gone. They walked without hurrying, their breath making the smaller clouds of air that had less distance to travel before it dispersed.

"He said she stays," Solar said. They were two blocks from the apartment.

"I know," Cheng said.

"In front of Jisung."

"I know," Cheng said again.

She looked at the street ahead. The streetlights were doing their amber work on the wet pavement. A dog somewhere nearby was making its evening announcements to no one in particular.

"What does it mean," she said. "Specifically. In DREAM terms."

Cheng walked for a moment before answering. He had the quality he used when he was deciding not how to say something but whether. "It means," he said carefully, "that you're not a guest anymore."

She held that. The cold moved around them.

"Is that good," she said.

Cheng looked at her sideways with the expression she knew best on him — not the logistics face, the one underneath it. "It means he trusts you more than he trusts most people," he said. "In Hyuck's world, that's not a small thing."

She looked at the sidewalk. She thought about she stays in Donghyuck's voice, the certainty of it. She thought about Jisung's calculation running and arriving somewhere and saying nothing.

"Cheng," she said.

"Yeah."

"The thing that happened this week," she said. "That made the room like that."

He was quiet.

"I'm not asking what it was," she said. "I just want to know—" she stopped. Found the right question. "Is it over."

Cheng considered this for a moment with the particular quality of someone who respected the precision of her ask. "For now," he said.

She nodded. For now was the answer she had expected and the answer she could work with. In Havenbrook, for now was the most honest thing available.

They walked the last block in the comfortable silence that had developed between them over three years of walking various blocks in various weathers toward various homes. At her door she turned.

"Cheng," she said.

He looked at her.

"When he said she stays," she said. "Were you surprised?"

Cheng's mouth moved in the way it moved when he was being very careful about not reacting. "No," he said. And then, because he was Cheng and occasionally gave more than the minimum: "I've known for a while."

She held that. The porch light made its amber contribution to the February evening. The bare tree in her front yard held its almost-not-bare branches, the barely-there suggestion of something building underneath the winter.

"Goodnight, Cheng," she said.

"Goodnight, Sol," he said.

She let herself in. She stood in the hallway and took off her jacket and stood still for a moment in the ordinary quiet of the house — refrigerator hum, the faint sound of her mother's television through the wall.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: home?

home, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i should've asked you first

Haechannie🐭: before i said it

Haechannie🐭: that wasn't my call to make for you

She held the phone. She read it twice.

it was the right call, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: i still should've asked

She held that. She thought about the specific nature of him — the person who put cigarettes in pockets and made tea and said it's your decision and the decision is yours and had now, for the first time, made a decision that was hers without asking. And had come home and told her he should have.

next time ask, she typed. this time it was okay.

Haechannie🐭: yeah?

yeah, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone down. She went to her room. She lay in the dark with the barely-visible stars and the refrigerator hum and the specific weight of a Saturday that had contained the public hallway and the couch and she stays and I should've asked you first, all of it in the same day, the warmth and the edge of it inseparable.

The dangerous and the tender.

She understood now, in a way she had only understood theoretically before, that they were never going to be separable. That was the deal. That was what she had been choosing since October, and was going to keep choosing, and was choosing right now in the dark of her room in February with her phone on her chest and his name in her contacts.

She was choosing it.

She fell asleep choosing it.


15: second from the bottom

The last Saturday of February.

She had been at the apartment for three hours, which was longer than usual, because the afternoon had the particular quality of afternoons that didn't announce an ending — the room settling into its Saturday warmth, the radiator doing its work, the specific ease of a space that had become familiar enough to exist inside without managing it. Renjun had finished whatever the ongoing project was and had closed his laptop with the finality of someone completing a chapter. Chenle had, at some point, migrated from the armchair to the floor, which was where he ended up when he'd been sitting too long, and was now lying on his back looking at the ceiling with the philosophical air of someone conducting a review of the week. Mark had left at four to deal with something he didn't explain. Jeno had arrived at four-fifteen to fill the space Mark had vacated, which was the kind of DREAM arithmetic Solar had stopped finding surprising.

She and Donghyuck had been on the couch for two hours.

Not doing anything specific. She had been reading — she was still working through the same book, a slow reader by necessity rather than preference, the book serving partly as a prop and partly as an actual book. He had been on his phone for a while, then not on his phone, then looking at the window, then looking at nothing in particular with the settled quality of someone who had given the week everything it required and had arrived somewhere that didn't ask for anything.

They had talked, in the way they talked when they were in the same room rather than on the phone — not continuously, not with the particular charge of the texts, but in the specific register of two people comfortable enough to let conversation arrive and leave without requiring it.
