She had said something about the book. He had said something about a song he'd heard that week. She had asked about Renjun's project, which Renjun had answered with a specificity that briefly derailed them all into a conversation about something technical she didn't entirely follow but followed well enough.

At six-fifteen she looked at the window and understood the light had changed.

"I should go," she said.

He looked at her. Not arguing — just registering. "Yeah," he said.

She gathered her things. The book went back in her bag. She put on her jacket, wound her scarf, went through the small archaeology of leaving that had developed its own routine — the bag, the jacket, the check of the couch cushion for her phone.

"Bye," she said to the room.

Renjun raised two fingers. Chenle said something from the floor without moving. Jeno waved.

Donghyuck stood. "I'll walk you down," he said.

They went down the stairs — the second step, the old wood smell, the cold air increasing with each floor. At the building's front door he pushed it open and they stood for a moment in the February evening, which had the specific quality of the last days of the month: the cold still present but with less conviction, the air slightly less sharp than January's, the city exhaling something cautious toward spring.

"Safe home," he said.

"Always," she said.

She started down the steps. She was two feet away when she felt it — the specific pull of almost-saying something, the accumulation of a Saturday and two months and everything underneath them arriving at the threshold of a sentence she hadn't prepared. She stopped on the step below him.

"Donghyuck," she said.

He looked at her. The porch light of the building next door was on, casting amber across the wet pavement. In the light he looked like himself — exactly himself, the red hair and the dark jacket and the expression that asked nothing.

She had been going to say something. She stood on the step for a moment with the sentence's ghost in her mouth and understood, standing there, that she wasn't ready yet. Not because she didn't know what it was. She had known what it was for a while. Just — not yet.

"Nothing," she said. "Goodnight."

The corner of his mouth moved. Not the public smile. The other one. "Goodnight, Sol," he said.

She walked home through the last of February.

He stood at the building's front door until she turned the corner.

He did this every Saturday, which was a thing he had never discussed with anyone and which nobody in the apartment had mentioned, which he understood was an act of collective tact on their part that he was not going to examine too closely. He stood until she turned the corner and was gone and then he went back upstairs and the building held its stairwell smell around him and the second step creaked.

Inside, the apartment had the quality it always had after she left — not quieter, exactly, but differently occupied, the specific negative space of a person's absence that was only noticeable if you had been aware of their presence. Chenle was still on the floor, now with his phone balanced on his chest. Renjun was looking at something on his reopened laptop. Jeno had the television on at low volume, showing something he wasn't watching.

Donghyuck dropped onto the couch.

The cushion she had been sitting on still held the specific indent of three hours. He did not look at this. He looked at the window, which was the thing he did when he needed to look at something that didn't look back.

"She almost said something," Chenle said, to the ceiling.

"Yeah," Donghyuck said.

"On the step," Chenle said. "She stopped and then she didn't say it."

"I know," Donghyuck said.

"What do you think it was," Chenle said.

Donghyuck was quiet for a moment. "Something she's not ready to say yet."

"But you know what it is," Chenle said.

Donghyuck said nothing.

"That's a yes," Chenle said, to the ceiling.

Renjun did not look up from his laptop. "If you know and she knows," he said, with the precision of someone completing a logical sequence, "then the remaining variable is time."

"Thank you," Donghyuck said flatly. "That's very helpful."

"I thought so," Renjun said.

Jeno turned the television up one notch, apparently deciding the room had reached a sufficient level of disclosure and was now eligible for something else. The show on the television was some competition program, the kind with a panel of judges who made faces at things.

Donghyuck looked at the window.

He thought about the step. The two feet of distance and the sentence she had swallowed. He had seen it happen — the specific quality of someone arriving at the edge of something and choosing to step back. He knew that quality. He had been exercising it himself since October, the ongoing discipline of someone who understood the specific difference between what she was ready for and what he was ready for and what the actual distance between those two states was.

The distance was closing. He could feel it the way you felt weather systems — not seeing them, just knowing from the quality of the air that something was coming.

He had been patient. He was going to keep being patient. He was constitutionally capable of patience in a way that he understood was unusual — DREAM had required patience of him since he was fourteen, the specific patience of someone who had learned that most things worth having required waiting for conditions to align. He had applied this to operations and to people and to everything in between. He could apply it here.

What he was less constitutionally capable of, he understood sitting on the couch in the last Saturday of February, was pretending he did not know exactly what was happening. He had known since October. Before October. He had known in August, standing in Cheng's driveway having asked for her locker location, that this was not standard. He had not been able to say what it was then, only what it wasn't, only that she was not routine.

She had never been routine.

He reached for his phone.

Haechannie🐭: home?

The response came in three minutes, which was about the right time for the walk.

Solar: home

Solar: your step creaks

He looked at that. He almost smiled.

second one from the bottom, he typed. been like that since i got here.

Solar: does it bother you

not anymore, he typed. used to. now it's just the sound of the building.

Solar: i think that's kind of nice actually

Solar: that something annoying becomes something familiar

He held the phone. He thought about that — the specific thing she had said, which was about more than the step and they both knew it. She did this sometimes: said the thing sideways, in the specific register of someone testing the edge of something without committing to going over it.

yeah, he typed. i think so too.

A pause.

Solar: goodnight donghyuck

goodnight sol, he typed.

He put the phone down. Chenle was still on the floor. The television was still doing the competition program. Renjun's laptop had gone to sleep.

Something annoying becomes something familiar.

He sat with that in the specific quiet of the apartment after she left, which was its own kind of presence.

Monday Jihoon was in the parking lot.

Not of the school — the lot of the convenience store on Fourth, which was two blocks from DMHS and which served as the informal gathering point for the kind of DMHS social activity that did not happen on school property. Solar saw him from across the street on her way to second period's coffee run, which she did on Mondays specifically because Mondays required it.

He was with two people she didn't recognize — not DMHS students, she could tell from the way they moved and the way they stood, the specific quality of people who were not accustomed to organizing themselves around a school's rhythms. One of them was on his phone. The other was talking to Jihoon with the body language of someone conducting business rather than conversation.

She kept walking.

She registered the scene with the fast inventory she had been applying to Jihoon's presence for two and a half years — who he was with, what the configuration meant, whether it was relevant to her. The two strangers had the quality of the men from the apartment — not DREAM, not anything she had a clean category for, just the operational world making itself visible at the corners of her day.

She understood something, crossing the street with her coffee order in her hand, that she had been understanding in pieces since the apartment Saturday: that the world she had been walking closer to for five months and the world she had been trying to walk away from for two and a half years were not entirely different worlds. They occupied the same city. They used the same streets. They were not the same thing — she knew they were not the same thing, knew the difference between Donghyuck and Jihoon in her bones in a way that required no analysis — but they existed in the same geography, and pretending otherwise was a luxury the geography did not offer.

She bought her coffee. She went back to school.

She thought about it between second and third period, standing at her locker with the combination done and the door open and not taking anything out. She thought about the men in the apartment and Jihoon in the parking lot and the specific moral arithmetic of choosing a world you could not fully separate from the one you had survived.

She thought about because it's you.

She thought about the step, and what she had almost said on it.

She thought: I know the difference. That's enough. That has to be enough.

She took her textbook out. She closed her locker. She went to class.

Tuesday she told Jiwoo.

Not about the parking lot — about Saturday. About the step. About almost saying it and stepping back.

They were at the corner of Elm and Fifth with the coffee that had become their Monday-Wednesday-Friday arrangement, though Jiwoo had decided Tuesday also counted now because of what she called extenuating circumstances that she had declined to specify but which Solar suspected meant she had been thinking and wanted somewhere to put it.

"I almost said it," Solar said.

Jiwoo did not ask what. She had known what for three weeks. "What stopped you," she said.

Solar held her coffee. The February morning was doing its end-of-month thing — not warm, but the specific not-warm that was different from January's not-warm, the cold less committed. "I don't know," she said. Which was not entirely true. "I know what it is. I know he knows. I know he knows that I know." She paused. "Saying it out loud makes it something you can lose."

Jiwoo was quiet for a moment. They walked. The bare trees had the almost-not-bare quality they'd been developing for weeks, the barely-there suggestion of something building underneath.

"You've already lost things," Jiwoo said. Not unkindly. Just accurately.

"I know," Solar said.

"And you're still here," Jiwoo said.

Solar looked at the sidewalk ahead of them. The school was visible at the end of the street, its lights on, the early arrivals making their way up the front path.

"I'm still here," she agreed.

"So," Jiwoo said.

Solar looked at her.

"Say it," Jiwoo said simply. "When you're ready. Not because I'm telling you to. But because—" she paused, finding something. "Because you've been building something since October, and the thing you're afraid of losing is already there. You're just deciding whether to hold it with two hands or one."

Solar held that for a moment. The school got closer. The morning did its ordinary thing around them — cars, a dog, the specific sounds of a Tuesday in February starting itself.

"Two hands is scarier," Solar said.

"Yeah," Jiwoo said. "That's kind of the point."

They reached the front path. The school day arranged itself around them the way it always did — the doors, the noise, the specific social physics of DMHS running its morning program. Solar went through the east entrance with her coffee and her bag and the sentence she had swallowed on a step last Saturday, and she carried it the way she carried things now — not buried, not managed, just held. Present. Waiting for the right conditions.

The conditions were coming. She could feel them the way Havenbrook felt the end of February — not seeing it yet, just knowing from the quality of the air that something was close.

She went to class.

The barely-not-bare trees held their barely-not-bare branches against the February sky.

Almost.


16: footage moves

March arrived the way Solar had been waiting for it to arrive — not dramatically, just with the specific quality of a month that had decided to be itself. The trees committed. The air lost the last of February's conviction about the cold. The days stretched by five minutes, then ten, the light lasting longer each evening in a way that changed the color of Havenbrook at four-thirty from the flat dark of winter to something closer to amber.

She noticed this on the first Monday of March, walking home from school, and understood that the city had been waiting too.

The first Saturday of March, the apartment was warm in a way that was different from its winter warm — not the radiator's effortful heat but something more ambient, the building holding the temperature of a day that had not required everything of it. The windows were not fogged. The light came through them with a different quality, something approaching actual light rather than the pale excuse for it that February had been offering.

Chenle had noticed this with the enthusiasm of someone who had been personally wronged by the cold and was now accepting its apology. He had opened the window a crack, which had produced a brief debate about whether the apartment was actually warm enough for an open window, which had been resolved by Renjun saying it's fifty-two degrees, Chenle and Chenle saying exactly and nobody else having the energy to continue.

The window stayed open.

Solar was on the couch, which was where she was on Saturdays, and the book was in her hands, which was where it was on Saturdays, and she was reading it more than usual because the March light coming through Chenle's cracked window was genuinely adequate for reading rather than performatively adequate.

Donghyuck was at the kitchen table.

This was less usual. He was normally on the couch by now, the shoulder-adjacent configuration that had become routine, but he had been at the table since she arrived, doing something that involved his phone and a piece of paper and what appeared to be genuine concentration. Jeno had glanced at him twice and then looked at Solar with the expression Jeno used when he thought something was funny and was being diplomatic about it.

"What's he doing," Solar said, quietly, to Jeno.

"Arguing with someone in writing," Jeno said, equally quietly, "which means it's Jisung."

She looked at the table. Donghyuck had his head down, pen moving. The piece of paper had writing on it — not notes, actual sentences, the specific density of someone making a case.

"They argue in writing," she said.

"When it's about something that matters," Jeno said. "Verbal arguments with Jisung go in circles. He writes better than he talks." He paused, considering. "Hyuck writes better than Jisung talks, I should say. Jisung writes just fine."

She looked at the piece of paper. At the pen moving. At the specific quality of his focus, which was different from his usual register — less ambient, more directed, the ease set aside for something that required actual effort.

She had not known this about him. Which meant there were still things she did not know, which was a thought she turned over briefly and found interesting rather than unsettling.

"Does it work," she said. "The writing."

Jeno considered. "Sometimes," he said. "When Jisung's in the right mood. It usually gets them further than talking, anyway." He stretched his arms above his head. "Hyuck's been at it since this morning. Whatever it is must matter."

She looked back at her book. She read a page without entirely absorbing it.

Twenty minutes later Donghyuck folded the piece of paper, put it in his jacket pocket, and came to the couch.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said.

"Jeno says you were arguing with Jisung in writing."

He looked at Jeno, who had the expression of someone who had made a calculation about disclosure and stood by it. "I was writing something down," Donghyuck said.

"For Jisung," she said.

"For a conversation later," he said. "That Jisung will be part of."

She looked at him. He looked at her with the expression of someone who was being technically accurate and knew she knew it.

"Okay," she said.

He settled onto the couch. The March light came through the cracked window. The radiator, in the specific way of radiators in March, had stopped trying as hard, the season having made its intentions clear.

"What are you reading," he said.

She showed him. He looked at the cover with the expression he always used for her book — interested but skeptical, the look of someone who had not read it and had opinions about it anyway.

"Are you going to read it or just look at it," he said.

"I was reading it," she said. "You sat down."

"I've been here for thirty seconds."

"Thirty disruptive seconds," she said.

The corner of his mouth moved. She looked back at her book. He looked at the window. The cracked window let in the specific sound of a March Saturday in Havenbrook — not silent, not loud, the city conducting its ordinary business at a volume appropriate for the first weekend of spring.

After a while he said, without preamble: "Jisung thinks we should expand the Maren Street operation."

She looked up from the book. He was looking at the window.

"I think we shouldn't," he said.

She held the page she'd been reading. He had not, in five months of Saturdays, said anything about the operational specifics of DREAM. Not to her. Not directly. She understood this was not an accident — the same careful instinct that made him give her the right width of door, the same instinct that had said she stays and then I should have asked you first.

He was choosing to tell her this. She understood it as a choice.

"Why does he want to expand it," she said. Carefully. In the register of someone receiving rather than asking.

"More territory, more revenue," he said. "Jisung's math is always correct. His math is always correct." He paused. "The problem is the math doesn't account for the variables he doesn't want to account for."

"Like what," she said.

He was quiet for a moment. The cracked window. The March sound.

"Like what it costs the people who live on Maren Street," he said. "When we expand into their block."

She held that. She thought about Maren Street — the dry cleaner, the Cha's building, the specific smell of the east side that she had grown up knowing from two blocks away. She thought about the people she had seen from the apartment window for five months, the specific geography of people who had organized their lives in the particular way of people who had learned to operate around whatever occupied their neighborhood.

"That's what the piece of paper was," she said.

"That's what the piece of paper was," he confirmed.

She looked at him. The profile of him in the March light, looking at the window. The red hair and the dark jacket and the specific quality of someone who had been carrying something and had, just now, set a piece of it down in a room he trusted enough to set it down in.

"Will it work," she said. "The piece of paper."

He was quiet for a moment. "I don't know," he said honestly. "Jisung has good reasons. He's not wrong about the math." He looked at the window. "I just think some things aren't in the math."

She looked back at her book. She did not read it. The March light came through the window and the radiator rested from its winter work and somewhere on the street below a car moved past with its window down and music briefly entering the room before it was gone.

She understood, sitting on the couch with the book open and his jacket sleeve three inches from her arm, that this was what she stays had meant in its fullest version. Not just that she was allowed to be in the room. But that the room was allowed to be what it actually was when she was in it.

She thought about what she had been afraid of. The specific fear of losing something by naming it. She thought about Jiwoo: two hands is scarier.

She thought: one hand isn't enough anymore.

She looked at the window. He looked at the window. The March Saturday moved through its afternoon.

Jihoon moved on a Wednesday.

Not to Solar directly. That was not how he moved — she had known this for two and a half years, had learned it in the specific way you learned things that had been applied to you repeatedly. He moved through channels. He positioned people. He arranged conditions.

The condition he arranged on a Wednesday in the second week of March was this: a video.

She found out from Jiwoo, who found out from a junior she knew, who had seen it circulating in a group chat that was large enough to have moved through most of the school's connected social world by third period. She did not see the video herself — she did not look for it, which was a decision she made before she had finished making it. She did not need to see it. She knew what it contained from the incident. She had always known it was there, had been knowing it was there for two and a half years, had organized her life around the fact of its existence.

What she had not organized for was Donghyuck's name being in the caption.

Jiwoo told her this in the bathroom on the third floor, the one nobody used, because Jiwoo had the specific instinct for where to have certain conversations. She said it quickly and directly in the way she said things that required both: it's the old footage. his name is in the caption. something about DREAM. it's been in three chats that I know of.

Solar stood at the sink and looked at her reflection and breathed.

She had been through worse. She reminded herself of this. She had been through the incident itself, the immediate aftermath, the fourteen months of survival years, the party in December. She had looked Jihoon in the face on his own ground and told him he didn't get to be the reason for anything in her life anymore.

This was him responding.

She had known he would respond. She had not known when. Now she knew when, which was Wednesday in the second week of March at some point between first and third period.

"How many chats," she said.

"Three that I know of," Jiwoo said. "Probably more."

"Has it left the school yet."

"I don't think so," Jiwoo said. "Not yet."

She held the word yet in the way you held a live thing. She looked at her reflection. The green eyes, the stillness. She had been building this face since fifteen. She needed it right now.

"Okay," she said.

"Sol—"

"I'm okay," she said. And meant it, which was the part that surprised her — that she was saying it and it was true, not the composure version of true but the actual version. "I've known this was coming," she said. "Since December. Maybe before." She straightened from the sink. "He wants me to fall apart. He's been building toward something that makes me fall apart."

Jiwoo looked at her with the expression she used when she was deciding whether to agree or argue. She decided to agree. "What are you going to do," she said.

Solar looked at her reflection one more time. "I'm going to tell Donghyuck," she said. "Before he hears it from someone else."

She texted him between third and fourth period, in the hallway, with the specific economy of someone who had decided the speed of the information mattered more than the packaging:

jihoon released the footage. it's circulating in school chats. your name is in the caption.

She sent it. She put the phone in her bag. She went to fourth period and sat down and took out her notebook and looked at the board and did not look at her phone for fifty minutes because fifty minutes of class was fifty minutes of class and falling apart in fourth period was not something she was going to do.

The response was there when fourth period ended.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: cheng told me twenty minutes ago

Haechannie🐭: sol

Haechannie🐭: are you okay

She stood in the hallway with the fourth-period noise around her and typed:

yes. I wanted to tell you myself. are you?

Haechannie🐭: yes

Haechannie🐭: this doesn't change anything

Haechannie🐭: on my end

Haechannie🐭: i need you to know that

I know, she typed. I know.

Haechannie🐭: where are you

fifth period hallway

Haechannie🐭: stay there

She looked at the text. She looked at the hallway. Students moved around her in the specific fifth-period-transition flow, backpacks and noise and the particular density of a school day between classes.

Two minutes later Donghyuck came around the corner.

He was moving at a pace that was not running but contained the decision not to run, the specific controlled speed of someone who had been somewhere else and had chosen to be here instead. He had his jacket on. His hands were not in his pockets, which was the first time she had ever seen him approach something with his hands out.

He stopped in front of her.

She looked at him. He looked at her.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said.

He looked at her the way he always looked at her — receiving, present, the same way. Not different. Not less. The same.

She felt something shift, standing in the fifth-period hallway of DMHS with her phone in her hand and Jihoon's footage circulating in group chats she wasn't looking at. Something that had been held at a particular tension for months releasing slightly, not because the problem was resolved but because he was here and his hands were out and he was looking at her the same way.

"I'm fine," she said.

"I know," he said. "I came anyway."

She held that for a moment. The hallway moved around them. Someone bumped her shoulder on the way past and she steadied and he reached out briefly — one hand on her arm, steadying, and then withdrawn. Three seconds.

Three seconds. The specific temperature of it.

"You have class," she said.

"I'll be late," he said.

"Donghyuck—"

"Sol." He said it in the even register. "Let me be here for three minutes."

She held his gaze. The hallway did its fifth-period thing around them. She thought about two hands. She thought about the barely-not-bare trees. She thought about I'm never in the middle of something that matters more than that.

"Okay," she said. "Three minutes."

He stood with her in the fifth-period hallway for three minutes. They did not talk. He looked at her and she looked at the hallway and the hallway went about its business and neither of them said anything because there was nothing that needed to be said, because he was there and his hands had been out when he arrived and she was not falling apart.

At the end of three minutes he said: "Go to class."

"You too," she said.

He looked at her one more time. The same way. Then he turned and went back down the hallway the way he had come.

She stood where she was for a moment. The hallway had largely cleared, the transition mostly done, the school arriving at its fifth-period state. She was going to be late. That was fine.

She went to class.

That evening Cheng called.

She picked up on the second ring, which was the ring she always picked up on when it was Cheng calling in the evening.

"It's not going anywhere yet," he said, without preamble. "The footage. It's still in school chats. I've been watching it."

"Okay," she said.

"Jihoon's testing," Cheng said. "Seeing how it moves. Whether it gets any traction." He paused. "The DREAM caption is the variable."

"I know," she said.

"It makes it interesting to people who wouldn't otherwise care about a DMHS girl," Cheng said. "DREAM has reach beyond the school. If it moves into those channels—"

"I know, Cheng."

"Yeah," he said. He was quiet for a moment. "Hyuck's not doing anything about it."

She held the phone. "What do you mean."

"I mean he could," Cheng said carefully. "In the way that DREAM could. He's choosing not to."

"Why," she said.

"Because you didn't ask him to," Cheng said. "And because he knows you wouldn't want that to be how it gets handled." A pause. "He told me specifically. That the decision about how to respond is yours."

She held the phone and looked at the ceiling and breathed once, carefully. The decision is yours. Since October. Consistently, completely. Every time.

"What do I do," she said. Not to Cheng specifically. Just — out loud.

"You already know," Cheng said.

She thought about December. The party. You don't get to be the reason. She had gone onto his ground and said it to his face.

"I do," she said.

"Yeah," Cheng said.

"Tell Donghyuck I'll call him later," she said.

"Tell him yourself," Cheng said, and hung up.

She sat in her room for a few minutes after that, the March evening outside her window doing its early-dark thing that was already slightly later than February's early dark. The tree in the front yard had its new green — the barely-not-bare had committed somewhere in the first week of March, the color thin and pale but present.

She picked up her phone. She opened the thread.

Cheng told me you're not doing anything, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

why

Haechannie🐭: you know why

because I didn't ask

Haechannie🐭: and because you'd want to handle it yourself if you decide it needs handling

She held that. If you decide it needs handling. He had handed it back to her completely — not just the decision but the frame. Whether it needed responding to at all was hers to determine.

does it need handling, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: that depends

Haechannie🐭: what does he want

She thought about Jihoon. What he wanted. Two and a half years of learning the method. The testing, the patience, the positioning. He had released the footage into school chats with DREAM in the caption. He was watching it move. He was seeing what it did.

What he wanted was for her to respond. To contact him, or to fight it, or to crumble. Any version of engagement. Any confirmation that he still had the thread.

he wants me to respond, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: so

She looked at the screen. She thought about the party in December, standing in front of him, saying you don't get to be the reason. She thought about the convoy of information she had been managing since the incident. She thought about being sixteen years old in Havenbrook with the specific life she had and the specific person who had been in her phone since October and the specific direction she was moving.

so I don't, she typed.

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: you know it might move further

Haechannie🐭: the footage

I know

Haechannie🐭: and you're still not going to respond

and I'm still not going to respond, she confirmed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: for what it's worth

Haechannie🐭: i think that's right

I know you do

Haechannie🐭: how do you know

She smiled at the phone in the March evening.

because you've always thought I was right, she typed. even when I didn't think so yet.

The three dots appeared and stayed for a long time.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: i have

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

She put the phone down. The tree outside her window held its new green in the March dark. The barely-not-bare had become, sometime while she wasn't watching, simply there — not qualified, not almost, just present.

She thought about the hallway. His hands out when he arrived. Three minutes. Let me be here for three minutes.

She thought about two hands.

She thought: soon.

The March dark held its particular quality — not February's grey, not the flat white of winter, but something new, something that had decided. She lay in it and let it hold her and the barely-visible stars on her ceiling did their barely-visible work and she fell asleep with the word soon sitting in her chest like something already true.


17: naming it

Not responding turned out to have its own texture.

She had expected silence to feel like absence — the specific negative quality of not doing the thing you would otherwise do. What it actually felt like was more like weight. The specific weight of holding a position against pressure, the way you held a door shut with your body. Not dramatic. Just continuous. Every morning she opened her phone and looked at the inbox and decided again. Every day the footage circulated in channels she wasn't looking at and she chose again not to look. Every hour that Jihoon waited for her to engage and she didn't was another hour of the decision being remade.

It was, she understood, its own kind of response. The silence was not neutral. It was chosen.

Jiwoo confirmed this on Wednesday when she said: "He's frustrated."

They were walking to school in the March morning, the light already different from February's — more actual, more interested in arriving. Solar held her coffee and looked at the sidewalk.

"How do you know," she said.

"Yuna," Jiwoo said. "She was talking at lunch yesterday. Jihoon expected something by now. He doesn't understand why you're not — " she paused. "Moving."

"I know why he doesn't understand," Solar said.

"Because you've always moved before," Jiwoo said.

"Because I've always moved before," Solar confirmed. She drank her coffee. The trees on either side of the street had their new green now — the pale tentative kind, committed without being confident yet. "He built a method around me. The method assumed I would respond to pressure."

"And you're not," Jiwoo said.

"And I'm not," Solar said.

They walked for half a block.

"Is it hard," Jiwoo said.

Solar thought about that honestly. "Parts of it," she said. "Not the not-responding part. That's made. The hard part is knowing it's out there. That I can't control what people think when they see it."

"You never could," Jiwoo said.

"I know," Solar said. "That's the hard part."

Jiwoo nodded. She drank her coffee. The school became visible at the end of the street, its lights on, the early arrivals making their way up the front path.

"For what it's worth," Jiwoo said, "I think it's the right call."

"You and Donghyuck," Solar said.

"We agree on most things," Jiwoo said — easy, offhand, the way you said things about people who had become real to you by proximity. She glanced at Solar sideways. "He's the kind of person who stands in a fifth-period hallway for three minutes because you didn't ask him not to." She paused. "Those are hard to find."

Solar looked at the sidewalk.

"Yeah," she said. "I know."

The footage stopped moving Thursday.

Not because Jihoon withdrew it — it remained wherever it had landed, available to anyone who went looking. But it stopped circulating. Things moved when they had novelty and stopped when they didn't, and the footage of a DMHS girl from two years ago had had its window and the window had closed. What was left was the footage in the places it had settled, waiting for the next time someone found it useful.

The footage stopped moving Thursday.

Not because Jihoon withdrew it — it remained wherever it had landed, available to anyone who went looking. But it stopped circulating. Things moved when they had novelty and stopped when they didn't, and the footage of a DMHS girl from two years ago had had its window and the window had closed.

Cheng told her Thursday evening with the specific flatness of someone delivering good news he didn't want to overstate.

"It's not gone," he said. "But it's not moving."

"The silence worked," she said.

"For now," he said. And then: "Hyuck says to tell you he's not surprised."

She thought about saying I know and decided to say it to the right person instead. She hung up and opened the thread.

Cheng says you're not surprised, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i told you

Haechannie🐭: you were right

we were right, she typed. it was a joint decision.

Haechannie🐭: it was your decision

Haechannie🐭: i just agreed with it

that counts

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i'm glad it worked

Haechannie🐭: but more than that

Haechannie🐭: i'm glad you did it your way

She held the phone. Outside, the March evening was doing something specific with the light — the longer day making itself known, the sky holding color later than it had in months.

me too, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone down and looked at the tree through the window for a while before she fell asleep.

Saturday she arrived eighteen minutes early.

The apartment was empty when she came through the door.

Not entirely — Renjun's laptop was on the couch, and the kitchen showed evidence of recent use, and the back room door was closed in its usual way, the light underneath it off. But no one was visible. The apartment had the quality of a place recently vacated, its warmth the residual kind, belonging to the walls more than to anyone currently in it.

She set her bag down. She sat on the couch.

She had been in this room enough Saturdays to know it by feel — the specific give of the cushion, the distance from the window, the radiator in the corner that knocked twice before settling. She knew which floorboard near the kitchen doorway shifted under weight. She knew the back room door's hinges made no sound opening but a specific small sound closing. She had learned these things the way you learned a place you had decided to belong to — not all at once, just incrementally, until one day the place was simply inside you.

She looked at the back room door. She thought about the men who had come out of it three weeks ago, the transaction completed, the apartment exhaling. She thought about the piece of paper Donghyuck had written for Jisung, folded, carried in his jacket pocket. She thought about it's what they do in Cheng's voice, which was the sentence she had been learning to hold without flinching.

The apartment was warm and still and completely ordinary. It was also the place where those things happened. Both of those things were true simultaneously and she had stopped needing to resolve them.

The front door opened.

Donghyuck came in with his jacket on and his hands free — not in his pockets, the way they were when he was going somewhere and had not yet arrived. He looked at the couch and registered her with the specific expression he used for pleasant surprises he had been hoping for.

"You're early," he said.

"You said one," she said.

"It's twelve forty-two," he said.

She checked her phone. He was correct. "I walked fast," she said.

The corner of his mouth moved. He came in and shrugged off his jacket — the dark one, worn soft at the elbows in the way of something that had been chosen and kept — and dropped it over the back of the chair. Underneath he had a grey hoodie, the sleeves pushed up, and the chain at his collar that he wore in most configurations: layered links, the kind that sat between casual and deliberate, that she had noticed in October and had become simply part of the picture since.

He sat on the couch.

"Everyone's gone until three," he said. "Mark had a thing. Chenle and Renjun went with him. Jeno is somewhere."

"Jisung?" she said.

"Also somewhere," he said.

She held the information of the empty apartment — the first time in five months of Saturdays, the specific difference of proximity without the social buffer of other people to absorb it. She was aware of it in the way she was aware of most things: fast, complete, filed.

"Okay," she said.

He looked at the window. She looked at him.

She had been in the habit of looking at him sideways for five months — the peripheral version, the version that gave her information without committing to the act of looking. She had catalogued him piece by piece in that register: the lean against walls, the coin-trick habit with the unlit cigarette, the way the not-quite smile arrived before he could manage it. But the sideways version had limits. The sideways version was still, at its core, a management strategy.

She looked at him directly.

The red of his hair was darker than it had been in winter — March light made it more itself, the color that was almost auburn from certain angles and closer to true red from others, currently falling across his forehead in the way it did when he hadn't pushed it back yet in the day. He had a strong jaw that softened when he wasn't performing anything, a quality his face had right now that it didn't have in the hallways of DMHS where the jaw sat slightly differently and the eyes did more work. Dark eyes with the depth of someone who paid attention to everything and revealed the results selectively. There was a small mole near the bridge of his nose she had noticed once in the library and never mentioned. The chain lay at his collarbone against the grey of the hoodie. His hands were in his lap — long-fingered, the kind that looked deliberate when they moved, which she had noticed when he turned the cigarette between them.

This was the Donghyuck face. Not the Haechan one. She had been looking at both long enough to know the difference from anywhere in a room, but this was the one she had — the one the apartment had, the one the texts had, the one that showed up in the honest moments.

She had been looking at it sideways for five months.

She was done looking sideways.

"I need to say something," she said.

He turned from the window and looked at her. Whatever he had been about to say dissolved. Something in his expression shifted to the specific quality of someone who had been waiting for a particular moment and understood it had arrived.

"Okay," he said.

The apartment was quiet around them in the specific way of an empty space built for more people than were currently in it. The radiator had settled into its twice-and-done knock. Outside, March was doing the thing it had been building toward all week — actual warmth, the first that had felt like a promise rather than an exception, the spring light coming through the window at the angle that meant mid-afternoon was genuinely bright.

She looked at him. She thought about the infrastructure conversation — October, the library, systems built to protect something and the architecture that either holds it or doesn't. She had been dismantling the relevant architecture since October, piece by piece, with the patience of someone who understood that both construction and demolition required care. This was the last wall. She had been standing at it since February with the sentence already formed.

"I know what this is," she said. "What we are." She held the weight of saying it out loud rather than thinking it — the specific difference between the two, which was larger than she had expected. "I've known since before December. Maybe before October." She looked at him directly, the way she had decided to look at things she was done looking sideways at. "I've been not saying it because saying it makes it something you can lose, and I have lost a lot of things. I didn't want to add this to that list."

He was very still.

"But I'm tired," she said. "Of holding it with one hand because two hands is scarier." She held his gaze. "I like you. I have liked you for a very long time. And I know you know that, and I know you know I know you know it, and I'm saying it out loud anyway because—" she let the real reason arrive without dressing it. "Because you deserve to hear it. As Donghyuck. From me. Out loud."

The apartment held the sentence.

He looked at her with the expression of someone who had been patient for a specific thing and was now in the moment of receiving it and had not, despite the patience, been fully prepared for the receiving. The ease he wore everywhere — the ambient ease of Haechan, the performing ease of someone who had learned young that ease was a form of authority — was entirely absent. What was left was just his face in the March light. The jaw not set. The eyes not doing extra work. Just him, in the grey hoodie, on the couch, looking at her the way he had looked at her since October — the same way, always the same way.

"Sol," he said. His voice had a quality she had not heard before — not different, just unmanaged. The way something sounded when the door was off it.

"You don't have to say anything back," she said. "This isn't a question."

"I know," he said. "I'm going to say something anyway."

She held that. The spring light through the window. The chain at his collarbone. His hands in his lap, still.

"I have liked you," he said, "since August." He said it the way he said true things — directly, without engineering. "Before you knew my name. Before you knew we went to the same school. Before October." He looked at her with the same directness she had given him, the honest exchange of it. "I asked Cheng to point out your locker because I wanted to know where you were in the building. I asked for your number because I wanted to talk to you. I put the cigarette in my pocket because I already knew you didn't like the smell." A pause. "Everything since October has been because I like you. As Donghyuck. Specifically." His jaw had the quality it had when he was choosing something carefully. "You didn't need to say it for me to mean it. But I'm glad you said it."

The apartment held the sentence. She held it.

She thought about August. August, when she had been walking the science wing detours, when the incident was fourteen months behind her and Jihoon was still a daily calculation and the boy who would eventually be in her phone every day had been in Cheng's driveway asking to know where her locker was. He had been watching out for her since August. She had known this. She had filed it repeatedly. She had never received it until this moment, in the March light, with him saying it directly.

"Since August," she said.

"Since August," he said.

She held that.

"That's a long time to be patient," she said.

Something moved through his expression — not the not-quite-smile, something quieter than that, the kind of thing that happened in the honest face when it had run out of ways to be managed. "You were worth being patient for," he said. Simply. Without dressing it up.

She looked at him. The red hair, the chain, the honest face, the grey hoodie, the hands in his lap. The dark jacket on the back of the chair behind him with the folded piece of paper still in the pocket, probably — the argument he'd written for Jisung about Maren Street, about what the math didn't account for. The person who was both things simultaneously, always: the warmth and the edge of it, the tender and the dangerous, the boy who put cigarettes in his pocket and wrote arguments on paper for people who wouldn't listen and had been watching out for her since August.

She was going to leave eventually. That was still true. The direction was still the direction. He knew this. He had known it longer than she'd known it herself and had never once asked her to change it.

She was still here now.

She reached over — not dramatically, not with announcement — and took his hand. Not the sidewalk version, not the shaking version. Just her hand in his, in the ordinary March afternoon. His fingers closed around hers the same way they always did — carefully, with the specific care of someone who understood the weight of what they were holding.

The chain caught the light. The spring warmth came through the window. The back room door stood closed across the room with its own specific history, and the jacket on the chair held its folded piece of paper, and the apartment was both things it had always been, and she was inside both of those things with both hands.

"Okay," he said, quietly.

"Okay," she said back.

They sat in the quiet of the apartment for a long time after that. The spring light moved through its afternoon angle, the warmth doing what warmth did with the specific surfaces of a room that had been cold all winter. Neither of them said anything for a while, because they had said the things that needed saying and what followed that was just this — the ordinary specific weight of being two people who had named something and were sitting inside it.

After a while he said, without looking away from the window: "Chenle is going to be insufferable."

She laughed — the real kind, involuntary, the one she had stopped managing in rooms that were safe. "Renjun too," she said.

"Renjun will make an observation," he said.

"With documentation," she said.

"He's been building a case for months," he said. "The tea. The mugs. The couch geometry. He has files."

She looked at the window. The chain at his collarbone. The spring light. His hand in hers. "Let him," she said.

"Yeah," he said. "Let him."

They were still on the couch when the door opened at three-fifteen.

Chenle came through first. He looked at the couch. He looked at their hands. He looked at the ceiling, then back at them, with the expression of someone processing through multiple frames of reference simultaneously.

"I have been waiting," he announced, to the room, "for five months."

"Hi Chenle," Solar said.

"Five months," Chenle said, with the dignity of someone who had endured something and been vindicated. "October. November. December. January. February. March." He counted them on his fingers. "Five."

Renjun came in behind him. He looked at the couch. Then at Donghyuck, with the expression of a filing system confirmed in order. "I'd like to note," he said, to the room, "that I said so in January."

"The tea," Donghyuck said.

"The tea," Renjun confirmed, and opened his laptop.

Mark came through last. He looked at the couch. He looked at Donghyuck. Something moved through his expression — not quite a smile, more than not quite, the specific thing Mark did when he had been watching something develop for a long time and was satisfied with where it landed. He nodded once. Then he went to the kitchen.

Which was, Solar had come to understand, the most eloquent thing Mark could do.

"Five months," Chenle said again, apparently not finished.

"Chenle," Donghyuck said.

"I'm just noting," Chenle said.

"You've noted it," Donghyuck said.

"Twice," Renjun said, without looking up.

"I might note it again," Chenle said.

Solar looked at the window. The March light. The apartment holding them all — Renjun's laptop, Chenle's ongoing accounting, the sound of Mark's kettle starting in the kitchen, the couch, the chain catching the afternoon, the jacket on the chair with its pocket, the spring warmth doing its particular work on a room that had been cold all winter.

She was here.

She had chosen to be here.

With both hands.

That was enough. In the specific way that enough in Havenbrook was the most honest thing available, and in the specific way she was learning to let enough be enough without immediately asking what came next — it was exactly that.


18: ordinary means real


The morning after felt ordinary, which was the specific thing she had not expected.

She woke up at seven-oh-four on a Sunday to the usual sounds — the refrigerator, the distant early-morning quality of the neighborhood before it had committed to the day, the grey-white light of a March morning coming through the curtains. She lay there for a moment and took stock of the previous day the way she took stock of most things: quickly, completely, with the particular inventory of someone who had learned that the contents of a day were best assessed before the day had a chance to reframe them.

Saturday. The empty apartment. The spring light. His hands. Since August. The hand in his. Chenle's five-months accounting. The kettle. The drive home through the March evening with the windows slightly down because it had been the first day warm enough to justify it.

She had said it. He had said it. They had sat with it on the couch for two hours with the gang world's residue present in the room — the jacket on the chair, the back room door, the folded piece of paper — and neither of those things had cancelled the other. Both things were true. Both things were still true this morning.

The refrigerator hummed. The grey-white light did its Sunday thing.

She picked up her phone.

Haechannie🐭: morning

She looked at that for a moment. He had sent it at seven-oh-two, which was two minutes before she woke up, which meant he had been awake. Which meant he had been thinking about whether to send it and had sent it.

morning, she typed.

fine, she typed. you?

yeah

Haechannie🐭: yesterday

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i've been thinking about it

me too, she typed. what are you thinking

A pause. The kind that meant he was deciding how much to say, which was a thing she had learned to read like weather.

Haechannie🐭: that it doesn't change anything

Haechannie🐭: and also that it changes everything

Haechannie🐭: at the same time

Haechannie🐭: which is weird

She held the phone. Outside, the Sunday morning was doing its particular thing with the light. She thought about what he had said — it doesn't change anything and also it changes everything — and understood he had arrived at the same place she had. The specific paradox of naming something you have both already known, which changes nothing about the knowing and changes everything about the naming.

yeah, she typed. that's exactly it.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: i just wanted to make sure we were in the same place

we are, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: sol

Haechannie🐭: what are you doing today

She looked at the ceiling. The barely-visible stars. The ordinary Sunday.

nothing specific, she typed. you?

Haechannie🐭: also nothing specific

Haechannie🐭: yet

She read the yet and understood it and typed: donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

it's seven in the morning

Haechannie🐭: i know what time it is

on a Sunday

Haechannie🐭: i know what day it is

She pressed her mouth flat. give me an hour, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah?

yeah, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i'll come to you

She put the phone down. She lay in the grey-white Sunday light for approximately forty-five seconds before she got up.

He arrived at nine-fifteen.

She opened the door and he was standing on her porch with his hands in his jacket pockets and the red hair doing what it did in the March morning light — darker at the roots, brighter where the sun found it, the color that was almost auburn from the angle he was standing at. He had the chain at his collar over a black t-shirt under the jacket, which meant he had gotten dressed in whatever order made sense at nine on a Sunday, which meant he had not thought too hard about it, which was its own kind of information.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said.

She stepped back from the doorway and he came in — his first time inside her house, which she registered as a specific crossing of a threshold, literally and otherwise. He stood in her hallway and looked at it with the unhurried inventory of someone who was noting things without needing to comment on them. The hooks by the door. Her bag on the floor. The particular way the hallway light worked in the morning.

"Your mom's out?" he said.

"Work," she said. "She works Sunday mornings."

He nodded. He looked at the hallway. She looked at him looking at it.

"Come on," she said.

She took him to the kitchen, which was where she had been heading anyway, because she had not eaten yet and neither had he probably, and food was the most ordinary possible context for whatever the morning was going to be, which was what she wanted. Ordinary. The specific ordinary of two people who had said a thing and were now learning to inhabit it in daylight.

She made coffee. He sat at the kitchen table — her mother's kitchen table, the one with the chair Solar had always taken and the one adjacent that her mother took — and looked out the kitchen window at the backyard, which was small and had a tree that was doing the same pale tentative green as every other tree in Havenbrook in March.

"This is a different tree than the front one," he observed.

"Different tree," she confirmed.

"The front one's further along," he said.

"More sun," she said.

He looked at the backyard tree for a moment. "It'll catch up," he said.

She handed him a mug. He took it with both hands the way she had seen him take most things that were warm — practical, present, not making anything of it. She sat across from him at the table, in her mother's chair, which was fine because her mother wasn't here.

They drank their coffee. The Sunday kitchen held them in its ordinary way — the refrigerator, the specific morning light through the window, the backyard tree making its slow commitment.

"You grew up here," he said. Not a question.

"My whole life," she said. "We moved here when I was three. I don't remember before."

He looked around the kitchen. She let him look — she understood he was building the picture of her in the place she had been before he knew her, the version of her that had existed in this kitchen before October, and she did not feel the need to manage what he found.

"The stars," he said. "On your ceiling."

She looked at him.

"You mentioned them once," he said. "In October. You said you'd put them up at twelve and never taken them down."

She had mentioned it once, in passing, in a text she had sent without much thought. He had kept it. He had been keeping things since August, which she was still adjusting to — the specific weight of someone who had been paying attention before she knew the attention was being paid.

"They're still up," she said.

"Can I see," he said.

She looked at him. He was asking about the glow-in-the-dark stars she had put on her ceiling at twelve years old and never taken down. She considered what it meant to take him upstairs, which was a different kind of threshold than the front door, which was the room where she had been lying awake on the important nights — the texts, the submission, the footage, the not-saying-it. The room with all the interior versions of the last five months stored in its walls.

"Yeah," she said.

Her room was the room. She had not cleaned it specifically because she had not known he was coming until an hour ago, which meant it was its actual state — the desk with the laptop and the peripheral and the headset hung on its stand, the bookshelf with the book she had been reading all year still sitting horizontal where she'd left it last night, the lamp, the scattered evidence of a person who lived inside their own space without performing it for anyone.

He stood in the doorway for a moment and looked at it the way he had looked at the kitchen — noting, unhurried, building the picture.

Then he looked up.

The stars on the ceiling were barely visible in daylight, which was their nature — they needed dark to be what they were, the faint phosphorescent green of something that only showed itself when the room had stopped performing. In the March morning light they were just small plastic shapes arranged in a rough approximation of constellations by a twelve-year-old who had been trying to make something permanent.

He looked at them for a moment.

"You put them there when you were twelve," he said.

"Yeah," she said.

"Before the incident," he said.

She looked at him. He was still looking at the ceiling.

"Yes," she said.

"So they were here through all of it," he said. "The whole time."

She held that. She had never thought about it in exactly those terms — the stars on the ceiling as witnesses, as the only thing in the room that predated the incident and had survived it unchanged because she had not taken them down. They had been there in October when she'd had the first texts. In December when she'd made peace with not fighting Jihoon. In February when she'd held the word soon in her chest. In March last night when she'd put the phone down and the okay was still sitting in her.

"Yeah," she said. "They were here through all of it."

He looked at them for another moment. Then he looked at her. The honest face in the morning light, the dark eyes that had the depth of someone who had just received information and was deciding where to put it.

"They're good stars," he said.

She almost laughed. She did laugh — short, involuntary, the real kind. "They're from a drugstore," she said. "They cost four dollars."

"Still good," he said, with the specific serenity of someone who had made a judgment and was not revisiting it.

She looked at the ceiling. He looked at her. The March morning did its ordinary Sunday thing around them.

They walked afterward — not to the east side, just through the neighborhood, the specific aimlessness of two people who had somewhere to be later and nowhere to be right now. The March air had the warmth in it that had arrived on Saturday and was keeping its promise, the specific warmth of a season that had finally committed.

She walked on his left. He had his hands in his pockets but differently than usual — not the performing-ease pockets, the actually-comfortable-in-the-world pockets. She had her hands in her jacket. Their shoulders were adjacent the way their shoulders were always adjacent.

"Chenle texted me this morning," he said.

"What did he say," she said.

"He said, and I'm quoting: 'I have been vindicated.'"

She laughed again. The real kind, again, on a Sunday morning in March. "What did you say back."

"I told him to eat breakfast," he said.

"Did he?"

"He sent a photo of cereal," he said. "With a trophy emoji."

She looked at the sidewalk ahead of them. The pale green of the committed trees. The specific quality of a Havenbrook Sunday morning in the first actual week of spring — quiet but not empty, the neighborhood doing its ordinary business, the smell of it different from winter, something opening up in the air.

"What about Renjun," she said.

"Renjun sent me a document," he said.

She looked at him. "A document."

"A document," he confirmed. "Three pages. Titled 'observed data.' The tea is in there. The mugs. The couch geometry. There's a graph."

"There is not a graph."

"There is absolutely a graph," he said. "He labeled the axes."

She stopped walking. He stopped walking. She looked at him with the expression she used for things she was genuinely uncertain whether to believe.

"Show me," she said.

He pulled out his phone. He showed her.

There was a graph.

She looked at it. He watched her look at it. The graph had two axes — time on the horizontal, proximity on the vertical, rendered in a simple line that started somewhere in October and had been moving steadily upward in a way that Renjun had apparently been tracking with the diligence of someone who took data seriously and had decided this particular data set was worth his time.

"He's been building this since October," she said.

"Since the tea," Donghyuck said. "The tea was the baseline data point."

She looked at the graph for another moment. Then she handed the phone back. She started walking again. He fell into step beside her.

"I like your friends," she said.

"They like you," he said. "Chenle especially. He's been rooting for this since the first night you came to the apartment."

"The mysterious orange drink night," she said.

"He never lets anyone try the mysterious orange drink," Donghyuck said. "He offered it to you specifically. That was a declaration."

She looked at the sidewalk. "What is the mysterious orange drink."

"No one knows," he said. "It's been there since before I got there. We don't ask."

She considered this. "That's either very normal or very concerning."

"In that apartment," he said, "it's both."

She smiled. The walk continued. The street was the street — the specific Havenbrook Sunday of it, the houses and the trees and the occasional car, the dog two blocks over that was apparently always conducting surveys regardless of the day or hour. His shoulder adjacent to hers. The warmth doing its thing.

Monday the school resumed its social physics with the particular energy of a Monday that followed a weekend where nothing publicly visible had happened but where the existing undercurrents had continued running.

Solar noticed the specific recalibration in the hallway by second period — not a new configuration, just the existing one slightly updated. She and Donghyuck's name had been a subject since February, and that subject had not been paused by the weekend. If anything the footage incident, and its subsequent silence, had clarified something in the school's assessment: whatever was happening there was apparently durable enough to survive Jihoon releasing the footage and Solar not flinching. That durability was itself information.

She had expected this. She walked through the hallways with the practiced composure she had been building since fifteen and did not let the assessment land anywhere specific.

What she had not expected was Jihoon waiting by her locker.

Not in the threatening way — he was not blocking it, not positioned in the geometry of intent. He was leaning against the adjacent wall with the specific lean she had known for two and a half years, hands in the grey jacket, watching her approach with an expression she had learned to read and was reading now: not the patience face, not the calculation face. Something she did not have a clean category for.

She stopped in front of her locker. She turned her combination. She kept her face level.

"Jihoon," she said.

"Sol," he said.

She opened her locker. She took out the books she needed. She put the ones she didn't back.

"The footage," he said.

"Yes," she said.

"You didn't respond," he said.

"No," she said.

He was quiet for a moment. She closed her locker and looked at him directly, the way she had looked at things directly since December.

"I'm trying to understand," he said, "what changed."

"I told you in December," she said. "At the party."

"You said I didn't get to be the reason," he said. "I'm asking what changed so that was possible."

She held his gaze. She thought about what had changed — October, November, December, January, February, March. The texts and the drive and the library and the cigarette and the hand and Saturday and Sunday morning in her kitchen with the coffee and the backyard tree and the stars on her ceiling. She thought about what had changed and knew she was not going to tell him what it was.

"I did," she said. "That's what changed."

He looked at her for a long moment. The hallway moved around them — students and the morning noise and the specific density of a DMHS Monday, none of which paused for this conversation.

"He's DREAM," Jihoon said.

"Yes," she said.

"You understand what that means," he said.

"Better than I did in September," she said. "Yes."

Something moved through his expression — not the smile, not the patience, something that was closer to the thing she had seen in the convenience store parking lot. The version that said I'm not the villain you've made me. She had told him he had made himself. She still believed that. But she understood, looking at him now, that he was also a person who had wanted something and had not known how to want it without destroying it, which did not make him not dangerous but made him comprehensible in a way she had not entirely allowed before.

She was not going to tell him this either.

"I'm going to class," she said.

He looked at her for one more moment. Then he stepped aside.

She walked to class. She did not look back. She thought about what he had said — you understand what that means — and understood that in his specific calculation it was a warning and not a revelation, which meant he still believed she was operating with incomplete information, which meant he did not know what she knew.

She knew what she knew.

She went to class and sat down and took out her notebook and thought: I know exactly what it means.

She told Jiwoo at lunch.

Not the Jihoon conversation — she would get to that. The other thing first. The Saturday thing, the Sunday morning thing, the graph. She told Jiwoo the way she had started telling her things — directly, in the corner of the cafeteria, with the specific ease of someone who had stopped needing to edit before speaking at least to this one person.

Jiwoo listened. She did not interrupt. She ate her lunch and listened and when Solar finished she put down her fork and looked at her with the expression she used when she was feeling several things and organizing them by priority.

"Renjun made a graph," she said finally.

"With labeled axes," Solar said.

"I want to see this graph," Jiwoo said.

"I'll send it to you," Solar said.

Jiwoo looked at the cafeteria for a moment. Then she looked back at Solar with the expression underneath the strategic one — the open one, the one from the first lunch, the one that never required anything back.

"Sol," she said.

"Yeah," Solar said.

"How does it feel," Jiwoo said. Not the polite version of the question — the actual one.

Solar thought about Sunday morning. The stars. His hands around the mug. The walk. His shoulder adjacent to hers. The way the kitchen had looked with him sitting in it, like a room that had been waiting for one more person to be complete.

"Like something I didn't know I was building toward," she said. "And now I'm here and it's — " she stopped, finding it. "It's very ordinary. And that's the part that's making me feel the most."

Jiwoo nodded slowly. "Because ordinary means real," she said.

"Because ordinary means real," Solar agreed.

They ate their lunch. The cafeteria did its Monday thing around them — the volume, the specific social physics of a new week, someone dropping a tray somewhere behind them to the usual brief acknowledgment. At the DREAM table across the room, Mark and Jeno were in what appeared to be a low-intensity debate about something that required Jeno to gesture twice. Renjun was on his laptop, which was always true. Donghyuck was not there yet, which meant he was somewhere else in the building, which was fine, which was ordinary, which was the specific ordinary of knowing where someone was even when they weren't visible.

"Jihoon was at my locker this morning," Solar said.

Jiwoo looked at her.

"He wanted to know what changed," Solar said. "I told him I did."

Jiwoo was quiet for a moment. "How did he take it."

"He stepped aside," Solar said.

Jiwoo held that. She picked up her fork. "Is that it, do you think? From him?"

Solar thought about the convenience store parking lot. The footage. The two and a half years of method. She thought about a person who had decided on an outcome and had been waiting for conditions to align and had watched the conditions not align in the specific way he'd needed them to.

"No," she said honestly. "I don't think so."

"But for now," Jiwoo said.

"For now," Solar said.

They ate their lunch. The cafeteria continued. The Monday arranged itself around them and Solar let it, which was something she had been learning — that the ordinary could hold even the not-ordinary things, that the world ran its Monday program whether or not you were in the middle of something significant, and that the middle of something significant was actually just where you lived, and the Monday program was not the interruption.

She was in the middle of something significant.

The Monday program ran.

Both things were true at once.

She was getting very good at holding both things at once.


19: the maren expansion

The second week of March ran quietly.

Not the February quiet — not the held-breath quality of something building underground. This was a different register, the quiet of a week that was going about its business without particular drama, the school and the east side and the city doing their ordinary things in the specific warmth of a March that had decided to be itself.

She and Donghyuck existed in it the way they had been existing in things since October — through the texts, through the Saturdays, through the route home after school that had accumulated into something without being named. What was different now was that the not-naming had been replaced with the having-named, which changed the texture without changing the form. The texts were the same texts. The couch was the same couch. He said Sol in the same register he always used. The difference was internal — some piece of the architecture she had been maintaining dismantled, the load-bearing piece, the one that had required the most effort to keep up. Without it the rest of the structure was still standing but it breathed differently.

She was learning to breathe differently.

On Wednesday he waited for her at the east entrance after school.

Not the side path — the main entrance, the one with the social density of end-of-day DMHS, the one where visibility was a given rather than a calculation. He was leaning against the wall with his hands in his pockets and the dark jacket and the chain at his collar, and three girls she recognized from the junior class were in the vicinity with the specific orbital quality that Solar now understood as the permanent condition of being near him in public. He was looking at his phone.

He looked up when she came through the door.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

One of the junior girls looked between them with the expression Solar had been cataloguing since February — the updated assessment, the recalibration of someone whose picture of the situation had recently been revised and was still processing the revision. Solar registered it without making anything of it. She had stopped making anything of it.

They walked the first three blocks of the route home the way they had been walking them since January — adjacent, not touching, the conversation running in the low easy register of people who had gotten comfortable enough with each other's silences to talk through them rather than around them. He said something about Jeno winning an argument about something she hadn't followed. She said something about a quiz she had done better on than expected. The ordinary specific dailiness of it, the version that existed in daylight rather than text.

At the corner of Fourth and Maren she saw Cheng.

He was coming from the direction of the east side — moving with the particular pace he used when he was coming from something rather than going toward it, a slightly different quality than his usual gait. He saw them at the same moment and changed course.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," Solar said. Donghyuck looked at him.

Cheng looked at Donghyuck with the expression he used for information transfer — the one that meant something needed to be said that required a specific context to say it in. "Can I walk with you," he said.

"Yeah," Donghyuck said.

They walked. Cheng fell into step on Donghyuck's other side, which put Donghyuck between them, which was a specific geometry that Solar noticed without commenting on. After a moment Cheng said, low enough that the street absorbed most of it: "Jisung got a yes."

Donghyuck was quiet for half a step.

"From who," he said.

"The Kim side," Cheng said. "They agreed to the Maren expansion last night."

Donghyuck's jaw shifted. Solar watched it happen from her peripheral vision — the specific micro-adjustment that meant something had landed in a place he wasn't ready for it. He looked at the street ahead with the quality she had seen in the apartment on compressed Saturdays: not anger exactly, something more like recognition. The recognition of someone who had made the argument and had the argument rejected and was now living in the reality of the rejection.

"Sol," he said, without looking at her.

"I heard," she said.

"You know what it means," he said.

"Maren Street expands," she said. "More territory. More of what goes with territory."

He looked at her then. The honest face and also something underneath it — something that was the Haechan face and the Donghyuck face simultaneously, the place where the two versions of him were not separate but the same. She held his gaze.

"More of what goes with territory," he said. "That's the accurate version."

"I know," she said.

Cheng said nothing. He was doing the thing he did when he had delivered information and was allowing it to be received — the particular diplomatic silence of someone who understood his role was courier and not interpreter.

They reached the point where Cheng usually turned. He turned. He nodded at Solar once — not a goodbye specifically, more the acknowledgment of someone who had delivered what he came to deliver and was satisfied it had been received. Then he was gone.

She and Donghyuck walked the next block without talking.

"The piece of paper didn't work," she said.

"The piece of paper didn't work," he confirmed.

"What happens now," she said.

He looked at the street. "Now Jisung runs the operation and I do my part of it," he said. "Which is what I always do."

"You disagree with it," she said.

"Yes," he said.

"But you're going to do your part," she said.

"Yes," he said. He said it the way he said things that were true and did not require justification — not defensively, not asking for her understanding, just stating the fact of it. He looked at her. "That's the part I can't give you a cleaner version of."

She held that. They were at the divergence point — the corner where their routes separated, the end of the walking-together section, the place that had accumulated its own specific weight over months of arriving at it. She thought about what he had said — I can't give you a cleaner version. He was not asking her to accept it or to leave it. He was just being accurate about what it was.

"I know," she said.

"Sol."

"I know," she said again. "I knew before Sunday. I knew before Saturday. I knew in October when I first understood what DREAM was and went to the apartment anyway." She looked at him directly. "I'm not asking for a cleaner version."

He held her gaze for a moment. The March afternoon light on his face — the red hair, the jaw, the dark eyes with their particular depth. The chain at his collar. The jacket that had been to places she didn't know the shape of and would go to more.

"Okay," he said.

"Okay," she said.

She turned toward her route. He turned toward his. She had gone three steps when he said, behind her:

"Sol."

She turned.

He was looking at her from the corner with his hands in his pockets and the honest face, the specific face he had when he was going to say something he meant without dressing it.

"Nothing I do in that operation is going to be about anything except the operation," he said. "I need you to know that."

She understood what he was telling her. That whatever happened on Maren Street — the expansion, Jisung's math, the operational world doing its operational things — it would not be about her. Not about her safety, not used as leverage, not something that would arrive back at her doorstep. He was drawing a line between his worlds and placing it where she could see it.

"I know," she said.

He nodded once. He went toward the east side. She went toward home.

She walked the last four blocks in the specific quality of someone carrying more than they had picked up today — not heavy, just weighted, the weight of information that was now part of the picture rather than adjacent to it. She had known what DREAM was. She had known what the operational world contained. She had chosen it with full information.

The full information had just become slightly fuller.

She was still choosing.

Thursday the Maren Street expansion was visible.

Not dramatically — not in the way that violence was visible, which she had no particular reason to expect yet.
