But in the specific way that territory's expansion was always visible to people who knew the geography: a different quality on certain blocks, the footprint of something that had reorganized itself, the city adjusting to a new configuration the way water adjusted to a reshaped container.

She noticed it on the walk to school. She noticed it in the specific quality of a block she had walked past for two years looking slightly different — not the buildings, not the street, just the sense of something redistributed. Like furniture rearranged in a room you knew well enough to feel the difference without identifying each piece.

She did not say anything about it.

She went to school. She did her classes. She had her lunch in the corner with Jiwoo, who was talking about something else entirely, which was fine, which was what she needed. She went to her afternoon classes. She walked home via a slightly different route than usual, not through calculation exactly, more through the specific instinct that had been developing since October: an awareness of the geography, a sense of where things were and weren't, the same instinct that had driven the science wing detours but recalibrated for a different purpose.

The text came at six-forty-seven.

Haechannie🐭: you okay today

yes, she typed. I noticed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: it's going to be visible for a little while

Haechannie🐭: as things settle

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: if anything feels wrong

Haechannie🐭: if something is off

Haechannie🐭: you tell me

I will, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i'm sorry it's visible

don't apologize for your life, she typed. The same thing she had said in February, which felt like a different year already. I know what I chose.

Haechannie🐭: i know

Haechannie🐭: i just

A pause. The three dots appearing and disappearing once.

Haechannie🐭: i want it to cost you as little as possible

She held the phone. The March evening outside her window had the quality of a day that had been long and was ready to be over. The tree in the front yard held its green in the diminishing light.

it's not costing me what you think it is, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: what do I think it is

you think it's costing me the version of normal I'd have without it, she typed. but I've never had normal. I had the incident and Jihoon and the survival years and two and a half years of managing. This is different from that. She looked at what she had typed. Sent it before she could reduce it. This is the version where I know what I'm in and chose it anyway. That's not the same as losing something.

The longest pause yet.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i hear you

Haechannie🐭: thank you for saying it

don't thank me, she typed. go do your thing.

Haechannie🐭: already on it

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

She put the phone down. She looked at the ceiling. The barely-visible stars, at a distance that required the dark to be seen properly.

She thought about what she had typed — this is the version where I know what I'm in and chose it anyway — and understood that she had just said the truest thing she had said about the situation in five months of knowing the situation. Not to Jiwoo. Not to herself in the quiet of her room. To him. Directly.

She had been learning to do that.

She was going to keep learning.

Friday was when Jihoon moved.

Not at school — he was more careful than that now, more careful than he had been in November when Yuna was the instrument and the school hallway was the stage. What he did on Friday was smaller and more precise and more considered, which meant it was more dangerous.

Solar found out from Cheng at seven in the evening.

Not a call this time — a text, which meant the information was not urgent but was significant: jihoon talked to someone from the Kim side today. about you. i don't know what he said yet but the fact that he's talking to them is the part that matters.

She read it twice. She sat with it.

The Kim side was the name she had heard once from Donghyuck, in passing, in the context of the Maren Street expansion. The Kim side was the operation that had given Jisung the yes. The Kim side was DREAM's business, not Jihoon's — Jihoon was unaffiliated, Jihoon had no institutional connection to any gang, which was exactly what made him dangerous in the way he was dangerous: no code, no loyalty, nothing that required him to play by anyone's rules.

Except now he was talking to the Kim side.

Which meant he had found the specific intersection of his world and Donghyuck's that he had been looking for since the footage didn't work. He had found the way to make her into a problem not just for Donghyuck personally but for DREAM operationally.

She texted Cheng back: does Donghyuck know.

Cheng: telling him now

She put the phone down. She picked it back up. She sat in her room in the March evening with the information that Jihoon had found the thread that led from her to DREAM and had pulled it, and she thought about what it meant to have chosen something with full information when the full information was still becoming fuller.

The phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: cheng told me

Haechannie🐭: sol

Haechannie🐭: don't be alone tonight

I'm at home, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: I know

Haechannie🐭: I mean don't be alone with the information

She held that. He meant: call Jiwoo, call your mother, do not sit in your room and manage this by yourself in the dark. He knew the pattern. He had been watching the pattern since October and had learned its shape well enough to name it before it completed itself.

She typed: okay.

Haechannie🐭: okay?

I'll call Jiwoo, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: Sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: this is going to get more complicated before it gets less

I know

Haechannie🐭: I need you to stay close

She held the word close. Not stay safe — stay close. The difference between a warning and a request. He was not telling her to be careful in the abstract. He was asking her to stay in his range.

I'm not going anywhere, she typed. Giving him his own sentence back again, which was a thing they had developed, the exchange of certain sentences because certain sentences had earned their weight.

Haechannie🐭: I know

Haechannie🐭: go call Jiwoo

goodnight donghyuck

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She called Jiwoo. Jiwoo picked up on the second ring and said, without Solar having said anything yet: "Tell me."

She told her.

Jiwoo was quiet for a moment after. Then: "He talked to the Kim side."

"Yes," Solar said.

"About you."

"About me and Donghyuck," Solar said. "About how I'm connected to DREAM. He's making me into a liability."

Another silence. "That's a different kind of weapon than the footage," Jiwoo said.

"Yes," Solar said. "It is."

"Because the footage was about your reputation," Jiwoo said, with the precision of someone thinking out loud. "And this is about his."

"About DREAM's," Solar said. "Which is the same thing operationally."

Jiwoo was quiet again — the thinking kind, not the worried kind, which was the specific quality that made her useful in moments like this. She thought before she spoke and the thinking was usually right.

"He escalated," Jiwoo said finally.

"Yes," Solar said.

"Because the footage didn't work and the direct conversation didn't work and he needed a new angle," Jiwoo said. "And the new angle is making you a problem for something that's bigger than just you and him."

"Yes," Solar said.

"Sol," Jiwoo said.

"Yeah."

"You're not going to stop seeing him," Jiwoo said. Not a question. Not a suggestion. Just the statement of a fact she had already assessed.

"No," Solar said.

"Okay," Jiwoo said. "Then we figure out what comes next." A pause. "Together. You and me and Cheng and—" she paused briefly, the pause of someone acknowledging a new category, "—him."

Solar held the phone. Outside the window the March dark had arrived fully, the street doing its evening thing under the amber streetlights. The tree held its green in the dark the way trees did — not performing it, just holding it, the green present whether you could see it or not.

"Yeah," Solar said. "Together."

They talked for an hour. Not solutions — there were no solutions yet, the situation too new to have solutions. Just the specific usefulness of saying things out loud to someone who received them without requiring them to be smaller than they were. Jiwoo was good at that. She had been good at it since the first lunch.

When Solar hung up she sat in the room for a moment. The barely-visible stars. The refrigerator hum. The house holding her in its ordinary way.

She thought about Jihoon talking to the Kim side. She thought about this is going to get more complicated before it gets less. She thought about the couch and the hand and Sunday morning and the stars and since August and everything that had accumulated into the specific weight of choosing something with full information.

She thought: more complicated before it gets less.

She thought: okay.

Not the concession version. Not the door-opening version. The third version — the one that was simply acknowledgment, the honest receipt of a fact, the specific courage of looking at a thing without requiring it to be different.

Okay.

The March dark held its particular quality outside the window.

She was still here.

She was still choosing.

The morning was coming.


20: an irregular tuesday

It started on a Tuesday.

Not dramatically — nothing that would become significant ever announced itself in advance. It started the way most things started in Havenbrook: with a text, at an unremarkable hour, from a reliable source.

Cheng: you busy tonight

She was at her desk doing homework, the lamp on, the March evening outside her window doing its longer-day thing — the light lasting later, the sky holding a pale blue past seven that it hadn't managed in winter. She had her headset on but was not playing, the queue open on her second monitor while she worked through a history reading that was not capturing her attention the way the book she actually wanted to read was.

not really, she typed. why

Cheng: haechan wants to know if you want to come by

Cheng: he said to tell you it's nothing operational

Cheng: just the guys

She looked at the text. Tuesday evenings at the apartment were not the usual configuration — Saturdays were the established pattern, the implicit arrangement. A Tuesday invitation was different. She held the phone and thought about the last two weeks: the expansion visible on Thursday, Jihoon and the Kim side on Friday, the stay close text. The specific quality of a world that had been rearranging itself around her whether she was paying attention or not.

tell him I'll come at eight, she typed.

The walk to the Cha's building on a Tuesday evening in March was different from the Saturday version.

Not the route — the route was the same. But the city had a different quality at eight on a Tuesday night: less of the weekend's social energy, more of the working-week's residual fatigue, the streets running at a lower volume, the specific amber of the streetlights more present without the competition of the day's light. The east side at this hour had the quality she had been building her awareness of since October — the particular way it organized itself in the dark, the geography she was learning to read the same way she had learned to read the school's hallways.

She had walked this route alone plenty of times. She had never walked it this late.

She noticed this without alarm. She noticed it the way she noticed most things — filed it, kept it, continued walking.

The Cha's building was the Cha's building in the dark, the front door with its porch light, the stairwell with its old wood smell and the second step that creaked. She came up the stairs and knocked — actually knocked, because Tuesday at eight felt different from Saturday at one — and the door opened and it was Mark.

He looked at her with the inventory expression he used for new configurations. "Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

He stepped back. She came in.

The apartment at eight on a Tuesday was different from the Saturday apartment. Not the room — the room was the same room, the same furniture and lamp and radiator. But the quality of occupation was different: less ambient, more purposeful, the specific feeling of people who were between things rather than settled into them. Renjun was on the couch with his laptop, which was its usual state, but the laptop had something on it she didn't recognize — not his usual project, something with more numbers. Jeno was in the kitchen, visible through the doorway, doing something with his phone that required his full attention. Chenle was absent. Jisung was absent.

Donghyuck was by the window.

He looked at her when she came in and something in his posture changed — not dramatically, just the specific fraction of tension she had learned to identify, releasing. "Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

She crossed the room and stood beside him at the window. Outside, Maren Street was doing its Tuesday evening thing — the dry cleaner closed, the lights on in the apartments above the storefronts, a few people on the sidewalk moving with the purposefulness of people going somewhere specific.

"How's the street," she said.

He looked at her sideways. "You notice things," he said.

"I've been noticing the street since Thursday," she said.

He looked back out the window. "It's different at night," he said. "The expansion is more visible after dark. During the day it's just geography. At night it's—" he stopped, finding the word. "Operation."

She looked at the street. She tried to see what he was seeing — the specific way the east side organized itself at night, the particular meaning of which lights were on and which weren't, which figures on the sidewalk were moving with purpose and which were positioned. She could read some of it. Not all of it.

"Teach me," she said.

He looked at her.

"Not everything," she said. "Just — what you see when you look at it."

He was quiet for a moment. She could feel him deciding — the same decision she had watched him make before, the specific calculation of someone who kept two separate registers and was determining what crossed from one to the other. He had drawn a line between the worlds and placed it where she could see it. This was her asking to see a little further past the line.

He looked back at the street.

"The dry cleaner," he said. "Closed at six. But the light in the back is still on."

She looked. It was.

"That means someone's using the space," he said. "Not the cleaner. Someone who has an arrangement with the cleaner."

"DREAM?" she said.

"Not DREAM," he said. "Not directly. Someone adjacent. Someone the Kim side uses." He paused. "That light being on means the Maren expansion is already operational. Not just agreed — running."

She held that. The light in the back of the dry cleaner, which she had walked past for years, which she had never thought about, which was now information. The street looked the same. She was reading it differently.

"What else," she said.

He looked at the sidewalk. "The man at the corner of Maren and Fifth. Grey jacket, been standing there for twelve minutes."

She found him. A figure at the corner, hands in pockets, apparently still.

"He's not waiting for anyone," Donghyuck said. "His body language is outward, not convergent. He's watching the block."

"For what," she said.

"For anything that doesn't belong," he said. "The Kim side runs point men when they're activating new territory. He's one of them."

She looked at the point man on the corner. Then she looked at Donghyuck, who was reading all of this from a second-floor apartment window with the ease of someone who had been reading it so long it no longer required effort.

"You do this every night," she said.

"When we're operational," he said. "Yes."

She held that. The lamp behind them made its warm circle. The window was slightly open — the first Tuesday it had been warm enough to keep it open at night — and the March air came through with the specific quality of a city at eight in the evening, ambient and cooled from the day.

"Is it ever—" she started.

"What," he said.

"Tiring," she said. "Watching everything all the time."

He was quiet for a long moment. "Yes," he said. "And no." He looked at the street. "It used to feel like weight. Now it mostly feels like sight. You stop noticing it the same way you stop noticing your heartbeat. It's just — running."

She thought about that. She thought about her own version of it — the hallway calculations, the geometry of avoidance, the fast inventory of every room she walked into. Two different kinds of vigilance. Two different reasons for it. The same underlying architecture.

"I know what you mean," she said.

He looked at her.

"The hallway detours," she said. "Knowing where Jihoon was before I entered a room. Checking exits." She looked at the street. "It stops feeling like work eventually. It just becomes how you see."

He was quiet for a moment. Then: "Yeah," he said. "Exactly."

They stood at the window together for a while. The street did its Tuesday evening thing. The point man at the corner changed position once — moving south on Fifth, then returning — which Donghyuck noted without comment. The light in the back of the dry cleaner stayed on.

Mark came to the window at some point, stood beside them briefly, looked at the street with the same reading quality, and went back to the table without saying anything. This was, Solar understood, a form of check-in — his way of registering that she was there and the situation was what it was and both of those things were acceptable.

At nine-fifteen Donghyuck's phone buzzed.

He looked at it. His expression did the specific thing it did when information arrived that required processing — not the honest face, not the public face, something in between, something operational. He typed a response. Put the phone in his jacket pocket.

He looked at her.

"Something came up," he said.

"Okay," she said.

"I need to go deal with it," he said. "It won't take long. An hour, maybe less." He held her gaze. "You can stay here. Mark's here."

She looked at him. She thought about the point man on the corner. The light in the dry cleaner. Jisung's operation running. What something came up meant at nine-fifteen on a Tuesday in the middle of an expansion.

"Is it related to the Maren thing," she said.

He didn't answer immediately, which was itself an answer.

"Yes," he said.

"Is it dangerous," she said.

He held her gaze for a moment. "It's manageable," he said. Which was not the same as no.

She held that. She thought about Cheng: it's what they do. She thought about that's the part I can't give you a cleaner version of. She thought about the piece of paper in the jacket pocket, the argument he had made and lost, the expansion running anyway. She thought about choosing this with full information and the full information becoming fuller by degrees, each degree arriving without announcement.

This was another degree.

"Go," she said.

He looked at her. "Sol—"

"I'll be here when you get back," she said. "Go."

Something moved through his expression — not relief exactly, something more complicated than relief, the specific quality of someone who had been bracing for an argument and had not gotten one and was sitting with the significance of that. He reached out briefly — his hand on her arm, three seconds, the same duration as the hallway in February — and then he went.

She heard him say something low to Mark on the way out. Mark responded. The front door closed.

The apartment held its Tuesday quiet.

She sat on the couch. Renjun was still at his laptop, the numbers on the screen reflecting in his glasses. Jeno had come out of the kitchen and was on the armchair with his own phone, scrolling through something with the distracted quality of someone waiting for a notification.

Nobody said anything for a few minutes.

Then Jeno looked up from his phone. "You doing okay?" he asked, in the easy register he used for most things.

"Yes," she said.

"He'll be back," Jeno said. "He's good at this stuff."

"I know," she said.

Jeno nodded and went back to his phone. Renjun had not looked up, which was Renjun's way of being present without making it a thing. Mark was at the table with a coffee and his own specific brand of occupied silence.

She was in the apartment without Donghyuck for the first time.

She held that. She thought about what that meant — that she was comfortable enough in this room with these people to be in it without him present, that the apartment had become hers enough to hold her outside of the specific context that had made it hers. That was something. She was not entirely sure what to do with it, so she picked up her book, which she had brought in her bag with the specific optimism of someone who knew there would be time between things.

She read. The apartment held its quiet. The March night did its thing outside the window, the point man on the corner presumably continuing his survey, the light in the dry cleaner staying on.

At ten forty-three the door opened.

Donghyuck came in.

She looked up from her book.

He had his jacket on still — he had been wearing it when he left, and he was wearing it now, but it had the quality of something that had been worn through something rather than simply worn. Not visibly different to someone who hadn't been paying attention for six months. But she had been paying attention. She could see the specific set of his shoulders, the way his jaw was positioned, the quality of his eyes when they found her across the room — present, yes, and something else, something that was the east side at night wearing off slowly, the operational register fading back into the version she knew.

He looked at her. She looked at him.

Mark, at the table, looked up from his coffee. Said nothing. Looked back down.

Donghyuck crossed the room. He didn't sit immediately — he stood for a moment, hands coming out of his pockets, the specific decompression of someone transitioning between states. Then he dropped onto the couch beside her.

Close. Closer than usual. His shoulder against hers rather than adjacent to it.

She did not move away.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said. His voice had the quality it had when he was coming back from something — lower, less ambient, more direct. The performing ease had not yet returned. She was looking at whatever was underneath it, the version that existed in the transition between Haechan and Donghyuck, the seam where the two met.

"You okay?" she said.

"Yeah," he said. "It got complicated. It worked out."

"Manageable," she said.

"Manageable," he confirmed.

She looked at the window. The street outside was the street. She thought about what it got complicated meant — the specific content of those three words at ten forty-three on a Tuesday night, what they contained that he was not going to give her the specifics of and she was not going to ask for. She thought about the light in the dry cleaner and the point man and the expansion running and the Kim side and all of it existing simultaneously with the couch and the shoulder against hers and the lamp doing its warm circle in the corner.

The warmth and the dangerous back to back.

Not back to back. The same moment. The same couch. The same shoulder.

She turned a page of her book, even though she had not been reading it. He looked at the window. Renjun's laptop made its quiet sound. The apartment breathed around them.

After a while he said, without looking at her: "You stayed."

"I said I would," she said.

"I know," he said. "I just—" he stopped. The pause of someone who has been bracing for something that didn't materialize and is still calibrating to the absence of it. "It means something. That you were here when I got back."

She looked at the window. The March night outside, the amber streetlights, the point man possibly still on his corner or possibly moved on — she didn't know, she couldn't see from this angle.

"I know what this is," she said. "I've known for a while. Staying is part of knowing."

He was quiet.

"I'm not going to pretend," she said, "that every time you go out there it doesn't — " she found the word. "Register. It registers. I notice when you leave and I notice when you come back and I notice the difference between the two." She looked at him. "But I'm not going to make you manage that. That's mine to carry."

He turned to look at her. The honest face in the lamplight — the jaw not set, the eyes not doing extra work. The red hair slightly disordered from whatever the night had been. The chain at his collar catching the lamp's warmth. The jacket still on, because the jacket was always on.

"You don't have to carry it alone," he said.

"I know," she said. "But you have enough to carry."

He held her gaze for a long moment. Then he reached over — not dramatically, just the natural continuation of sitting close — and took her hand. Not the crisis version, not the three-seconds version. Just her hand in his, on the couch, at ten forty-three on a Tuesday in March, with the apartment around them and the east side outside the window and everything that was true simultaneously true.

"Sol," he said.

"Yeah," she said.

"I'm glad you're here," he said. "I don't mean tonight specifically. I mean—" he paused. "Here. In this. Knowing what it is and being here anyway."

She thought about October. About the first text and the not-making-anything-of-it and the fourteen months of survival before that. She thought about the file that had gotten too full. About the barely-visible stars and the door she had decided to walk through. About Jiwoo: two hands is scarier. About it's not costing me what you think it is.

"I know what you mean," she said.

His hand in hers. The lamp. The window.

"I'm glad too," she said.

Cheng walked her home at eleven-thirty.

He had appeared at the apartment at eleven — materializing the way Cheng materialized, without announcement, with the specific quality of someone who had been nearby and had timed his arrival correctly. Solar understood, without it being said, that he had probably always been nearby. That was Cheng's function in her life and he had been performing it since long before she had fully understood what it was.

They walked through the March night, which was mild and dark and smelled of the east side in the specific way she had been learning to read — the mineral thing, the takeout grease, the concrete that had absorbed decades of the neighborhood's particular life. She had grown up near this smell. She had grown up two blocks from it. She was inside it now rather than adjacent to it.

"He went out tonight," she said.

"I know," Cheng said.

"Something operational," she said. "The Maren thing."

"Yes," Cheng said.

She walked for half a block. "Was it dangerous," she said. The same question she had asked Donghyuck and gotten manageable from. She was asking Cheng because Cheng had a different relationship with the truth of it.

Cheng was quiet for a moment — the calibrated silence, deciding the dose. "There's a version of operational that's just logistics," he said carefully. "Moving things, confirming things, holding territory. That version has risk but it's controlled." He paused. "Tonight was closer to the other version."

She held that. "The other version," she said.

"The version where something unexpected happens and you have to respond to it," Cheng said. "The Kim side is new. New arrangements are unstable. When arrangements are unstable, things come up."

"He said it got complicated," she said.

"Yeah," Cheng said. "It did."

She looked at the sidewalk. The streetlights doing their amber work on the wet pavement — it had rained briefly while she was in the apartment, the first rain in a week, and the March pavement held it in shallow mirrors. She stepped around one.

"Cheng," she said.

"Yeah."

"Is it going to keep getting more complicated," she said.

He considered this with the specific quality he brought to questions that deserved real answers. "The expansion is new," he said. "New operations are unstable. Unstable operations generate—" he paused. "Events. The events usually settle. Usually takes a few months."

"A few months," she said.

"Yeah," he said.

She absorbed that. A few months. The school year would finish. Summer would arrive. Solar would be seventeen in the summer. The submission was still in its silence. Everything was still becoming what it was.

"Is he good at it," she said. "What he does."

Cheng looked at her sideways with the expression that was not quite surprise and was not quite not surprise. "Yes," he said. "He's very good at it."

"I know he is," she said. "I wanted to hear someone else say it."

Cheng was quiet for a moment. "He's the best I've seen at what he does," he said. "And I've seen a lot." He said it the way he said true things — flatly, without drama, the fact delivered as a fact. "The guys follow him for a reason. Not because of DREAM's hierarchy. Because of him specifically."

She held that. She thought about the apartment — the specific way the room went quiet when Donghyuck was thinking. The way Mark communicated with him in the shorthand of people who had been through enough together to have a private language. The way Chenle and Renjun and Jeno moved around him with the ease of people who had long since decided to trust his judgment. The piece of paper for Jisung, the argument made and lost, the fact that he made the argument at all.

"I know," she said.

They reached her door. The porch light was on. The tree in the front yard had its new green in the darkness, the pale committed green that had arrived with March and was keeping its promise.

"Sol," Cheng said.

She looked at him.

"Tonight was the first time you saw it with the jacket off," he said. Not the jacket literally — the ease, the performing ease of Haechan in the daytime. The version that came back wearing the night. "There'll be more nights like it."

"I know," she said.

"You good with that," he said.

She thought about the hand in hers on the couch at ten forty-three. About I'm glad you're here. About staying is part of knowing.

"Yes," she said.

Cheng nodded once — the nod of someone filing a verdict and accepting it. "Okay," he said.

"Goodnight, Cheng," she said.

"Goodnight, Sol," he said.

She let herself in. She stood in the hallway in the dark — her jacket still on, which she noticed this time, which meant she had learned something from the hallway-floor conversation in October. She took it off. She hung it on the hook.

She went upstairs.

She lay in her room in the March dark with the barely-visible stars on the ceiling and thought about the east side at night and the window and the dry cleaner light and the point man on the corner and the jacket that had been worn through something and the hand in hers afterward and I'm glad you're here.

She thought about what Cheng had said: there'll be more nights like it.

She thought: I know.

She thought: good.

Not bravado. Not the performance of being fine with something she wasn't fine with. Just the genuine receipt of a fact she had been preparing herself for since October, which had arrived on a Tuesday in March and had been what it was, and she had been there, and she was still here.

She opened her phone.

Haechannie🐭: home?

home, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: thank you for tonight

don't thank me, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: i know you always say that

Haechannie🐭: i'm saying it anyway

She held the phone. The barely-visible stars. The refrigerator hum. The March dark.

okay, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone down. She looked at the ceiling.

She thought about the east side at night — the specific quality of it, the amber streetlights and the wet pavement and the point man on the corner and the back room door with its light off now and the apartment breathing around people she had come to know well enough to be in the room without the one she'd come for.

She had been adjacent to the east side her whole life.

She was inside it now.

The ceiling held its barely-visible light.

She fell asleep inside it.


21: divergence point

The week after the Tuesday felt different.

Not alarming — not the compressed-air quality of a week carrying something operational. Just different in the specific way of weeks that follow significant events: the world running its ordinary program while you run a slightly updated version of yourself inside it. She went to school. She did her classes. She ate her lunch in the corner with Jiwoo. She walked the route home. She did the homework. She played ranked in the evenings and climbed and the streak went up and came down and went up again.

What was different was that she was doing all of it with the east side at night inside her.

Not as a weight — it was not weight exactly. More like a recalibration. The way her vision of the street had changed when Donghyuck taught her to read it from the window: the same street, a different layer of information accessible in it. She walked to school and noticed things she had been walking past for years — the dry cleaner, the light that was off in the morning and on at night, the specific corner where the point man had stood. She moved through her day with the usual composure and inside the composure she was running a parallel inventory that she had not been running before Tuesday.

She thought about what Donghyuck had said: you stop noticing it the same way you stop noticing your heartbeat. It's just running.

She understood that now in a way she had understood it theoretically before. The vigilance was not new for her — she had been reading rooms and calculating exits since she was fifteen. What was new was the specific geography she was reading. The east side's grammar. The language of a neighborhood that ran two different programs depending on the hour.

She was learning the second program.

Thursday morning she arrived at school at the same time as Donghyuck.

Not planned — the overlap of two people who had routes that converged at a certain point, which had been happening since January but which had a different quality now. He came around the corner of Fifth and Maren as she was turning off her street onto the main approach, and they fell into step without discussing it, their shoulders adjacent, the March morning doing its committed spring thing around them.

He had his jacket on. He had the chain at his collar. He looked like himself in the morning light — the red hair not yet fully awake, the easy quality of someone who had not yet put on the full performing register of the school day. This was the in-between version, the one that existed in the first twenty minutes before DMHS and its social physics took over.

She had a particular fondness for this version.

"How'd you sleep," he said.

"Fine," she said. "You?"

"Late," he said. "Something ran long."

She understood what something ran long meant in his register. She filed it with the other information.

"The Maren thing?" she said.

He glanced at her sideways. Not surprised — he had stopped being surprised that she read these things correctly. "Adjacent to it," he said. "Loose end from Tuesday."

"The Kim side?"

"Not directly," he said. "The Kim side has layers. It's the layers that are complicated."

She absorbed that. The school was visible at the end of the street, its lights on, the early arrivals making their way up the front path. The social physics had not yet engaged — there was a bubble of ordinary around them, the specific ordinary of a morning before the day had its audiences.

"Are the layers going to keep being complicated," she said.

"For a while," he said. "New operations are unstable. Cheng tell you that?"

"He said a few months," she said.

"Yeah," he said. "Probably."

She looked at the school. She thought about the school year — sophomore year ending, summer coming, junior year ahead. A few months of instability mapped against that timeline put the settling somewhere in the fall, maybe later. She held the math without alarm.

"Okay," she said.

He looked at her. "You keep saying okay," he said.

"Because I keep meaning it," she said.

The corner of his mouth moved — the real version, the one that arrived early in the morning before the managed version was fully operational. She looked at it and felt the specific warmth of a thing seen in its unguarded state.

They reached the school's front path. The bubble of ordinary dissolved on contact with DMHS — students flowing through the entrance, the specific morning density of the place, two sophomore girls visible near the front door who clocked Donghyuck's arrival with the specific attention he attracted and tracked to his adjacency to Solar and updated their picture accordingly.

He didn't look at them.

She did, briefly — not to acknowledge, just to register. She registered them and let them pass through her inventory without landing and looked at the entrance instead.

"Later," he said.

"Later," she said.

He went through the main entrance. She went through the side door. They diverged into the school's separate streams the way they always did, which was practiced and easy and which she had stopped thinking of as a strategy. It was just how they moved.

Chemistry was the third period she spent least productively on a reliable basis.

It wasn't that she disliked chemistry — she was neutral about it, which was somehow worse than disliking it, because neutrality provided no friction to push against. She sat in the second row next to a girl named Park Soyeon who was excellent at chemistry and had the quality of excellence that made you feel welcome in its presence rather than diminished by it, which Solar appreciated. Soyeon helped her through labs with the unhurried patience of someone for whom the material was obvious and who remembered that this was not universal.

On Thursday, during a lab involving a color-change reaction she kept losing track of, Solar became aware of something.

She was sitting at a lab bench with a burner and a beaker and Soyeon's efficient hands managing most of the procedural elements, and she was thinking about the dry cleaner light and the point man's position and the Kim side's layers, and she understood with sudden clarity that this was the shape of her life now: the beaker and the burner and also the east side at night, both of them running simultaneously in the same brain, the same person.

She had thought, when she was in the first months of the incident's aftermath, that the specific exhaustion of managing two registers — the performed composure and the actual interior — was the thing she would eventually outgrow. She had been waiting for the day when the two versions aligned, when she could stop performing one and simply be the other.

What she understood now, watching the color-change reaction she had lost track of finally complete itself in orange, was that the two registers were never going to align into one. There was always going to be the version of her that existed in the world's sight — the school, the hallways, the social physics — and the version underneath it that was doing its own work. Jihoon had created the split, but the split was not going away when Jihoon did. It was just going to be hers rather than his.

What had changed was that the version underneath was no longer just survival. It was this — the east side's grammar, the Maren Street expansion, Donghyuck reading the window. The thing she had chosen rather than the thing that had been done to her.

That was different.

"Sol," Soyeon said.

She looked up. "Sorry."

"The titration," Soyeon said, with the patience of someone who had been waiting a few seconds.

"Right," Solar said, and picked up the burette.

At lunch Jiwoo was already in the corner when Solar arrived, with the expression she wore when she had been thinking about something and had organized her thoughts into a form ready for delivery.

"Tell me about Tuesday," she said, before Solar had finished sitting down.

Solar looked at her. "Cheng told you."

"Cheng told me you went to the apartment and Haechan went out on an operation and you waited and he came back," Jiwoo said. "He didn't tell me what that was like. I'm asking you."

Solar set her bag down. She looked at the cafeteria — the usual Thursday volume, a group of juniors at the far end engaged in what appeared to be an ongoing debate that required standing up periodically. The DREAM table visible across the room, Mark eating with his usual efficiency, Jeno saying something to Renjun that Renjun responded to without looking up.

"It was—" she started, then found the right word. "Clarifying."

"Clarifying," Jiwoo repeated. "That's a solar-word."

"It was real," Solar said. "In a way it hadn't been before. Not scarier exactly. Just more specific." She looked at the table. "He came back and he was—" she paused. "Wearing it. The night. And then it came off slowly and he sat beside me and he was just him again. Both of those things were true in the same hour."

Jiwoo held that. She ate a bite of her lunch. "How did you feel," she said. "During the part where you were waiting."

"Present," Solar said. Which surprised her even as she said it. "I kept expecting to feel afraid but I mostly just felt — in it. Like it was mine to be in."

Jiwoo nodded slowly, the way she nodded when she was taking something seriously. "And after?"

"After was fine," Solar said. "Cheng walked me home. Donghyuck texted when I got there."

"Did you tell your mom?" Jiwoo said.

"No," Solar said.

"Are you going to?"

Solar considered. "Not the specific version," she said. "She knows Donghyuck is DREAM. She doesn't ask questions she doesn't want the answers to." She held her coffee. "I think she trusts me to know what I'm doing."

"Do you?" Jiwoo said. Not challenging — genuinely asking.

Solar looked at the table. She thought about the color-change reaction in chemistry. The two registers running simultaneously. The thing she had chosen rather than the thing that had been done to her.

"Yes," she said. "More than I have about most things."

Jiwoo looked at her for a moment — the open expression, the one from the first lunch. "Okay," she said. "Good."

They ate their lunch. The cafeteria did its Thursday thing. At some point during the second half of the period Solar became aware of Donghyuck coming through the cafeteria's main entrance, which she was aware of in the specific way she was always aware of his presence — not loudly, not turning to track it, just registered, the room's center of gravity noting the addition.

He stopped at the DREAM table briefly — said something to Mark, who responded, some logistical exchange — and then came toward the corner.

Solar looked at her tray.

Jiwoo looked at her tray too, with enormous diplomatic focus.

Donghyuck stopped at the end of the corner table. "Hey," he said. To both of them, but not equally.

"Hey," Solar said.

Jiwoo said nothing, which was itself a communication, and then said "hi" in a register of someone who had decided to be unobtrusive, which was a register Jiwoo almost never used and which was therefore conspicuous.

Donghyuck looked at Jiwoo with the expression of someone who had encountered a known variable. "Jiwoo," he said.

"Haechan," she said. Then, with the precision of someone delivering something she had been waiting to deliver: "I've heard a lot about you."

"Good things?" he said.

"Specific things," she said.

Something in his expression moved — the not-quite-smile, registering the specific quality of specific. He looked at Solar. Solar looked at her tray.

"Can I sit for a minute," he said.

"It's a cafeteria," Solar said.

He sat. The corner table with three people had a different quality than its usual two — a small reconfiguration, the social physics of DMHS noting the new arrangement and updating its assessment, which Solar registered in the peripheral awareness of someone who had been living inside DMHS's social physics for two and a half years.

She had stopped caring about the assessment some time ago. She noted it anyway.

"Your name is in Renjun's graph," Donghyuck told Jiwoo.

Jiwoo looked at him. "I want to see this graph."

"Sol has it," he said.

"Sol hasn't sent it to me," Jiwoo said, with the accusatory quality of someone who had been told something and then not received it.

"I forgot," Solar said.

"You didn't forget," Jiwoo said.

"I was managing my own reaction to seeing the graph," Solar said. "There was a process involved."

"What process," Jiwoo said.

"Acceptance," Solar said.

Donghyuck looked between them with the expression of someone watching something they find deeply satisfying. He said nothing. He had the quality, in this specific configuration, of a person who was exactly where he wanted to be — not the performing ease of Haechan in the school hallways, not the operational register of the east side at night, but the third version, the one that existed in the specific intersection of his life that was hers. The unguarded morning version but midday.

She watched him have it and understood something she had been circling since Tuesday: that the two registers were not a burden he carried separately. He carried them simultaneously, the way she was learning to carry her own. The warmth and the danger were not two different people. They were the same person, always, in every room — it was just that different rooms made different things visible.

She was the room that made the third version visible.

She thought about that for a moment.

"Send me the graph," Jiwoo said.

"After school," Solar said.

"Now," Jiwoo said.

Solar sent her the graph.

Jiwoo looked at it. She looked at it for a long time, with the expression of someone reviewing a document. "The axes are labeled," she said.

"I told you," Donghyuck said.

"Time and proximity," Jiwoo said. "He used the word proximity."

"It's technically accurate," Solar said.

"It's extremely accurate," Jiwoo said, still looking at the graph. "This is documentation. This is evidence. Renjun should work for the government."

"He would be very good at it," Donghyuck said.

"Terrifying, actually," Jiwoo said. "But effective."

They sat in the cafeteria corner for the rest of the lunch period, the three of them, which was a configuration that had not existed before and which settled into itself with the ease of something that had been waiting to exist. Jiwoo asked Donghyuck three questions that were carefully calibrated to tell her things she had already assessed and confirm them. He answered with the specific quality of someone who understood he was being evaluated and was comfortable with the evaluation. Solar ate her lunch and said nothing in particular and felt the specific warmth of two people she trusted existing in the same room together, which was an arrangement she had not had since the incident and had not fully let herself register wanting until now.

The bell rang. The cafeteria dispersed.

Jiwoo stood and looked at Donghyuck with the expression she used when she had arrived at a verdict. "You're okay," she said. Simply.

"Thanks," he said.

"Don't thank me," she said. "That's a high bar from me." She picked up her tray. She looked at Solar once — the open look, the one from the first lunch, confirming something. Then she was gone.

Donghyuck looked at Solar.

"High bar," Solar said.

"I gathered," he said.

"She meant it," Solar said.

"I know," he said. He picked up his jacket from the back of the chair. "She loves you."

"Yeah," Solar said. "She does."

He stood. The cafeteria was mostly empty now, the lunch period over, the space resuming its between-periods quality. He looked at her in the empty corner with the unguarded expression — the honest face, the midday version of the morning version.

"See you after school," he said.

"Divergence point," she said.

"Divergence point," he confirmed.

He went back to the DREAM table, picked up something Mark handed him, and was back in the school's social physics — Haechan, the reputation, the gravity. She watched it happen in the thirty seconds it took him to cross the cafeteria floor. The performing ease coming up like a tide, not replacing anything, just covering it.

She watched him cover it and knew what was underneath.

That was hers.

That same Friday, the east side ran late.

Donghyuck knew it was going to run late before it started — the specific quality of the briefing Mark gave at seven-thirty had the texture of something that was going to find complications, the Kim side's new arrangement still settling into its shape, the Maren expansion three weeks old and not yet reliable. New operations had a break-in period. This one was still breaking.

He had his jacket on. He had his phone in his pocket and his hands free and the particular focus he put on like a second jacket when operational — not the performing ease of the school day, not the warmth of the apartment. This was the third version, the one that existed in the east side at night, that most people who knew Haechan had never seen and most people who saw it had never known anything else about him.

Mark briefed them at the Cha's building: him, Jeno, Renjun working the information side from the apartment. The Kim side had a node on the new Maren territory that was supposed to have been handed over cleanly a week ago. It had not been handed over cleanly. There was a question about who the node actually belonged to now — the old operator, who had not fully vacated, or the new arrangement, which had not fully arrived. In the gap between those two states something had been operating that shouldn't have been.

They were going to close the gap.

"How much pushback," Jeno asked.

"Unknown," Mark said. "The old operator is local. He's been on that block for four years. He knows what he's losing."

"Does he know who he's losing it to," Donghyuck said.

"He knows it's DREAM," Mark said. "He doesn't know the specifics of the Kim arrangement." He looked at Donghyuck. "Which means he might try something."

Donghyuck considered this. He thought about the old operator — four years on a block, watching his territory absorbed into someone else's expansion, the specific grievance of a man who had built something small and was being outgrown by something larger. He understood the shape of that grievance. He did not enjoy what understanding it required him to do.

"We go in clean," he said. "No escalation unless he makes it necessary."

"Agreed," Mark said.

Jeno nodded. They went.

The block in question was three streets into the new Maren territory — a narrow one, the kind that ran perpendicular to the main street and existed in the particular geography of the east side that people who didn't live there didn't know about. Donghyuck had learned these streets when he was fifteen, two years before he was formally DREAM, when he had understood that the east side was going to be his world and had decided to know it completely rather than incompletely.

He knew this street. He knew the building on the left with the bad drainage that flooded the bottom unit every spring. He knew the woman on the second floor of the building on the right who kept irregular hours and had a small dog that announced everything. He knew the specific way the streetlight at the far end was angled so that it cast a shadow over the middle third of the block, which was where you wanted to be if you needed not to be visible from either end.

He knew all of this and moved through it accordingly, Jeno half a block behind him on the parallel street, the two of them covering the geometry without discussing it because they had covered this kind of geometry together enough times that discussing it was redundant.

The old operator was exactly where Renjun had said he would be — at the entrance to the building that housed the node, a man in his early thirties with the specific exhausted quality of someone who had been waiting for a conversation he didn't want to have and had decided to have it standing up.

He looked at Donghyuck when he arrived. Not surprised. Not aggressive. The look of someone who had known this was coming and had chosen to meet it rather than avoid it.

"Haechan," he said. He said it the way people said the name who knew what it meant — not with the school's particular reverence, not with fear exactly, just with the recognition of a name that was a category.

"Mr. Yoon," Donghyuck said.

The man looked at him. He was shorter than Donghyuck by several inches, which was not the relevant variable. The relevant variable was that he had four years on this block and had decided to stand here rather than simply vacate, which meant he had something he wanted to say.

"The arrangement was supposed to be clean," Mr. Yoon said.

"It will be," Donghyuck said.

"It hasn't been," Mr. Yoon said. "The Kim side sends people who don't know the block. They don't know the woman on the second floor keeps a dog. They don't know the drainage on the left building. They go in loud and the block notices and the block remembers."

Donghyuck held this. It was, he noted, an accurate complaint. The Kim side's people were not east side people. They had the arrangement and they did not have the knowledge, which was exactly the kind of instability that came with new operations.

"I hear you," he said.

"Then fix it," Mr. Yoon said. "I'm not asking to stay. I understand the situation. I'm asking for it to be done in a way that doesn't burn what I spent four years building."

Donghyuck looked at him for a moment. He thought about the piece of paper he had written for Jisung — the argument about the people on Maren Street, the variables the math didn't account for. He had lost that argument. He was standing in its consequences.

"I'll talk to the Kim side," he said.

Mr. Yoon looked at him with the expression of someone deciding whether to believe this.

"I don't make promises I don't keep," Donghyuck said. "You've been on the east side long enough to know my name. You know what that means."

A long moment. The small dog on the second floor made its brief announcement. The streetlight at the far end did its angled work on the middle of the block.

"Alright," Mr. Yoon said.

"Give me a week," Donghyuck said. "The Kim side sends people who know the block by then, or they send nobody until they do."

Mr. Yoon nodded once. He stepped back from the entrance. Donghyuck went in, confirmed the node's status with the efficiency of someone doing what needed doing, and came back out.

Mr. Yoon was still there.

"You're younger than I expected," Mr. Yoon said.

Donghyuck looked at him. "I know," he said.

He walked back to the parallel street. Jeno fell into step beside him.

"How'd it go," Jeno asked.

"Fine," Donghyuck said. "He had a legitimate complaint."

"They usually do," Jeno said.

"I'm going to bring it to Jisung," Donghyuck said.

Jeno glanced at him. "Another piece of paper?"

"Probably," Donghyuck said.

Jeno was quiet for half a block. "He's not going to like it."

"He doesn't have to like it," Donghyuck said. "He has to consider it."

They walked. The east side at eleven on a Friday night ran its particular program — the nodes operating, the point men doing their surveys, the specific ambient life of a neighborhood that had two different metabolisms depending on the hour. He read it as he walked without requiring effort, the way he read everything in this geography. The heartbeat of a place he had learned by necessity and had stayed in by choice.

He thought about the node and the old operator and the dog on the second floor and all the things the expansion had not accounted for. He thought about Jisung's math, which was always correct, and the variables Jisung refused to put in the math, which kept arriving anyway in the form of men like Mr. Yoon who had spent four years building something small in a neighborhood that had its own specific dignity.

He thought about Solar at the window on Tuesday, learning to read the street. The specific quality of her attention — the same attention she brought to everything, the fast complete inventory that was her particular skill. She had looked at the dry cleaner light and understood it immediately. She had looked at the point man and asked the right questions.

He had been watching her learn the east side's grammar for months. He understood, walking back through the Maren streets at eleven on a Friday, that he had been teaching her without entirely deciding to. Not the operational specifics — those he kept, those were their own register. But the language underneath. The way a city had two faces and what it meant to know both of them.

She knew both faces of the city she had grown up adjacent to. She had grown up on its border and was now inside it and had said I'm not asking for a cleaner version and staying is part of knowing and good when Cheng told her there would be more nights like Tuesday.

He thought about the cafeteria today. The specific decision of going to the corner table — not calculated, just the natural continuation of wanting to be where she was, which was a thing he had been doing since October in various configurations and which had acquired a different quality since Saturday. He had sat in the cafeteria corner with Solar and Jiwoo and had felt, for twenty minutes, the specific ease of someone who was not performing anything. The unguarded version. The third register.

He had walked back across the cafeteria afterward and the performing ease had come up the way it always did, the Haechan register engaging automatically because that was what the school required, and he had been aware, in the way he was always aware of her at a distance, that she was watching him put it on.

He had wondered what she made of it. He thought she probably made the same thing he would make of it if he watched her do it — the specific recognition of someone who knew what was underneath.

That was, he thought, the most precisely accurate thing about them: they were both people who ran two registers simultaneously, who had learned to perform one while living in the other, who had arrived at this specific arrangement from different directions but with the same architecture.

He had known since August that she was specific. He had not known, in August, how specifically she would be specific.

He had been finding out incrementally since October.

He was still finding out.

Jeno had gone ahead — his building was on the parallel street, they had diverged at the corner without commenting on it, the easy efficiency of people who had been moving through the same geography long enough to separate without discussion. Donghyuck walked the last few blocks alone, through the east side at eleven, through the streets he had learned at fifteen because he had decided to know them completely.

He got back to the Cha's building and climbed the stairs — the second step, the old wood smell, the particular exhale of the building at this hour. Inside, Renjun's laptop was closed, which meant Renjun had wrapped up and gone to bed or was close to it. Mark was at the table with a coffee and the specific quality of someone still processing the evening.

"Clean?" Mark asked.

"Clean," Donghyuck confirmed. "I'm going to bring the Kim side complaint to Jisung."

Mark looked at him. "You want backup for that conversation."

"I'll write it down first," Donghyuck said. "He does better with things on paper."

"He does better with things that confirm what he already thinks," Mark said, but without heat. It was an observation rather than a criticism. Mark rarely criticized. He observed with the precision of someone who had been conducting inventories since before DREAM was what it was now.

"Yeah," Donghyuck said. "But the written version is harder to dismiss."

He sat on the couch. The apartment was quiet in the specific way of rooms at the end of a late Friday — the things that had happened settling into the walls, the operational register slowly unwinding. He looked at the window. The street below was quieter than eleven usually was, the expansion's new activity having run through its Friday and arrived at the specific hour when even operational things required rest.

He took out his phone.

He looked at the thread at the top of the screen. The last message was from this afternoon — see you after school / divergence point / divergence point. The easy back-and-forth of a Friday that had been ordinary in the way that ordinary things were now ordinary: the cafeteria, the divergence point, the route home, the evening apart.

He typed: you still up?

He looked at it. Deleted it. It was eleven-fifteen on a Friday and she had school things and he had just gotten back from the kind of Friday she did not need to think about at eleven-fifteen.

He put the phone in his pocket.

He sat on the couch for a while in the particular quiet of the Cha's apartment after a late run, the kind of quiet that had been his for three years — the east side settling, the operational world between its frequencies, the building holding its breath. It was not a bad quiet. It was the quiet of a life he had chosen and knew completely, the heartbeat-quiet, the not-noticing-it-anymore quiet.

What was different now, in the quiet, was that it had a different texture than it had had in October. In October he had sat on this couch after late runs and the quiet had been its own closed system. Now it had a specific quality — the specific quality of someone who had somewhere to be in the morning.

Saturday at one. The couch with the right configuration. Renjun's laptop. The radiator. Chenle at the window. The particular warmth of a space that had expanded to include something it had not contained before.

He had somewhere to be in the morning.

That was new.

He sat with it in the east side quiet and did not examine it too carefully, because some things were better held than examined, which was a thing he had learned from her without her having said it.

He went to bed.

The east side ran its late Friday program outside the window.

Saturday was coming.


22: the climb

April came in warm and stayed warm, which was not what Havenbrook usually did with April.

Usually April was the month of false promises — two days of actual warmth followed by a week of the cold reasserting itself, the city and the season in an ongoing negotiation that neither side won cleanly. This year April arrived and committed. The trees that had been tentative in March were fully themselves by the second week of April, the green no longer pale but actual, the branches filling in with the specific abundance of a season that had decided to be done waiting.

Solar noticed this the way she noticed most things — on the walk to school, registering the change, filing it. The air was different. The morning light was different. Even DMHS looked different in April — the building the same building, but the students moving through its orbit with the specific restlessness of people who could smell the end of the school year and were choosing between the anxiety of finals and the relief of summer.

Six weeks. The school year had six weeks left.

She had been counting since the middle of March, which was not like her — she had never been a countdown person, had never found comfort in the arithmetic of remaining time. But something about the end of sophomore year felt significant in a way that previous year-ends hadn't, the specific weight of a year that had changed the shape of everything, and she was aware of it running out.

The walk to school on Monday morning of the first week of April put her at the convergence point at the same time as Donghyuck for the third week in a row, which was no longer coincidence but something that had organized itself through the natural gravity of two people whose mornings had found their rhythm. He came around the corner of Fifth and Maren with his jacket on and his hands in his pockets and the chain at his collar and the specific quality of not-yet-fully-awake that she had come to find specifically his.

"Morning," he said.

"Morning," she said.

They fell into step. The April air moved around them — warm, the specific warmth of a morning that had not required them to brace against it, the first in months. She had her jacket on out of habit but didn't need it, which she registered as its own small marker of time passing.

"How was the weekend," she said.

"Quiet," he said. "Mostly."

She had learned the specific vocabulary of mostly in his register — it meant something had happened that didn't require her attention but had required his. She held it and let it be.

"Streak?" he asked.

"Twenty-three," she said.

He glanced at her. "Since when."

"Thursday," she said. "I got on a run."

"What map," he said.

"Mostly Ilios and Oasis," she said. "The rotation was good for me. And I had a pocket Sigma that understood what I was doing."

"Pocket Sigma," he said, in the register of someone who played enough to know what that meant.

"He was very good," she said. "We didn't speak. We just played."

"The best kind," he said.

She looked at the sidewalk. "I hit Masters two last night," she said. Casually. The way she said things that were significant but that she had decided not to perform significance about.

He was quiet for two steps. Then: "You were Masters one in October."

"Yes," she said.

"You climbed a division in six months," he said.

"I play a lot," she said.

He looked at her sideways with the expression he used when she was being modest in a way he found inaccurate. "Sol," he said.

"Donghyuck," she said.

"That's not normal," he said.

"I know," she said. "I just—" she looked at the school ahead of them, its lights on, the early crowd filtering through the entrance. "It's the one place where things resolve cleanly. You win or you lose. The variables are known. I play better when other things are — " she found the word. "Complicated."

He was quiet for a moment. "The last six months have been complicated," he said.

"Yes," she said. "They have."

He looked at the school. "Masters two," he said.

"Masters two," she confirmed.

"By the time you leave for Seoul," he said — and it was the first time either of them had said it directly in a while, leave for Seoul, the direction held in the sentence like a weight they both knew was there — "you'll be Top 500."

She held that. The school was close now, the front path beginning, the social physics of DMHS engaging around them. She thought about by the time you leave for Seoul and the specific quality with which he said it — not resignation, not performance of being fine with it, just the honest statement of something he had already done the math on. She had always been going to leave. He had always known it. It sat between them not as a wound but as a fact, the kind of fact you learn to live inside.

"Probably," she said.

"Definitely," he said.

They reached the school's front path and the bubble of ordinary dissolved. The social physics engaged immediately — three junior girls clocking his arrival, a sophomore boy calling something across the path to Jeno who had materialized from the direction of the parking lot, the specific morning density of DMHS doing its program. Donghyuck's performing ease came up the way it always did, the transition she could see happening from close enough to know what it covered.

At the main entrance he stopped. He turned to look at her — not the performing ease, the actual look, brief and direct.

"Masters two," he said. One more time. Like he was making sure it was filed correctly.

She pressed her mouth flat. "Go to class," she said.

He went. She watched him go — the ease with which he moved through the school's social physics, the gravity of his presence, the girls adjusting their trajectories. The DMHS version, full and complete, inhabiting the role the school had built for him.

She went through the side door.

The cafeteria had updated its assessment.

This was the specific thing she noticed in April that had not been precisely true in March: the assessment had not just adjusted, it had settled. The uncertainty of the winter — is something happening, what is it, what does it mean — had resolved into a kind of established fact. Solar Lee and Haechan were something. The school did not have clean language for what they were, because the school had never seen this specific configuration before, but it had accepted the fact of it the way it accepted most established facts: by incorporating it into the social architecture and moving on.

What this meant practically was that the specific hostility had changed shape. The sophomores who had been using the incident as currency since the footage circulated in March had largely moved on to other currencies — the footage having not produced the response they expected, the silence having defused it, the specific social logic of if she's not scared of it, it's not useful having taken effect. Bora still existed. The assessments still ran. But the active hostility had become passive, which was different.

What had replaced it was something Solar did not entirely know how to read: a kind of wary curiosity. Not acceptance — DMHS was not going to rehabilitate her reputation in six weeks, and she was not asking it to. But the specific quality of a school that was watching something and had not yet decided what to do with it.

She ate her lunch with Jiwoo on Tuesday and felt the cafeteria's attention at a low hum rather than the previous spike.

"It's different," Jiwoo observed, unwrapping something with the decisive efficiency she brought to everything.

"Yes," Solar said.

"Because he sat here," Jiwoo said. "Last week. In public. In front of everyone." She looked at the cafeteria. "DMHS doesn't know what to do with that. It's still processing."

"It'll process itself into something," Solar said.

"Probably," Jiwoo agreed. "The question is what." She took a bite. "Bora's been quiet."

"I noticed."

"Yuna too," Jiwoo said. "Which is more interesting."

Solar looked at her. "Why more interesting."

"Because Bora goes quiet when she's lost the thread," Jiwoo said. "Yuna goes quiet when something is changing direction." She chewed thoughtfully. "Yuna being quiet means the direction is changing."

Solar held that. She thought about Yuna's specific function — Jihoon's channel, the instrument he used when he wanted messages to travel without his hands on them. Yuna going quiet could mean Jihoon had no current message to send. Or it could mean the message was coming through a different channel.

She filed this without saying it. Jiwoo had given her the observation. She would give Jiwoo the conclusion when she had enough to make one.

"How are things," Jiwoo asked, in the register she used for the question that meant how are things with him.

"Good," Solar said. And meant it. "Different since Tuesday."

"Tuesday in what sense."

"The apartment Tuesday," Solar said. "The waiting." She looked at her food. "Something settled after that. I can't entirely explain what. Something about seeing it and deciding to stay saw something in me that—" she stopped. Found it. "Solidified."

Jiwoo looked at her. "You're not afraid of it anymore," she said.

Solar considered. "I don't think I was ever afraid of it," she said. "I was afraid of what I would feel when I saw it. Afraid of the version of me that would have to decide." She looked at the cafeteria. "I decided. It wasn't as hard as I thought."

"Because you'd already been deciding for months," Jiwoo said.

"Yes," Solar said. "The Tuesday decision was just the one where I got to watch myself do it."

Jiwoo nodded — the nod of someone filing a verdict. "You're good," she said.

"I'm good," Solar said.

"Okay," Jiwoo said. And that was that.

Wednesday Donghyuck did a thing.

She did not find out about it immediately — she found out about it from Cheng, at the divergence point on Thursday morning, who mentioned it with the careful neutrality he used for information he wanted her to have but did not want to present as significant.

"The Kim side sent someone to school yesterday," Cheng said.

She looked at him. "To DMHS."

"Not inside," Cheng said. "The parking lot. Someone who doesn't go here, standing in the student parking lot during the lunch period."

She processed this. "That's a message," she said.

"It's a message," Cheng confirmed.

"For DREAM," she said. "Or for someone specifically."

Cheng was quiet for a moment — the dose-managing quality. "The person was positioned," he said, "with a clear sightline to the side entrance."

She understood. The side entrance was the one she used. Had been using since freshman year, specifically because it reduced her probability of running into Jihoon. It was her entrance. Whoever the Kim side had sent had been positioned to see her.

"Donghyuck found out," she said. It wasn't a question.

"He was in the main lot," Cheng said. "He saw the person. He recognized them."

"What did he do," she said.

"He walked over," Cheng said. "And had a conversation."

She held that. Walked over and had a conversation. She thought about what that meant in Donghyuck's register — the performing ease set aside, the operational version fully present, approaching someone from the Kim side in a school parking lot with the specific quality of someone who understood exactly what was being communicated and was communicating back.

"What kind of conversation," she said.

Cheng looked at the school ahead of them. "The kind," he said, "where the other person leaves."

Solar absorbed this. The morning was warm, the April air doing its committed thing, the divergence point approaching.

"He didn't tell me," she said.

"No," Cheng said.

She held that for a moment. Not hurt — she understood why he hadn't. He had handled something that was a threat to her and had handled it as Haechan, in the parking lot, with the other register fully engaged. Telling her would have required telling her, and telling her would have made it something she had to carry, and he had spent six months being very precise about what she had to carry.

"I need to know when things happen," she said. "That's what we said."

"I know," Cheng said. "That's why I'm telling you."

"He should have told me," she said.

"I know that too," Cheng said.

She walked in silence for a moment. The school was close. The morning was warm. Someone from the Kim side had stood in the student parking lot looking at her entrance, and Donghyuck had walked over and had the kind of conversation where the other person left, and he had not said a word to her about it.

She thought about I need you to tell me things. She thought about I don't want to find out after. She thought about the specific shape of his protectiveness — which was the version she trusted, which was never controlling, which always asked before it acted. And then she thought about a school parking lot and a person from the Kim side and what the calculation had looked like in his head in that moment.

He had acted. He had not asked. He had walked over and had a conversation.

She understood why. She was still going to say something.

She found him at the east entrance after school.

Not the side exit — she had gone to the east entrance specifically, which was where he was, which was the specific geography of finding someone who was usually there. He was leaning against the wall with his hands in his pockets and his jacket on and the chain and the red hair in the April afternoon light, and two girls from the junior class were in his orbit with the usual orbital quality, and he was doing the performing ease thing with the ease of someone who had been doing it for years.

He saw her coming.

The performing ease adjusted. Not went away — that was too visible, too public, DMHS watching everything. But the quality of it shifted by a degree that she was close enough to catch, the equivalent of a frequency change, the same words in a slightly different register.

The junior girls clocked the shift. They clocked Solar. They conducted their assessment. One of them said something to the other in a register too low to carry.

Solar stopped in front of him. "Hey," she said.

"Hey," he said.

She looked at him with the level expression she used for things she had decided to address directly. She had decided this on the walk from the divergence point, the arithmetic of it clear: she was going to say it in public because she was done conducting her life in the margins of things.

"The parking lot," she said. Not quietly.

He held her gaze. Something moved through his expression — the assessment, the calculation of what she knew and how she felt about it.

"Cheng," he said.

"Cheng," she confirmed.

He was quiet for a moment. The junior girls had gone very still with the specific stillness of people who had become involuntary witnesses to something and were unsure of the protocol.

"I handled it," he said.

"I know you handled it," she said. "That's not the question."

He looked at her. She looked at him. The east entrance and the April afternoon and the specific weight of something being said in a place where DMHS could see it.

"You should have told me," she said.

A long pause. "Yeah," he said. "I know."

"That's what we said," she said. "Tell me when things happen."

"I know," he said again. "I didn't want—" he stopped. Started again. "It was contained. I didn't want to make it into something you had to think about."

"I understand why you did that," she said. "And you still should have told me."

He held her gaze. The honest face, present even through the school's performance overlay. "Okay," he said.

"Okay?" she said.

"Okay," he said. "I'll tell you. Next time." He held her gaze. "All the times."

She absorbed that. The junior girls had found somewhere else to be, which she registered peripherally. The east entrance crowd was thinning, the school day dispersing into the April afternoon.

"Good," she said.

He looked at her for a moment — the specific look she had been cataloguing since October, the one that was receiving and present and the same. "You came to the east entrance," he said.

"Yes," she said.

"You usually take the side exit," he said.

"I know where you are," she said. "I came to where you are."

Something in his expression moved — past the performing ease, past the management, arriving somewhere that was just him. "Okay," he said. The word in the specific register of someone who has just been given something they weren't expecting.

"Divergence point," she said.

"Divergence point," he said.

They walked.

That Saturday the apartment had a quality she hadn't felt since February's compressed weeks — not the full operational weight, but something with edges. Mark was at the table with Jeno, both of them looking at something on a phone that required their mutual attention. Jisung had been in and out twice since she arrived, which was unusual — Jisung either stayed or didn't. The in-and-out quality meant he was managing something with a short timeline.

Renjun was on the couch. His laptop was open but he wasn't typing — he was reading, which was different from his usual mode of active documentation.

Donghyuck was beside her. The shoulder-adjacent position, her book open in her lap that she was not reading, the April afternoon coming through the window.

"The Kim side sent someone to the school," she said, quietly enough for the room's ambient noise to absorb it.

"I know," he said. "I told Cheng to tell you."
