She looked at him. "You told Cheng to tell me."

"After the fact," he said. "I know. Not the same as telling you myself." He looked at the window. "I've been thinking about that."

"And?" she said.

"And the calculation was wrong," he said. Flatly. Not defensive. "I made it in real time and it was the wrong call."

She held that. She thought about six months of him being very precise about what he did and didn't do — the cigarette in the pocket, the she stays, the I should have asked you first. He made mistakes occasionally. He acknowledged them the way he acknowledged everything: directly, without requiring her to accept or reject the acknowledgment.

"Why did they send someone," she said. "What were they communicating."

He was quiet for a moment. She knew the pause — not the Cheng dose-managing pause, but the Donghyuck considering-how-much-to-give pause, which was a different animal. This one resolved faster than usual.

"Jihoon gave them something," he said. "About you specifically. About your proximity to DREAM's operation." He looked at the window. "The Kim side is using it."

"Using it how," she said.

"As leverage," he said. "They want something from DREAM — something related to the Maren arrangement that Jisung agreed to and that I don't fully agree with. They've found a pressure point."

"I'm the pressure point," she said.

"You're the pressure point," he confirmed. He said it without apology, which she appreciated — not because she wanted him to not care, but because she had told him not to apologize for his life and he had taken that seriously.

"What do they want," she said.

"Specific territory," he said. "Three blocks further into the east side than the current Maren arrangement. Jisung's arrangement covers Maren and two blocks adjacent. They want a third block." He paused. "There's a building on that block that DREAM has had an arrangement with for two years. They want the arrangement."

She processed this. "And they're using me as leverage to get it."

"They're using you as leverage to get Jisung to agree to something he hasn't agreed to yet," Donghyuck said. "And to get me to stop arguing against the expansion."

She looked at him. "Are you going to stop arguing against it."

He looked back at her. The direct look, the honest face. "No," he said.

She held that. Mark looked up from the table briefly — not at them, just a general inventory of the room — and looked back down. Jeno said something low. The apartment held its April Saturday warmth, the edges underneath it.

"So they'll escalate," she said.

"Probably," he said.

"When," she said.

"When they've decided the message from the parking lot wasn't sufficient," he said. "When they've decided they need to send a bigger message."

She absorbed this with the specific quality she had been developing — the fast complete inventory, the filing without alarm. A bigger message. She thought about what a bigger message looked like in the operational vocabulary she had been learning. She thought about the men who had come out of the back room in February. She thought about it got complicated at ten forty-three.

"Okay," she said.

He looked at her.

"I'm not—" she stopped. Found the right thing. "I'm not going to pretend I'm not aware that this is moving toward something. I am aware. I've been aware since Cheng told me this morning." She looked at the window. "I just need you to keep telling me things. So that when it moves I'm not behind."

"I'll tell you," he said. "Everything I can tell you."

"And the things you can't tell me," she said.

"I'll tell you those too," he said. "When I can."

She nodded. The apartment held them. The window held its April afternoon. Outside, the east side ran its Saturday program, and somewhere in it the Kim side's calculation was continuing to run, and Jihoon's transaction was accruing its return, and the specific architecture of something building was doing its patient work.

She opened her book.

She read a page. Not the usual not-reading — actually read a page, the words going in, the story doing its thing. The specific ordinary of a Saturday afternoon on a couch in the east side, with the weight of things underneath it, both of those simultaneously true.

She turned the page.

He looked at the window.

The April afternoon held its light.

Sunday she played ranked for four hours.

Not because she was managing something — because she wanted to, which was the original reason, the reason that existed before the game became an emotional thermometer. She pulled up the client and queued and locked Kiriko and played with the specific joy of someone who was simply good at something and had time to be good at it.

She climbed one rank point. Then two. Then lost one and gained it back and gained another. The streak had broken on Thursday — some matches did that, the variables going sideways, the team composition failing to cohere — but the climb itself was intact. She was Grandmaster four and climbing, the slow patient work of someone who played with consistency rather than brilliance, who understood that the game rewarded people who made correct decisions continuously over people who made spectacular ones occasionally.

Support was like that. Kiriko was like that. You kept people alive. You enabled the team to do its work. You carried the match from the position nobody was watching.

She had been doing this since she was thirteen.

She won the fourth match with a play she had never made before — a Swift Step through a wall she hadn't known was traversable, appearing behind an enemy flanker who had been shredding her backline, the precise Kitsune Rush timing that turned the fight in the space of four seconds. Her team won on the back of it. The post-game screen showed her stats in the column she still allowed herself to look at for exactly three seconds.

She looked for four.

She texted Donghyuck after: made a play today that I didn't know I could make

Haechannie🐭: yeah?

Haechannie🐭: what kind

She described it. He understood it — not perfectly, he was not the player she was, but he played enough to follow the mechanics and ask the right questions. They texted about it for twenty minutes, the specific low-stakes pleasure of talking about something with someone who was interested without being condescending, who could follow the thread without having to be told what mattered.

it's the kind of play, she typed, that makes you realize you've been better than you thought for longer than you knew

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: that's been true about more than overwatch for a while now

She held the phone. The Sunday afternoon was doing its thing outside her window — the April light, the committed green, the specific quality of a Havenbrook Sunday that had stopped requiring anything from her.

I know, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: do you

yes, she typed. I'm learning to.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: keep playing

I am, she typed.

She opened the queue. The matchmaking screen cycled. Thirty seconds, forty.

The match found her.

She locked Kiriko.

The April afternoon held its light, and the east side held its weight, and both of those things were true simultaneously, and she played.


23: scabbed knuckles

The second week of April had a specific quality to it that Solar had been noticing since Monday — a tension in the air that was not the school's social weather, not Jihoon's particular brand of patience, but something that ran underneath both of those and had its own frequency. She had been learning to read frequencies for six months. She recognized this one without being able to name it yet.

Donghyuck was present all week but differently. Not absent — the divergence point every morning, the texts, Saturday at the apartment. But the quality of his attention had an edge she had learned to recognize as the operational register running close to the surface. Not covering the Donghyuck version. Just adjacent to it, the way two registers could coexist without one replacing the other.

She did not ask. She had learned the specific discipline of waiting for him to tell her rather than asking, which was not the same as not knowing something was there.

On Wednesday he told her at the divergence point, in the particular morning quiet of the route before DMHS engaged:

"The Kim side tested the Maren line last night."

She looked at him. "How," she said.

"Someone came onto the block," he said. "The building Mr. Yoon used to operate. The one they want. They sent two people at eleven-thirty."

"What did they do," she said.

"Nothing yet," he said. "They were establishing presence. Making sure DREAM knew the line was going to be pushed."

She understood the grammar of this — the same grammar as the parking lot, the same grammar as the dry cleaner light. Establishing presence was its own message. We are here. We are going to be here. What are you going to do about it.

"What did DREAM do," she said.

"Sent someone back," he said. "Four people."

She absorbed that. Four people, in response to two. The arithmetic of escalation — you matched and exceeded to establish that the return would always be disproportionate, which was what made further testing expensive.

"Was it—" she started.

"Controlled," he said. "Nobody was hurt. They moved the two off the block."

She held moved the two off the block in the way she held his operational language — receiving the surface, understanding the depth underneath it, not requiring the full translation.

"Are they going to try again," she said.

"Yes," he said. "This was a probe. They were taking the temperature."

"And what's the temperature," she said.

He looked at her sideways with the expression he used when she asked the right question. "Hot enough that they'll think twice," he said. "Not hot enough that they'll stop."

She looked at the school ahead of them. The April morning, the committed green, the students filing through the entrance. DMHS running its ordinary program regardless of what ran underneath Havenbrook at eleven-thirty.

"Okay," she said.

"Okay," he confirmed.

They reached the school and the bubble dissolved and the ordinary program engaged and she carried the information through the day in the specific internal pocket she had developed for things she needed to hold without being visibly holding them.

Thursday at school Bora was not quiet.

This was notable specifically because of the preceding weeks of quiet — Bora going quiet after the footage circulated, Bora going quiet after the parking lot, the specific silence of someone who had been waiting for the right moment to surface. The right moment had apparently arrived.

Solar became aware of it between second and third period, in the hallway near the science wing — not a confrontation, not the October geometry of intent. Just Bora, with her usual two flanking people, in a configuration that Solar registered immediately as something that had been positioned rather than arrived at naturally.

"Sol," Bora called.

Solar stopped. She turned. She had learned, in two and a half years, the specific cost-benefit of stopping versus not stopping — ignoring required a different kind of energy than facing, and she had enough energy today.

"Bora," she said.

Bora had the expression Solar had categorized since freshman year: not malicious exactly, just performing confidence with the specific quality of someone who believed they had information. "I just wanted to ask," she said, in the tone of someone who had decided casual was the appropriate register, "how things are going. With — " she made a gesture that encompassed something Solar was supposed to understand. "Everything."

"Fine," Solar said. "Thanks."

"Because I heard," Bora said, and this was the tell — the I heard was never neutral in Bora's mouth, it was always the preamble to something — "that there was something with the Kim side? And your — " another gesture. "Situation."

Solar looked at her. She processed the information of Bora knowing this — not alarmed, because Bora knowing things was the standard operating condition of DMHS, but noting it. The Kim side's probe of the Maren line had been Wednesday night. It was Thursday morning. The information had moved through some channel she would have to identify, because the speed and direction of information in Havenbrook always had a source.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Solar said, in the level register she used for things she did not intend to confirm.

Bora's expression updated. She had expected a different response — something with more purchase on it, something that gave her something back. The level register gave her nothing.

"Okay," Bora said, after a moment. Not satisfied — recalibrating. "Just checking in."

"Appreciate it," Solar said, and went to third period.

She texted Cheng between third and fourth: Bora knows something about the Kim side. How?

The response came in seven minutes:

Cheng: not bora

Cheng: yuna

She held the phone. Yuna. Jiwoo's observation from Tuesday: Yuna goes quiet when something is changing direction. The direction had changed. The Kim side's probe had produced information that traveled through Yuna to Bora, which meant Jihoon was still actively managing the channel, still running the specific architecture of using the school's social infrastructure as an information network.

He was not done. He was not even close to done.

She texted back: Is he escalating or positioning?

Cheng: positioning

Cheng: he wants DREAM to know he knows

Cheng: and he wants you to know that he knows that DREAM knows

She read that twice. The three layers of it. Jihoon communicating to DREAM through her through Bora through Yuna: I see what's happening, I'm in the middle of it, my position is relevant.

He was reminding everyone that he existed. That the transaction with the Kim side was his transaction, that the lever was his lever, that Solar being a pressure point was his specific contribution and he wanted credit for it.

She went to fourth period.

She understood something she had been assembling piece by piece since the locker conversation in March: Jihoon was not operating for outcomes anymore. He was operating for position. He wanted to matter in the specific geography of DREAM's world and the Kim side's world and Solar's life, even if mattering required causing damage. Especially if it did.

This was more dangerous than wanting something specific. People who wanted specific things could be negotiated with. People who wanted to matter couldn't be.

She told Donghyuck at the divergence point on Friday.

He was quiet for longer than usual after she finished — the thinking pause, not the dose-managing one. They walked half a block before he spoke.

"He's positioning for leverage inside the Kim side," he said. "Not just using them. Trying to establish himself as a necessary variable."

"So that when this resolves," she said, "however it resolves, he has a seat at whatever table is left."

"Yes," he said. He looked at the sidewalk. "Which means he's not going to pull back. Pulling back costs him position."

"He doesn't have an exit," she said.

"He does," Donghyuck said. "He just doesn't want it." He glanced at her. "That's the version of him that's actually dangerous. Not the one who wants something. The one who has decided that being dangerous is itself a kind of identity."

She held that. They were at the school. The April morning was doing what it did, warm and specific, the green fully committed in the trees.

"What do we do," she said.

He looked at her. The we had arrived without announcement — she had said it without deciding to, the specific pronoun landing correctly. She did not take it back.

"Right now," he said, "we go to school."

She almost smiled. "And after school."

"After school," he said, "we go to the divergence point."

"And this weekend," she said.

"This weekend," he said, "you come to the apartment and I figure out the next piece of this."

She looked at him. He looked back at her with the honest face in the April morning light — the red hair, the dark eyes, the chain at his collar, the jacket over a dark hoodie. The same face in the same light, which she had been looking at since October and which she understood had become the face she looked for first when she entered any room he might be in.

"Okay," she said.

He nodded once. They went through their respective doors.

Saturday arrived with the weight it had been building toward all week.

She felt it the moment she climbed the stairs to the apartment — not the February compressed-air quality, something more present than that, something that had moved from atmospheric to specific. The second step creaked. The building exhaled its old wood smell. She pushed the door open.

The apartment was full.

All of them. Mark at the table — not with coffee, with nothing in his hands, which was unusual — Jeno beside him with the focused quality he had when something had required him the previous night. Renjun on the couch, laptop open but not his documentation screen, something else. Chenle in the armchair with his phone and none of his usual window enthusiasm. Jisung in the kitchen doorway, leaning against the frame, watching the room with the assessment that never left his face but which was sharper today.

Donghyuck stood at the window.

He looked at her when she came in — the inventory look, the fast check that she was fine — and nodded once.

She set her bag down. She sat on the couch. She looked at the room and understood from its specific quality that something had happened after Wednesday's probe. Not Wednesday. After. More recently than that.

Mark spoke first. Not to her — to the room. "Last night," he said, "the Kim side came back to the Maren line." He said it with the flat economy of someone delivering a briefing. "Not two people this time."

Solar looked at him. He was looking at the table.

"Six," Jeno said. He said it without inflection, the way he said operational things — as facts, not as events. "Six people, eleven p.m., the building on the corner of Maren and Third."

"What happened," Solar said.

The room registered that she had asked. Not uncomfortably — they had accepted her as someone who asked, who was in the room fully enough to ask. Jisung's assessment ran on her briefly and returned to whatever it was calculating.

Mark looked at her. "We responded," he said.

She held that. We responded. She thought about Wednesday — four people sent to move two off the block. She thought about the arithmetic of escalation, the specific progression of it, what came after four when the other side sent six.

"How," she said.

Mark looked at Donghyuck.

Donghyuck looked at her from the window. The honest face. The unmanaged version. "We made it clear," he said, "that the Maren line is not negotiable."

She understood. She understood the full depth of made it clear in his register, the specific content of those three words when applied to six people at eleven p.m. on a corner. She had been learning this language for six months. She was fluent enough.

"Is anyone hurt," she said.

"Not ours," Jeno said.

She held the negative space of not ours. She looked at Donghyuck. He looked back at her with the honest face and did not qualify the negative space, because qualifying it would require explaining it, and explaining it would require words she understood he was not going to give her in a room with everyone present.

She did not ask him to.

She looked at the window. The April Saturday outside was doing its specific thing — warm, the east side in daylight looking nothing like the east side at eleven p.m., the street running its ordinary program. She thought about what the corner of Maren and Third had looked like at eleven last night and understood she was not going to know precisely, which was a boundary she had accepted when she accepted the broader arrangement.

"The Kim side is going to respond to the response," she said.

"Yes," Mark said.

"When," she said.

"We don't know yet," Renjun said, from the couch. He said it with the precision of someone who had been tracking a data set. "The pattern so far is seventy-two hour intervals. Wednesday probe, Friday response. If the pattern holds, Monday or Tuesday."

"Does the pattern hold," she said.

Renjun looked at her with the expression he used when a question was better than expected. "Usually," he said. "Not always."

She nodded. She looked at the room — all of them, the specific people she had been spending Saturdays with since October, who were also the people who had responded to six at eleven p.m. on Maren and Third. Both of those things simultaneously. The warmth of the apartment and the specific edge underneath it, inseparable, the same room.

Jisung spoke from the kitchen doorway. He was looking at her with the assessment expression, which was the only one she had ever seen on his face. "You're processing," he said. Not an accusation. Just an observation.

"Yes," she said.

"Good," he said. And went back into the kitchen.

She looked at Donghyuck. He was still at the window. His hands were in the dark hoodie's pocket now — not the jacket, the jacket was on the chair, which meant he had been in the apartment long enough to take it off, which meant he had been here since last night probably. The chain at his collar. The red hair. The honest face.

He looked like himself. Not worse for whatever last night had been. Not better. Just himself, which was the thing she had come to understand was the most resilient quality he had — he was always himself, in every register, the warmth and the dangerous and the honest and the operational all the same person, all the same face.

She looked at him and understood she had been afraid of this moment without naming the fear — the moment where she saw what DREAM's response actually looked like from the inside and had to decide if it changed something. She had been holding the potential of this moment since February.

It did not change something.

That was not bravado. It was information, the same way most things she received were information — fast, complete, filed. She had chosen this with full information and the full information had just arrived at a new level of specificity and the choosing held.

She looked back at the window.

"Okay," she said. To the room. To all of them.

Mark looked at her. Jeno looked at her. Renjun's typing had slowed. Even Chenle, in the armchair, was looking at her with the expression he used for things he found significant.

Donghyuck did not look away from the window. But the line of his shoulders changed — the specific fraction of tension releasing, the same fraction she had been reading since October, the Donghyuck-specific release that meant he had been holding something and had been given permission to set it down.

"Okay," he said.

After.

The apartment found its Saturday register eventually — not the full warmth, not entirely, the weight of the week still present in the air. But Chenle migrated to the window at some point and pressed his nose against the glass and announced that it was warm enough to have the window open, which Renjun accepted with the expression of someone who had learned to pick battles. Mark made coffee. Jeno stretched out on the floor, which was apparently more comfortable than the couch when you had been operational the previous night, and fell asleep within four minutes with the ease of someone whose body had learned to take rest where it found it.

Solar sat on the couch and did not read her book and did not pretend to.

Donghyuck came away from the window eventually and sat beside her. Closer than the shoulder-adjacent position — close enough that their arms were in contact, which was a different degree of proximity than usual, and she registered it with the same attention she registered everything.

"You're okay," he said. Not asking.

"I'm okay," she confirmed.

He was quiet. Outside, Chenle's window had admitted the April air, which moved through the apartment with the specific warmth of a season that had decided. The coffee in the kitchen was doing its coffee smell. Jeno was audibly asleep.

She reached over and took his hand.

Her thumb found his knuckles.

She stilled.

The skin there was not the skin she had held in December or in March — it was different, rougher, the specific texture of something that had been through something and had not yet fully healed. The scabbing was uneven under her thumb, the kind that came from impact rather than accident, from a specific kind of contact that she understood without requiring it to be named.

She did not move her hand.

She did not say anything.

Neither did he.

She held his hand — the scabbed knuckles, the warmth underneath, the same hand it had always been — and looked at the window, and he looked at the window, and the April air moved through the room, and Jeno breathed his steady asleep breathing on the floor, and neither of them said a word about what her thumb had found.

There was nothing to say. She had found it. She had stayed. That was the whole sentence.

"Sol," he said, after a while.

"Yeah," she said.

"What you did just now," he said. "In the room. The okay."

She looked at the window.

"That was—" he stopped. She could feel him finding the right word. "That was the bravest thing I've seen you do. And I've seen you do brave things."

She held that. She thought about the party in December, standing in front of Jihoon on his own ground. She thought about the footage and the silence. She thought about the east entrance, going to where he was instead of away.

"It wasn't brave," she said. "It was just honest."

"That's what brave is," he said.

She looked at him. The honest face. The red hair catching the April light from Chenle's open window, the specific color it was in this light — not the dark of the east side at night, not the winter flat, but the full red of it, the color it was when the day was being fully itself.

"I heard something," she said. "In the room. When Jeno said not ours."

He was quiet.

"I'm not asking you to tell me the rest of it," she said. "I know there's a rest of it. I'm just saying I heard it."

He looked at the window. Her thumb was still on his knuckles.

"Two of theirs," he said, quietly. "Not serious. But not nothing."

She held that. The negative space filled in completely now — the scabbing under her thumb and the two of theirs and the corner of Maren and Third at eleven p.m. landing together, the full picture assembled without a single word she had asked for.

She did not flinch. She breathed. She did not move her hand.

"Thank you," she said. "For telling me."

"Yeah," he said.

They sat in the Saturday apartment with the April air coming through Chenle's open window, and Jeno asleep on the floor, and Renjun's laptop doing its work, and Mark's coffee in the kitchen, and the east side outside running its daylight program over the memory of what the same streets had looked like at eleven the previous night.

Both things. The same room.

Her thumb on his knuckles.

His hand holding hers.

That night she played ranked.

Not to process — she had processed, or was processing in the way that processing happened: slowly, in the background, without requiring her to sit still and attend to it. She played because she wanted to play, because it was Saturday night and the queue was there and Kiriko was there and the game was the game.

She won four straight. The streak rebuilt from where it had broken Thursday. The specific pleasure of consistent decisions producing consistent results, the game rewarding what she brought to it.

At one in the morning she sent a text:

still up?

The response came in three minutes.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: how's the streak

four tonight, she typed. rebuilding.

Haechannie🐭: good

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: you good? for real

She looked at the screen. She thought about the apartment. Not ours. Two of theirs. The hand in hers. Both things, the same room.

for real, she typed. yes.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: i believe you

I know you do, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She locked her screen. She opened the queue. The matchmaking screen cycled — thirty seconds, forty, the familiar wait.

The match found her.

She locked Kiriko.

The April night outside her window held its committed warmth, and the east side held its history from the night before, and she held her mouse and played, and all of those things were true simultaneously, and that was the shape of her life, and she was inside it, and she was okay.


24: on a tuesday

It happened on a Tuesday.

Not a significant Tuesday — not the Tuesday of the footage, not the Tuesday of the apartment at nine-fifteen. Just a Tuesday in the third week of April, the kind that existed in the middle of a week the way most Tuesdays did: unremarkably, between Monday and Wednesday, doing nothing to distinguish itself from either.

She would think about this later. That it happened on a Tuesday. That it did not require a significant occasion to become significant.

The week had been quiet in the operational sense — quieter than the previous week, the Kim side's pattern holding in the way Renjun had said patterns usually held, the seventy-two-hour interval producing nothing on Monday or Tuesday as anticipated. The east side was running its ordinary program. The school was running its ordinary program. The April warmth had settled into something that felt less like a promise and more like a fact — the kind of warmth you stopped noticing because it was simply what outside felt like now.

She had a free period on Tuesdays at two.

She had been spending them in the library since October — the third-floor library with Ms. Park and the armchairs and the bean bags the administration didn't acknowledge. It was the room where the first silence had happened, where he had sat across from her at the study table in December and she had written her best paper of the year. She still went there. The room had accumulated something from that afternoon that she returned to without fully naming.

On this Tuesday she arrived at the library at two and found it empty.

Not entirely — Ms. Park was at the front desk, and two junior boys were at a far table doing something that involved both their laptops and a significant degree of mutual frustration, and there was the usual ambient quality of a room that had been occupied all day and was in a between-people moment. But the armchairs by the window were empty, and the couch from October was empty, and she sat down in the armchair and opened her book.

She read two pages.

The library door opened at two-twelve.

She knew before she looked. She knew from the quality of the air change, the specific way a room registered his entry — not dramatic, not announced, just the gravity adjusting. She looked anyway.

Donghyuck stood in the doorway with his jacket on and his hands coming out of his pockets and the expression of someone who had decided something and was acting on the decision before he could revise it.

"Hey," he said, quietly. Library register.

"Hey," she said.

He came in. He sat in the armchair across from her — not the study table, the armchairs, the ones by the window that were specifically not the study table configuration. He looked at her book. He looked at her.

"Free period," he said.

"Me too, apparently," she said.

Ms. Park had not looked up from her desk. The junior boys were still mutually frustrated. The library did its library thing around them.

"I was going to text you," he said.

"You found me instead," she said.

"You're predictable," he said. "In a good way."

She looked at him. "How did you know I'd be here."

"You come here on Tuesdays," he said. "Since October. I noticed."

She held that. Since October. He had noticed in October and had been noting it for six months and had never said anything, the same way he had noted her locker in August and noted her not liking cigarette smoke and noted the temperature of her texts and noted all of it, the ongoing inventory of someone who paid attention because paying attention was simply what he did.

"You could have just texted," she said.

"I know," he said.

She looked at him. He looked at her. The window behind them was doing its April afternoon thing — the light coming in at the angle that made the dust in the air visible, the same angle as December, the same library, a different Tuesday.

"What did you want to say," she said. "That you were going to text."

He was quiet for a moment. Not the thinking pause — something else, something closer to the quality he had on the step in February when she had almost said something and hadn't. The edge of something.

"I've been thinking about the scabbing," he said.

She held her book. She waited.

"You felt it and you didn't move," he said. "And I've been thinking about that since Saturday." He looked at the window. "I've been thinking about a lot of things since Saturday."

"What things," she said.

He looked back at her. The honest face. The full version of it, the one that had nothing managing it. "That you keep choosing this," he said. "Every time it gets more real, you choose it again. And I—" he stopped. Started differently. "I keep waiting for the version of it that's too much. The version where you look at what this actually is and decide it's not what you signed up for."

"Donghyuck," she said.

"I know," he said. "I know you've said it. I know you've shown it. I'm not—" he paused. "I'm not doubting you. I'm just telling you what's been running."

She looked at him for a moment. She thought about October — eight weeks of slow building before she knew his name in her phone was the same person as the one on the school wall. She thought about December, the party, the sidewalk, I'm not him. She thought about the knuckles under her thumb and the staying.

"I'm not going to stop choosing it," she said. "Because of what it is."

"I know," he said.

"Then stop waiting for me to," she said. Not harsh — just direct, the specific directness she used for things she had decided not to leave ambiguous. "I'm here. I was here last Saturday and I'm here now and I'm going to be here on the Saturdays after this." She held his gaze. "Stop managing for the version of me that leaves."

He looked at her for a long moment.

Something in his expression did the thing it did when she said something that arrived somewhere he had not been expecting — the specific quality of a person receiving rather than composing, the slight stilling that meant information had landed correctly.

"Okay," he said.

"Okay," she said.

The library held its particular afternoon quiet. Ms. Park turned a page. The junior boys reached some kind of resolution that required one of them to close his laptop with finality. The April light did its angled thing through the window.

She looked at him. He looked at her.

The distance between the armchairs was approximately four feet, which was the distance it had always been in this room, which was the distance that had existed since December when they had sat at the study table and she had written her paper and the first silence had happened and neither of them had known yet what the silence contained.

Four feet was not very far.

She understood, sitting in the armchair in the April library on a Tuesday at two-fifteen, that she had been managing the four feet the same way she had been managing everything — with the specific discipline of someone who understood that proximity had costs and had been deciding the costs were manageable. She had been making that calculation since October. She had gotten very good at it.

She was tired of making it.

She stood up.

He watched her stand without moving — not surprised, the way he was rarely surprised, but present in the specific way of someone who understood something was happening and was choosing not to interfere with its arrival.

She crossed the four feet.

She sat on the arm of his chair — not in it, not on his lap, just on the arm, adjacent, the closest version of the armchair configuration that was possible. Which put her approximately six inches from him rather than four feet. Which was, she understood, a different kind of distance entirely.

He looked up at her. The honest face from below, the dark eyes with their particular depth, the red hair, the chain at his collar catching the afternoon light.

She looked at him.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said. His voice had the quality it had in the mornings before the school day engaged — lower, less ambient, the performing ease not yet operational. The unguarded version.

She had been looking at the unguarded version since October from a variety of distances. This was the closest distance yet.

She put her hand on the side of his face.

Not dramatically — not cupping his face, not pulling him anywhere. Just her palm against his jaw, the specific warmth of it, her thumb near the corner of his cheekbone. He went very still. Not the tension-still — the receiving-still, the kind that meant something was landing and he was letting it land.

She looked at him for a moment. At the red hair and the dark eyes and the mole near his nose she had noticed in this library in December and never mentioned. At the jaw that softened when he wasn't performing anything. At the honest face, fully present, looking up at her with the expression she had been cataloguing since October and which she understood now was the expression of someone who had been patient for a very long time and had just been told, without words, that the patience was over.

She leaned down.

She kissed him.

Not with announcement. Not with the weight of seven months attached to it — or rather, with all of it attached to it but carried lightly, the way things were light when they were finally where they were supposed to be. His mouth was warm and still for exactly one second — the receiving-still — and then it wasn't still, and his hand came up and found the back of her arm, just below her shoulder, and held it there with the same care that he held everything, and the library was the library and Ms. Park was at her desk and the April light was doing its thing through the window.

She pulled back after a moment.

She looked at him.

He looked at her.

His expression had the quality it had never had before in any of the configurations she had catalogued — not the honest face exactly, not the performing ease, not the operational register. Something underneath all three, the version that existed when all the layers were gone and only the actual thing remained.

"Hey," she said again.

He almost laughed. "Hey," he said.

She was still sitting on the arm of his chair. His hand was still on her arm. The library was still the library. Ms. Park had turned another page. The junior boys had left at some point without her noticing.

"Okay," she said.

"Okay," he said.

Neither of them moved for a moment.

Then she stood, because the library was the library and she was not going to live in the armchair forever, and she went back to her chair and picked up her book and opened it to the page she'd been on. He looked at the window with the expression of someone who was very deliberately not performing serenity and was almost achieving it.

She read a page.

She read another.

"Sol," he said.

She looked up.

"Since October," he said. He said it the way he said true things — directly, without dressing it. "I've been waiting to do that since October."

She looked at him for a moment.

"I know," she said. "Me too."

He looked at the window. The not-quite-smile arrived at the corner of his mouth and stayed there, unmanaged, doing what it wanted.

She went back to her book.

She did not read a single word of it for the next forty minutes and was not remotely sorry.

After the free period she went to class.

She walked through the DMHS hallways with the usual composure and inside the composure something was running differently — not visibly, not in any way that produced information for the social machinery of the school to process. Just internally. The specific quality of someone who has crossed a threshold and is living on the other side of it.

Fifth period was English. She sat down and took out her notebook and looked at the board and thought about the library and thought about since October and thought about the specific warmth of his jaw under her palm.

She took notes on the lesson. She did it accurately. She was a good student in the specific way of someone who had learned to function in two registers simultaneously, the surface and the interior, both running correctly at once.

After fifth period she went to the east entrance.

He was already there — not in the wall-lean configuration, not the Haechan-holding-court configuration. Just standing near the door with his hands in his jacket pockets, looking at his phone, waiting. The performing ease was there — it was always there in the school's orbit — but underneath it, when he looked up and saw her, something came through that was the other version.

"Hey," she said.

"Hey," he said.

They walked. The divergence point. The April afternoon. Their shoulders adjacent.

Neither of them said anything about the library for three blocks. Not because it was a secret — not because either of them had decided not to talk about it. Just because it was so fully present between them that naming it would have been redundant, the way you didn't narrate your own heartbeat.

At the divergence point she stopped.

He stopped.

She looked at him. He looked at her.

She had a thought, standing at the corner of Fifth and wherever, that she had been standing at divergence points with him for four months and that this was the last one that would feel the way the previous ones had felt — the specific weight of something almost-said, the distance maintained, the calculation running. That was over now. The divergence point still existed. The calculation did not.

"Later," she said.

He looked at her with the expression. The one from the library, the version underneath everything, present in the April afternoon in the specific way it had not been present before today.

"Later," he said.

She walked home.

She was smiling before she reached the end of the block and she did not press her mouth flat.

She let it be what it was.

That evening, at nine-forty-three, her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: hey

hey, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: how's the streak

haven't played yet tonight, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: the library

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i've been thinking about it

me too

Haechannie🐭: what are you thinking

She held the phone. She thought about the armchair and the four feet and the palm on his jaw and the since October and the way he'd looked at her with the expression that had all the layers gone.

that it was always going to be a tuesday, she typed. I don't know why. It just was.

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: it really was

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: i'm glad it was you

She held the phone. She read it twice.

what does that mean, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: it means

Haechannie🐭: i've been careful about most things for a long time

Haechannie🐭: and i'm glad the thing i stopped being careful about was you

She put the phone down on her chest. The ceiling. The barely-visible stars. The refrigerator hum. The ordinary room on a Tuesday in April that had become something other than ordinary without requiring it to be announced.

me too, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She locked the screen. She lay in the dark for a while. Then she opened the queue, put her headset on, and played ranked because she was happy and she played better when she was happy and the streak was not going to build itself.

She locked Kiriko.

She played.

The April night held its warmth outside the window.

The barely-visible stars held their light.

She won.


25: rooftop reality check

The last Saturday of April was warm enough that the city had stopped having opinions about the temperature.

That was the specific thing about late April in Havenbrook — not that it was hot, not yet, but that the cold had finally, conclusively given up its argument. The air simply was what it was: warm, uncommitted to anything dramatic, the kind of warmth that allowed the city to exhale. The trees were fully themselves. The park two blocks from DMHS had its green back. The east side smelled different in this warmth — the mineral thing still present underneath, but the top layer changed, something looser and more present, the neighborhood's specific metabolism running faster than it did in winter.

She arrived at the Cha's building at the usual time.

The apartment was quieter than she expected — not the operational compressed-air quiet, just the ordinary quiet of a Saturday afternoon that had not yet gathered itself into anything. Mark was absent, which Renjun explained by gesturing vaguely at the window in the way he gestured at most things that didn't require elaboration. Jeno was on the floor doing what appeared to be stretching with the focused attention of someone whose body had been through something and was being systematically corrected. Chenle was at the window, which was his default position in warm weather, the window fully open now, the April air moving through freely.

Jisung was in the kitchen. He was always in the kitchen or the back room. She had stopped tracking which.

Donghyuck was not in the apartment.

"Roof," Chenle said, without turning from the window, with the ease of someone who had learned to provide this information proactively.

She looked at Renjun.

"He goes up there," Renjun confirmed, still typing. "When he needs to think. Or when he doesn't want to think. One of those."

"Which one today," she said.

Renjun considered. "Probably the second one," he said.

She left her bag on the couch. She went back out to the stairwell and up the extra flight — past the third floor, up the narrow stairs she had never taken, to the door that Donghyuck had mentioned once in a text at eleven p.m.: second one from the bottom / been like that since I got here. Not this door. The building's top door, which opened onto the roof with the specific reluctance of something that had been opened and closed many times without ever being properly maintained.

The roof was what she had expected from his description and also more than that.

The water tower stood at the far end — taller than she'd thought, the old galvanized kind, its legs slightly rusted at the base. The parapet wall ran around the perimeter, waist-high, concrete with the specific weathering of something that had been exposed to a lot of Havenbrook. The door behind her let onto a flat surface of tar and gravel that caught the afternoon light in the way flat surfaces did — not beautifully, just honestly, showing everything it was.

The view was the three-block view he had described. More than that from up here — she could see further than the window allowed, the east side spreading out in its specific geography, the blocks she had been learning to read from the ground now laid out from above. She could see the dry cleaner. She could see the corner of Maren and Fifth. She could see the block where the Kim side had probed, and the building that Mr. Yoon had operated out of, and the three blocks of territory that were the current line of contention.

She could read it from here.

She stood at the parapet for a moment, looking.

Donghyuck was sitting with his back against the water tower's base, his legs stretched out, jacket off for the first time she could remember seeing in the open air. It was on the ground beside him. He had his phone in his lap and his face turned up toward the sky with the specific quality of someone who was not doing anything. Not thinking operationally, not composing a response, not performing ease for anyone. Just — being. His face in the afternoon light with nothing managing it.

This was the version she had been seeing in increments since October — the honest face, the unguarded version. But even those versions had a context: the apartment, the library, the divergence point. They existed inside something that was either school or DREAM or the specific space between those two worlds.

This was different.

This was just him. On a roof on a Saturday afternoon with his jacket off, his face toward the sky, doing nothing at all.

She understood something she had been circling without naming — that she had never seen him not perform. The east entrance, the apartment, the operational nights, the divergence point — even the most unguarded versions of him existed inside a context that required some version of the Haechan register to be running. He was always the person who moved through the east side with a specific authority, who managed encounters, who directed and protected and held the room. Always, in some form, Haechan.

Here he was just Donghyuck. The specific ordinary of a person existing without any register required.

He heard her and looked over.

Not startled — she had made noise on the door, the old hinges announcing her. He looked at her with the expression of someone noting an arrival they had not planned for but were not opposed to. Something in his face changed slightly — not engaging the performing ease, not the operational register. Just the third version, the one from the library, present and warm and specifically his.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

She came across the roof and sat beside him with her back against the water tower's base, her shoulder adjacent to his, the jacket between them on the ground. The gravel was slightly warm from the afternoon sun. The water tower made its low groan above them — the temperature change sound, the building doing its work.

"This is yours," she said.

"Yeah," he said.

"The guys don't come up here?"

"Mark does sometimes," he said. "The others not really. Chenle came up once and announced that it needed plants and never came back."

She almost smiled. "What did you say."

"I said it was a roof," he said. "He said that wasn't a reason."

She looked at the view. The east side from above, all the geography she had been learning laid out in its actual shape — the grid of it, the specific way the streets ran, the blocks she could now identify by their operational significance. She had been learning this city from the ground for her whole life and from the window for six months. From up here it had a different clarity, the kind that came from altitude, the kind that showed you the shape of things rather than the texture.

"You can see everything from here," she said.

"Yeah," he said. "That's why I come up."

"Not to think," she said.

"To see," he said. "There's a difference."

She considered that. She thought about the distinction between thinking and seeing — thinking required intention, required a problem to apply itself to, required the operational register or the planning register or some register. Seeing was just receiving. Taking in the shape of things without needing to do anything with it.

She had not done a lot of pure seeing. She had been running some register or another since she was fourteen.

She looked at the east side from above and tried to just see it.

The dry cleaner. The corner of Maren and Fifth. The park, further away than she expected from this height. The school visible as a specific roofline two blocks north, which she had not realized was that close. Her street running east from the school, and somewhere along it her house, the one with the tree in the front yard that had its full April green from up here, barely distinguishable from every other tree except that she knew which one it was.

"I can see my house," she said.

He followed her gaze. He looked for a moment. "Yeah," he said. "I know which one."

She looked at him. He was looking at the street where her house was with the expression of someone confirming something they had known and were now seeing from a new angle.

"Have you looked at it from up here before," she said.

"Once or twice," he said. He said it simply, without self-consciousness, the way he said things that were true and required no additional framing.

She held that. He had sat on this roof and looked at the street where her house was. Not in a surveilling way — not operationally, not with purpose. Just the way you looked at things from a high place when you knew where to look. She was a coordinate in the geography he lived inside, the same way the dry cleaner was, the same way the corner of Maren and Fifth was. She was in the map.

She looked back at the view.

"Tell me something," she said. "About you. Not DREAM, not school. Something that's just yours."

He was quiet for a moment. Not the managing quiet — the thinking quiet, the brief pause that meant he was taking the question seriously.

"Music," he said.

She looked at him.

"I play," he said. "Piano, mostly. Sometimes guitar. I started when I was nine because my mother made me and then she stopped making me and I kept going." He looked at the water tower. "I don't play in front of people. I haven't in a long time."

She held that. The specific texture of it — something learned under compulsion, kept out of genuine want, maintained privately. The piano in whatever room he went to when no register was required.

"Why not," she said. "In front of people."

"Because it's the version of me that has nothing on top of it," he said. "And I'm careful about what I show that version to." He glanced at her sideways. The corner of his mouth moved. "You're going to tell me that's ironic, given."

"Given the library," she said.

"Given the library," he confirmed.

She looked at the view. She thought about the library — the armchair and the four feet and her palm on his jaw. The version of him with all the layers gone. She had gone to the four feet. He had let her.

"Play for me sometime," she said.

He was quiet.

"Not now," she said. "Not today. Just — sometime."

He looked at the east side for a moment. "Yeah," he said. "Okay."

They sat against the water tower in the late April afternoon with the view laid out in front of them. The water tower made its sounds. The gravel held its warmth. Below them, the east side ran its Saturday program, and the apartment below them had Renjun's typing and Jeno's stretching and Chenle at the window and Jisung in the kitchen, all of it present and accounted for and three floors down.

Up here was just this.

She thought about the question she had asked — tell me something that's just yours — and understood that she had been asking it for months without using the words. She had been asking it through the texts and the divergence points and the armchairs and the couch. She had been assembling the answer piece by piece: the coin-trick habit with the cigarette, the arguing in writing, the patient that was specifically patience rather than distance, the specific quality of someone who had learned to carry two registers simultaneously and had gotten so good at it that the seam between them was nearly invisible.

The piano was the thing that made the seam visible. Something learned at nine, kept privately, shown to nobody. The version with nothing on top.

She was looking at that version right now. It had a specific quality in the afternoon light — softer than she expected from someone who carried the weight he carried, more ordinary, the specific ordinary of a person who was seventeen years old and sat on a roof on Saturdays and looked at the city because seeing it was different from thinking about it.

"Can I ask you something," he said.

"Yes," she said.

"The Seoul thing," he said. "When you picture it — not the audition, not the practical part. Just — what you think it's going to be."

She held the question. She thought about it the way she was learning to think about things: without the usual management, without the version she had prepared for other people to hear. Just what it actually was.

"Loud," she said. "I think it's going to be very loud. And very fast. And I think I'm going to be completely out of my depth for a long time before I'm not." She looked at the east side from above. "I think it's going to ask me to be things I don't know how to be yet." She paused. "And I think I'm going to do it anyway."

He was quiet for a moment. "Yeah," he said. "That sounds right."

"Does that scare you," she said. "The out-of-your-depth part."

"Not for you," he said. "You've been out of your depth before. You know how to swim in it."

She looked at him. He was looking at the view with the honest face, the April light finding the specific color of his hair — the full red of it, the color it was when nothing was filtering it.

"You're not worried about the loud and fast part," she said.

"I'm worried about the parts you don't tell me about," he said. "The ones you manage alone because you've decided they're too small to mention." He glanced at her. "That's the thing I lose sleep over."

She held that. She thought about the submission, kept for months before she told anyone. She thought about the science wing detours, maintained for a year before she let anyone close enough to know the calculation. She thought about the specific architecture of someone who had learned to carry everything alone and was actively, imperfectly, trying to unlearn it.

"I'm working on that," she said.

"I know," he said. "I'm not telling you to be different. I'm just telling you what I think about."

She looked at the view. Her house somewhere in the green. The school. The east side. The full geography of the only city she had ever lived in, laid out from above.

"What do you think happens," she said. "After I go."

He was quiet for a moment that was longer than his usual pauses. The water tower groaned. Below them, the city made its Saturday sounds.

"I think you go," he said, "and you become what you're going to become, and I'm here doing what I do." He looked at the sky, the late afternoon blue of it. "And I think we figure out what that means as it happens. Not before."

"That's very calm of you," she said.

"It's not calm," he said. "It's just — the alternative is spending what's left before you go being afraid of what comes after. And I'm not going to do that."

She held that for a long moment. She thought about the word what's left — the specific weight of it, the acknowledgment it contained that there was a fixed point and they both knew where it was and were choosing not to make it the center of everything.

She reached over and found his hand. His fingers closed around hers — she felt his knuckles without looking, the scabbing still present, not healed yet. She held it anyway. She always held it anyway.

"Donghyuck," she said.

"Yeah," he said.

"I'm not going to stop texting you," she said. "When I go. Whatever happens. You're still going to be the first thread at the top."

He was quiet.

"I know that doesn't solve the thing you're actually worried about," she said. "But I wanted to say it."

He turned to look at her. The full version of the honest face in the afternoon light. The mole near his nose she had never mentioned. The dark eyes with their depth. The version with nothing managing it, on a roof on a Saturday, with nobody watching.

"I know," he said. "I know you will."

She held his gaze. She thought about October — the first text, this is cheng's friend, the specific ordinary of a Tuesday night in October when she had been in her room with her laptop and her ranked queue and had received a text from a number she didn't know and had answered it.

Seven months. The full length of the school year she was about to finish.

She leaned over and kissed him on the side of his jaw — not the mouth, just his jaw, brief and specific, the way you kissed someone when words weren't the right container.

He made a sound that was not quite a word. His hand tightened around hers for a moment.

She looked back at the view.

He looked back at the view.

The water tower groaned. The city spread out below them in its specific geography. The April afternoon held its warmth without requiring anything from it.

They sat like that until the light changed.

When they went back downstairs Chenle was still at the window and turned to look at them with the expression he had been wearing since October whenever they came into a room together — the expression of someone who had been vindicated by events and was not going to let that go unremarked.

"The roof," he said, to no one in particular.

"Yes," Donghyuck said, in the voice he used for Chenle specifically, which was a register Solar had come to identify as fond and exhausted simultaneously.

"Together," Chenle said.

"Chenle," Donghyuck said.

"I'm just noting," Chenle said.

"You always just note things," Renjun observed, from the couch, without looking up.

"Someone has to," Chenle said, with dignity, and turned back to his window.

Solar sat on the couch. She picked up her book. She opened it to the page she'd been on, which she found without checking because she had been at the same page for approximately three weeks.

Donghyuck sat beside her. His shoulder against hers. His hand finding hers on the couch cushion between them, the scabbed knuckles and the warmth, both things simultaneously.

She read.

He looked at the window.

The apartment held them in its Saturday warmth, the April air coming through Chenle's open window, the east side running its program below, the roof above where they had just been, the view that was the shape of the city they both knew, the house with the tree in the front yard that he could pick out from three floors up.

She turned a page.

He didn't look away from the window.

Both of them exactly where they were supposed to be.


26: the dark red car

May arrived and the school changed.

Not dramatically — DMHS did not do dramatic. But there was a specific quality to the first week of May that she had been aware of since freshman year: the particular compression of a school year that could feel itself ending, the final stretch of things making themselves known. The hallways ran at a slightly higher frequency. The teachers whose patience had been quietly depleting since September were now running on empty, their instructions coming in the clipped registers of people who were managing a countdown. The students, for their part, were in the specific condition of people who were both exhausted and unable to stop — too close to the end to coast, too tired to pretend they weren't.

Finals were in three weeks.

Solar had two AP exams, three finals, and a history paper she had been accumulating notes for since March but had not yet assembled into a coherent argument. She had a lab report for chemistry that required her to make sense of a semester's worth of data collected with Soyeon's patient assistance. She had a Spanish oral exam she was quietly confident about and a math final she was quietly not confident about.

She was going to do all of this while the Kim side ran its pattern and Jihoon ran his position and Donghyuck came to the divergence point every morning and the east side held its breath.

This was, she understood, simply the shape of her life. The math final and the Maren expansion and his hand in hers — all of it simultaneously, all of it real, none of it canceling out the others.

She made a study schedule on a Sunday night in the first week of May. She put it on her wall. She looked at it and felt, for the first time since October, a very specific and very ordinary kind of anxiety that had nothing to do with DREAM or Jihoon — just the anxiety of someone who had a lot to do and a specific amount of time to do it in.

She found, sitting at her desk with the study schedule on the wall and the lamp on and the ranked queue minimized on her second monitor, that she had missed this feeling. The specific ordinary anxiety of a school year. The kind that resolved by doing rather than by managing.

She opened her history notes and started writing.

Monday of the second week of May, Jiwoo arrived at the corner of Elm and Fifth with two coffees and the expression of someone who had been making a document.

"Okay," Jiwoo announced, handing over the cup. "I have made a study plan."

Solar looked at her. "For you or for me."

"For both of us," Jiwoo said. "It's coordinated. We cover the same material at the same times so we can call each other when we get stuck."

"When did you make this."

"Last night," Jiwoo said. "I couldn't sleep. I organized instead." She pulled out her phone and showed Solar a color-coded spreadsheet with columns for subject, date, duration, and something labeled emergency backup plan.

"Jiwoo," Solar said.

"It's good," Jiwoo said. "The color coding is intuitive."

"It's very good," Solar said. "It's also possibly the most Jiwoo thing I've ever seen."

"The emergency backup plan column is just in case we get behind," Jiwoo said.

"What's the emergency backup plan."

"Pull an all-nighter on the Wednesday before finals and drink an inadvisable amount of coffee," Jiwoo said. "But written down it sounds more like a plan."

Solar looked at the spreadsheet. She thought about the specific quality of three years of friendship with Jiwoo and understood that this spreadsheet was one of the most affectionate things Jiwoo had ever done for her, because it was Jiwoo at her most Jiwoo — organized and combustible and entirely on Solar's side.

"Thank you," she said.

"Don't thank me," Jiwoo said. "Just follow the plan."

"With the color coding," Solar said.

"With the color coding," Jiwoo agreed.

They walked to school in the May morning, warm enough now that neither of them needed jackets. The air had the specific quality of a city that had stopped arguing about the season and simply arrived in it.

"How are things," Jiwoo asked. The register she always used for the question that meant more than its words.

"Good," Solar said. "The Kim side has been quiet this week."

"That's not what I was asking about," Jiwoo said.

Solar held her coffee. "The roof was Saturday," she said.

Jiwoo was quiet for a moment. "The roof," she repeated.

"He took me up," Solar said. "Above the apartment. He goes up there to think. Or not think. We sat there for a while."

"And?" Jiwoo prompted.

Solar thought about the jaw kiss. The view. Since I was nine. The piano. What's left.

"He told me he plays piano," she said. "Since he was nine. He doesn't play for people."

Jiwoo was quiet.

"He said he's careful about what he shows the version of himself that has nothing on top of it," Solar said.

Jiwoo walked for half a block in silence. Then: "Sol."

"Yeah," Solar said.

"You understand what he's telling you when he says that."

"Yes," Solar said. "I know what he's telling me."

"Okay," Jiwoo said, softly. "Okay."

They reached the school. The May morning arranged itself around them.

"I'm going to miss you," Jiwoo said. The same thing she had said in October, matter-of-fact, without performance. "When you go."

"I know," Solar said. "I'm going to miss you too. So much."

Jiwoo nodded once. "But you're going," she said.

"I'm going," Solar confirmed.

"Good," Jiwoo said. And that was that.

Wednesday evening, the text came from Cheng.

She was at her desk with her history notes, the lamp on, the study schedule with Tuesday's blocks completed and Wednesday's half-done. She had been working through the Reconstruction argument with the specific focus of someone who had found the thread of it and was following it without losing it.

Her phone buzzed.

Cheng: you free to talk

yes, she typed. call me.

He called in thirty seconds.

"The Kim side made contact with Jihoon again," Cheng said, without preamble. The flat register. "Yesterday. We found out today."

"What kind of contact," she said.

A pause. "Jihoon gave them something new," he said. "Not information this time. Something more specific." Another pause. "He gave them a pattern."

"My pattern," she said.

"Your route," Cheng said. "To and from school. The times. The specific streets. He's been tracking it. For how long we don't know."

She held the phone. She thought about the last three years of routes — the science wing detours, the side exits, the specific choreography of avoidance she had developed and then modified as the threat shifted. The specific vulnerability of a person whose avoidance had become its own kind of pattern.

"He's given them a map," she said.

"Yes," Cheng said.

"What does the Kim side do with a map," she said.

The longest pause he had given her in months. "They use it," he said. "When they've decided the message needs to be delivered directly rather than through channels."

She understood. Not through Bora, not through Yuna, not through the school's information network. Directly. To her. On a street she walked at a predictable time.

"When," she said.

"We don't know yet," Cheng said. "Hyuck is working on that."

"He knows," she said.

"He knows everything I just told you," Cheng said. "He asked me to tell you tonight. He didn't want you to find out tomorrow."

She held the phone. She thought about tell me when things happen. I don't want to find out after. He was telling her now, through Cheng's voice, at eight forty-seven on a Wednesday.

"Okay," she said.

"Sol," Cheng said.

"I'm okay," she said. "I'm processing."

"I know," he said. "I'm going to tell you one more thing."

She waited.

"Hyuck wants to pick you up," Cheng said. "Morning and afternoon. Different routes every day — he'll vary them so there's no pattern to track. He's not going to tell you the routes in advance. Some days he will. Some days he won't."

She held that. The dark red car. The October rain. The enclosed space that removed the distance management strategy she had been running since the first week of school. Now repurposed as the thing that got her off predictable streets entirely — not adjusting the pattern but removing her from it altogether.

"That's going to be visible," she said. "At school."

"Yes," Cheng said.

"DMHS is going to update its assessment," she said.

"Yes," Cheng said. "He knows."

She was quiet for a moment. She thought about what it meant — Haechan's car at the front entrance every morning, Solar getting out of it, the school processing that image.

She had stopped caring what the social physics did with anything a while ago.

"Okay," she said. "Tell him okay."

"Tell him yourself," Cheng said, and hung up.

She opened the thread immediately.

Cheng told me, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: I wanted you to know tonight

I know, she typed. thank you.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: I'm going to pick you up

Haechannie🐭: starting tomorrow

Haechannie🐭: morning and afternoon

Haechannie🐭: is that okay

She looked at the screen. She thought about the rain drive — the first time the distance management strategy had failed because the car removed it. She thought about everything that had happened since that October night. The same car, now, at her door.

yes, she typed. it's okay.

Haechannie🐭: okay

Haechannie🐭: I'll be at your door at seven forty-five

Haechannie🐭: I'll vary the routes

Haechannie🐭: some days I'll tell you

Haechannie🐭: some days I won't

She understood. The pattern was the vulnerability. The car eliminated the pattern. Jihoon had given the Kim side a map. Donghyuck was rendering the map obsolete.

okay, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: go finish your studying

how did you know I was studying

Haechannie🐭: because it's finals season and you are you

Haechannie🐭: go

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She put the phone down. She looked at the study schedule on the wall. She opened her history document, found the thread of the argument where she'd left it, and wrote for another hour and a half.

She submitted the paper at ten-twelve.

She set her alarm for seven-thirty and fell asleep with the specific ordinary tiredness of someone who had done a full day's work.

Thursday morning at seven forty-four, the dark red car was at her door.

She had seen it once before — the October rain, the heat coming on, the specific enclosed quality of a space that changed things without either of them acknowledging what it was changing. She had thought about that drive more times than she had intended to.

Now it was at her door. In the May morning. Deliberately.

She came down the front steps. Through the windshield she could see him reach across and open the passenger door from inside — not getting out, just the arm across, the door swinging open — and she got in and pulled it closed.

The car had the same quality it always had: warm, specific, the chain at his collar catching the morning light, the red hair in the particular way it looked before the school day had its chance at it.

"Hey," he said.

"Hey," she said.

He pulled away from the curb. The route was not her usual — she registered the turns immediately, different streets, the same general direction through blocks she used occasionally rather than daily. She tracked it without commenting.

"Different every day," he said.

"I know," she said.

The car moved through the May morning. She watched the city do its warm-weather thing outside the window and thought about October — the rain, the dark, the seven blocks she had called a twelve-minute walk. The same car. The same enclosed space. Everything else different.

"He's going to know the pattern changed," she said.

"Yes," Donghyuck said. "That's the message."

She understood. Not just that the pattern was unpredictable — that it was unpredictable because of him. Because DREAM had made a visible and deliberate statement about Solar's position relative to theirs. Jihoon's map was now useless and the reason it was useless was standing in the east side's social grammar as clearly as a flag on a corner.

He pulled up to the school's front entrance.

The effect was immediate.

She felt it before she got out — the specific recalibration of the social ecosystem, the thing she had been feeling all year in increments. But this was not an increment. This was the dark red car at the front entrance where the main crowd arrived, where the students who had been processing Haechan-and-Solar since October were now processing something they had no ambiguity about. Not the side entrance. Not the parking lot. The front.

Students on the path stopped their conversations mid-sentence. She saw it happen — the specific quality of attention shifting, heads turning, the school's social machinery engaging with something that had just updated the entire picture in a single image.

Donghyuck was not at the east entrance. The east entrance, where he was every morning, where the girls orbited, where DMHS had understood him to exist in the school's geography — was empty. He was here. At the front. In a car. With Solar Lee in the passenger seat.

She looked at him. He was looking at the school with the expression of someone who had made a decision and was not revisiting it. The performing ease was present — it was always present when DMHS was watching — but underneath it, clearly, the other version. The one that had made a choice.

"Later," she said.

He looked at her. "Later," he said.

She got out. She closed the door. She walked through the front entrance with her chin level and her composure exactly where it had always been, and she felt the school's attention the way she felt weather — present, noted, not requiring a response.

The whispers started before she reached the first hallway.

Not hostile — the hostility had depleted itself. This was something different: the specific register of a school that had been watching something build for eight months and had just received confirmation of what it had been building toward. Not cruelty. Just the machinery of DMHS doing what it always did — processing, categorizing, updating.

She heard Haechan and Solar in the same breath three times before she reached her locker. She heard that's his car once from someone who apparently knew what his car looked like, which meant more people had been paying attention to more details than she had tracked. She heard one senior girl say to another, in a register that was not hostile but was very much assessing: she got out of the front entrance. Like he wanted everyone to see.

Solar opened her locker. She looked at her reflection in the small mirror inside.

She looked like herself. She had always looked like herself.

She closed her locker and went to first period and the school day arranged itself around the new information the way school days did — absorbing it, redistributing it, making it the current context through which everything else was interpreted.

By lunch the consensus had formed.

Jiwoo delivered it at the corner table with the precision she brought to social analysis: "The word being used is official. Capital O. I have heard it from four different people in three different social circles."

"Official," Solar said.

"As far as DMHS is concerned," Jiwoo said, "you arrived in his car at the front entrance where everyone could see, which means he wanted everyone to see, which means it's deliberate, which means it's official." She ate a bite of her lunch. "Bora has updated her position."

"What's her position," Solar said.

"Strategic neutrality," Jiwoo said. "Which from Bora is a white flag." She paused. "The girls who usually crowd the east entrance were apparently confused this morning when he wasn't there."

"He was at my door at seven forty-five," Solar said.

Jiwoo looked at her with the expression of someone who had been told a fact that confirmed every assessment she had made. "He was at your door," she said.

"Yes," Solar said.

Jiwoo set her fork down. "Solar Lee," she said, in the register she used for things that required the full name.

"Jiwoo," Solar said.

"He came to your house," Jiwoo said. "And drove you to school. In front of everyone."

"Yes," Solar said. "That's the point."

"The point being," Jiwoo said, "that Jihoon's map is useless and the reason it's useless is—"

"Yes," Solar said.

Jiwoo picked her fork back up. She was quiet for a moment with the quality of someone who had several things to say and was choosing the right one. "How did it feel," she said finally. "Getting out of that car."

Solar considered. She thought about the front entrance, the heads turning, the whispers, the like he wanted everyone to see. She thought about walking through the front doors with her chin level and her composure where it always was.

"Ordinary," she said. "It felt ordinary."

Jiwoo looked at her.

"Not ordinary like it didn't matter," Solar said. "Ordinary like it was just — true. Like I was just walking into school. Like it was just what was happening." She held her coffee. "I didn't have to perform anything."

Jiwoo held that for a long moment. Then she nodded once — not the strategic nod, the real one, the one from the first lunch. "That's the whole thing, isn't it," she said softly. "That's the whole thing."

"Yes," Solar said.

They ate their lunch. The cafeteria ran its May program around them — the end-of-year energy, the exhausted restlessness, the specific social atmosphere of a school that had just received a significant update to its ongoing picture and was still processing it.

At the DREAM table, Mark was eating. Jeno was talking to Renjun. Donghyuck was not there, which was usual — he was somewhere in the building, doing whatever the school day required of him, existing in the DMHS social physics as Haechan with the performing ease fully engaged.

She had seen him twenty minutes ago in the car. She would see him at the front entrance after school.

Both things were true.
