After school the dark red car was at the front entrance again.

The afternoon crowd processed this with the same energy as the morning — the update running again, this time with the additional information that it was not a one-time occurrence. He was picking her up. In front of everyone. Again.

She got in. The door closed. The car moved.

"History paper," she said.

"How'd it go," he said.

"Good," she said. "I found the argument."

"What's the argument," he said.

She told him. He listened with the quality he brought to things she was interested in — genuinely, following the thread, asking the right question at the right point. She talked through it for most of the drive home and he said yeah in the places where the logic landed and wait, why in the places where it needed more, and she answered the wait, whys and the argument became clearer in the explaining of it.

He pulled up to her door.

"That's a good argument," he said.

"I know," she said. "I just needed to say it out loud."

He looked at her. The honest face in the afternoon light. "You can say things out loud to me," he said. "Whenever."

"I know," she said. "I'm learning to."

She got out. She went inside.

She stood in the hallway and took off her jacket and stood still for a moment in the ordinary quiet of the house.

The May afternoon held its warmth outside the window.

She was almost ready.

She did not know this yet.

But she was almost ready.

27: wrong argument

The week before finals had its own specific quality — not the anxiety of the approach but the strange stillness of the final days, when the studying had been done and the papers submitted and all that remained was the execution. She had followed Jiwoo's color-coded plan with the discipline of someone who had decided to trust the plan, and the plan had worked, and by Thursday of the last week of school she had done everything she could do and was waiting for the exams to arrive.

This produced a particular kind of freedom. Not the freedom of having no obligations, but the freedom of having completed the ones that could be completed. She could not move toward the remaining ones any faster. She could only exist in the days between.

She was getting better at existing in the days between.

Thursday morning the car was at her door at seven forty-five.

He had a playlist that ran in the mornings — not curated, the shuffle quality of someone who had been adding things over a long time without organizing them. She had learned it over six days: the bass line that started most Thursdays for some reason, the almost-classical thing she had heard twice, the song she recognized from somewhere she couldn't place. She had not asked about it. He had not explained it. It just ran.

Today it was something she had never heard, slow and unhurried, the kind of song that made the city outside the window look like it was being filmed.

She watched the route. Different from yesterday — he went south before going east, threading through blocks she was still building into her internal map. She tracked the turns with the same attention she brought to all his routes now, the map in her head growing incrementally more complete each day.

"Last exam is next Friday," she said.

"Then summer," he said.

"Then summer," she confirmed.

He glanced at her briefly. "You have plans."

She thought about the submission in its silence. About the ranked queue. About the specific shape of a summer that existed in the gap between the year ending and whatever came next.

"Practice," she said. "Songs and dance. The audition component, whenever the callback comes." She looked at the window. "And I want to push toward Top 500. I have the summer."

He was quiet for a moment. She felt the thing he wasn't saying — the I told you that had been building since October, the one he used with the specific restraint of someone who knew he was right and didn't require her to confirm it.

"Don't," she said.

"I haven't said anything," he said.

"You were about to think it loudly," she said.

"I'm always thinking it loudly," he said. "You've never asked me to stop."

She held her coffee. "Fair," she said.

He pulled up to the front entrance. She reached for the door handle.

"Sol," he said.

She looked at him.

"Wait for me," he said. "I'll park and come around."

She held the door half-open. The morning was doing its May thing outside — warm, the school in its finals-week register, students moving through the front path with the specific exhausted urgency of people who were almost finished. She thought about the first time he had dropped her here, six days ago, and the school updating its picture in real time. She thought about what wait for me meant as a public statement on top of everything the car had already said.

She thought: yes.

She got out. She stood on the path. She waited.

It took him four minutes to park and come around the building. She saw him before he saw her — the jacket, the chain, the performing ease not yet fully engaged, the morning version of him moving through the school's front approach with the specific quality of someone who had not yet become Haechan for the day.

He saw her and stopped. A very brief stop — half a step — and then he kept walking, and he was carrying something in his expression that she had come to recognize as the third version: not performing, not operational. Just his.

He stopped beside her.

"You waited," he said.

"You asked me to," she said.

"I didn't know if you would," he said.

She looked at him. "You can ask me things," she said. "I'll tell you if the answer is no."

Something moved through his expression. He nodded — not the small operational nod, the other one, the one that meant received.

They walked through the front entrance together.

DMHS processed this with the specific attention it had been giving everything related to them since Thursday of the previous week. She felt it in the peripheral awareness she had developed for it — the specific recalibration of a social ecosystem noting the latest update to an ongoing picture. Not hostile. Just attentive.

She had stopped caring what attentive meant a while ago.

They diverged at the first hallway junction — his class was east, hers was north — and he said later in the easy register and she said later back and went to first period and the school day arranged itself around what had just been added to its ongoing archive.

She sat her AP History exam at eight-thirty.

Three hours. She went in with the Reconstruction argument she had been building since March and the specific confidence of someone who has done the work and knows they have done the work. She wrote for two and a half hours, used the remaining time to check what she had written, found it was what she had intended.

She came out at eleven-thirty into the May sunshine and found Jiwoo on the front steps with two gas station coffees and a specific expression.

"The second essay," Jiwoo said, immediately.

"The infrastructure argument," Solar said.

"I argued the political will," Jiwoo said.

Solar looked at her. "That's the wrong argument."

"I know that now," Jiwoo said, with the dignity of someone who had already processed their grief and arrived at acceptance. "I committed forty minutes in."

"Did you recover," Solar said.

"Stylistically," Jiwoo said. "I built a very elegant case for the incorrect position."

Solar accepted her coffee. "You'll be fine."

"I know I'll be fine," Jiwoo said. "I'm mourning the argument I didn't make."

They sat on the front steps in the sunshine, talking about the exam with the specific combination of relief and retrospective anxiety that always followed, and the May air was warm and the school year was almost over and Solar let herself simply be in it.

After a while Jiwoo looked at her with the expression she used when she had been sitting on something.

"The Kim side has been quiet for over a week," Jiwoo said. Flat. Observational.

Solar looked at her.

"And Jihoon has been very quiet," Jiwoo said. "That's not patience. That's preparation." She held her coffee. "I just wanted to say it. Out loud. To you."

Solar looked at the sunshine on the steps. She thought about the map Jihoon had given them, now useless. She thought about the seventy-two-hour intervals and the weeks since the last probe.

"I know," she said.

"Does Donghyuck know," Jiwoo said.

"Yes," Solar said.

Jiwoo nodded. She looked at the steps. "I wanted to say it in case today feels like the last ordinary one," she said. "So you'd know I was paying attention."

Solar held that. She looked at Jiwoo — three years of this, the first lunch and the cracker and the graph and everything that had followed — and understood that this was the most Jiwoo thing she could have done: not perform concern, not manage Solar's reaction to the information, just say the thing plainly and let it be received.

"I know you're paying attention," Solar said.

"I know you know," Jiwoo said. "I said it anyway."

The school day continued. Solar sat her Spanish oral in the afternoon, which went well. She came out into the late afternoon with two exams behind her and the May sunshine gold on the front path.

The car was at the front entrance.

She got in. The door closed. He looked at her.

"History and Spanish," she said. "Both fine."

"Math tomorrow," he said.

"I know," she said.

He pulled away. Today's route went west first — new streets, the continuing map. She watched them pass and thought about the exam and Jiwoo on the steps and the last ordinary day and whether today was one.

"The year is ending," she said. Not dramatically — just placing the thought somewhere outside her head.

He looked at her briefly.

"This specific version of things," she said. "The car, the divergence point, Saturdays at the apartment. It's all going to change shape when summer starts." She looked at the window. "I've been aware of it all day."

He was quiet for a moment. "It doesn't disappear," he said. "It changes shape."

"I know," she said. "I'm not worried about it. I just—" she found the right word. "I'm paying attention to it. The way Jiwoo said."

He nodded. "Yeah," he said. "Me too."

They drove through the May afternoon in the comfortable register that the car had developed over six days — not silence, not conversation, something between them that was its own specific warmth. The playlist was still running, something she had heard before that she still couldn't place.

"The piano," she said.

He looked at her.

"Tell me about it," she said. "You said you'd play for me sometime. I'm not asking for that yet. Just tell me about it."

He was quiet for a moment — the thinking kind, taking the question seriously.

"I started when I was nine," he said. "My mother's idea. She had a very specific picture of what a son who played piano looked like." He looked at the road. "I was terrible at it for two years. The teacher had a method I couldn't follow — kept reading the music wrong, kept getting corrected. It felt like—" he paused. "Like trying to speak in a language that had no words for what I wanted to say."

"When did that change," she said.

"The teacher gave up on the sheet music," he said. "Let me listen instead and find it myself. Turned out I could do that — hear something and find where it went." He glanced at her. "I played for three hours that first day. My mother came to find me and I hadn't noticed the time."

She held that. Him at eleven — before DREAM, before the east side — finding something that asked nothing from him except his ears.

"Do you still play like that," she said. "By ear."

"Yes," he said. "I've never learned to read it properly."

"That's how you are with everything," she said. "You hear things and you find where they go."

He looked at her sideways with the expression of someone being correctly perceived.

"Don't make it poetic," he said.

"It is poetic," she said.

"It's just how I work," he said.

"That's what poetic means," she said.

He pulled up to her door. He put the car in park. He looked at the house for a moment — her house, the one he had been coming to every morning for six days, the one with the tree in the front yard that he could pick out from the roof of the Cha's building three floors up.

He looked at her.

She looked at him.

She reached over and put her hand on his jaw — the same thing she had done in the library, the palm against the jaw, the specific warmth of it — and kissed him once, brief and deliberate, in the afternoon light in the car in front of her house.

She pulled back. She looked at him. The honest face, all the layers gone.

"Hi," she said.

"Hi," he said.

She held his face for one more moment — the mole near his nose, the dark eyes, the red hair catching the gold of the afternoon. The full picture, in the car, at her door.

Then she let go. She got out. She closed the door.

She stood on the porch and watched the car pull away and then went inside and stood in the hallway in the ordinary quiet of the house — refrigerator hum, her mother's television, the specific sounds of the only home she had ever had.

She went upstairs. She put her headset on. She queued for ranked and locked Kiriko and played with the loose ease of someone carrying something good into the evening.

She won four straight.

At ten-fifteen his text came.

Haechannie🐭: hey

hey, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: the car

yeah

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: I keep thinking about it

me too, she typed. all evening.

Haechannie🐭: good

A pause.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: whatever happens

Haechannie🐭: you know where I am

She held the phone. She thought about the piano at eleven years old, three hours without noticing. She thought about him finding where things went. She thought about the car at her door every morning, the map she had been building, the year changing shape.

I know where you are, she typed. goodnight donghyuck.

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

She locked her screen. She put the phone down on her chest. The barely-visible stars on the ceiling. The May dark outside the window. The ordinary sounds of the house.

She thought: whatever happens.

She thought: I know where he is.

She fell asleep in the last ordinary night before the thing that was coming arrived, and she did not know it was the last one, and she held it as if it might be, and that was enough.


28: the moon doesn't scream

She sat her math final on Friday afternoon.

It went better than expected and worse than hoped, which was approximately the outcome Jiwoo's color-coded plan had been preparing her for since May began. She came out of the exam room at three-fifteen into the May sunshine with the specific feeling of someone who had done what they could and was done with the question of whether it was enough.

The school year was over.

She stood on the front steps for a moment and let that land. Two years of DMHS behind her. The incident and the fourteen months and October and everything since. She had survived sophomore year in both senses — academically and otherwise — and the May sunshine was warm on her face and the dark red car was at the front entrance.

She went down the steps. She got in.

He looked at her.

"Done," she said.

"How was it."

"Survivable," she said.

He pulled away. She looked at his profile in the afternoon light — the red hair, the chain, the specific quality of someone who had been waiting for her and was not going to say so. She thought about the year. About all the versions of this drive since October — the rain drive, the December drives, the route-varying May drives. The car had become its own kind of room. She was comfortable in it the way she was comfortable in very few spaces.

"East side?" she said.

"East side," he confirmed.

The apartment was warm in the full May way, the windows open, the east side's evening air coming through freely. The school year being over had a specific quality in the apartment too — the members slightly looser than a weeknight, the operational register running but not foregrounded, the ease of people at the end of a week who did not have to be anywhere for a few hours.

Renjun was on the couch with his laptop open but not in documentation mode — watching something, which was unusual enough that Chenle had migrated from the window to look over his shoulder with dedicated attention. Mark was at the table with Jeno, talking in the low register they used for things with a practical dimension. Jisung had come out of the kitchen for once and was leaning against the wall near the hallway with his coffee, looking at his phone.

She sat on the couch. Donghyuck sat beside her.

Not the shoulder-adjacent position. Closer than that — his shoulder properly against hers, and then, after a moment, with the unhurried quality of someone who had decided something and was not making an event of it, he shifted and put his head on her shoulder.

She looked down at him.

He was looking at her book.

"What page are you on," he said.

"Forty-seven," she said.

"You've been on forty-seven since March," he said.

"I've been busy," she said.

"Read," he said.

She looked at the page. The weight of his head on her shoulder was specific and warm — not heavy, just present, the gravity of someone who had decided this was where they wanted to be.

Chenle looked over from Renjun's screen and saw them. He made an expression at Renjun. Renjun, without looking up, said: "Don't."

"I wasn't going to say anything," Chenle said.

"I know what you were going to do," Renjun said.

"The face," Chenle said, "is not a statement."

"The face," Renjun said, "is absolutely a statement."

Donghyuck did not move his head. "Chenle," he said.

"I'm watching Renjun's video," Chenle said, with the dignity of someone establishing an alibi.

She read another sentence. She read another. The apartment ran its Friday evening around her — the warmth, the open window, Jeno's voice low at the table, the east side outside doing what the east side did at seven on a Friday in May.

After a while Mark looked up from the table. "Jihoon's throwing tonight," he said. To the room, but to Donghyuck specifically.

The quality of the room adjusted — not dramatically, just the specific recalibration that happened whenever Jihoon's name entered a space.

"I know," Donghyuck said. He had not moved his head.

"We're going at ten," Mark said. "You and Jeno."

"Yeah," Donghyuck said.

She held her book. She thought about Jihoon's parties — the specific function they served as nodes in DREAM's network, the reason DREAM tolerated his existence despite the history, the operational logic of it. They went because the parties were infrastructure. They hated him and they went anyway.

"What time do you need to be there," she said.

"Ten," he said. "We have a few hours."

She turned the page. His head was still on her shoulder. The weight of it. The specific warmth of someone who had decided this was where they wanted to be on the last day of the school year, on a Friday evening with two hours before he had to be somewhere.

She read.

He read with her.

At eight-forty-five an urgent conversation started at the table.

She felt it begin before she could identify the content — the specific shift in the room's register, Mark's voice dropping lower, Jisung coming off the wall and setting his coffee down, Jeno pulling his chair in. She caught fragments as the room reorganized itself: Jihoon's name again, the party, tonight, nine-thirty, the usual arrangement. Renjun pulling up something on his laptop. The word confirmation from Jisung, and then a number she didn't catch, and then Mark saying something about a contact at the venue that needed verifying before ten.

The Jihoon party. The node. The operational texture of tolerating something you couldn't afford not to tolerate.

Donghyuck lifted his head from her shoulder.

She looked at him. He was already tracking the table, reading the room with the speed she had watched him read rooms for eight months.

"I'll be a few minutes," he said.

"Go," she said.

She closed her book. She looked at the room — all of them at the table now, the DREAM operational register fully engaged, the warmth of the Friday evening set aside for however long this took.

She was not required. She was not excluded. She was simply not part of this particular configuration, and she had learned the specific grace of that — of knowing when to step out.

"I'm going to get some air," she said. To Donghyuck specifically. She held his gaze and let him see that she was fine, that this was her choice.

He looked at her for one moment — the fast inventory, the check. She held herself steady under it.

He nodded once.

She picked up her jacket and went to the door.

The May night outside the Cha's building was the warmest it had been.

She stood on the front step for a moment and breathed it in — the east side at eight-forty-five on a Friday, the specific quality of it at this hour. The streetlights doing their amber work on the pavement. The dry cleaner closed, its back light off. The street relatively quiet, the Friday energy still gathering.

She went down the steps and stood on the pavement near the dark red car. Not going anywhere — just outside, just breathing, the warm air on her face after the apartment's interior.

She thought about the school year ending. About the specific weight of a year that had begun with the incident's aftermath still fresh and had arrived here — at the east side at night, outside a building she knew by its second-step creak and its old wood smell, standing near a car that came to her door every morning.

She had come a long way from the science wing detours.

She was still thinking this when she heard the footsteps.

Not from the building. From the street — coming from the north end of Maren, the direction of Third, the blocks the Kim side had been probing since April. The footsteps had a specific quality: deliberate, unhurried, the pace of someone going exactly where they meant to go.

She turned.

He was not someone she recognized from any previous encounter. Younger than she expected — late twenties, built like someone who had been doing physical work for a long time. Dark jacket. Hands visible, both of them, which meant he was choosing to show they were empty, which meant he understood that empty hands still communicated things.

He stopped six feet from her.

He looked at her with the flat assessment quality she had learned to recognize as the operational world's specific coldness — not the coldness of cruelty exactly, more the coldness of someone for whom she was a variable rather than a person.

"Solar Lee," he said. The flat register of someone confirming a fact.

She held herself level. "Yes," she said.

He reached into his jacket — slowly, deliberately, showing her the gesture before completing it. What he produced was not a weapon. It was a phone. He held the screen toward her — a video playing, the footage she had known existed for three years, the specific night and the specific party and the specific proof of what Jihoon had made her do when she was fourteen years old.

He let it play for a few seconds and pocketed the phone.

"The Kim side has been patient," he said. "DREAM has been given opportunities to respond to a reasonable request. Three blocks of Maren Street. A building on the corner of Third. That's all that's being asked." He took a step toward her. "The footage goes out otherwise. Widely. With full context — including who Lee Haechan is and what he does." Another step. He was close enough now that she could see the specific quality of his eyes — not cruel, which would have been easier. Just cold. Operational. The specific coldness of someone who had been told to deliver a message and was delivering it, who had no particular feeling about her either way, who was a mechanism.

"It's unfortunate," he said, quietly, "that you've made yourself part of this. Haechan's girl." He said it with the specific contempt of someone using a category to reduce a person. "If you'd stayed away from the east side this would have nothing to do with you."

He reached out.

Not the grammar of establishing proximity this time. His hand closed hard around her upper arm and yanked — not far, just enough, the specific language of someone demonstrating that he could, that the distance between them was his to control. She stumbled forward a half-step and his grip shifted, fingers digging in, and he was very close now, close enough that she could smell cigarette smoke and something chemical underneath it, close enough that when he spoke his voice was barely above a murmur and she heard every word of it with perfect clarity.

"You're going to pass the message," he said. "Or I'm going to put the footage everywhere your name is going to be in the next few years. Every platform. Every outlet. Every place that's going to matter when you're trying to be something." His grip tightened. "And then I'm going to make sure the people who care about Haechan understand exactly what kind of girl he's been spending his time on." He let that sit for a second. "Or you can just tell him to give us the three blocks. That's all this is."

His free hand came up.

It found her jaw — not a slap, not violence yet, just the flat of his palm against her face turning it toward him, forcing her to look at him directly, the specific degradation of being handled like an object. "Look at me when I'm talking to you," he said. "I want you to understand that this is a courtesy. The next person they send won't have a message."

She held herself completely still.

She was aware of everything simultaneously — the grip on her arm, the hand on her jaw, the six feet to the building door, the amber light, the dog still barking somewhere on a different street, the May night continuing its indifferent program around the specific geography of what was happening to her. She was aware that her heart was beating very fast and that her breathing was controlled and that she had not made a sound and was not going to make a sound.

"It doesn't matter," she musters out, entirely relaxed, like none of this was a big deal, "that doesn't scare me."

For a moment, the look in his eyes was almost something like that of pity, as if this was sad to him, like he felt so bad for a girl so caught up in her own beliefs. For he believed that women should always be afraid, that they never had a right to act like they could be fearless. They must always fear, must always be convinced that they will never be able to stand up for themselves. 

He leaned closer, and closer, she realized then, that something was happening she did not allow her mind to name. She turned her head to the side, only for him to angrily snap her face to look back at him. His other hand went from her arm to her waist, trailing down her body, fingers edging the hem of her shirt.

Still, Solar was as calm as she had ever been. She was so calm, that she realized then, nothing was happening. She did not hear footsteps, did not hear the creak of the rusted apartment door. It was utter silence, just the night and everything that lived in it. The moon casted a brief, yellow-toned light over the area, but it did not call out for help.

This meant, in reality, that no one had come yet. Did she need saving? She asked herself, she wanted to believe she was brave, that she could always handle everything herself. The silence meant strictly that Haechan did not know, that he was too engrossed in that conversation.

She exhaled once, and held his gaze. She did not pull away from his hand on her face. She let him believe he had her completely.

"Let go," she said. Flat. Direct. The level register she had been developing since fifteen and which had survived two and a half years of Jihoon and the incident and the survival years and October and everything since.

He laughed — a short, contemptuous sound. His hand went under her shirt, his skin was rough against the soft of her stomach. She had an urge, a complete and utter urge to vomit. It was escalating, quickly, into something that started as mere intimidation and was now becoming something that she would never quite be able to wash off. Her jaw ached from the grip, it was something that would leave more than a sly mark.

"You don't give orders here," he said. "You're—"

She heard the door.

She did not look. She did not need to look. She knew the sound of that door — the specific hinges, the specific weight of it, the way it opened and caught for half a second before swinging fully, differently from every other door she had learned. She had been learning that building for eight months.

She felt rather than heard him behind her.

The man's eyes moved from her face to something over her shoulder.

His expression changed.

Not fear — she registered this specifically, the way she registered most things. He was not a person who frightened easily, she understood that from the operational quality of him, from the way he had crossed the street and delivered his message and put his hands on her without hesitation. But something in him updated. The flat assessment running a new variable and arriving at a different output. The specific calculation of someone who had just understood what was standing behind the person he was holding.

His grip on her jaw loosened, his hand slid out from under her shirt. Not because he was retreating. Because something behind her had changed the room the way Donghyuck changed rooms — before anyone fully understood why, before any explicit action had been taken, the simple fact of his presence doing what it always did.

She held herself completely still.

"Sol," Donghyuck mumbles, low toned.

Quietly. Directly behind her. The same register as always — not raised, not performing anything. Just present. The way he was always present.

She knew what he was asking.

Not asking if she was okay. Not asking what had happened. He had come through the door and he had seen it — the grip, the hand on her face, the man's thumb pressing into her arm. He knew.

He was asking if she wanted him to handle it.

She thought about three years of that footage existing somewhere. She thought about widely, with full context. She thought about his name attached to it and what it would do to him, to DREAM, to the specific world he had built in the east side that Jihoon had been trying to use her to dismantle. She thought about the hand on her face and the thumb pressing into her arm and the specific contempt of Haechan's girl and the voice saying you don't give orders here.

She thought about you didn't do anything. / You didn't ask.

She was going to carry whatever came next. She had always known she was going to carry it. That was the deal she had made with herself somewhere between October and May, somewhere between the cigarette in the pocket and the scabbed knuckles and his head on her shoulder and the page she was on.

"Handle it," she said.

She stepped to the side.

Donghyuck moved.

Not forward first — sideways, two steps that repositioned him between her and the man before the man had fully processed that she had moved, the geometry of the alley changing so fast that the man's instinct was to track Solar and by the time he corrected, Donghyuck was already inside his reach.

The first thing Donghyuck did was take the man's wrist — the hand that had been on her face — and bend it back with a specific precise force that produced a sound she felt in her back teeth and a sound from the man that was not a word. The man's free hand came up immediately and Donghyuck caught it with his other hand, not blocking, redirecting, using the man's own momentum to turn him and drive him backward into the alley wall. The impact was hard enough that the man's head snapped back against the brick and he lost a second — not long, barely a second — and in that second Donghyuck's elbow connected with the man's jaw in a short tight arc that had no wasted motion in it at all.

The man did not go down. She had expected him to go down and he did not.

He shook his head once, fast, and came back swinging — a wide hook that would have connected badly if it had landed. It did not land. Donghyuck was not where it was aimed. He was to the left, close, and he hit the man twice in the ribs with the flat economy of someone who knew exactly where it would do the most work. She heard the man's breath leave him. She heard the specific sound of ribs taking that kind of force. The man bent forward instinctively and Donghyuck's knee came up and met him on the way down.

She did not look away.

She had told herself she would not watch. She understood now that she had known all along she would watch. This was hers. She had said the word. She was going to see every second of what the word had set in motion.

The man straightened again — she had to give him that, he straightened again, blood on his face now, the operational coldness replaced by something rawer, the mechanism becoming a person in pain who was angry about it. He reached into his jacket.

Everything that happened in the next four seconds she would carry for the rest of her life in the specific granular detail of moments that branded themselves onto memory.

Donghyuck's hand caught the man's wrist before whatever was in the jacket cleared the fabric. He drove the man's arm sideways and down, against his own knee, and the sound was the same sound as the wrist had been, louder, and whatever had been in the jacket fell on the pavement and skittered away into the dark, and the man made a sound that was part rage and part something that would become pain later when the adrenaline cleared.

He was on one knee now.

Donghyuck let go of the wrist.

She had expected him to step back. He did not step back.

He crouched down to the man's level and he said something — very quietly, directly into the man's face, close enough that she could not hear the words from where she was standing. She saw the man's expression change as he heard it. The rawness draining out of it. The operational cold returning, briefly, as a reflex — and then something underneath that, something that was neither cold nor raw, something closer to understanding what was actually happening and how it was going to end.

The man said something back. One word, maybe two.

Donghyuck straightened.

What happened next was not loud. It was not cinematic. It was very fast and very deliberate and when it was done the man was on the ground and not moving and the alley was quiet in the specific way that alleys were quiet when something had reached its conclusion.

The May night continued around it. The dog somewhere still doing its survey. The dry cleaner's sign still humming. The amber streetlight still working.

She stood near the dark red car.

She looked at what she had set in motion.

She did not move.

She had said two words. Handle it. Three syllables, said quietly, with the full information of someone who had been learning what those two syllables meant for eight months. She had said it and stepped to the side and watched and she was going to carry this — not as a wound, she had enough wounds, those were not hers to choose — but as a weight. The specific gravity of a decision made with open eyes and complete information, chosen rather than suffered.

The weight was considerable.

It was hers.

She was going to carry it for a long time.

That was fine.

The door to the building opened.

Mark came through it — not running, not alarmed, the specific controlled quality of someone who had heard what he needed to hear and was doing the necessary inventory. He looked at the alley. He looked at Donghyuck. He looked at Solar.

"You alright," he offered to her. Flat. Direct.

"Yes," she said.

He looked at the alley once more with the operational assessment. Then he looked at Donghyuck. Something passed between them in the private language of people who had been through enough together to communicate without words.

"I'll handle the rest," Mark assures, completely casually unbothered about the entire thing, or so it seemed.

Donghyuck looked at Solar, there was something in those brown eyes of his. He was searching her face, doing that thing he did well, reading. She pursed her lips for a second, registering the small amount of what she could register from what just occurred. After that, she pondered, it was less likely she was going to be able to go home as if nothing happened.

"Go inside," she hums, landing on the decision. "I'll be right behind you."

He went. She stood in the alley for one more moment — the May night, the amber light, the still thing she had put there with a word. She looked at it directly, watched as Mark was preparing to put the body in a giant bag. Whether it was simply a garbage bag or a body bag, she didn't have the space in her head to register. All she could process, in the slightest, was the blood; it was everywhere, pooling onto Mark's shoes, leaking from several spots in the man's body where he had been beaten and torn apart. 

He's very good at it, repeated in her head, she thought about the tone of Cheng's voice, like he was underselling it, knew it, and wasn't afraid of showing that fact. She wondered, how many times he had seen something like this happen?

Then, she thought, how many times had Haechan done something like this? How many times had the boy who looked at her like the world was meant for no more than cherishing be the same one who ended a man's life with the unforgiving force of his own heel? 

The answer didn't come, and she didn't expect it to.

Mark glanced up at her, obviously noting that she was still standing there under the moonlight. For a second, he looked like he wanted to say something.

He didn't.

Solar closed her eyes, exhaling once. The skin of her stomach burned with the nauseating touch of a stranger that knew too much. 

She turned, and heading back up the steps.

The apartment had the specific quality it always had after something operational — compressed, present, the warmth of the Friday evening set aside. Renjun's laptop was open to something she didn't recognize. Chenle was not at the window. Jisung was in the kitchen doorway with his phone to his ear, speaking low.

Nobody said anything to her when she came through the door.

She crossed the room to where Donghyuck was standing by the window — the same window, the same position she had found him in on ordinary Saturdays. He turned when she came to him.

She put her arms around him.

Not a crisis hold. Not the sidewalk shaking version. Just this — her arms around him and the apartment around them and the specific warmth of a person who had just done something terrible and necessary and was coming back from it. She felt the uneven quality of his breathing, the operational register releasing by degrees. His arms came around her. His hands found her back, careful, the same hands.

She held on.

He held on.

"Sol," he said. Low, rough at the edges.

"I know," she said.

"Are you—"

"Yes," she said. "I'm okay."

His arms tightened for a moment. Then his hands moved — finding her arm, where the man had grabbed it, checking with the specific gentle thoroughness of someone who needed to know.

"It's fine," she said. "I'm fine."

He was quiet. His hands very gentle on her arm — the same hands, the force and the tenderness in the same hour, both genuinely his.

She thought: the warmth and the dangerous are not separable.

She had known this since February. She was living it in her body now.

She stepped back — not far, just enough to look at him. At the honest face in the apartment light. Not different. Not less. The same.

"The party," she said.

He looked at her.

"You still have to go," she said. "At ten."

He held her gaze. She could see the calculation — the DREAM arithmetic of obligation and want.

"I'll take you home first," he said.

"You don't have to—"

"Sol." The quiet register. Decided. "I'm taking you home first."

She understood what he was doing — not I'll send someone, but I will do this and then the other thing. Both obligations real. Both his.

"Okay," she said.

He drove her home through the May night on a route she had not seen before — the longest variation yet, deliberately winding. She tracked it without commenting. She understood it.

The car was quiet. Not the comfortable playlist quiet of the morning drives. The specific quiet of two people who had just been through something and were in the process of coming back into the ordinary world.

She watched the city move past the windows. The east side giving way to wider streets. The May night warm enough that she cracked the window and the air came in with the specific smell of late spring in Havenbrook — the green and the concrete and underneath it the mineral thing that had been in the air since she was a child.

"The footage," she said.

He was quiet.

"He said it was going out regardless," she said. "That the decision had been made."

"I know," he said.

"Is that true."

He was quiet for a moment. "It might be," he said. "Or it might be what they say when they want to establish they're not negotiating." He looked at the road. "Tonight changed something. They'll recalculate."

"How long," she said.

"I don't know," he said.

She held that. The footage still existing somewhere. The Kim side still holding it. Jihoon still positioned wherever he was. All of that still true.

"I'm not sorry," she said.

He looked at her sideways.

"About what I asked you to do," she said. "I'm going to carry it. But I'm not sorry."

He was quiet long enough that she had turned back to the window before he spoke.

"I know," he said. "That's what I'll think about, when I think about tonight."

"What do you mean," she said.

"I mean you stood there," he said. "He had his hand under your shirt, hand on your jaw, and you held your ground waited. You heard the door and you didn't move. You just held your ground and said it." He looked at the road. "You'd already done the math."

"I had," she said.

"I know you had," he said. "That's the part I can't stop thinking about." A pause. "I've known since August that you were specific. I didn't know how specific."

She held that for the rest of the drive.

He pulled up to her door. The porch light was on. The tree in the front yard was its full May green in the amber light — the committed green of a season that had decided to be itself.

He put the car in park.

She did not get out immediately.

She turned to look at him. He was looking at her — the same way he always looked at her. The receiving expression, the one that asked nothing.

She reached over and put her hand against his jaw. His eyes closed for a moment — the specific quality of someone receiving something they have needed. She kissed him.

Different from the library. Different from the car in the afternoon. This was the specific quality of a kiss that had the weight of the night in it — not desperate, not urgent, just the fullness of it, two people who had just been through something together and were still here. His hand found the side of her face — the scabbed knuckles against her jaw, the roughness and the warmth, the same hands — and held it there.

She pulled back.

"Go to the party," she said. "Be careful."

"Always," he said.

"I mean it," she said.

"I know," he said. "I know you mean it." He held her gaze. "Sol."

"Yeah," she said.

"Thank you," he said.

"Don't thank me," she said.

"I know you always say that," he said. "I'm saying it anyway."

She looked at him for one more moment. The red hair. The dark eyes. The chain. The jacket. The full picture.

"Go," she said.

She got out. She went up the porch steps. She heard the car still idling behind her, waiting until she was inside. She let herself in. She closed the door. She locked it.

She stood in the hallway.

Her jacket still on. She took it off. She hung it on the hook. She stood still.

The refrigerator hummed. Her mother's television was off — she had gone to bed. The house held its ordinary quiet around the specific non-ordinary thing she had just brought home with her.

She went upstairs.

She lay on her bed in the May dark. She looked at the ceiling. The barely-visible stars.

She had been looking at those stars since she was twelve. They had been there through all of it. They would be there through whatever came next.

Her phone buzzed.

Haechannie🐭: inside?

inside, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: lock the door

already done, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: good

A pause. The three dots appearing and disappearing twice.

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: I've been thinking about what you said

Haechannie🐭: in the car

which part

Haechannie🐭: the part where you said you're not sorry

She held the phone. She thought about the alley. About the hand on her arm. About the door opening and knowing without turning around. About standing her ground and waiting.

I'm not, she typed. I knew what I was asking. I'd do it again.

The longest pause yet.

Haechannie🐭: I know you would

Haechannie🐭: that's the part I can't stop thinking about

She held the phone. She thought about I've known since August that you were specific. I didn't know how specific.

go do your thing, she typed. text me when you're done.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: goodnight sol

goodnight donghyuck, she typed.

She locked her screen. She put the phone on her chest. She looked at the barely-visible stars and thought about the weight she was carrying — not the wound version, not the managed version. Just the weight of a decision made with open eyes and complete information. Chosen rather than suffered.

It was hers.

She was going to carry it for a long time.

She looked at the ceiling.

The stars held their light.

She fell asleep inside the weight of what she had chosen, which was the specific weight of someone who had stopped being managed by her life and started managing it, which was not lighter.

But it was hers.

And that made all the difference.


29: seventeen

The days after the night had a specific texture.

Not heavy — she had expected heavy, had braced for the specific weight of something irreversible pressing down on the ordinary days. What she found instead was the particular quality of life that continued past a fixed point: the world running its program, the June sun arriving with its full commitment, the east side doing what the east side did, and her inside all of it carrying a thing that had changed the shape of everything without changing the surface of anything.

She had made her peace with it on the first morning. Not the peace of resolution — the peace of someone who had looked at what they had done and found they could still look at themselves in the mirror. She had said the word. She was going to carry it. Both of those things were true. Neither of them required management.

What she had not expected was the specific quality of the silence from the Kim side.

Cheng confirmed it on Sunday. Not loudly, not with the flat dose-managing register. Just: "They've gone quiet."

"How quiet," she couldn't help but ask.

"Completely," he confirms. "No contact. No positioning. Nothing on Maren." He held his coffee — they were at her kitchen table, Sunday morning, her mother at work, the June light coming through the window at the specific angle of a day that had decided to be warm. "Losing someone that way, specifically here, on our territory — that's a message. They're recalculating."

"How long," she piqued.

"I don't know," he shrugs, honestly. "Could be weeks. Could be longer." He looked at the window. "They might not come back at all. Or they might come back different."

She absorbed that. "And the footage."

Cheng was quiet for a moment. "Still exists," he admits. "Still Jihoon's. But the Kim side isn't touching it right now. Their leverage cost them something. They're figuring out what that means."

She nodded. She drank her coffee. The June morning did its ordinary thing outside the window and she let it.


+


Donghyuck texted on Saturday evening, when the party would have ended and the operational world would have cycled back to whatever passed for rest.

Haechannie🐭: everything okay on your end today

yes, she typed. you?

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: sol

yeah

Haechannie🐭: I need to see you

Not a question. Not the usual register — not are you free or come by Saturday. Just the direct statement of something needed.

when, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: whenever you can

Haechannie🐭: soon

tomorrow, she typed. afternoon.

Haechannie🐭: yeah

Haechannie🐭: okay

She put the phone down. She thought about the party. About him going to Jihoon's node at ten on the night of, the specific operational discipline of someone who had done something and then done the next thing because the next thing was required. She had been thinking about that since Friday — the specific quality of a person who could carry what he had done and go to a party an hour later because the party was infrastructure and infrastructure did not pause for the weight of the night.

She thought, this is what I chose.

Not with alarm. Just with the clarity of someone finishing a sentence she had started a long time ago.

+

Sunday afternoon he came to her house.

Not the car — he walked, which she had not expected. She saw him coming from the porch, the red hair in the June afternoon light, the jacket that had become simply part of what he looked like, the chain. He came up the path with his hands in his pockets and stopped at the bottom of the steps.

She was sitting on the porch. She had been there for ten minutes, which she had not admitted to herself was waiting.

"Hey," she offers.

"Hey," he returns, as flat as he typically tended to be.

He came up the steps. He sat beside her on the porch — not inside, just the porch, the June afternoon around them, the street doing its Sunday thing. The tree in the front yard fully itself now, the green committed and actual, no longer tentative.

Neither of them said anything for a moment.

She had been in various kinds of silence with him since October. She knew the textures of them — the apartment silence, the car silence, the divergence point silence, the roof silence. This one was different. This was the silence of two people sitting inside something they had both been part of and were both still processing, each from their own side of it.

"Sol," he mutters.

"Yeah," she pipes in return, as casual as ever.

"I've been thinking about that night," he said. "Specifically about what you did."

"I asked you to handle something," she said.

"You stood there with his hand on your face," he said. "And you waited." He looked at the street. "I keep thinking about the fact that you heard me come through the door and you still didn't look."

"I knew it was you," she explains.

"I know," he hums. "That's what I keep thinking about."

She looked at the tree. The June afternoon. The specific ordinary of a Sunday street. "I knew the sound of the door," she said. "I've been learning that building for eight months."

"I know," he said again, though his voice was that of a whisper.

They sat for a moment.

"Are you okay?" she asked. Not performing the question — actually concerned. The specific asking of someone who understood that what he had done on Friday had a cost for him too, a different cost, the specific weight of someone who had done this before and knew what it meant and did it anyway.

He was quiet for a moment. "Yes," he nods after a while, once the thoughts calmed down. "It's not the first time." He said it the way he said true things — without dressing it, without asking her to receive it any particular way. "It doesn't get easier. But it doesn't get harder either. It's just the shape of it."

"The shape of it," she repeats.

"Yeah," he said.

She held that. She thought about what Cheng had said months ago, he's the best I've seen at what he does. And I've seen a lot. She thought about what that meant — not just the capability, but the cost of the capability, the specific thing a person had to become to be very good at something like that. The way the warmth and the dangerous were inseparable not just in the world but in him. Both genuinely his.

She reached over and found his hand. His fingers closed around hers. The knuckles still rough — she could feel the specific texture of them, adding a new layer on top of April's layer, the ongoing physical record of a life lived in a particular way.

She held it.

"I'm not sorry," she asserts, confidently. "I told you in the car. I meant it."

"I know you meant it," he agrees.

"And I'm not going anywhere," she affirms, still as confident.

He looked at her. The honest face in the Sunday afternoon light. "I know," he hums. "I know that now in a way I didn't know before Friday."

She held his gaze. "What's different?" she questioned, because the not knowing was seemingly becoming harder to do.

"Before Friday," he goes on, "I knew you chose this. I believed you chose it. But there was always a part of me that wondered — when it was actually in front of you, when it was real and immediate and not a concept — whether you'd still choose it." He looked at the street. "I know the answer now."

She held that for a long moment. The June afternoon. The tree. His hand in hers.

"You've been waiting for that," she puzzles. "This whole time."

"Not waiting," he shook his head, fringe falling in his face. "Just — holding space for the possibility that the answer might be different than I hoped." He looked at her. "It wasn't."

She looked at him. At the red hair and the dark eyes and the honest face and the chain and the jacket and the person who had been in her phone since October and had, since Friday, a different quality — not changed, just known more completely.

"No," she mutters in agreement. "It wasn't."

They sat on the porch in the June afternoon until the light changed and the street began its Sunday evening thing and neither of them required anything else from the silence.

+

Her birthday was on a Thursday.

She turned seventeen in the specific quiet of a June morning — the light early and warm, the house ordinary, the refrigerator humming, her mother asleep still. She lay in bed for a few minutes after her alarm and looked at the ceiling and thought: seventeen.

It landed differently than she had expected.

She had turned sixteen in the aftermath of the incident's second year — the anniversary of the thing that had defined the shape of her life since fourteen, the specific weight of a birthday spent surviving. Seventeen was different. Seventeen was on the other side of October and December and the library and the roof and the night. Seventeen was the first birthday she had in a long time that felt like an arrival rather than a continuation.

She got up. She made coffee. She sat at the kitchen table in the June morning and let it be what it was.

Her phone had been buzzing since midnight — the specific cascade of birthday texts from people who tracked the date, the social architecture of a digital world marking the occasion whether she attended to it or not. She scrolled through them with the mild attention she gave to most ambient things.

At the bottom of the notifications, sent at exactly midnight, was a text from Jiwoo:Jiwoo: HAPPY BIRTHDAY SOLAR LEEJiwoo: I have been awake for this exact momentJiwoo: you are not allowed to do anything today that I have not approvedJiwoo: this is a formal notice

She smiled. She typed back: noted. what's approved?

Jiwoo: I'm coming over at noonJiwoo: bring nothing
Jiwoo: which I know makes no sense because it's your own house BUT my point remainsJiwoo: I have everything

She put the phone down. She drank her coffee. June did its thing outside the kitchen window.

+


Jiwoo arrived at noon with a bag that was, by volume, roughly one-third food and two-thirds whatever Jiwoo had decided constituted a proper birthday. She came through the door with the specific energy she brought to things she had been planning, which was the energy of someone who had been thinking about this for a while and was satisfied with the result.

"Sit down," Jiwoo instructed, setting the bag on the kitchen table with the authority of someone who had claimed the space.

"It's my house," Solar bluntly stated the obvious.

"Sit down," Jiwoo ignored her, demanding again.

Solar sat down.

What emerged from the bag: an unreasonable amount of Solar's specific favorite things, assembled with the accuracy of someone who had been paying close attention for three years. The coffee from the gas station that was aggressively warm and aggressively mediocre, which had become the specific taste of their friendship. The snacks Solar had mentioned once, three months ago, in passing — Jiwoo had filed it. A small cake, not store-bought, the kind that was clearly homemade and clearly someone's first attempt at a specific design that had not quite worked out the way it was meant to but which was entirely recognizable as intentional. And a card, handwritten, that Solar read and then put face-down on the table because if she read it again she was going to cry in front of Jiwoo which she was not going to do.

"Don't read it again," Jiwoo said, as if she knew.

"I wasn't going to," Solar defended.

"I know that face," Jiwoo wrinkled her nose playfully.

"I don't have a face," Solar whined.

"You have a very specific face," Jiwoo corrected, sternly, and cut the cake.

They ate the cake at the kitchen table in the June afternoon and talked about things that were and were not the night, about summer and what came next and the ranked queue and the audition prep and Jiwoo's own summer plans, about the specific relief of the school year being done and the specific anxiety of junior year approaching, about everything and nothing in the way they had been talking since the first lunch when Jiwoo had sat down uninvited and demanded one true thing.

At some point Solar admitted, "I'm glad you sat down."

Jiwoo looked at her.

"That first lunch," Solar depicts. "I'm glad you just — sat down."

Jiwoo held her coffee cup with both hands. The open expression, the one from the first lunch. "So am I," she said. "Obviously." She took a sip. "You gave me the most unhinged one-true-thing I've ever heard and I knew immediately you were going to be worth the trouble."

"Was I?" Solar's curiosity got the best of her.

"Considerable trouble," Jiwoo pondered for a second, before shrugging. "Absolutely worth it."

Solar looked at the table. At the cake and the coffee and the card she had put face-down. At three years of this, the specific friendship that had developed in the corner of the cafeteria and the gas station coffee route and the color-coded study plan and everything in between."I'm going to miss you," Solar mumbled. "When I go."

"I know," Jiwoo said. "We've said that." She looked at Solar with the expression of someone who had already done all the grieving they were going to do about this and had arrived somewhere clear. "But you're going to come back and you're going to be something and I'm going to be able to say I knew you when you were on forty-seven for six months." She took another bite of cake, chewing obnoxiously loud, and with her mouth open, continued, "Plus, we've got a lot ahead of us then anyway. Junior year, baby!" She screeched like a child, as if this was groundbreaking news, the fact that they were becoming juniors.

Solar laughed. The real kind. "I finished the book," she pivots, deciding this was now what was worthwhile information.

Jiwoo looked at her with genuine surprise. "When?"

"Last week," Solar said.

"Was it good?"

"Yes," Solar said. "It was very good. I should have read it faster."

"Should have," Jiwoo agreed, and ate the last of her cake.

+

Cheng texted at three.Cheng: happy birthdayCheng: I have something for youCheng: later tonight if you're free

She texted back: free after six.

Cheng: I'll come to yours

He arrived at six-thirty, which was Cheng's version of punctual, with a coffee she had not asked for, similar to Jiwoo in that regard, although Solar Lee would never complain about too much coffee; and a small package wrapped in brown paper that had the quality of something wrapped by someone who did not own tape and had made do with what was available.

"You could've just handed it to me," she giggles, looking at the wrapping.

"Presentation matters," Cheng shakes his head, proud of his work, clearly.

"You used a rubber band," she snorted.

"It's holding," he snaps.

She opened it. Inside was a small notebook — not the generic kind, the specific kind, the brand she had mentioned once in passing in a text conversation about taking notes for the audition prep, because she preferred paper to digital for certain things. He had found it. He had remembered.

She looked at it for a moment.

"Cheng," she gushed, a pout forming on her lips.

"It's just a notebook," he was playing it off.

"It's exactly the right notebook," she disagreed, unable to hide the big grin spreading across her face.

He held his coffee and looked at the window with the expression he used when he was receiving something he did not want to make into a moment. "You're going to need it," he said. "For the audition prep. For everything after."

She held the notebook. She thought about Cheng — eighth grade, before everything, the friend who had been there before the incident and after it and through everything in between and who had connected her to Donghyuck and walked her home more times than she could count and given her information in doses calibrated for what she could handle and never once required her to be grateful for any of it.

"Thank you," she said. "For everything. Not just this."

He looked at her with the third expression — not the logistics face, not the underneath-it face. The specific one for things that had been true for a long time and did not require naming."Go be seventeen," he tells her instead. "It's a good year for you."

She looked at him. "How do you know?"

"Because you've been building toward it for the last two years," he said. "You're going to feel it this year." He finished his coffee. "Trust me."

She trusted him. She had trusted him since eighth grade. It had never been wrong.

+

At nine-fifteen, the text came.

Haechannie🐭: heyHaechannie🐭: happy birthday sol

She smiled at the screen. thank you, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: seventeen

seventeen, she confirmed.

Haechannie🐭: come outside

She looked at the screen. She looked at the window — the June evening, the porch light already on, the tree in the front yard holding the last of the day's light in its leaves.

I'm in my pajamas, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: I knowHaechannie🐭: come outside anyway

She looked at the phone for a moment, too shocked to even question his I know. She put it down. She went to the window and looked out.

The dark red car was at the curb.

She grabbed the closest jacket — not hers, her mother's, hanging on the hook by the door — and went outside.

He was leaning against the car with his hands in his jacket pockets, the chain at his collar catching the porch light's amber, the red hair in the specific quality of June evening light which was different from any other light she had seen on him. He was looking at her with the expression she had been cataloguing since October.

"Hi," she offered aloud, from the porch steps.

"Hi," he returned, that smile peeking on the corner of his lips. "Happy birthday."

She came down the steps. She stopped in front of him. She looked at him — the full picture, in the June evening, at her door, where he had been coming since May.

"You didn't have to come," she tells him.

"I know," he nods, once.

"It's nine-fifteen," she points out.

"I know what time it is," he nods again, casually.

She looked at him. He looked back at her.

"Come on," he decides, standing up straight from leaning against his car. His hand opens the passenger side door for her, and he motions once with his hand, as if she should have been expecting any of this.

"Where?" was the first thing that came out of her mouth, and then, "Hyuck, I'm in my pajamas."

"Doesn't matter, Sol. It's just somewhere," he assures her. "Just a drive. It's your birthday."

She got in the car.

+

He drove her through Havenbrook at night.

Not a specific destination — the city, the streets she knew and the streets she was still learning, the east side and beyond it and the wider geography of the only city she had ever lived in. The playlist ran something she recognized for once — a song she had mentioned to him weeks ago, in passing, the kind of passing mention that should not have been retained and had been.

She looked out the window. The June night was warm enough that she had cracked her window, and the air moved through the car with the specific smell of Havenbrook in early summer — the green and the pavement and the mineral thing underneath all of it, the smell she had grown up knowing as simply the smell of where she lived.

She had been watching this city change for seventeen years. She had watched it from the inside of a life that had not always been kind to her, through the incident and the survival years and the science wing detours and the apartment and the car at her door every morning. She had learned the east side's grammar and the school's social physics and the specific quality of every season it offered.

She was going to leave it.

Not yet. Not for a while. But the direction was set and she was moving in it and the city knew it even if it hadn't been told.

"Can I ask you something?" she broke the silence.

"Mm," he hummed.

"When I leave," she starts. "What do you think happens... To you... To this?" She gestured between them, which had become the gesture for the thing that did not have a clean label and didn't need one. "Like... Specifically."

He was quiet for a moment. The city moved past the windows. She watched him think — the brief stillness, the considering.

"I think," he said, "that you go. And you become what you're going to become." He looked at the road. "And I think I'm here. Building the thing I'm building. For as long as it takes." He was quiet for a moment. "And I think you're still in my phone. As the first thread. And I'm still in yours."

"You've been the first thread since October," she mutters.

"I know," he hums. "I've been checking."

She looked at him sideways. He was looking at the road with the expression of someone who had said something true and was not going to walk it back.

"Donghyuck," she breaths out his name.

"Yeah," he glances over at her, eyes shining under the moonlight peeking through the windows.

"I've been checking too," she whispers.

He said nothing. But something in the set of his shoulders shifted — the specific fraction of something held, released.

They drove through Havenbrook in the June night, the city laid out around them in its full specific geography, and she looked at all of it — the streets she had been afraid of and the streets she had learned and the east side that had become hers and the school that was almost behind her and the building on Maren Street with its second-step creak and its old wood smell and its roof where you could see her house.

At some point he pulled over. Not dramatically — just pulled over, on a street she recognized as being near the park, the big park where they had walked in April. He put the car in park and reached into the back seat.

He produced a small paper bag.

Not wrapped with a rubber band. Not wrapped in anything — just a paper bag, folded over at the top, the kind from the coffee shop on Fifth that she went to occasionally and had mentioned to him in the same conversation where she had mentioned the notebook brand she preferred and the song she liked and the specific Overwatch map that was hardest for her to play Kiriko on.She looked at the bag. She looked at him.

"Open it," he encourages.

She opened it.

Inside was a small thing — a keychain, the kind that was specifically unremarkable on its own, a simple design, the kind you found at the particular shop on the east side that sold exactly this type of thing. But attached to it was a small charm: a mouse. The specific mouse emoji made physical, small and silver and completely ridiculous.

She stared at it.Haechannie🐭.

She had saved his contact as that in October, the small mouse face, because it had felt right in the way that certain things felt right before you fully understood why.

She looked at him.

He was looking at the window with the expression of someone who was being very serene about a decision they had made and were not revisiting.

"It's a mouse," she states.

"Yes," he responds.

"You got me a mouse keychain," she repeats what they both already knew.

"For your keys," he explains. "For when you go. So you have — " he stopped. Started differently. "So you have something from here. That's small enough to carry anywhere."

She held the keychain in her hand. The small silver mouse. The specific weight of something that was nothing and was everything, the way the most important things often were.

She thought about the contact name she had given him in October — the unconscious accuracy of it, Haechannie🐭, the mouse, the warm private thing that only existed in her phone. And now it was in her hand. Small and silver and ridiculous and specifically his.

She looked at him. He was still looking at the window.

"Hey," she said.

He looked at her. The honest face in the June night, the amber light from outside finding the specific color of his hair.

She kissed him.

Not the library kiss or the car kiss or the weight-of-the-night kiss. The birthday kiss — which turned out to be its own specific kind, warm and unhurried and tasting of the June evening, both of them exactly where they were and completely present in it.

He was not a rough kisser nor a careful one; he was the exact perfect mixture of both that made you feel like you were in a movie you desperately did not want to see the end of. His hand found her cheek, his thumb grazed over the softness of the skin, down her jawline.
She pulled back.

She looked at him.

She held the mouse keychain in her hand.

"Thank you," she said. "For coming. For the drive. For this." She looked at the keychain. "For all of it."

He looked at her with the expression that had nothing managing it. "Happy birthday, Sol," he mutters under his breath. "Seventeen looks right on you."

She looked out the window at the Havenbrook night. The June dark. The park trees. The east side somewhere behind them. All of it — every version of this city she had ever lived in, laid out around the specific car on the specific street in the specific night of the specific day she turned seventeen.

"Yeah," she agrees after a moment. "It does."

He drove her home.

She fell asleep that night with the mouse keychain on her nightstand, the barely-visible stars on the ceiling, the June dark outside the window.

She was seventeen.

She had come a long way.

She had further to go.

Both of those things were true, which was the specific shape of being seventeen in Havenbrook on the east side, carrying a weight that was hers and a direction that was hers and a person who had been the first thread since October and was going to stay there.

The stars held their light.

She slept.



30: summer held

The first morning of summer arrived the way it always did — ordinary, unhurried, entirely indifferent to the fact that everything had changed.

Solar woke at eight-fifteen. The light was already the specific quality of June light, the particular commitment of it, the way it came through the curtains and made no apology for being there. The refrigerator hummed from the kitchen. Her mother had already left for work — she could tell by the specific quality of the quiet, the house with no one else in it, a sound she had learned to read before she was ten.

She looked at the ceiling for a moment.

Then she turned her head and looked at the nightstand.

The mouse keychain was where she had left it. Small and silver in the morning light, the kind of thing that was nothing and was everything, resting on the wood like it had always lived there.

She held it for a moment. She did not examine what she felt. Some things were better held than examined.

She got up.

Summer, it turned out, was its own specific grammar.

The school year had structure that didn't require choosing — the alarm, the morning route, the bell schedule, the way her days had been organized from the outside since she was six years old. Summer removed that structure entirely and replaced it with the unnerving freedom of a day that was only what she made it.

She made it deliberate.

By the second morning she had a routine, assembled without quite deciding to assemble it. Ranked queue in the morning while the day was still cool — the window open, the east side waking up outside, the specific ambient sound of a Havenbrook morning in summer filtering through the screen. She had the summer. She had said so. Top 500 was a real number with a real gap between it and where she was, and the gap was what mornings were for.

The notebook Cheng had given her lived on her desk. She opened it in the afternoons — the specific notebook for the specific purpose, the one he had remembered from a passing mention in a text conversation three months ago. She had been filling it gradually since her birthday: song choices written and crossed out and rewritten, notes on breath support, notes on what her voice did in the upper register when she was tired versus when she was rested, which was different and mattered. A list of choreography she had been learning from her phone propped against the desk — her floor cleared, the small available square of it, which was not much but was enough.

The practicing was the hardest part. Not technically — technically she had been working at it long enough that the hard edges had softened into something approaching fluency. The hard part was the recording. Watching herself back on her phone with the specific discomfort of encountering her own voice and body from the outside, the gap between what it felt like and what it looked like, the work of closing that gap incrementally. She did it anyway. Every afternoon. Because the notebook was filling up and the gap was getting smaller and that was what the summer was for.

The evenings were less structured.

The evenings were when he showed up.

The first time was the Saturday after her birthday, two days into summer.

She had not been expecting him — or had been, in the specific way she had learned not to name, the way she had categorized since October as simply the ambient awareness of someone specific. She was at her desk with the notebook open when her phone lit up.

Haechannie🐭: you busy

She looked at the screen. She looked at the notebook. She looked at the screen again.

no, she typed.

Haechannie🐭: come outside

She went outside.

He was at the curb in the dark red car, window down, the specific quality of a Saturday afternoon in Havenbrook doing its thing around him — the sound of the east side, the June warmth, the tree in her front yard with its full summer green. He was not leaning on the car. He was just sitting in it, window down, which was its own specific posture, the posture of someone who had shown up and was not making it into a production.

"I'm not in my pajamas this time," she said, coming down the porch steps.

He looked at her. The corner of his mouth moved. "I know."

She got in the car.

He didn't tell her where they were going. She didn't ask. She had learned by now that the not-telling was not evasion — it was just how he did things, the specific preference for arrival over announcement. She watched the city through the window instead, the Saturday Havenbrook afternoon moving past in the way it did when you had nowhere particular to be.

He parked eventually at a place she recognized — the overlook on the west side of the park, where the ground rose slightly and the city's skyline was visible in the distance, the east side's specific geometry laid out below the line of trees. She had been here before, years ago, with different people for different reasons. It had the specific quality of somewhere that had been other things and was now this.

They sat on the hood of the car. The June warmth was real — not the polite warmth of May but the committed warmth of a summer that had arrived and intended to stay. Below them, the city ran its Saturday program, unhurried and various.

He had brought food. She had not known about the food — it had been in the back seat, a bag from the place on Fourth she had mentioned once in October, the kind of passing mention that should not have been retained and had been.

She looked at the bag. She looked at him.

He was looking at the city.

"You remembered that," she said.

"I remember most things," he said.

She held the bag. She thought about the song in the birthday drive — the one she had mentioned in passing weeks ago. The keychain. The specific pattern of someone who collected small information about her and produced it in unexpected moments without making an event of the collecting.

"You're going to keep doing that," she said. "Aren't you."

He turned and looked at her with the honest face, the one that had nothing managing it. "Probably," he said.

She opened the bag.

They ate on the hood of the car in the June afternoon with the city below them and the summer starting and neither of them requiring it to be anything other than what it was.

The rhythm established itself over the following weeks.

Not scheduled — she would have resisted scheduled, and he seemed to understand this the way he understood most things about her, with the specific accuracy of someone who had been paying close attention for a long time. What emerged instead was the rhythm of chosen contact, the texture of two people in the same city in the summer with no external architecture organizing their proximity. He showed up at her door or she walked to the apartment, and neither of those things required announcement to feel expected.

What was different from the school year — what she noticed in the particular way she noticed things, cataloguing it without deciding to — was the quality of the contact itself. The school year had given them margins: the car ride, the apartment Saturdays, the specific narrow windows of time that held what they were. Summer had no margins. Summer had entire afternoons that could be anything, and what he chose to do with them was show up and stay.

He was present in a different way. Not louder — he was not a loud person. But more decided. The way he sat closer without acknowledging that he was sitting closer. The way he reached for her hand first, one afternoon on the overlook, not in a moment of weight or significance, just in the easy way of someone who had made a decision and was no longer negotiating it. His hand finding hers the way you reach for something you have already decided is yours to reach for.

She had noticed, in the months since October, that she had been the one who crossed distances. The library. The car. The birthday. It had made sense at the time — she was the one with the walls, so her crossing them had been the thing that moved.

But the walls were lower now. And he had noticed.

She let him reach.

He had to go to Jihoon's on a Thursday night in the second week of June.

She knew about it the way she knew about all of them — not because he told her in advance, but because she had learned to read the specific quality of his availability, the nights he went quiet at a particular hour and the nights he didn't. He texted her that afternoon.

Haechannie🐭: tonight's Jihoon's

I know, she typed. you and Jeno?

Haechannie🐭: yeah
