"Leaked the HYBE confirmation," she confirmed, pressing back into the seat. "Through the social world. With the specific implication attached." She looked at the windshield. "He's not releasing the footage yet. He's making sure everyone knows it exists before he does."

Donghyuck's hands tightened on the wheel. She didn't look at them but she felt the change in the air — the temperature of him shifting, the anger that had been building since spring gathering itself into something that was very close to the surface.

"The footage," she added. "The drug documentation. He's positioning it so that when it comes out, everything is already in the air." She turned to look at him. "He already has what he needs. He's had it since February."

His jaw moved. Once. He stared through the windshield at the October street.

She watched him hold it — the control, the deliberate architecture of a man who did not lose composure, pressing against something that was very much asking to be lost.

"I'm going to handle it," he said.

"Donghyuck—"

"I know what you're going to say." His voice was rough at the edges in a way it almost never was. "I know."

She held the door handle. She thought about what handle it meant from him. She thought about what was coming — had known what was coming since April, had felt it building in the quality of him every single day since September. The punch. The confrontation with Jisung. The things that had been accumulating since May of sophomore year finally asking to be released.

She wanted to say don't.

She held the door handle and did not say don't because she wasn't certain she meant it — because part of her understood there was no version of this that resolved without something happening that only Donghyuck could make happen.

She reached over.

She took his face in her hands, turned him toward her, and kissed him — not the charged version, not the desperate one. The quiet kind. The specific warmth of I can't say what I need to say so I'm saying it like this kissed into the corner of his mouth, his jaw, slow and deliberate, and she felt him exhale against her like something that had been held very tightly was briefly released.

When she pulled back his forehead dropped against hers.

"Just come back," she breathed against his skin.

His hands covered hers where she still held his face.

"I always come back," he told her, and his voice was raw in a way she filed alongside the eight minutes and the pavement and every other piece of him she had been carrying for three years.

She held his face for one more moment.

Then she got out of the car.

The October east side held its program around her — the amber light, the leaves at their peak, the last beautiful October of all her Octobers in this car.

She walked to her front door.

The barely-visible stars were inside, waiting.

She didn't look up at them when she got into bed.

She already knew what they would say.


59: what he can't argue

The apartment was empty except for the two of them.

Jeno had left an hour ago. Mark was somewhere operational. Renjun and Chenle had gone down to the corner store and hadn't come back, which happened often enough to be unremarkable. The October evening settled into the apartment through the cracked window — the specific cool of a season that had made up its mind, the amber light from the street below crossing the ceiling.

Donghyuck was at the table.

He had been at the table for forty minutes. The piece of paper in front of him was not the first — two others sat filled and pushed aside, the sprawl of someone building a case across drafts and not finding the version that said the thing the way it needed to be said. The pen moved. Stopped. Moved again.

The lock turned.

Jisung came through the door with the quality he always carried — not loudness, just presence, the flat certainty of someone who had never questioned his right to be somewhere. He crossed the room with the inventory expression and stopped when he saw the table.

He looked at the pieces of paper.

His jaw adjusted — the smallest thing, the tell. He crossed to the table and stopped at its edge, standing, not sitting. He looked at the drafts. He looked at the pen in Donghyuck's hand.

"How many is that," Jisung asked. The nothing register — perfectly calibrated, having this conversation on operational terms.

Donghyuck set the pen down and looked up. The ease was off. Not anger yet — just the full attention of someone who had been deciding something for a long time and had finished deciding it. "Enough to know I'm not writing another one after this."

Something moved through Jisung's expression — not enough to name, but there. He picked up the piece of paper, read it with the focused efficiency he brought to everything, set it down. "The argument is the same."

"Because you haven't addressed it."

"I've addressed it every time." Jisung moved from the table — repositioning, choosing his angles the way he always did. He came to stand near the window, the October street behind him. "The expansion is necessary. The Kim side is quiet because they understand force and force requires territory."

"The math supports the expansion," Donghyuck said. "I've never disputed the math."

"Then what are you disputing."

Donghyuck stood. He did not want to have this conversation sitting down. He stood and looked at Jisung across the apartment and when he spoke his voice had the quality it carried when he had been deciding whether to say something for a very long time.

"Mrs. Cha asked me in March if her building was going to be part of the arrangement," he said. "I told her no. I told her the arrangement was the commercial nodes on the ground floor of Maren, not the residential above it. She thanked me and went upstairs." He held Jisung's gaze. "Three blocks east puts her building inside the line. Everything I told her becomes a lie."

"The decision hasn't been made yet," Jisung said.

"It's already made. We both know it's already made." Donghyuck's voice stayed even — and that was the specific thing about it, the register that had always gotten further under Jisung's skin than raised volume ever could. "And when you move the line, every person on those three blocks wakes up one day inside DREAM's territory without having been asked. Mrs. Cha. The man at the fruit stand. The woman at the laundromat. People who have been living their lives on those streets their entire lives — you know them. You've walked those blocks. You remember every face." He paused. The October air came through the window. "You just decided they don't count."

"That is not—"

"I'm not finished."

Jisung stopped.

Donghyuck looked at him — and what crossed his face in that moment was not managed, not performed, not the version of himself he brought to every room where something required him to be careful. It was the two and a half years of it. The weight of every piece of paper, every argument made and lost, every block the math moved through, Jihoon's footage and what Jihoon had built around a fourteen-year-old girl and the specific knowledge of what that cost her and what it was still costing her — all of it present, none of it held back.

"You want to talk about liability?" His voice dropped — not quieter in the way of restraint, quieter in the way of something that had found its target. "You've been calling her a liability for two years. You've been telling me my judgment is compromised, that I'm running the operation around a girl who's leaving, that I've lost the clarity that keeps us alive." He took a step toward the table. Toward Jisung. "You want to know what I actually think?"

Jisung held his position.

"I think you look at her and you see everything you already carved out of yourself," Donghyuck continued, his voice still low, still precise, still carrying the specific flat register of someone delivering the true thing rather than the heated thing. "The parts that notice people. The parts that give a damn about Mrs. Cha's window boxes. The parts that understand that running something doesn't mean the people inside it stop existing as people." He held Jisung's gaze. "And I think you call it liability because that's easier than admitting you used to have those parts too. Before you decided the math was cleaner without them."

The silence that followed was total.

The October street ran outside. The amber light. The cracked window.

Jisung looked at him. And for a moment — the briefest, most specific moment — something ran through his face that was not the calculation. Something that lived underneath the nothing and the operational logic and every cold correct assessment he had ever made, something that the last two minutes had surfaced in a way that no amount of math could process back out of existence.

He moved.

Three steps, fast and cold, and his fist connected with Donghyuck's jaw with the full weight of a man who could not refute what he had just been told and had converted it into the one language remaining.

Donghyuck's head snapped sideways. Blood flooded his mouth immediately — split the inside of his cheek clean on impact, copper and sharp. He did not go down. He turned back and looked at Jisung, and the anger came forward entirely. No management. No operational precision. Everything he had been carrying since sophomore year given full permission to be present in this room, right now, tonight.

He swung.

The impact was different from Jisung's. Not cold. Not precise. It had every piece of paper behind it, and every block the math had moved through, and two and a half years of knowing exactly what was being decided and watching it get decided anyway. It connected with Jisung's cheekbone with a force that turned him sideways and sent him crashing into the chair at the table, the chair going over, Jisung going with it.

He was up fast — Jisung was always fast, the specific thing about him that could not be trained away — getting his feet under him, throwing the chair aside, coming back with two tight hits to Donghyuck's ribs and a third that clipped his shoulder. Donghyuck absorbed them without retreating, which meant Jisung's follow-through had nowhere clean to land, which meant when he reached for a fourth Donghyuck was already inside his reach and his elbow caught Jisung across the mouth.

The sound of it was ugly.

Jisung's head went back. He hit the wall behind him and Donghyuck followed him there, one hand grabbing his collar, the other driving two shots into his ribs — not fast, not rushed, deliberate and heavy, the kind that compacted and didn't leave, the kind that were going to matter for the next several days every time Jisung breathed. Jisung's face cracked through the nothing into something raw. He got a hand between them and shoved hard enough to create distance and Donghyuck stepped back and let him have it.

They stood in the apartment. Both breathing. The table knocked to the side. The pieces of paper scattered across the floor.

Jisung's lower lip had split where the elbow landed, blood on his chin, already running. His cheekbone had started to show. His ribs — he was holding his side with the specific controlled quality of someone who had decided not to show how much it cost, which was the only thing Jisung ever decided when he lost. He looked at Donghyuck across the apartment with the expression that was not the calculation and not the nothing. The first fully unmanaged thing.

Donghyuck's jaw had started to bruise. Otherwise he looked like someone the room had not touched, which was the specific and particular unfairness of fighting someone who was better at it and tonight had given themselves every permission.

Jisung looked at the papers on the floor.

He looked at Donghyuck.

He straightened, released his ribs, and crossed to the door with the measured quality of someone choosing dignity because the alternative was not available. He walked out.

The door closed.

The building held its quiet.

Donghyuck stood where he was. He pressed the back of his hand to his jaw, felt the heat already there. He looked at the papers on the floor — all three drafts, scattered across the apartment he had been sitting in for forty minutes trying to find the right version of an argument Jisung was never going to hear.

He crossed the room and picked them up. One by one. He stacked them. He set them on the table and stood with his hands resting on the surface, looking at them.

The pen was still where he had set it down.

He looked at it for a long moment.

He left it there.

The door opened twenty minutes later.

Mark came through first, Renjun and Chenle behind him with a bag from the corner store, already mid-conversation about something that died the moment they crossed the threshold. The apartment registered on all three of them in the same instant — the table at an angle, the chair still not quite right, the specific quality of a room that had held something and not fully exhaled yet.

Chenle's eyes moved from the table to Donghyuck. He went quiet in the way Chenle went quiet, which was total and immediate and nothing like his usual register.

Renjun looked at the floor. Then at Donghyuck's jaw. He set the bag down without a word.

Mark looked at the room the way Mark looked at everything — taking full inventory, placing everything in its correct location, arriving at the complete picture in the time it took most people to notice one detail. His eyes settled on Donghyuck at the table.

He crossed the room. He picked up the chair that had gone over and set it back in its place. He straightened the table. He did it without being asked, without making it a gesture, with the specific economy of someone who understood that some things required a person to simply deal with them.

He looked at Donghyuck.

"You good," he asked. Not what happened. Not where's Jisung. Just the one question that mattered.

Donghyuck looked at him. His jaw had deepened to a bruise in the time since the door closed. "Yeah," he said. "I'm good."

Mark nodded once. He went to the kitchen.

Renjun sat on the couch. Chenle followed him. The bag from the corner store stayed on the counter. The apartment settled back into its quiet — not the easy quiet of a regular evening but the specific quiet of people who had grown up inside the same world and understood that some things did not require explanation, only presence.

The October street ran outside.

The pen stayed where it was.


60: orange bottle

She had told herself it was tolerance.

That was the word she kept returning to in the weeks after Yuna's delivery — tolerance, the clinical distance of it, the way it made the thing she was doing sound like a medical fact rather than a choice. Her body had adjusted. The threshold had moved. She needed a little more to get level, and level was all she was trying to get — not high, not absent, just the place where the anxiety released its grip on her chest and she could breathe without the specific weight of everything she was carrying pressing down on her sternum.

She had not told him it was getting worse. They had agreed she would tell him. She had agreed she would tell him. But every time she assembled the sentence she felt the specific shape of what it would do to his face, the way the worry would settle into him and become another thing he was carrying around on her behalf, and she told herself she was handling it. She told herself one more week and she'd have it managed.

The pills from Yuna had felt different from the first one. She'd noticed it immediately — the way the effect arrived faster, hit the floor of her anxiety harder, the sedation coming down like a curtain rather than a slow dimming. She'd told herself it was the tolerance again. She'd told herself her body was processing differently. She'd told herself a lot of things in October, standing at her dresser with the orange prescription bottle that wasn't a prescription, taking one pill and then, when one wasn't enough, taking another.

Tonight she had taken two.

The first had not touched it. The withdrawal had been building since afternoon — the specific creeping nausea, the way her skin felt wrong, the anxiety that was not her regular anxiety but the chemical version, the one that lived in her body rather than her head. She had waited. She had tried to wait. At nine o'clock she had taken the second pill and sat on the edge of her bed and waited for level.

What came instead was not level.

It arrived faster than it ever had — not the slow settling she was used to but a wave, the floor of her dropping, her body going heavy in a way that felt less like relief and more like weight. She lay back on the bed because sitting required something she no longer had. The ceiling moved in a way ceilings did not move. Her hands felt far away. She was aware of the light being on, the October dark outside her window, her mother somewhere downstairs in the specific quiet of a weeknight, the television, the familiar sounds of the house — all of it registering at a remove, like something she was hearing through water.

She tried to sit up.

Her body declined.

She thought: something is wrong. The thought arrived clearly, which was almost worse — she was coherent enough to know that something was wrong and not coherent enough to do anything about it. Her breathing had slowed. She could feel it slowing, the specific labor of each breath, the way her chest wasn't moving the way it was supposed to move. Her lips felt strange. She turned her head toward the window and the effort of it was enormous.

The tap came at the glass.

Three times. The pattern she knew. Yet, she didn't have the strength to open it. It was like her body was leaving her.

He had come straight from Maren Street.

His jaw had stopped actively hurting and settled into the deep specific throb of something that was going to be worse tomorrow, the bruise already developed enough to feel with two fingers if he pressed it, which he did not. He'd walked the twelve blocks with his hands in his pockets and the October cold doing what October cold did, the adrenaline from the apartment burning off somewhere around the seventh block and leaving behind it the specific exhaustion of something that had been carried for a very long time and had finally been put down, though not cleanly, and not in a way that resolved anything.

He had not planned on going to her window. He'd been heading for it before he decided to — the way certain things happened when the rest of the night had emptied out and the only place that felt like the right direction was the one he was already walking toward. He turned onto her street. The upstairs light was on. He crossed the yard, checked the downstairs windows — the blue flicker of the television, her mother settled in for the night — and went around to the side of the house.

He climbed the way he had climbed before, the familiar route, the windowsill he knew. He tapped the glass three times. The pattern.

Nothing.

He waited. Tapped again.

The light was on inside. He could see it through the curtain — the warm yellow of her room, the shadow of the furniture. He pressed his face closer to the glass and looked through the gap at the curtain's edge.

She was on the bed.

She was on the bed and she was not moving, and the specific quality of the stillness was wrong. It was not the stillness of sleep. Her body was positioned the way a body positioned itself when it had simply stopped — one arm at her side, her head turned toward the window, her chest barely moving. From here he could see her lips.

He had the window open in four seconds.

He came through it without sound — weight distributed, feet finding the floor with the economy of someone who had been in enough situations requiring silence to have made it a reflex — and crossed to the bed in three steps and crouched in front of her.

Her eyes were open. Barely — the heavy half-mast of someone fighting to stay above a surface that kept pulling them under. Her pupils were wrong. Tiny. The specific pinpoint that his body recognized before his mind named it, the knowledge arriving the way knowledge arrived when you had grown up in a world where you learned certain things whether you wanted to or not.

He put two fingers to her throat. Her pulse was there — slow, too slow, but there.

Her lips moved.

"Hey." His voice was nothing — not a whisper, less than a whisper, the specific register of someone in a house where a woman slept one floor below. He put his hand against her face. "Hey. Look at me."

Her eyes moved to him. It cost her something visible.

"There you are." He kept his hand against her jaw, kept his voice at nothing, kept his eyes on hers. "Stay with me. You're going to stay with me."

Her mouth moved again. The word was shapeless but he read it — okay — or the attempt at it, the intention of it. He took it.

He looked around the room. The dresser. The orange bottle on its surface, two others beside it. He looked back at her — at the shallow labor of her chest, the color of her lips, the way her hand lay completely open and still at her side.

"I need you to sit up," he breathed. "I need you to help me."

She tried. He could see her trying — the effort moving through her body from somewhere deep and arriving at her arms as almost nothing. He got his hands under her and brought her up himself, slow and careful, her weight against him. She made a sound — not a word, just a sound, the small involuntary expression of a body being moved when movement was difficult. He stopped. Listened. Below them the television continued. Her mother did not move.

He held her upright on the edge of the bed. Her head dropped against his shoulder. He kept one hand flat on her back and felt her breathing — shallow, too shallow, the specific labor of lungs that were being asked to do something they could barely do.

"You need to throw up," he said against her hair. Still nothing, less than nothing. "I know. I know you don't want to. You need to."

She shook her head — the smallest movement, the resistance of someone who felt terrible and was being asked to feel worse.

"Solar." His hand on her back, steady. Her name in his mouth at the register of a secret. "Now."

He got her to the bathroom the way he'd gotten her off the bed — slowly, carrying most of it, her hand gripping his forearm with whatever she had. He eased the door shut behind them. No lock — the click of a lock was too loud. He held it closed with his foot and held her over the toilet and kept one hand on her hair and the other on her back and waited.

It took four minutes. He counted them against the silence of the house, the television below, the October dark outside the small bathroom window. When it was done he got her water from the tap with his hand cupped and made her drink it slowly, three times, until her color started to come back and her breathing deepened and the specific wrongness he had seen in her lips began to recede.

He sat on the bathroom floor with his back against the tub and pulled her against him and stayed there. Her breathing was the only thing. He monitored it the way he monitored things that required monitoring — with the full attention of someone who understood the cost of looking away.

At some point she turned her face up to his.

Her eyes were clearer now. Not all the way — still heavy, still slow — but present in the way she hadn't been when he came through the window. She looked at his face with the specific focus of someone assembling information gradually, detail by detail.

Her hand came up. Her fingers found his jaw — the bruise, the heat of it. She touched it with the careful quality of someone who understood they were touching something that hurt.

He let her.

Her brow pulled together. The question was in her face without words — what happened, what did this, where were you tonight — all of it present and none of it voiced because voicing required energy she didn't have yet.

"Later," he breathed. Not dismissing it. Just later. She held his gaze for another moment and then her head went back against his chest and her hand dropped and he felt the specific shift of her body when it finally gave itself over to sleep — real sleep, not the terrifying sedation of before, the genuine unconsciousness of a body that had been through something and needed to be nowhere for a while.

He waited twenty minutes.

He moved her back to the bed the way he'd moved her everywhere else tonight — carefully, quietly, the specific economy of someone who had one job and was not going to fail it. He laid her down. He checked her breathing again. He stood in the middle of her room for a moment and looked at her — the rise and fall of her chest, steady now, the color of her face back to something recognizable.

Then he went to the dresser.

He picked up the orange bottle. He turned it in his hand — no label, the street supply never had labels, the bottle itself secondhand, repurposed. He opened it and tipped one pill into his palm and looked at it in the light.

He had seen these before.

Not from her. From the east side, from the pipeline he knew the shape of, from the specific distribution chain that ran through the commercial nodes on Maren Street and outward. The color was slightly off from what pharmaceutical Xanax pressed to — not enough to notice if you weren't looking, not enough to register if you'd taken it before and your body told you it was the same thing it had always been. But he was looking. He had grown up learning to look.

He knew what he was holding.

He stood in her room in the October dark with the pill in his palm and the knowledge of it sitting fully assembled in his chest, and for a long moment he was very still. Her breathing behind him, steady. Her mother one floor below, the television. The east side outside the window, the amber light, the three blocks that were already decided.

He tipped the pill back into the bottle.

He picked up the other two bottles. He put all three in his jacket pocket.

He went back to the bed. He crouched in front of her and looked at her face for a moment — the specific face of her, sleeping, the October dark around her. He thought about Jihoon's footage. He thought about Yuna in the hallway. He thought about what it meant that the pills had changed and who was in the position to change them and what that person had decided when he made that decision.

He thought about what he was going to do with that information.

He stood. He crossed to the window. He went out the way he came in — weight distributed, no sound, the night air hitting him at the windowsill, cold and sharp and fully October. He pulled the window down behind him. He climbed back down to the ground.

He stood at the window for a moment. The October dark outside. Her breathing behind him, steady.

He did not leave.

He took his jacket off and folded it over the desk chair. He checked the window — latched, the cool sealed out. He pulled the second blanket from the foot of her bed up over her. He sat on the floor with his back against the bed frame, close enough to hear the rhythm of her breathing, his legs stretched out in front of him and his hands in his lap, three bottles in his jacket pocket across the room.

Below them the television went quiet. Her mother's footsteps moved through the house — the specific geography of someone ending their night, the bathroom, the creak of a door, the house going still. He sat without moving through all of it. His jaw throbbed. He did not examine it.

He sat.

Sometime past midnight she stirred — not waking, just shifting, the small restless movements of a body beginning to metabolize what it had been given and starting to register what was coming. He got up and got her more water from the bathroom and held the glass while she drank it without fully opening her eyes. She made a sound that was not a word. He set the glass on the nightstand and went back to the floor.

He sat until the October dark thinned into the specific grey of early morning — the hour before the east side started, the hour that belonged to nobody, the specific silence of a world that had not yet remembered it had places to be.

He took out his phone. He texted Mark.

not coming in today

The response came in four minutes, which meant Mark had been awake.

okay

Then, after a pause: you good

yeah, he typed. later

He put the phone away.

When the grey through the curtain became actual morning light she woke properly — not all at once, gradually, the way she woke when something in her body was working through something. He heard the change in her breathing first. He got up off the floor and sat on the edge of the bed and waited.

Her eyes opened. She looked at the ceiling for a moment. Then she turned her head and found him.

He watched her put it together — the sequence of the previous night moving through her face in pieces, the arriving and the bathroom and the floor and then, beneath all of it, the specific expression of someone registering that he was still here. That he had stayed.

She looked at him the way she looked at him when she had something to say and was deciding whether to say it.

He spoke first. Quiet — the house still sleeping around them, the morning at its first register. "How do you feel."

She considered the question with the honesty she brought to things when she was too tired to bring anything else. "Wrong," she said. Her voice was rough. "Not like last night. Different wrong."

He nodded. He had been expecting this — the starting of it, the body beginning its inventory of what was no longer there. "That's going to last a few days," he said. "Maybe more."

She looked at him. Something moved through her face — not fear exactly, but the specific sobriety of someone who had just received information about what was coming and was taking it in rather than deflecting it.

"The pills," she said.

"I took them," he said.

She held his gaze. The full weight of what that meant assembled itself between them in the morning quiet — that they were gone, that she was not going to have access to them, that whatever the next several days held she was going to hold them without what she had been using to hold everything else.

Her mouth pressed together once. She looked at the ceiling. She nodded — the smallest thing, but a real one. The specific nod of someone who understood that the thing being asked of them was necessary even though it was going to be very hard.

He reached out and put his hand over hers on the blanket.

"I'm not going anywhere today," he said.

She looked back at him. Her eyes were tired and her face was pale and she was still the most specific person he had ever sat across from in the morning. She turned her hand over and held his.

Outside the window, October continued its program. The east side waking up, the street below beginning its day, the amber light becoming actual morning. School was starting somewhere across the city without either of them.

Neither of them moved.


61: always in each other's orbit

The nausea arrived at seven in the morning like something that had been waiting outside the door.

She had been half-awake since six — the specific restless half-sleep of a body that could not settle, the sheets wrong against her skin, the October morning too bright through the curtains even though the curtains were doing their job. She had been aware of him on the floor, the quiet of him, the way his breathing had told her all night that he was still there. At some point before dawn she had heard him shift — the soft sound of him getting up, crossing to the window, coming back. Checking. She had filed this without fully surfacing from sleep.

At seven the nausea surfaced her completely.

She made it to the bathroom. He was behind her in four seconds — she had not heard him move but he was there, one hand on her back, the other holding her hair back with the specific practicality of someone who was not going to make this more than what it was. When it passed he got her water without being asked. She sat on the bathroom floor with her back against the tub and held the glass and felt the specific misery of a body announcing itself at full volume.

"This is going to be the worst part," he said. He was crouched in front of her, forearms on his knees, looking at her with the honest face. Not pitying. Just present. "The first day usually is."

She looked at him over the rim of the glass. "Usually," she said.

He held her gaze. "Yeah," he said. "Usually."

She looked at him for a moment. "I've done this before," she said. "Not this — not like this. But something like it. When I was fourteen."

Something moved through his expression — not surprise, not pity. Recognition. The specific quality of someone who had just had a thing confirmed that they had already suspected. "I know," he said. "This is worse."

"Yeah," she said. "It is."

She absorbed that. She filed it. She did not ask yet — she did not have the energy yet — but she filed it in the specific place where things went when she intended to return to them.

Her phone was on the nightstand. She had heard it going since seven-fifteen — the specific cascade of notifications that meant the school day had started and her absence had been registered by the people who tracked her. She let it go until eight-thirty, when the volume of it became its own kind of noise.

He brought it to her. She was back in bed by then, propped against the headboard with the extra blanket pulled up, the nausea retreated into the background hum it was apparently going to maintain all day. He sat at the foot of the bed and looked at his own phone while she looked at hers, and the room held the particular quiet of two people in close proximity doing separate things, which was its own specific kind of intimacy.

Cheng first.

Cheng: you're not here

Cheng: sol

Cheng: okay it's been two periods

Cheng: is everything okay

She typed: I'm okay. Sick. Staying home.

His response came in under a minute: okay. let me know if you need anything.

Then, after a pause: both of you?

She looked at the text. She looked at the back of Donghyuck's head. She typed: yes.

Cheng: okay

Cheng, who had known them both for long enough and well enough, asked nothing further. That was the specific thing about Cheng.

Jiwoo was not Cheng.

Jiwoo: SOLAR LEE

Jiwoo: where are you

Jiwoo: I went to your locker AND your homeroom

Jiwoo: Mrs. Park said you're absent?????

Jiwoo: are you sick

Jiwoo: are you okay

Jiwoo: SOLAR

She typed: I'm okay. Sick. Staying home today.

The response was immediate: what kind of sick

Solar looked at the ceiling. She thought about the specific quality of three years of Jiwoo — the color-coded spreadsheets, the gas station coffee, the I can hear your face, the you were never afraid of him. The best friend who had been reading her since freshman year and had been getting closer to the shape of this for weeks.

She typed: not great. stomach thing. I'll explain more later.

Jiwoo: is he there

Solar's mouth curved — the involuntary kind, even through the nausea. yes

Jiwoo: okay

Jiwoo: okay good

Jiwoo: I'm bringing you things after school

Jiwoo: do not tell me not to

I won't, Solar typed. Then: Jiwoo.

Jiwoo: yeah

Thank you for not asking more right now.

A pause. Longer than Jiwoo's usual response time, which meant she was deciding something.

Jiwoo: you can tell me when you're ready

Jiwoo: I'll be there at four

Solar put the phone face-down on the blanket. She looked at the ceiling. The nausea hummed. Outside the window October continued its indifferent program — the east side, the amber light, the street below moving through its Wednesday the way streets moved through Wednesdays.

Enough to be worried. Not the full picture. Jiwoo was going to arrive at four with her bag of things and her face that already knew more than Solar had said, and Solar was going to have to decide how much more to give her.

Later. That was a later problem.

The bad hour came at eleven.

She did not have a better name for it — just the hour when everything the body had been quietly doing became loud, when the cold sweat came and the skin felt wrong and the restlessness arrived in the specific form of not being able to stay still and not being able to move either. She had not known it would feel like this. She had understood, abstractly, that withdrawal was difficult. The abstract version had not accounted for the specific texture of it — the way her legs felt like they needed to be somewhere else, the way her jaw ached, the way the nausea had settled into a permanent low-grade presence that colored everything.

He was in the chair by her desk. He had been there since ten — not hovering, not performing vigilance, just present in the room the way he was present in rooms, the easy quality of someone who had decided to be somewhere and had stopped needing to justify the decision. He had his phone. He had gotten water twice without being asked. He had said almost nothing for an hour, which was the specific thing she needed from him and which he had somehow understood without her saying it.

When the bad hour arrived he moved to the bed without being asked.

He sat above the covers, back against the headboard beside her, close enough that she could feel the warmth of him. She had her knees pulled to her chest and her forehead against them and she was doing the thing she did when things were difficult which was holding very still and waiting for it to pass.

His hand found her back. Flat. Still. The specific weight of someone who understood that this was not a thing to be fixed, only accompanied.

They stayed like that.

At some point the bad hour thinned into something more manageable. Her legs stopped insisting. The cold sweat dried. She uncurled by degrees — not all the way, but enough to turn her head against her knees and look at him sideways.

He looked back.

"You said usually," she said. Her voice was rough from the morning. "Earlier. You said the first day is usually the worst."

He held her gaze. Something moved through his face — the deciding quality, the thing she recognized as him determining how much to give. He gave it.

"I was fourteen," he said. His voice was even. Not the performing-calm even — the other kind, the kind that came from having had enough time with something that it no longer required management. "First year running with DREAM. Jisung was already in it, Mark was already in it. I wanted in." He looked at the window. "I was fourteen and I thought it was going to be one thing and it was a different thing."

She listened. She did not move.

"There were parties," he continued. "Jihoon's parties, before they were what they became — back then they were just the parties, the circuit, the place the product moved and the place you went if you wanted to be in the room where things were decided." He paused. "I was fourteen and stupid and thought trying things was part of the experience. Nobody told me to. Nobody offered. I just—" he stopped. Looked for the right word and apparently did not find one he wanted to use. "I just did."

"What was it," she asked.

"Pills," he said. "Not what I took them to be. The supply on that circuit has never been what it's sold as." His jaw moved — not quite a smile, not quite anything. The expression of someone making a specific accounting. "Took me three weeks to understand what had happened. Another two to get through the other side of it."

She looked at him. She held the weight of it — him at fourteen, in Jihoon's parties, moving through the same rooms she had been moved through on Jihoon's arm. The specific geography of it assembling itself in her chest.

"Jihoon's parties," she said.

"Yeah," he said.

She was quiet for a moment. He was quiet. The October morning held them both.

"I was at those parties," she said. "At fourteen."

He looked at her. The thing in his face was not surprise — which told her everything about whether he had thought about this before. He had. He had been carrying the specific mathematics of it for however long he had known the shape of her history, and he had not said it until now, until this room, until a day when the distance between them had been reduced to nothing by a night that had required him to stay.

"I know," he said.

Two words. The weight of them was specific and total — not I suspected or I figured but I know, the settled acknowledgment of someone who had done the math a long time ago and had been holding it carefully.

"We were in the same rooms," she said.

"Yeah," he said. "We were."

She looked at the window. She thought about fourteen — the specific geography of being fourteen at Jihoon's parties, the way those rooms had felt, what she had understood about them then and what she understood about them now. She thought about him at fourteen in those same rooms, new to DREAM, learning a world he had decided he wanted to belong to. Both of them in the same space, before either of them knew the other's name.

"You wouldn't have known who I was," she said.

"No," he said. "Not then."

"And I wouldn't have—"

"No," he said again. Quietly. Just accurate.

She held that. The specific ache of it — not grief exactly, not regret, something more precise. The sense of two trajectories running in parallel, close enough to touch, not touching. The sense of Jihoon as the connective tissue between them going further back than she had known, the original thing that had occupied both their histories before they had histories with each other.

"He was already building something then," she said. Not a question.

"Yeah," Donghyuck said. "He was always building something. That's the thing about Jihoon. He's never been impulsive. Everything he does—" he stopped. His voice was still even. But something underneath it had shifted, a degree of tension in the jaw, the specific quality of someone keeping something contained because the room and the day did not require it unconstrained. "He plans. He positions. He waits."

She looked at him. The bruise on his jaw had deepened overnight — purple now, the yellow beginning at the edges. She thought about the question she had asked in the dark with the last of her coherence and the word he had given her: later.

"The bruise," she said.

He looked at her.

"You said later," she said.

He was quiet for a moment. Then: "Jisung."

She held his gaze. She waited.

"The expansion," he said. "The line moving east." He paused. "I said some things. He disagreed with the way I said them."

She thought about the three blocks east. She thought about Mrs. Cha's window boxes and what she had understood since the night Cheng had told her about the new timeline. She thought about every piece of paper he had written since sophomore year and what it had cost him to keep writing them.

She reached out and touched his jaw the way she had touched it in the dark — careful, the specific pressure of someone who understood they were touching something that hurt and was choosing to touch it anyway.

He let her.

"Did you win," she asked.

Something moved through his expression. Not the smile — the thing underneath the smile, the thing that was more honest than it. "No," he said. "I never win."

"That's not true," she said.

He looked at her. The honest face, open in the October morning, the bruise and the tiredness and underneath both of those the thing that had been there since the first Saturday in sophomore year and had never once in all that time been something other than what it was.

"No," he said, after a moment. "I guess it isn't."

+

Jiwoo arrived at four-oh-three.

Solar heard the knock from the bed — three times, the specific Jiwoo knock, slightly more urgent than a knock needed to be. She heard him go downstairs, which she had not asked him to do and which she registered as the thing it was — him deciding that Jiwoo should not have to knock twice. She heard the door. She heard Jiwoo's voice, low, saying something she could not make out. She heard his response, equally low. Then footsteps on the stairs.

Jiwoo came through the door with the bag — the same instinct as always, the assembling of specific things — and stopped when she saw Solar's face.

She did not say anything for a moment. She crossed the room and sat on the edge of the bed and looked at Solar with the expression she used when she was taking full inventory before deciding what to produce.

"Hi," Solar said.

"Hi," Jiwoo said. Her voice was careful in the specific way it was careful when she was managing the distance between what she knew and what she was going to ask. She reached into the bag. She produced the gas station coffee — two of them, the aggressively mediocre ones, the specific taste of their friendship going back three years.

She held one out.

Solar took it. She held it in both hands. The warmth of it was specific and familiar and landed somewhere in her chest that the day had left open.

"You don't have to tell me everything," Jiwoo said. "Not today."

"Okay," Solar said.

"But I want you to know," Jiwoo continued, looking at her with the direct quality she brought to things that mattered, "that I've been watching your face for three years and I know what it looks like when something is wrong and I know what it looks like when something has been wrong for a while." She paused. "And I'm not going anywhere."

Solar looked at her. The nausea was still there, low and persistent. Her legs ached. The October afternoon came through the curtains doing the October thing, the amber light, the east side outside.

"I know," Solar said. "I know you're not."

Jiwoo nodded. She settled onto the bed the way she settled into Solar's spaces — with the ease of someone who had been doing it for long enough that the space had adjusted to accommodate her. She did not ask more. She opened her own coffee. She looked at the window.

They sat.

From downstairs, the quiet of him moving through her house — the specific sound of someone who had been there all day and had learned its geography, the kitchen, the tap, the ordinary sounds of a person who had decided to stay and was making no production of it.

Jiwoo heard it. She looked at Solar.

Solar looked back.

Something passed between them that did not require words — the specific communication of a friendship that had survived enough to develop its own language. Jiwoo's expression moved through something, arrived somewhere warm, and stayed there.

She looked back at the window.

"Okay," she said, simply.

Outside, October ran its late afternoon. The east side in the amber light, the street doing its thing, the season holding its specific quality of time moving in only one direction.

Inside, the room held all three of them — Solar in the bed, Jiwoo at her side, him somewhere below them in the house — in the particular warmth of a day that had been very hard and was ending anyway, the way days did, regardless.

Something almost surfaced in her. The word for it was specific and she knew its shape and it sat very close to the surface of the afternoon, close enough that if the day had been different — if Jiwoo hadn't been here, if the nausea hadn't been humming, if the October light hadn't been doing the exact thing it was doing — it might have arrived.

It didn't arrive.

But it was close.

It was the closest it had ever been.


62: confirming what he knows already

She smelled it before she heard him.

Butter. Something warm. The specific domestic smell of a kitchen being used by someone who knew what they were doing with it, drifting up through the floorboards of the house in the way smells moved through old houses — through the gaps, through the vents, arriving at the second floor as a quiet announcement.

She opened her eyes.

The room was darker than it had been. The October afternoon had moved further toward evening while she slept, the amber light through the curtains gone thin and low. Jiwoo was still on the bed beside her — legs crossed, phone in hand, doing the thing she did when she was waiting for something and had decided to be patient about it. She looked up when Solar stirred.

"He's been down there for forty minutes," Jiwoo observed, with the expression of someone who had been registering this for some time and had not yet decided how to categorize it.

Solar turned her head toward the door. The smell was more specific now — eggs, definitely. Something sweet underneath it.

"Did he—" she started.

"I don't know," Jiwoo said. "I haven't investigated." A pause. "I was going to. But then I didn't want to miss the context."

They heard his footsteps on the stairs — the specific weight of him, unhurried, the creak of the third step from the top that had been creaking since Solar was nine years old. The door opened.

He came through it carrying two plates balanced with the careful economy of someone who had figured out the geometry of it and was not going to drop anything. His hair was doing the thing it did when he had been awake too long and had stopped managing it — dark and falling forward into his face, the specific dishevelment of someone who had not slept in over twenty-four hours and was not going to mention it. His shirt was the same one from last night. He had pushed the sleeves up.

On each plate: pancakes, slightly uneven at the edges in the way of homemade ones. Eggs on the side. A glass of water balanced in the crook of his elbow with the focused precision of someone who had committed to not making two trips.

He set Jiwoo's plate on the nightstand. He set Solar's on the bed in front of her. He set the water beside it. He straightened and looked at them both with the expression that was not asking for a response.

Jiwoo stared at the plate.

Then she looked at Solar.

Then she looked at him.

"Okay," she said, slowly, in the register of someone revising a previously held position. "Okay." She picked up the fork. She pointed it at him with the specific authority of Jiwoo making a declaration. "I want you to know that this is genuinely the most romantic thing I have personally witnessed. From a gang member. In this kitchen. On a Thursday."

Something moved at the corner of his mouth. Not quite the smile — the thing adjacent to it, the thing that happened when he found something unexpectedly funny and was not going to admit it. He looked at Solar.

Solar looked at the pancakes. She looked at him. Something in her chest did the thing it did in the specific moments when he was simply and entirely himself with no management around it — when the performing ease was absent and what remained was just him, standing in her room with his hair in his face and two plates he had made from whatever her kitchen had available because she had not eaten in twenty-four hours and it had not occurred to him to do anything other than fix that.

"Thank you," she said.

He held her gaze for a moment. Then he sat in the chair and picked up his own fork from his pocket — he had brought one for himself, she realized, and had not brought a plate — and reached over and took a piece of her pancake without asking.

"Donghyuck," she said.

"You have too much," he said, without looking up.

Jiwoo made a sound that was not quite a laugh. Solar looked at her. Jiwoo looked back with the expression that was not quite anything she could name except that it was warm and very Jiwoo and was going to require a conversation later that Solar was going to have to be more functional to participate in.

She ate.

It was the first thing she had kept down all day. The specific relief of it — her body accepting something without immediately rejecting it — landed somewhere practical and real. The pancakes were better than they had any right to be from her kitchen at eight in the evening on a Thursday with whatever he had found in the cabinets.

She did not say this. She ate approximately half of what was on the plate before her body indicated it was done.

Jiwoo finished hers and looked at the empty plate with the expression of someone genuinely surprised at themselves. She set it on the nightstand and looked at Donghyuck.

"Is there more," she asked.

"Batter's still down there," he said.

"I'm going to investigate," she announced, and stood, and then paused with the specific quality of someone who had just thought of something useful. She looked at Solar. "Do you have Tylenol? For the aches."

Solar considered. "Medicine cabinet. Or under the sink, maybe. I'm not sure."

Jiwoo looked at Donghyuck. "I'll need a guide. I don't know where anything is in this house."

This was, Solar noted, not entirely true. Jiwoo had been in this house since freshman year and knew its geography reasonably well. But she said nothing. She registered the specific quality of Jiwoo's expression — the decision that had been made somewhere in the last forty minutes while she was sitting on the bed being patient — and she let it happen.

Donghyuck looked at Jiwoo for a moment. Something moved between them — the briefest exchange, the specific communication of two people who were each, separately, smarter than average and had both arrived at the same understanding about what this moment was.

He stood. He followed Jiwoo out the door.

Solar leaned back against the headboard and looked at the ceiling and listened to their footsteps go down the stairs together.

The kitchen was the way kitchens were after someone had cooked in them — the pan on the stove, the bowl of remaining batter on the counter, the specific comfortable disorder of a space that had been used. Jiwoo went to the cabinet above the refrigerator with the confidence of someone who had decided to commit to the premise, and found nothing. She tried the one beside the sink. Also nothing.

Donghyuck was at the counter, looking at the batter, not making more pancakes.

Jiwoo turned from the cabinet and looked at him. She crossed her arms. She leaned against the counter across from him.

"Tell me," she said.

He looked at her. The assessment — quick, specific, the quality he brought to rooms he was trying to read. He looked at the counter for a moment. Then he looked at her.

"The pills she's been taking," he said. "They're not what she thought they were."

Jiwoo held very still. "How long."

"The current supply came from Yuna. Junior year, and now." He paused. "The supply changed somewhere between then and now. What she had before and what she got this time are different things."

"Different how," Jiwoo said. Flat. The specific register of someone who wanted the accurate version rather than the managed version.

He looked at her steadily. "Different enough to put her on the floor last night."

The kitchen held the sound of that. The refrigerator's hum. The October dark pressing against the window above the sink.

Jiwoo's jaw tightened once. He watched her do the thing he had watched her do upstairs — the assembling, the pieces finding their positions, the full picture arriving in a mind that was very good at building pictures. She did not perform the arrival. She just stood across from him in Solar's kitchen and let it land.

"Yuna," she said. "Which means the supply came from somewhere above Yuna."

He said nothing. He did not need to.

"Jihoon," she said.

Still nothing. Still looking at her. She read the confirmation in it the way she read everything — directly, without requiring it to be stated.

She looked at the window. The October street outside. A long moment passed in which she was somewhere internal, doing something with the information that was not grief exactly and not rage exactly but had the specific texture of both being present simultaneously and being managed rather than expressed.

Then she looked back at him.

"The first time," she said. "At fourteen. That was him too."

"Yeah," he said.

She exhaled through her nose — once, controlled. "What are you going to do about it."

"Handle it," he said. "Not tonight. But soon."

She looked at him the way she had looked at him on the porch in his jacket, the inventory running its final pass. He let it run. Whatever she arrived at she kept to herself, except for the single thing she said next, which she said in the flat direct register of someone who had decided to trust a person and was being clear about the terms.

"If you handle it in a way that comes back on her," she said, "I will make your life very difficult."

He held her gaze. "It won't," he said.

"I know it won't," she said. "I just wanted to say it."

Something at the corner of his mouth — the same thing as upstairs, the thing that was not quite the smile. "Okay," he said.

Jiwoo looked at the cabinet above the refrigerator again. She reached up and opened the one she hadn't tried yet and found, on the second shelf, a bottle of Tylenol behind a box of cold medicine and a very old tube of something she did not examine.

She held it up.

"Found it," she announced.

He turned and poured the remaining batter into the pan.

She was asleep again by nine-thirty.

The Tylenol had taken the edge off the aches. The pancakes had held. The specific depletion of a body coming through something difficult had done the rest — pulling her under cleanly, the deep even sleep of someone who had nothing left to spend on being conscious.

Donghyuck sat in the chair and looked at her for a long moment.

Then he reached into his jacket pocket. He held the two bottles in his palm — the orange of them, the street supply, the repurposed containers that had no label and no pharmacy and no chain of custody except the one he had spent the last two days tracing backward to its source. He looked at them the way he looked at things he had decided about.

He stood. He went to the door. He paused and looked back at her once — the rise and fall of her chest, steady, the specific color of her face returning to something that was hers. Then he went downstairs.

Jiwoo was on the couch with her phone. She looked up.

"I'll be back in two hours," he said. "Her mother gets home at nine. Can you—"

"Yes," Jiwoo said. She did not look up from her phone again.

He went out.

Park was in the back room of the dry cleaner on Maren East, the back light on, the specific unhurried quality of someone who had been in this business long enough to have stopped performing anything. He looked at Donghyuck over his glasses when he came through the rear entrance and did not say anything.

Donghyuck put both bottles on the table.

Park picked up the first one. He opened it, tipped a single pill into his palm, held it under the light. Set it down. Picked up the second. The same process — the focused attention of someone reading something that required accurate reading.

He set the second bottle down.

He looked at Donghyuck.

"Where did these come from," he asked. His voice was flat — not alarmed. Precise.

"East circuit," Donghyuck said. "Through Yuna."

Park made the sound he made when information confirmed something he had been tracking. He reached for the kit on the shelf behind him — the strips, the vials, the equipment of someone who ran these verifications regularly — and worked without commentary. Four minutes. Clean and efficient, the way Park did everything.

He looked at the strip.

"Fentanyl," he said. "Full substitution. No alprazolam in either bottle. Whoever pressed these wasn't cutting product — they pulled the product entirely and replaced it." He set the strip down. "Whoever was taking these thinking it was Xanax—"

"I know," Donghyuck said.

Park looked at him for a moment. Then he looked at the bottles. "You want documentation."

"Keep one," Donghyuck said. "I take the other."

Park nodded. He kept the first bottle. He handed the second back. The arrangement was its own language — Park's bottle was documentation, chain of custody, the hard evidence version of what Donghyuck already knew. If it was ever needed, it existed.

Donghyuck put the remaining bottle in his jacket pocket.

He looked at it for a moment before he zipped the pocket closed.

Full substitution. The specific language of it. Not an accident, not a contaminated batch, not the ordinary danger of a supply chain that had never been clean. A deliberate replacement. Someone at the source level of the east circuit had looked at what Solar was taking and had decided to change it to something that would cost more, require more, escalate faster, and be invisible to anyone who didn't know what to look for.

The architecture of it was patient. Deliberate. Built by someone who understood exactly what they were doing and had done it anyway.

He knew exactly who that was.

He went back out through the rear entrance into the October night.

The east side ran its Thursday program around him — the amber light, the street doing what streets did in the hours after dinner and before midnight, the specific ordinary texture of a world that had not stopped while the last two days happened inside a second-floor bedroom six blocks north.

He stood outside the dry cleaner for a moment with his hands in his pockets and the bottle in the zipped pocket of his jacket and the specific cold knowledge of what came next sitting fully assembled in his chest.

Not tonight.

The cold version required more than tonight's anger, which was not cold. Tonight's anger was the real thing — present and specific and carrying the full weight of her on the floor, her breathing wrong, the color of her lips, her voice at less than a whisper saying okay because she was trying to reassure him when she was the one who needed reassuring. Tonight's anger would put Jihoon through a wall and leave evidence and solve nothing.

What he was building required the other version. The one that arrived when the anger had been processed into something more precise. The one that used what Park had just confirmed as the instrument rather than the motivation.

He knew how to build that version. He had learned it young.

He turned north toward Solar's street and walked.

The lights were on downstairs when he came up the porch steps. Through the window he could see the specific shape of Jiwoo on the couch — and beside her, her mother, the two of them in the careful posture of people finding their way through a conversation neither of them had fully prepared for.

He did not go in.

He sat on the top porch step and looked at the October street and waited. The east side settled into its late evening register around him. A car passed. Someone's window was lit two houses down. The season ran its cold program through the air.

After twenty minutes the door opened behind him.

Jiwoo came out. She pulled the door to behind her and sat on the step beside him — not close, just adjacent — and looked at the street.

"Her mother thinks it's a stomach virus," she said. "I was very convincing."

"Thank you," he said.

She looked at the street. "Did you get what you needed."

"Yeah," he said.

A pause. The October cold. The street.

"It's him," she said. Not a question.

"Yeah," he said.

Jiwoo nodded once. She pulled her sleeves over her hands. She looked at the east side doing its thing in the dark, the amber light, the season fully committed to what it was.

"Okay," she said.

The same okay as in the kitchen. The same terms. The specific word of someone who had decided to trust a process they were not going to be part of and was being clear that the trust had a boundary and the boundary was Solar.

He understood the boundary.

"I'm going back in," Jiwoo said. She stood. She looked down at him — the last inventory, the final pass of it. Whatever she concluded she kept. "Go home and sleep," she said. "You look terrible."

He looked up at her.

"You do," she said, without apology. "Go."

He stayed on the step for another moment after she went inside. He looked at the street. He thought about what was in his jacket pocket and what it confirmed and what it was going to be used for.

Then he stood.

He went home.
